question_html: Two small solid metal balls A and B of radii R and 2 R having charge densities {tex}2 \sigma{/tex} and {tex}3 \sigma{/tex} respectively are kept far apart. Find the charge densities on A and B after they are connected by a conducting wire.
question_text: Two small solid metal balls A and B of radii R and 2 R having charge densities {tex}2 \sigma{/tex} and {tex}3 \sigma{/tex} respectively are kept far apart. Find the charge densities on A and B after they are connected by a conducting wire.
explanation: For ball A {tex} {q}_1 =2 \sigma \times 4 \pi {R}^2 {/tex} {tex} =8 \pi {R}^2 \sigma {/tex} For ball B {tex} {q}_2 =3 \sigma \times 4 \pi(2 {R})^2 {/tex} {tex} =48 \pi {R}^2 \sigma {/tex} {tex} \text { Total charge }({Q})={q}_1+{q}_2 {/tex} {tex} =56 \pi {R}^2 \sigma {/tex} When balls A and B are connected by a wire, their potentials will be equal Let {tex}q{/tex} be the charge on ball {tex}A{/tex} and {tex}(Q-q){/tex} be the charge on the ball {tex}B{/tex} after connecting wire. {tex} \frac{K q}{R} =\frac{K(Q-q)}{2 R} {/tex} {tex} 2 q =Q-q {/tex} {tex} q =\frac{Q}{3} {/tex} {tex} Q-\frac{Q}{3}=\frac{112 \pi R^2 \sigma}{3} {/tex} {tex} \sigma_A =\frac{\frac{56 \pi R^2 \sigma}{3}}{4 \pi R^2} {/tex} {tex} =\frac{14}{3} \sigma {/tex} {tex} \sigma_B =\frac{112 \pi R^2 \sigma}{3} {/tex} {tex} =\frac{7}{3} \sigma {/tex}
solution: For ball A {tex} {q}_1 =2 \sigma \times 4 \pi {R}^2 {/tex} {tex} =8 \pi {R}^2 \sigma {/tex} For ball B {tex} {q}_2 =3 \sigma \times 4 \pi(2 {R})^2 {/tex} {tex} =48 \pi {R}^2 \sigma {/tex} {tex} \text { Total charge }({Q})={q}_1+{q}_2 {/tex} {tex} =56 \pi {R}^2 \sigma {/tex} When balls A and B are connected by a wire, their potentials will be equal Let {tex}q{/tex} be the charge on ball {tex}A{/tex} and {tex}(Q-q){/tex} be the charge on the ball {tex}B{/tex} after connecting wire. {tex} \frac{K q}{R} =\frac{K(Q-q)}{2 R} {/tex} {tex} 2 q =Q-q {/tex} {tex} q =\frac{Q}{3} {/tex} {tex} Q-\frac{Q}{3}=\frac{112 \pi R^2 \sigma}{3} {/tex} {tex} \sigma_A =\frac{\frac{56 \pi R^2 \sigma}{3}}{4 \pi R^2} {/tex} {tex} =\frac{14}{3} \sigma {/tex} {tex} \sigma_B =\frac{112 \pi R^2 \sigma}{3} {/tex} {tex} =\frac{7}{3} \sigma {/tex}
solution_html:
For ball A
{tex} {q}_1 =2 \sigma \times 4 \pi {R}^2 {/tex}
{tex} =8 \pi {R}^2 \sigma {/tex}
For ball B
{tex} {q}_2 =3 \sigma \times 4 \pi(2 {R})^2 {/tex}
{tex} =48 \pi {R}^2 \sigma {/tex}
{tex} \text { Total charge }({Q})={q}_1+{q}_2 {/tex}
{tex} =56 \pi {R}^2 \sigma {/tex}
When balls A and B are connected by a wire, their potentials will be equal Let {tex}q{/tex} be the charge on ball {tex}A{/tex} and {tex}(Q-q){/tex} be the charge on the ball {tex}B{/tex} after connecting wire.
{tex} \frac{K q}{R} =\frac{K(Q-q)}{2 R} {/tex}
{tex} 2 q =Q-q {/tex}
{tex} q =\frac{Q}{3} {/tex}
{tex} Q-\frac{Q}{3}=\frac{112 \pi R^2 \sigma}{3} {/tex}
{tex} \sigma_A =\frac{\frac{56 \pi R^2 \sigma}{3}}{4 \pi R^2} {/tex}
{tex} =\frac{14}{3} \sigma {/tex}
{tex} \sigma_B =\frac{112 \pi R^2 \sigma}{3} {/tex}
{tex} =\frac{7}{3} \sigma {/tex}

subject: Physics
question_html:
A parallel plate capacitor has plate area A and plate separation d. Half of the space between the plates is filled with a material of dielectric constant K in two ways as shown in the figure.

Find the values of the capacitance of the capacitors in the two cases.
question_text: A parallel plate capacitor has plate area A and plate separation d. Half of the space between the plates is filled with a material of dielectric constant K in two ways as shown in the figure. Find the values of the capacitance of the capacitors in the two cases.
explanation: {tex} \frac{1}{C} =\frac{1}{K\left(\frac{\varepsilon_0 A}{d / 2}\right)}+\frac{1}{\frac{\varepsilon_0 A}{d / 2}} {/tex} {tex} \frac{1}{C} =\frac{d}{2 K \varepsilon_0 A}+\frac{d}{2 \varepsilon_0 A} {/tex} {tex} =\left(\frac{1}{K}+1\right) \frac{d}{2 \varepsilon_0 A} {/tex} {tex} C =\left(\frac{2 K}{K+1}\right) \frac{\varepsilon_0 A}{d} {/tex} {tex} C =\frac{\varepsilon_0 A K}{2 d}+\frac{\varepsilon_0 A}{2 d} {/tex} {tex} =\left(\frac{K+1}{2}\right) \frac{\varepsilon_0 A}{d} {/tex}
solution: {tex} \frac{1}{C} =\frac{1}{K\left(\frac{\varepsilon_0 A}{d / 2}\right)}+\frac{1}{\frac{\varepsilon_0 A}{d / 2}} {/tex} {tex} \frac{1}{C} =\frac{d}{2 K \varepsilon_0 A}+\frac{d}{2 \varepsilon_0 A} {/tex} {tex} =\left(\frac{1}{K}+1\right) \frac{d}{2 \varepsilon_0 A} {/tex} {tex} C =\left(\frac{2 K}{K+1}\right) \frac{\varepsilon_0 A}{d} {/tex} {tex} C =\frac{\varepsilon_0 A K}{2 d}+\frac{\varepsilon_0 A}{2 d} {/tex} {tex} =\left(\frac{K+1}{2}\right) \frac{\varepsilon_0 A}{d} {/tex}
solution_html:
1. {tex} \frac{1}{C} =\frac{1}{K\left(\frac{\varepsilon_0 A}{d / 2}\right)}+\frac{1}{\frac{\varepsilon_0 A}{d / 2}} {/tex}
    {tex} \frac{1}{C} =\frac{d}{2 K \varepsilon_0 A}+\frac{d}{2 \varepsilon_0 A} {/tex}
    {tex} =\left(\frac{1}{K}+1\right) \frac{d}{2 \varepsilon_0 A} {/tex}
    {tex} C =\left(\frac{2 K}{K+1}\right) \frac{\varepsilon_0 A}{d} {/tex}
  2. {tex} C =\frac{\varepsilon_0 A K}{2 d}+\frac{\varepsilon_0 A}{2 d} {/tex}
    {tex} =\left(\frac{K+1}{2}\right) \frac{\varepsilon_0 A}{d} {/tex}

question_html:
1. An electric dipole of dipole moment {tex}p{/tex} consists of point charges q and -q, separated by 2 a. Derive an expression for electric potential in terms of its dipole moment at a point at a distance {tex}{x}(\gg {a}){/tex} from its centre and lying
    1. along its axis, and
    2. along its bisector line.
  2. An electric dipole of dipole moment {tex}\vec{p}=(0.8 \hat{i}+0.6 \hat{j}) 10^{-29} {Cm}{/tex} is placed in an electric field {tex}\overrightarrow{{E}}=1.0 \times 10^{7} \hat{{k}} \frac{{V}}{{m}}{/tex}. Calculate the magnitude of the torque acting on it and the angle it makes with the x-axis, at this instant.
question_text: An electric dipole of dipole moment {tex}p{/tex} consists of point charges q and -q, separated by 2 a. Derive an expression for electric potential in terms of its dipole moment at a point at a distance {tex}{x}(\gg {a}){/tex} from its centre and lying along its axis, and along its bisector line. An electric dipole of dipole moment {tex}\vec{p}=(0.8 \hat{i}+0.6 \hat{j}) 10^{-29} {Cm}{/tex} is placed in an electric field {tex}\overrightarrow{{E}}=1.0 \times 10^{7} \hat{{k}} \frac{{V}}{{m}}{/tex} . Calculate the magnitude of the torque acting on it and the angle it makes with the x-axis, at this instant.
explanation: Along its axis {tex} {V}_{-} =\frac{-{kq}}{{x}+{a}} {/tex} {tex} {V}_{+} =\frac{{kq}}{{x}-{a}} {/tex} {tex} {V} ={V}_{-}+{V}_{+} {/tex} {tex} ={kq}\left(\frac{-1}{{x}+{a}}+\frac{1}{{x}-{a}}\right) {/tex} {tex} ={kq} \frac{2 {a}}{\left({x}^2-{a}^2\right)}=\frac{{kp}}{{x}^2-{a}^2} {/tex} {tex}\mathrm{x} \gg \mathrm{a} \ \therefore \mathrm{~V}=\frac{\mathrm{kp}}{\mathrm{x}^2}{/tex} Along the bisector line {tex} \vec{\tau} =\vec{p} \times \vec{E} {/tex} {tex} =(0.8 \hat{i}+0.6 \hat{j}) \times 10^{-29} \times\left(1 \times 10^7\right) \hat{k} {/tex} {tex} =[0.8(-\hat{j})+0.6 \hat{i}] \times 10^{-22} {/tex} {tex} \tau =\left[\sqrt{(0.8)^2+(0.6)^2}\right] \times 10^{-22} {/tex} {tex} =10^{-22} {Nm} {/tex} {tex} \tan \alpha =\frac{|0.8|}{0.6} {/tex} {tex} \alpha =\tan ^{-1}\left(\frac{4}{3}\right) {/tex} {tex} \alpha =53^{\circ} {/tex}
solution: Along its axis {tex} {V}_{-} =\frac{-{kq}}{{x}+{a}} {/tex} {tex} {V}_{+} =\frac{{kq}}{{x}-{a}} {/tex} {tex} {V} ={V}_{-}+{V}_{+} {/tex} {tex} ={kq}\left(\frac{-1}{{x}+{a}}+\frac{1}{{x}-{a}}\right) {/tex} {tex} ={kq} \frac{2 {a}}{\left({x}^2-{a}^2\right)}=\frac{{kp}}{{x}^2-{a}^2} {/tex} {tex}\mathrm{x} \gg \mathrm{a} \ \therefore \mathrm{~V}=\frac{\mathrm{kp}}{\mathrm{x}^2}{/tex} Along the bisector line {tex} \vec{\tau} =\vec{p} \times \vec{E} {/tex} {tex} =(0.8 \hat{i}+0.6 \hat{j}) \times 10^{-29} \times\left(1 \times 10^7\right) \hat{k} {/tex} {tex} =[0.8(-\hat{j})+0.6 \hat{i}] \times 10^{-22} {/tex} {tex} \tau =\left[\sqrt{(0.8)^2+(0.6)^2}\right] \times 10^{-22} {/tex} {tex} =10^{-22} {Nm} {/tex} {tex} \tan \alpha =\frac{|0.8|}{0.6} {/tex} {tex} \alpha =\tan ^{-1}\left(\frac{4}{3}\right) {/tex} {tex} \alpha =53^{\circ} {/tex}
solution_html:
1. Along its axis

      {tex} {V}_{-} =\frac{-{kq}}{{x}+{a}} {/tex}
      {tex} {V}_{+} =\frac{{kq}}{{x}-{a}} {/tex}
      {tex} {V} ={V}_{-}+{V}_{+} {/tex}
      {tex} ={kq}\left(\frac{-1}{{x}+{a}}+\frac{1}{{x}-{a}}\right) {/tex}
      {tex} ={kq} \frac{2 {a}}{\left({x}^2-{a}^2\right)}=\frac{{kp}}{{x}^2-{a}^2} {/tex}
      {tex}\mathrm{x} \gg \mathrm{a} \ \therefore \mathrm{~V}=\frac{\mathrm{kp}}{\mathrm{x}^2}{/tex}
    2. Along the bisector line
  2. {tex} \vec{\tau} =\vec{p} \times \vec{E} {/tex}
    {tex} =(0.8 \hat{i}+0.6 \hat{j}) \times 10^{-29} \times\left(1 \times 10^7\right) \hat{k} {/tex}
    {tex} =[0.8(-\hat{j})+0.6 \hat{i}] \times 10^{-22} {/tex}
    {tex} \tau =\left[\sqrt{(0.8)^2+(0.6)^2}\right] \times 10^{-22} {/tex}
    {tex} =10^{-22} {Nm} {/tex}
    {tex} \tan \alpha =\frac{|0.8|}{0.6} {/tex}
    {tex} \alpha =\tan ^{-1}\left(\frac{4}{3}\right) {/tex}
    {tex} \alpha =53^{\circ} {/tex}

subject: Physics
question_html:
1. The electric field in a region is given by {tex}\vec{E}=40 x \hat{i} N / C{/tex}. Find the amount of work done in taking a unit positive charge from a point {tex}(0,3 {~m}){/tex} to the point {tex}(5 {~m}, 0){/tex}.
  2. A charge Q is distributed over two concentric hollow spheres of radii {tex}r{/tex} and {tex}R( \gt r){/tex} such that their surface charge densities are equal. Find:
    1. the electric field, and
    2. the potential
      at their common centre.
question_text: The electric field in a region is given by {tex}\vec{E}=40 x \hat{i} N / C{/tex} . Find the amount of work done in taking a unit positive charge from a point {tex}(0,3 {~m}){/tex} to the point {tex}(5 {~m}, 0){/tex} . A charge Q is distributed over two concentric hollow spheres of radii {tex}r{/tex} and {tex}R( \gt r){/tex} such that their surface charge densities are equal. Find: the electric field, and the potential at their common centre.
explanation: {tex}V =-\int \vec{E} \cdot \overrightarrow{d r}{/tex} {tex}=-\int 40 x d x{/tex} {tex}=-20 x^{2}{/tex} Potential at {tex}{A}(0,3 {~m}), V_{A}=0{/tex} Potential at B (5m, 0), {tex}V_{B}=-500 {~V}{/tex} Work done in taking a unit positive charge from a point {tex}(0,3 {~m}){/tex} to the point {tex}(5 {~m}{/tex} , 0) {tex}{W} ={q}\left({~V}_{{B}}-{V}_{{A}}\right){/tex} {tex}=1(-500-0){/tex} {tex}{W} =-500 {~J}{/tex} Electric field at the common centre will be zero as the charge enclosed by the inner sphere is zero. {tex}\because \text { Surface charge densities are equal }{/tex} {tex}\frac{{q}}{4 \pi {r}^{2}}=\frac{{Q}-{q}}{4 \pi {R}^{2}}{/tex} {tex}{q}=\frac{{Qr}^{2}}{{R}^{2}+{r}^{2}}{/tex} Potential at common centre {tex}{V} =\frac{{kq}}{{r}}+\frac{{k}({Q}-{q})}{{R}}{/tex} {tex}V =\frac{{k}}{{r}} \frac{{Qr}^{2}}{\left({R}^{2}+{r}^{2}\right)}+\frac{{k}}{{R}}\left[{Q}-\frac{{Qr}^{2}}{\left({R}^{2}+{r}^{2}\right)}\right]{/tex} {tex}V =\frac{{kQ}({R}+{r})}{{R}^{2}+{r}^{2}}{/tex}
solution: {tex}V =-\int \vec{E} \cdot \overrightarrow{d r}{/tex} {tex}=-\int 40 x d x{/tex} {tex}=-20 x^{2}{/tex} Potential at {tex}{A}(0,3 {~m}), V_{A}=0{/tex} Potential at B (5m, 0), {tex}V_{B}=-500 {~V}{/tex} Work done in taking a unit positive charge from a point {tex}(0,3 {~m}){/tex} to the point {tex}(5 {~m}{/tex} , 0) {tex}{W} ={q}\left({~V}_{{B}}-{V}_{{A}}\right){/tex} {tex}=1(-500-0){/tex} {tex}{W} =-500 {~J}{/tex} Electric field at the common centre will be zero as the charge enclosed by the inner sphere is zero. {tex}\because \text { Surface charge densities are equal }{/tex} {tex}\frac{{q}}{4 \pi {r}^{2}}=\frac{{Q}-{q}}{4 \pi {R}^{2}}{/tex} {tex}{q}=\frac{{Qr}^{2}}{{R}^{2}+{r}^{2}}{/tex} Potential at common centre {tex}{V} =\frac{{kq}}{{r}}+\frac{{k}({Q}-{q})}{{R}}{/tex} {tex}V =\frac{{k}}{{r}} \frac{{Qr}^{2}}{\left({R}^{2}+{r}^{2}\right)}+\frac{{k}}{{R}}\left[{Q}-\frac{{Qr}^{2}}{\left({R}^{2}+{r}^{2}\right)}\right]{/tex} {tex}V =\frac{{kQ}({R}+{r})}{{R}^{2}+{r}^{2}}{/tex}
solution_html:
1. {tex}V =-\int \vec{E} \cdot \overrightarrow{d r}{/tex}
    {tex}=-\int 40 x d x{/tex}
    {tex}=-20 x^{2}{/tex}
    Potential at {tex}{A}(0,3 {~m}), V_{A}=0{/tex}
    Potential at B (5m, 0), {tex}V_{B}=-500 {~V}{/tex}
    Work done in taking a unit positive charge from a point {tex}(0,3 {~m}){/tex} to the point {tex}(5 {~m}{/tex}, 0)
    {tex}{W} ={q}\left({~V}_{{B}}-{V}_{{A}}\right){/tex}
    {tex}=1(-500-0){/tex}
    {tex}{W} =-500 {~J}{/tex}
    1. Electric field at the common centre will be zero as the charge enclosed by the inner sphere is zero.
    2. {tex}\because \text { Surface charge densities are equal }{/tex}
      {tex}\frac{{q}}{4 \pi {r}^{2}}=\frac{{Q}-{q}}{4 \pi {R}^{2}}{/tex}
      {tex}{q}=\frac{{Qr}^{2}}{{R}^{2}+{r}^{2}}{/tex}
      Potential at common centre
      {tex}{V} =\frac{{kq}}{{r}}+\frac{{k}({Q}-{q})}{{R}}{/tex}
      {tex}V =\frac{{k}}{{r}} \frac{{Qr}^{2}}{\left({R}^{2}+{r}^{2}\right)}+\frac{{k}}{{R}}\left[{Q}-\frac{{Qr}^{2}}{\left({R}^{2}+{r}^{2}\right)}\right]{/tex}
      {tex}V =\frac{{kQ}({R}+{r})}{{R}^{2}+{r}^{2}}{/tex}

subject: Physics
question_html:
1. A parallel plate capacitor with plate area A and plate separation {tex}d{/tex} has a capacitance {tex}C_{0}{/tex}. A slab of dielectric constant {tex}K{/tex} having area A and thickness {tex}\left(\frac{d}{4}\right){/tex} is inserted in the capacitor, parallel to the plates. Find the new value of its capacitance.
  2. You are provided with a large number of {tex}1 \mu {~F}{/tex} identical capacitors and a power supply of 1200 V. The dielectric medium used in each capacitor can withstand up to 200 V only. Find the minimum number of capacitors and their arrangement, required to build a capacitor system of equivalent capacitance of {tex}2 \mu {~F}{/tex} for use with this supply.
question_text: A parallel plate capacitor with plate area A and plate separation {tex}d{/tex} has a capacitance {tex}C_{0}{/tex} . A slab of dielectric constant {tex}K{/tex} having area A and thickness {tex}\left(\frac{d}{4}\right){/tex} is inserted in the capacitor, parallel to the plates. Find the new value of its capacitance. You are provided with a large number of {tex}1 \mu {~F}{/tex} identical capacitors and a power supply of 1200 V. The dielectric medium used in each capacitor can withstand up to 200 V only. Find the minimum number of capacitors and their arrangement, required to build a capacitor system of equivalent capacitance of {tex}2 \mu {~F}{/tex} for use with this supply.
explanation: {tex} {C}_0 =\frac{\varepsilon_0 {~A}}{{~d}} {/tex} {tex} {C} =\frac{\varepsilon_0 {~A}}{({~d}-{t})+\frac{{t}}{K}} {/tex} {tex} {t} ={d} / 4 {/tex} {tex} {C} =\frac{\varepsilon_0 {~A}}{\left({~d}-\frac{{d}}{4}\right)+\frac{{d}}{4 {~K}}}=\frac{\varepsilon_0 {~A}}{{~d}\left(\frac{3}{4}+\frac{1}{4 {~K}}\right)} {/tex} {tex} ={C}_0 \frac{4 {~K}}{(3 {~K}+1)} {/tex} Each capacitance can withstand 200 V No. of capacitors in each row {tex}=\frac{1200}{200}=6{/tex} Net capacitance of each row {tex}=1 / 6 \mu {~F}{/tex} Number of rows {tex}={n}{/tex} {tex} C_{e q} =C_1+C_2+------+C_n {/tex} {tex} C_{e q} =\frac{1}{6}+\frac{1}{6}+-----n {/tex} {tex} 2 =\frac{n}{6} {/tex} {tex} \therefore n=12 {/tex} {tex} \text { Total no. of capacitors in the arrangement } =6 \times 12 {/tex} {tex} =72 {/tex}
solution: {tex} {C}_0 =\frac{\varepsilon_0 {~A}}{{~d}} {/tex} {tex} {C} =\frac{\varepsilon_0 {~A}}{({~d}-{t})+\frac{{t}}{K}} {/tex} {tex} {t} ={d} / 4 {/tex} {tex} {C} =\frac{\varepsilon_0 {~A}}{\left({~d}-\frac{{d}}{4}\right)+\frac{{d}}{4 {~K}}}=\frac{\varepsilon_0 {~A}}{{~d}\left(\frac{3}{4}+\frac{1}{4 {~K}}\right)} {/tex} {tex} ={C}_0 \frac{4 {~K}}{(3 {~K}+1)} {/tex} Each capacitance can withstand 200 V No. of capacitors in each row {tex}=\frac{1200}{200}=6{/tex} Net capacitance of each row {tex}=1 / 6 \mu {~F}{/tex} Number of rows {tex}={n}{/tex} {tex} C_{e q} =C_1+C_2+------+C_n {/tex} {tex} C_{e q} =\frac{1}{6}+\frac{1}{6}+-----n {/tex} {tex} 2 =\frac{n}{6} {/tex} {tex} \therefore n=12 {/tex} {tex} \text { Total no. of capacitors in the arrangement } =6 \times 12 {/tex} {tex} =72 {/tex}
solution_html:
1. {tex} {C}_0 =\frac{\varepsilon_0 {~A}}{{~d}} {/tex}
    {tex} {C} =\frac{\varepsilon_0 {~A}}{({~d}-{t})+\frac{{t}}{K}} {/tex}
    {tex} {t} ={d} / 4 {/tex}
    {tex} {C} =\frac{\varepsilon_0 {~A}}{\left({~d}-\frac{{d}}{4}\right)+\frac{{d}}{4 {~K}}}=\frac{\varepsilon_0 {~A}}{{~d}\left(\frac{3}{4}+\frac{1}{4 {~K}}\right)} {/tex}
    {tex} ={C}_0 \frac{4 {~K}}{(3 {~K}+1)} {/tex}
  2. Each capacitance can withstand 200 V
    No. of capacitors in each row {tex}=\frac{1200}{200}=6{/tex}
    Net capacitance of each row {tex}=1 / 6 \mu {~F}{/tex}
    Number of rows {tex}={n}{/tex}
    {tex} C_{e q} =C_1+C_2+------+C_n {/tex}
    {tex} C_{e q} =\frac{1}{6}+\frac{1}{6}+-----n {/tex}
    {tex} 2 =\frac{n}{6} {/tex}
    {tex} \therefore n=12 {/tex}
    {tex} \text { Total no. of capacitors in the arrangement } =6 \times 12 {/tex}
    {tex} =72 {/tex}

subject: Physics
question_html:
1. "There is a limit to the amount of charge that can be stored on a given capacitor." Explain.
  2. A capacitor is charged by a battery to a potential difference V. It is disconnected from the battery and connected across another identical uncharged capacitor. Calculate the ratio of total energy stored in the combination to the initial energy stored in the capacitor.
question_text: "There is a limit to the amount of charge that can be stored on a given capacitor." Explain. A capacitor is charged by a battery to a potential difference V. It is disconnected from the battery and connected across another identical uncharged capacitor. Calculate the ratio of total energy stored in the combination to the initial energy stored in the capacitor.
explanation: When a conductor holds a large amount of charge its potential is also high. If electric field becomes high enough, the atoms or molecules of surrounding medium gets ionized. A breakdown occurs in medium and charge on conductor get neutralized or leaks away. The common potential after the connection of two capacitors {tex} \text { Given } V_1=V, V_2=0 {/tex} {tex} C_1=C_2=C {/tex} {tex} = \frac{{CV}}{2 {C}} {/tex} {tex} = \frac{{V}}{2} {/tex} {tex} {U}_i= \frac{1}{2} C V^2 {/tex} {tex} {U}_f =\frac{1}{2} C^{\prime} {V}^{\prime 2} {/tex} {tex} =\frac{1}{2} \times 2 {C} \times\left(\frac{{V}}{2}\right)^2 {/tex} {tex} =\frac{1}{4} {CV}^2 {/tex} {tex} \frac{{U}_{{f}}}{U_{{i}}} =\frac{1}{2} {/tex}
solution: When a conductor holds a large amount of charge its potential is also high. If electric field becomes high enough, the atoms or molecules of surrounding medium gets ionized. A breakdown occurs in medium and charge on conductor get neutralized or leaks away. The common potential after the connection of two capacitors {tex} \text { Given } V_1=V, V_2=0 {/tex} {tex} C_1=C_2=C {/tex} {tex} = \frac{{CV}}{2 {C}} {/tex} {tex} = \frac{{V}}{2} {/tex} {tex} {U}_i= \frac{1}{2} C V^2 {/tex} {tex} {U}_f =\frac{1}{2} C^{\prime} {V}^{\prime 2} {/tex} {tex} =\frac{1}{2} \times 2 {C} \times\left(\frac{{V}}{2}\right)^2 {/tex} {tex} =\frac{1}{4} {CV}^2 {/tex} {tex} \frac{{U}_{{f}}}{U_{{i}}} =\frac{1}{2} {/tex}
solution_html:
1. When a conductor holds a large amount of charge its potential is also high. If electric field becomes high enough, the atoms or molecules of surrounding medium gets ionized. A breakdown occurs in medium and charge on conductor get neutralized or leaks away.
  2. The common potential after the connection of two capacitors
    {tex} \text { Given } V_1=V, V_2=0 {/tex}
    {tex} C_1=C_2=C {/tex}
    {tex} = \frac{{CV}}{2 {C}} {/tex}
    {tex} = \frac{{V}}{2} {/tex}
    {tex} {U}_i= \frac{1}{2} C V^2 {/tex}
    {tex} {U}_f =\frac{1}{2} C^{\prime} {V}^{\prime 2} {/tex}
    {tex} =\frac{1}{2} \times 2 {C} \times\left(\frac{{V}}{2}\right)^2 {/tex}
    {tex} =\frac{1}{4} {CV}^2 {/tex}

question_html:
A capacitor of plate area A and plate separation d is charged by a battery to voltage V . The battery is disconnected and plates are slowly pulled apart till the separation becomes 2d. Find the value of:

  1. potential difference between the plates,
  2. electric field between the plates,
  3. work done in pulling the plates apart.
question_text: A capacitor of plate area A and plate separation d is charged by a battery to voltage V . The battery is disconnected and plates are slowly pulled apart till the separation becomes 2d. Find the value of: potential difference between the plates, electric field between the plates, work done in pulling the plates apart.
explanation: As battery is dis connected, charge remain same {tex} C^{\prime}=\frac{C}{2} {/tex} {tex} {q}={q}^{\prime} {/tex} {tex} {CV}={C}^{\prime} {V}^{\prime} {/tex} {tex} {CV}=\frac{{C}}{2} {~V}^{\prime} {/tex} {tex} {V}^{\prime}=2 {~V} {/tex} {tex} {E}^{\prime} =\frac{{V}^{\prime}}{{d}^{\prime}} {/tex} {tex} =\frac{2 {~V}}{2 {~d}} {/tex} {tex} =\frac{{V}}{{d}}={E} {/tex} {tex} {W} ={U}^{\prime}-{U} {/tex} {tex} =\frac{1}{2} \times \frac{C}{2} \times(2 {~V})^2-\frac{1}{2} {CV}^2 {/tex} {tex} =\frac{1}{2} {CV}^2 {/tex}
solution: As battery is dis connected, charge remain same {tex} C^{\prime}=\frac{C}{2} {/tex} {tex} {q}={q}^{\prime} {/tex} {tex} {CV}={C}^{\prime} {V}^{\prime} {/tex} {tex} {CV}=\frac{{C}}{2} {~V}^{\prime} {/tex} {tex} {V}^{\prime}=2 {~V} {/tex} {tex} {E}^{\prime} =\frac{{V}^{\prime}}{{d}^{\prime}} {/tex} {tex} =\frac{2 {~V}}{2 {~d}} {/tex} {tex} =\frac{{V}}{{d}}={E} {/tex} {tex} {W} ={U}^{\prime}-{U} {/tex} {tex} =\frac{1}{2} \times \frac{C}{2} \times(2 {~V})^2-\frac{1}{2} {CV}^2 {/tex} {tex} =\frac{1}{2} {CV}^2 {/tex}
solution_html:
As battery is dis connected, charge remain same
{tex} C^{\prime}=\frac{C}{2} {/tex}

  1. {tex} {q}={q}^{\prime} {/tex}
    {tex} {CV}={C}^{\prime} {V}^{\prime} {/tex}
    {tex} {CV}=\frac{{C}}{2} {~V}^{\prime} {/tex}
    {tex} {V}^{\prime}=2 {~V} {/tex}
  2. {tex} {E}^{\prime} =\frac{{V}^{\prime}}{{d}^{\prime}} {/tex}
    {tex} =\frac{2 {~V}}{2 {~d}} {/tex}
    {tex} =\frac{{V}}{{d}}={E} {/tex}
  3. {tex} {W} ={U}^{\prime}-{U} {/tex}
    {tex} =\frac{1}{2} \times \frac{C}{2} \times(2 {~V})^2-\frac{1}{2} {CV}^2 {/tex}
    {tex} =\frac{1}{2} {CV}^2 {/tex}

subject: Physics
question_html:
1. Show that Gauss's theorem is consistent with Coulomb's law. Using it, derive an expression for the electric field due to a uniformly charged thin spherical shell of radius r at a point at a distance {tex}y{/tex} from the centre of the shell such that (I) {tex}{y} \gt {r}{/tex}, and (II) {tex}{y} \lt {r}{/tex}.
  2. A point charge of +2 nC is kept at the origin of a three-dimensional coordinate system. Find the type and magnitude of the charge which should be kept at ({tex}0,0,-6 {~m}{/tex}) so that the potential due to the system becomes zero at ({tex}0,0,2 {~m}{/tex}).
question_text: Show that Gauss's theorem is consistent with Coulomb's law. Using it, derive an expression for the electric field due to a uniformly charged thin spherical shell of radius r at a point at a distance {tex}y{/tex} from the centre of the shell such that (I) {tex}{y} \gt {r}{/tex} , and (II) {tex}{y} \lt {r}{/tex} . A point charge of +2 nC is kept at the origin of a three-dimensional coordinate system. Find the type and magnitude of the charge which should be kept at ( {tex}0,0,-6 {~m}{/tex} ) so that the potential due to the system becomes zero at ( {tex}0,0,2 {~m}{/tex} ).
explanation: According to Gauss's theorem {tex}\oint \overrightarrow{{E}} . {d} \overrightarrow{{~s}}=\frac{{q}}{\varepsilon_{{o}}}{/tex} {tex}{E}=\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{{o}}} \frac{{q}}{{r}^{2}}{/tex} According to Coulomb's law, force on charge {tex}{q}_{{o}}{/tex} in this field {tex}{F}=\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{{o}}} \frac{{qq}_{{o}}}{{r}^{2}}{/tex} Therefore, Gauss's law is consistent with Coulomb's law Electric flux through Gaussian surface {tex}{E} \times 4 \pi {y}^{2}{/tex} The charge enclosed by the surface {tex}\sigma \times 4 \pi {r}^{2}{/tex} Using Gauss theorem {tex}{E}\left(4 \pi {y}^{2}\right)=\frac{\sigma 4 \pi {r}^{2}}{\varepsilon_{0}}{/tex} {tex}\overrightarrow{{E}}=\frac{{q}}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0} {y}^{2}} \hat{r}{/tex} For y < r The charge enclosed by Gaussian surface {tex}=0{/tex} Using Gauss theorem Electric flux {tex}={E}\left(4 \pi {y}^{2}\right)=0{/tex} i.e. {tex}E=0 (y \lt r){/tex} Let the charge is kept at A be q Potential at point B due to charge at the origin O and charge (q) at A {tex}{V}={V}_{1}+{V}_{2}{/tex} {tex}{V}=\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}}\left[\frac{2 \times 10^{-9}}{2}+\frac{{q}}{6+2}\right]{/tex} {tex}\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}}\left[10^{-9}+\frac{{q}}{8}\right]=0{/tex} {tex}{q}=-8 \times 10^{-9} {C}{/tex}
solution: According to Gauss's theorem {tex}\oint \overrightarrow{{E}} . {d} \overrightarrow{{~s}}=\frac{{q}}{\varepsilon_{{o}}}{/tex} {tex}{E}=\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{{o}}} \frac{{q}}{{r}^{2}}{/tex} According to Coulomb's law, force on charge {tex}{q}_{{o}}{/tex} in this field {tex}{F}=\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{{o}}} \frac{{qq}_{{o}}}{{r}^{2}}{/tex} Therefore, Gauss's law is consistent with Coulomb's law Electric flux through Gaussian surface {tex}{E} \times 4 \pi {y}^{2}{/tex} The charge enclosed by the surface {tex}\sigma \times 4 \pi {r}^{2}{/tex} Using Gauss theorem {tex}{E}\left(4 \pi {y}^{2}\right)=\frac{\sigma 4 \pi {r}^{2}}{\varepsilon_{0}}{/tex} {tex}\overrightarrow{{E}}=\frac{{q}}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0} {y}^{2}} \hat{r}{/tex} For y < r The charge enclosed by Gaussian surface {tex}=0{/tex} Using Gauss theorem Electric flux {tex}={E}\left(4 \pi {y}^{2}\right)=0{/tex} i.e. {tex}E=0 (y \lt r){/tex} Let the charge is kept at A be q Potential at point B due to charge at the origin O and charge (q) at A {tex}{V}={V}_{1}+{V}_{2}{/tex} {tex}{V}=\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}}\left[\frac{2 \times 10^{-9}}{2}+\frac{{q}}{6+2}\right]{/tex} {tex}\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}}\left[10^{-9}+\frac{{q}}{8}\right]=0{/tex} {tex}{q}=-8 \times 10^{-9} {C}{/tex}
solution_html:
1. According to Gauss's theorem
    {tex}\oint \overrightarrow{{E}} . {d} \overrightarrow{{~s}}=\frac{{q}}{\varepsilon_{{o}}}{/tex}
    {tex}{E}=\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{{o}}} \frac{{q}}{{r}^{2}}{/tex}
    According to Coulomb's law, force on charge {tex}{q}_{{o}}{/tex} in this field
    {tex}{F}=\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{{o}}} \frac{{qq}_{{o}}}{{r}^{2}}{/tex}
    Therefore, Gauss's law is consistent with Coulomb's law

    1. Electric flux through Gaussian surface {tex}{E} \times 4 \pi {y}^{2}{/tex}
      The charge enclosed by the surface {tex}\sigma \times 4 \pi {r}^{2}{/tex}
      Using Gauss theorem
      {tex}{E}\left(4 \pi {y}^{2}\right)=\frac{\sigma 4 \pi {r}^{2}}{\varepsilon_{0}}{/tex}
      {tex}\overrightarrow{{E}}=\frac{{q}}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0} {y}^{2}} \hat{r}{/tex}
    2. For y < r

      The charge enclosed by Gaussian surface {tex}=0{/tex}
      Using Gauss theorem
      Electric flux {tex}={E}\left(4 \pi {y}^{2}\right)=0{/tex}
      i.e. {tex}E=0 (y \lt r){/tex}

  2. Let the charge is kept at A be q
    Potential at point B due to charge at the origin O and charge (q) at A
    {tex}{V}={V}_{1}+{V}_{2}{/tex}
    {tex}{V}=\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}}\left[\frac{2 \times 10^{-9}}{2}+\frac{{q}}{6+2}\right]{/tex}
    {tex}\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}}\left[10^{-9}+\frac{{q}}{8}\right]=0{/tex}
    {tex}{q}=-8 \times 10^{-9} {C}{/tex}

subject: Physics
question_html:
1. Two point charges +q and -q are held at (a, 0) and (-a, 0) in x-y plane. Obtain an expression for the net electric field due to the charges at a point ({tex}0, {y}{/tex} ). Hence, find electric field at a far off point ({tex}{y} \gg {a}{/tex}).
  2. Three point charges of {tex}-2 {nC},-1 {nC}{/tex}, and +5 nC are kept at the vertices {tex}{A}, {B}{/tex} and C of an equilateral triangle of side 0.2 m. Find the total amount of work done in shifting the charges from A to {tex}{A}_{1}, {~B}{/tex} to {tex}{B}_{1}{/tex} and C to {tex}{C}_{1}{/tex}. Here {tex}{A}_{1}, {~B}_{1}{/tex} and {tex}{C}_{1}{/tex} are the midpoints of sides {tex}{AB}, {BC}{/tex} and CA, respectively.
question_text: Two point charges +q and -q are held at (a, 0) and (-a, 0) in x-y plane. Obtain an expression for the net electric field due to the charges at a point ( {tex}0, {y}{/tex} ). Hence, find electric field at a far off point ( {tex}{y} \gg {a}{/tex} ). Three point charges of {tex}-2 {nC},-1 {nC}{/tex} , and +5 nC are kept at the vertices {tex}{A}, {B}{/tex} and C of an equilateral triangle of side 0.2 m. Find the total amount of work done in shifting the charges from A to {tex}{A}_{1}, {~B}{/tex} to {tex}{B}_{1}{/tex} and C to {tex}{C}_{1}{/tex} . Here {tex}{A}_{1}, {~B}_{1}{/tex} and {tex}{C}_{1}{/tex} are the midpoints of sides {tex}{AB}, {BC}{/tex} and CA, respectively.
explanation: Magnitude of electric field due to the two charges +q and -q are given by {tex}{E}_{+{q}}=\frac{{q}}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{{o}}} \frac{1}{{y}^{2}+{a}^{2}}{/tex} {tex}{E}_{-{q}}=\frac{{q}}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{{o}}} \frac{1}{{y}^{2}+{a}^{2}}{/tex} Components normal to the dipole axis cancel out. The components along the dipole axis add up. The total electric field is opposite to the dipole moment will be given by- {tex}\overrightarrow{{E}}=-\left({E}_{+{q}}+{E}_{-{q}}\right) \cos \theta \hat{{p}}{/tex} {tex}=-\frac{2 {qa}}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{{o}}\left({y}^{2}+{a}^{2}\right)^{3 / 2}} \hat{{p}} {/tex} {tex}(\hat{{p}}{/tex} is a unit vector along dipole moment {tex}){/tex} At large distance ( {tex}{y} \gg {a}{/tex} ) {tex}\overrightarrow{{E}}=\frac{-2 {qa}}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{{o}} y^{3}} \hat{{p}}{/tex} Initial electrostatic potential energy of the system {tex}{U}_{1}=\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{{o}}}\left(\frac{{q}_{{A}} {q}_{{B}}}{{AB}}+\frac{{q}_{{C}} {q}_{{A}}}{{AC}}+\frac{{q}_{C} {q}_{{B}}}{{BC}}\right){/tex} {tex}=\frac{9 \times 10^{9}}{0.2}[(-2 \times-1)+(-2 \times 5){/tex} {tex}+(-1 \times 5)] \times 10^{-18}{/tex} {tex}{U}_{1}=-5.85 \times 10^{-7} {~J}{/tex} {tex}{U}_{2}=\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{{o}}}\left(\frac{{q}_{{A}_{1}} {q}_{{B}_{1}}}{{~A}_{1} {~B}_{1}}+\frac{{q}_{{C}_{1}} {q}_{{A}_{1}}}{{~A}_{1} {C}_{1}}+\frac{{q}_{{c}_{1}} {q}_{{B}_{1}}}{{~B}_{1} {C}_{1}}\right){/tex} {tex}{U}_{2}=-11.7 \times 10^{-7} {~J}{/tex} {tex}{W}={U}_{2}-{U}_{1}=-5.85 \times 10^{-7} {~J}{/tex}
solution: Magnitude of electric field due to the two charges +q and -q are given by {tex}{E}_{+{q}}=\frac{{q}}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{{o}}} \frac{1}{{y}^{2}+{a}^{2}}{/tex} {tex}{E}_{-{q}}=\frac{{q}}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{{o}}} \frac{1}{{y}^{2}+{a}^{2}}{/tex} Components normal to the dipole axis cancel out. The components along the dipole axis add up. The total electric field is opposite to the dipole moment will be given by- {tex}\overrightarrow{{E}}=-\left({E}_{+{q}}+{E}_{-{q}}\right) \cos \theta \hat{{p}}{/tex} {tex}=-\frac{2 {qa}}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{{o}}\left({y}^{2}+{a}^{2}\right)^{3 / 2}} \hat{{p}} {/tex} {tex}(\hat{{p}}{/tex} is a unit vector along dipole moment {tex}){/tex} At large distance ( {tex}{y} \gg {a}{/tex} ) {tex}\overrightarrow{{E}}=\frac{-2 {qa}}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{{o}} y^{3}} \hat{{p}}{/tex} Initial electrostatic potential energy of the system {tex}{U}_{1}=\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{{o}}}\left(\frac{{q}_{{A}} {q}_{{B}}}{{AB}}+\frac{{q}_{{C}} {q}_{{A}}}{{AC}}+\frac{{q}_{C} {q}_{{B}}}{{BC}}\right){/tex} {tex}=\frac{9 \times 10^{9}}{0.2}[(-2 \times-1)+(-2 \times 5){/tex} {tex}+(-1 \times 5)] \times 10^{-18}{/tex} {tex}{U}_{1}=-5.85 \times 10^{-7} {~J}{/tex} {tex}{U}_{2}=\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{{o}}}\left(\frac{{q}_{{A}_{1}} {q}_{{B}_{1}}}{{~A}_{1} {~B}_{1}}+\frac{{q}_{{C}_{1}} {q}_{{A}_{1}}}{{~A}_{1} {C}_{1}}+\frac{{q}_{{c}_{1}} {q}_{{B}_{1}}}{{~B}_{1} {C}_{1}}\right){/tex} {tex}{U}_{2}=-11.7 \times 10^{-7} {~J}{/tex} {tex}{W}={U}_{2}-{U}_{1}=-5.85 \times 10^{-7} {~J}{/tex}
solution_html:
1. Magnitude of electric field due to the two charges +q and -q are given by
    {tex}{E}_{+{q}}=\frac{{q}}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{{o}}} \frac{1}{{y}^{2}+{a}^{2}}{/tex}
    {tex}{E}_{-{q}}=\frac{{q}}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{{o}}} \frac{1}{{y}^{2}+{a}^{2}}{/tex}
    Components normal to the dipole axis cancel out.
    The components along the dipole axis add up.
    The total electric field is opposite to the dipole moment will be given by-
    {tex}\overrightarrow{{E}}=-\left({E}_{+{q}}+{E}_{-{q}}\right) \cos \theta \hat{{p}}{/tex}
    {tex}=-\frac{2 {qa}}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{{o}}\left({y}^{2}+{a}^{2}\right)^{3 / 2}} \hat{{p}} {/tex} {tex}(\hat{{p}}{/tex} is a unit vector along dipole moment{tex}){/tex}
    At large distance ({tex}{y} \gg {a}{/tex})
    {tex}\overrightarrow{{E}}=\frac{-2 {qa}}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{{o}} y^{3}} \hat{{p}}{/tex}

  2. Initial electrostatic potential energy of the system
    {tex}{U}_{1}=\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{{o}}}\left(\frac{{q}_{{A}} {q}_{{B}}}{{AB}}+\frac{{q}_{{C}} {q}_{{A}}}{{AC}}+\frac{{q}_{C} {q}_{{B}}}{{BC}}\right){/tex}
    {tex}=\frac{9 \times 10^{9}}{0.2}[(-2 \times-1)+(-2 \times 5){/tex}{tex}+(-1 \times 5)] \times 10^{-18}{/tex}
    {tex}{U}_{1}=-5.85 \times 10^{-7} {~J}{/tex}
    {tex}{U}_{2}=\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{{o}}}\left(\frac{{q}_{{A}_{1}} {q}_{{B}_{1}}}{{~A}_{1} {~B}_{1}}+\frac{{q}_{{C}_{1}} {q}_{{A}_{1}}}{{~A}_{1} {C}_{1}}+\frac{{q}_{{c}_{1}} {q}_{{B}_{1}}}{{~B}_{1} {C}_{1}}\right){/tex}
    {tex}{U}_{2}=-11.7 \times 10^{-7} {~J}{/tex}
    {tex}{W}={U}_{2}-{U}_{1}=-5.85 \times 10^{-7} {~J}{/tex}

The spherical shells {tex}A{/tex}, {tex}B{/tex} and {tex}C{/tex} are given charges {tex}+6 q{/tex}, {tex}-4 q{/tex}, and {tex}14 q{/tex} respectively.   2. A charge {tex}-6 \mu {C}{/tex} is placed at the centre B of a semicircle of radius 5 cm, as shown in the figure. An equal and opposite charge is placed at point D at a distance of 10 cm from B. A charge {tex}+5 \mu {C}{/tex} is moved from point 'C' to point 'A' along the circumference. Calculate the work done on the charge.
question_text: Consider three metal spherical shells {tex}{A}, {B}{/tex} and C, each of radius R. Each shell is having a concentric metal ball of radius {tex}\frac {R} {10}{/tex} . The spherical shells {tex}A{/tex} , {tex}B{/tex} and {tex}C{/tex} are given charges {tex}+6 q{/tex} , {tex}-4 q{/tex} , and {tex}14 q{/tex} respectively. Their inner metal balls are also given charges {tex}-2 q,+8 q{/tex} and {tex}-10 q{/tex} respectively. Compare the magnitude of the electric fields due to shells {tex}{A}, {B}{/tex} and C at a distance 3 R from their centres. A charge {tex}-6 \mu {C}{/tex} is placed at the centre B of a semicircle of radius 5 cm, as shown in the figure. An equal and opposite charge is placed at point D at a distance of 10 cm from B. A charge {tex}+5 \mu {C}{/tex} is moved from point 'C' to point 'A' along the circumference. Calculate the work done on the charge.
explanation: Total charge for {tex}{A}={/tex} Total charge for {tex}{B}={/tex} Total charge for {tex}{C}={ }_{+4} {q}{/tex} As , {tex}{E}=\frac{k Q}{r^{2}}{/tex} Since {tex}{Q}=4 {q}{/tex} and {tex}{r}=3 {R}{/tex} {tex}{E} =\frac{{k}(4 q)}{9 R^{2}}=\frac{4 k q}{9 R^{2}}{/tex} {tex}\therefore {E}_{{A}} ={E}_{{B}}={E}_{{c}}{/tex} {tex}{V}_{{c}} =\left[\frac{k \times 6 \times 10^{-6}}{5 \times 10^{-2}}-\frac{k \times 6 \times 10^{-6}}{5 \times 10^{-2}}\right]{/tex} {tex}=0{/tex} {tex}V_{A} =\left[\frac{k \times 6 \times 10^{-6}}{15 \times 10^{-2}}-\frac{k \times 6 \times 10^{-6}}{5 \times 10^{-2}}\right]{/tex} {tex}=\frac{k \times 6 \times 10^{-6}}{10^{-2}}\left[\frac{1-3}{15}\right]{/tex} {tex}=-9 \times 10^{9} \times 6 \times 10^{-6} \times 2{/tex} {tex}=-7.2 \times 10^{5} {~V}{/tex} {tex}{~W} =q\left[V_{A}-V_{c}\right]{/tex} {tex}=5 \times 10^{-6}\left[-7.2 \times 10^{5}-0\right]{/tex} {tex}{W} =-3.6 {~J}{/tex}
solution: Total charge for {tex}{A}={/tex} Total charge for {tex}{B}={/tex} Total charge for {tex}{C}={ }_{+4} {q}{/tex} As , {tex}{E}=\frac{k Q}{r^{2}}{/tex} Since {tex}{Q}=4 {q}{/tex} and {tex}{r}=3 {R}{/tex} {tex}{E} =\frac{{k}(4 q)}{9 R^{2}}=\frac{4 k q}{9 R^{2}}{/tex} {tex}\therefore {E}_{{A}} ={E}_{{B}}={E}_{{c}}{/tex} {tex}{V}_{{c}} =\left[\frac{k \times 6 \times 10^{-6}}{5 \times 10^{-2}}-\frac{k \times 6 \times 10^{-6}}{5 \times 10^{-2}}\right]{/tex} {tex}=0{/tex} {tex}V_{A} =\left[\frac{k \times 6 \times 10^{-6}}{15 \times 10^{-2}}-\frac{k \times 6 \times 10^{-6}}{5 \times 10^{-2}}\right]{/tex} {tex}=\frac{k \times 6 \times 10^{-6}}{10^{-2}}\left[\frac{1-3}{15}\right]{/tex} {tex}=-9 \times 10^{9} \times 6 \times 10^{-6} \times 2{/tex} {tex}=-7.2 \times 10^{5} {~V}{/tex} {tex}{~W} =q\left[V_{A}-V_{c}\right]{/tex} {tex}=5 \times 10^{-6}\left[-7.2 \times 10^{5}-0\right]{/tex} {tex}{W} =-3.6 {~J}{/tex}
solution_html:
1. Total charge for {tex}{A}={/tex} Total charge for {tex}{B}={/tex} Total charge for {tex}{C}={ }_{+4} {q}{/tex}
    As , {tex}{E}=\frac{k Q}{r^{2}}{/tex}
    Since {tex}{Q}=4 {q}{/tex} and {tex}{r}=3 {R}{/tex}
    {tex}{E} =\frac{{k}(4 q)}{9 R^{2}}=\frac{4 k q}{9 R^{2}}{/tex}
    {tex}\therefore {E}_{{A}} ={E}_{{B}}={E}_{{c}}{/tex}
  2. {tex}{V}_{{c}} =\left[\frac{k \times 6 \times 10^{-6}}{5 \times 10^{-2}}-\frac{k \times 6 \times 10^{-6}}{5 \times 10^{-2}}\right]{/tex}
    {tex}=0{/tex}
    {tex}V_{A} =\left[\frac{k \times 6 \times 10^{-6}}{15 \times 10^{-2}}-\frac{k \times 6 \times 10^{-6}}{5 \times 10^{-2}}\right]{/tex}
    {tex}=\frac{k \times 6 \times 10^{-6}}{10^{-2}}\left[\frac{1-3}{15}\right]{/tex}
    {tex}=-9 \times 10^{9} \times 6 \times 10^{-6} \times 2{/tex}
    {tex}=-7.2 \times 10^{5} {~V}{/tex}
    {tex}{~W} =q\left[V_{A}-V_{c}\right]{/tex}
    {tex}=5 \times 10^{-6}\left[-7.2 \times 10^{5}-0\right]{/tex}
    {tex}{W} =-3.6 {~J}{/tex}

subject: Physics
question_html:
1. Two point charges {tex}5 \mu {C}{/tex} and {tex}-1 \mu {C}{/tex} are placed at points (-3 cm, 0,0) and ({tex}3 {~cm}, 0,0{/tex}) respectively. An external electric field {tex}\vec{E}=\frac{A}{r^{2}} \hat{r}{/tex} where {tex}A=3 \times 10^{5} {Vm}{/tex} is switched on in the region. Calculate the change in electrostatic energy of the system due to the electric field.
  2. A system of two conductors is placed in air and they have net charge of {tex}+80 \mu {C}{/tex} and {tex}-80 \mu {C}{/tex} which causes a potential difference of 16 V between them.
    1. Find the capacitance of the system.
    2. If the air between the capacitor is replaced by a dielectric medium of dielectric constant 3, what will be the potential difference between the two conductors?
    3. If the charges on two conductors are changed to {tex}+160 \mu {C}{/tex} and {tex}-160 \mu {C}{/tex}, will the capacitance of the system change? Give reason for your answer.
question_text: Two point charges {tex}5 \mu {C}{/tex} and {tex}-1 \mu {C}{/tex} are placed at points (-3 cm, 0,0) and ( {tex}3 {~cm}, 0,0{/tex} ) respectively. An external electric field {tex}\vec{E}=\frac{A}{r^{2}} \hat{r}{/tex} where {tex}A=3 \times 10^{5} {Vm}{/tex} is switched on in the region. Calculate the change in electrostatic energy of the system due to the electric field. A system of two conductors is placed in air and they have net charge of {tex}+80 \mu {C}{/tex} and {tex}-80 \mu {C}{/tex} which causes a potential difference of 16 V between them. Find the capacitance of the system. If the air between the capacitor is replaced by a dielectric medium of dielectric constant 3, what will be the potential difference between the two conductors? If the charges on two conductors are changed to {tex}+160 \mu {C}{/tex} and {tex}-160 \mu {C}{/tex} , will the capacitance of the system change? Give reason for your answer.
explanation: {tex}\vec{E}=\frac{3 \times 10^{5}}{r^{2}} \hat{r}{/tex} (Given) {tex}d V=-\vec{E} \cdot {~d} \vec{r}{/tex} Electrostatic energy of the system in the absence of the field {tex}{U}_{{i}}=\frac{K q_{1} q_{2}}{r_{12}}{/tex} Electrostatic energy in the presence of the field {tex}{U}_{{f}} =\frac{K q_{1} q_{2}}{r_{12}}+q_{1} V\left(\vec{r}_{1}\right)+q_{2} V\left(\vec{r}_{2}\right){/tex} {tex}\Delta {U} ={U}_{{f}}-{U}_{{i}}=q_{1} V\left(\vec{r}_{1}\right)+q_{2} V\left(\vec{r}_{2}\right){/tex} {tex}\Delta {U} =\frac{5 \times 10^{-6} \times 3 \times 10^{5}}{3 \times 10^{-2}}-\frac{1 \times 10^{-6} \times 3 \times 10^{5}}{3 \times 10^{-2}}{/tex} {tex}=40 {~J}{/tex} {tex}{C}=\frac{Q}{V}=\frac{80}{16}=5 \mu {~F}{/tex} {tex}C^{\prime}=K C{/tex} {tex}=3 \times 5 \mu {~F}=15 \mu {~F}{/tex} {tex}{V}^{\prime}=\frac{{Q}}{{C}^{\prime}}=\frac{80 \mu {C}}{15 \mu {~F}}=5.33 {~V}{/tex} No, The capacitance of the system depends on its geometry.
solution: {tex}\vec{E}=\frac{3 \times 10^{5}}{r^{2}} \hat{r}{/tex} (Given) {tex}d V=-\vec{E} \cdot {~d} \vec{r}{/tex} Electrostatic energy of the system in the absence of the field {tex}{U}_{{i}}=\frac{K q_{1} q_{2}}{r_{12}}{/tex} Electrostatic energy in the presence of the field {tex}{U}_{{f}} =\frac{K q_{1} q_{2}}{r_{12}}+q_{1} V\left(\vec{r}_{1}\right)+q_{2} V\left(\vec{r}_{2}\right){/tex} {tex}\Delta {U} ={U}_{{f}}-{U}_{{i}}=q_{1} V\left(\vec{r}_{1}\right)+q_{2} V\left(\vec{r}_{2}\right){/tex} {tex}\Delta {U} =\frac{5 \times 10^{-6} \times 3 \times 10^{5}}{3 \times 10^{-2}}-\frac{1 \times 10^{-6} \times 3 \times 10^{5}}{3 \times 10^{-2}}{/tex} {tex}=40 {~J}{/tex} {tex}{C}=\frac{Q}{V}=\frac{80}{16}=5 \mu {~F}{/tex} {tex}C^{\prime}=K C{/tex} {tex}=3 \times 5 \mu {~F}=15 \mu {~F}{/tex} {tex}{V}^{\prime}=\frac{{Q}}{{C}^{\prime}}=\frac{80 \mu {C}}{15 \mu {~F}}=5.33 {~V}{/tex} No, The capacitance of the system depends on its geometry.
solution_html:
1. {tex}\vec{E}=\frac{3 \times 10^{5}}{r^{2}} \hat{r}{/tex} (Given) {tex}d V=-\vec{E} \cdot {~d} \vec{r}{/tex}
    Electrostatic energy of the system in the absence of the field
    {tex}{U}_{{i}}=\frac{K q_{1} q_{2}}{r_{12}}{/tex}
    Electrostatic energy in the presence of the field
    {tex}{U}_{{f}} =\frac{K q_{1} q_{2}}{r_{12}}+q_{1} V\left(\vec{r}_{1}\right)+q_{2} V\left(\vec{r}_{2}\right){/tex}
    {tex}\Delta {U} ={U}_{{f}}-{U}_{{i}}=q_{1} V\left(\vec{r}_{1}\right)+q_{2} V\left(\vec{r}_{2}\right){/tex}
    {tex}\Delta {U} =\frac{5 \times 10^{-6} \times 3 \times 10^{5}}{3 \times 10^{-2}}-\frac{1 \times 10^{-6} \times 3 \times 10^{5}}{3 \times 10^{-2}}{/tex}
    {tex}=40 {~J}{/tex}
    1. {tex}{C}=\frac{Q}{V}=\frac{80}{16}=5 \mu {~F}{/tex}
    2. {tex}C^{\prime}=K C{/tex}
      {tex}=3 \times 5 \mu {~F}=15 \mu {~F}{/tex}
      {tex}{V}^{\prime}=\frac{{Q}}{{C}^{\prime}}=\frac{80 \mu {C}}{15 \mu {~F}}=5.33 {~V}{/tex}
    3. No,
      The capacitance of the system depends on its geometry.

question_html:
1. A parallel plate capacitor A of capacitance C is charged by a battery to a potential 'V'. The battery is disconnected and an uncharged identical capacitor B is connected across it. Calculate for the capacitor A the new value of the:
    1. charge
    2. potential difference
    3. energy stored
      Justify your answers.
  2. Draw the pattern of electric field lines due to:
    1. positively charged conducting sphere, and
    2. an electric dipole.
question_text: A parallel plate capacitor A of capacitance C is charged by a battery to a potential 'V'. The battery is disconnected and an uncharged identical capacitor B is connected across it. Calculate for the capacitor A the new value of the: charge potential difference energy stored Justify your answers. Draw the pattern of electric field lines due to: positively charged conducting sphere, and an electric dipole.
explanation: Charge will become half Charge will flow from capacitors A (high potential) to capacitor B (low potential) till they achieve equilibrium. Alternatively {tex} q_A=c v q_B=0 {/tex} {tex} V_{\text {common }}=\frac{q_A+q_B}{c_A+c_B}=\frac{{cV}}{2 {c}} {/tex} {tex} =\frac{{v}}{2} {/tex} {tex} q_A^{\prime}={cv}_{\text {common }}=\frac{{cV}}{2}=\frac{q_A}{2} {/tex} Potential difference will become half. {tex} V_{\text {common }} =\frac{q_A+q_B}{c_A+c_B} {/tex} {tex} =\frac{{cv}+0}{2 {c}}=\frac{{v}}{2} {/tex} Energy stored will becomes one fourth {tex} U_A =\frac{1}{2} C V^2 {/tex} {tex} U_A^{\prime} =\frac{1}{2} C V_{\text {common }}^2 {/tex} {tex} =\frac{1}{2} C\left(\frac{V}{2}\right)^2 {/tex} {tex} =\frac{U_A}{4} {/tex}
solution: Charge will become half Charge will flow from capacitors A (high potential) to capacitor B (low potential) till they achieve equilibrium. Alternatively {tex} q_A=c v q_B=0 {/tex} {tex} V_{\text {common }}=\frac{q_A+q_B}{c_A+c_B}=\frac{{cV}}{2 {c}} {/tex} {tex} =\frac{{v}}{2} {/tex} {tex} q_A^{\prime}={cv}_{\text {common }}=\frac{{cV}}{2}=\frac{q_A}{2} {/tex} Potential difference will become half. {tex} V_{\text {common }} =\frac{q_A+q_B}{c_A+c_B} {/tex} {tex} =\frac{{cv}+0}{2 {c}}=\frac{{v}}{2} {/tex} Energy stored will becomes one fourth {tex} U_A =\frac{1}{2} C V^2 {/tex} {tex} U_A^{\prime} =\frac{1}{2} C V_{\text {common }}^2 {/tex} {tex} =\frac{1}{2} C\left(\frac{V}{2}\right)^2 {/tex} {tex} =\frac{U_A}{4} {/tex}
solution_html:
1. Charge will become half
      Charge will flow from capacitors A (high potential) to capacitor B (low potential) till they achieve equilibrium.
      Alternatively
      {tex} q_A=c v q_B=0 {/tex}
      {tex} V_{\text {common }}=\frac{q_A+q_B}{c_A+c_B}=\frac{{cV}}{2 {c}} {/tex}
      {tex} =\frac{{v}}{2} {/tex}
      {tex} q_A^{\prime}={cv}_{\text {common }}=\frac{{cV}}{2}=\frac{q_A}{2} {/tex}
    2. Potential difference will become half.
      {tex} V_{\text {common }} =\frac{q_A+q_B}{c_A+c_B} {/tex}
      {tex} =\frac{{cv}+0}{2 {c}}=\frac{{v}}{2} {/tex}
    3. Energy stored will becomes one fourth
      {tex} U_A =\frac{1}{2} C V^2 {/tex}
      {tex} U_A^{\prime} =\frac{1}{2} C V_{\text {common }}^2 {/tex}
      {tex} =\frac{1}{2} C\left(\frac{V}{2}\right)^2 {/tex}
      {tex} =\frac{U_A}{4} {/tex}
    1.  
    2.

question_html:
Read the following paragraph and answer the questions that follow. (4 {tex}\times{/tex} 1 = 4)
A capacitor is a system of two conductors separated by an insulator. In practice, the two conductors have charges Q and -Q with potential difference {tex}V=V_1-V_2{/tex} between them. The ratio {tex}\frac{Q}{V}{/tex} is a constant, denoted by C and is called the capacitance of the capacitor. It is independent of Q or V. It depends only on the geometrical configuration (shape, size, separation) of the two conductors and the medium separating the conductors. When a parallel plate capacitor is charged, the electric field {tex}{E}_0{/tex} is localised between the plates and is uniform throughout. When a slab of a dielectric is inserted between the charged plates (charge density {tex}\sigma{/tex}), the dielectric is polarised by the field. Consequently opposite charges appear on the faces of the slab, near the plates, with surface charge density of magnitude {tex}\sigma_{{p}}{/tex}. For a linear dielectric {tex}\sigma_p{/tex} is proportional to {tex}{E}_0{/tex}. Introduction of a dielectric changes the electric field, and hence, the capacitance of a capacitor, and hence, the energy stored in the capacitor.
Like resistors, capacitors can also be arranged in series or in parallel or in a combination of series and parallel.

  1. Consider a capacitor of capacitance C, with plate area A and plate separation d, filled with air [Fig. (a)]. The distance between the plates is increased to 2d and one of the plates is shifted as shown in Fig. (b). The capacitance of the new system now is:
    1. {tex}\frac{{C}}{4}{/tex}
    2. {tex}\frac{{C}}{2}{/tex}
    3. 2 C
    4. 4 C
  2. A slab (area A and thickness {tex}d_1{/tex}) of a linear dielectric of dielectric constant K is inserted between charged plates (charge density {tex}\sigma{/tex}) of a parallel plate capacitor [plate area A and plate separation {tex}\left.{d}\left(>{d}_1\right)\right]{/tex} and opposite charges with charge density of magnitude {tex}\sigma_{{p}}{/tex} appear on the faces of the slab. The dielectric constant K is given by:
    1. {tex}\frac{\sigma+\sigma_p}{\sigma}{/tex}
    2. {tex}\frac{\sigma}{\sigma-\sigma_p}{/tex}
    3. {tex}\frac{\sigma+\sigma_p}{\sigma_p}{/tex}
    4. {tex}\frac{\sigma}{\sigma_p}{/tex}
  3. An electric field E is established between the plates of an air filled parallel plate capacitor, with charges Q and {tex}-{Q} . {V}{/tex} is the volume of the space enclosed between the plates. The energy stored in the capacitor is:
    1. {tex} \frac{1}{2} \varepsilon_0 {E}^2{/tex}
    2. {tex} \varepsilon_0 {Q}^2 {E}{/tex}
    3. {tex} \frac{1}{2} \varepsilon_0 {E}^2 {~V}{/tex}
    4. {tex}\varepsilon_0 {EQV}{/tex}
    1. Three capacitors {tex}{A}, {B}{/tex} and M, each of capacitance C are connected to a capacitor N of capacitance 2 C and a battery as shown in the figure. If the charges on {tex}A{/tex} and {tex}N{/tex} are {tex}Q{/tex} and {tex}{Q}^{\prime}{/tex} respectively, then {tex}\frac{{Q}^{\prime}}{{Q}}{/tex} is:
      1. {tex}\frac{1}{6}{/tex}
      2. {tex}\frac{1}{3}{/tex}
      3. 3
      4. 6

        OR

    2. A slab (area A and thickness {tex}\frac{{d}}{2}{/tex}) of dielectric constant K is inserted in a parallel plate capacitor of plate area A and plate separation d . If C and {tex}{C}_0{/tex} are the capacitances of the capacitors with and without the dielectric, then {tex}\frac{{C}}{{C}_0}{/tex} is:
      1. {tex}\frac{{K}+1}{2 {~K}}{/tex}
      2. {tex}\frac{2 {~K}}{{~K}+1}{/tex}
      3. {tex}\frac{{K}}{{K}-1}{/tex}
      4. {tex}\frac{{K}-1}{{~K}}{/tex}

If the charges on {tex}A{/tex} and {tex}N{/tex} are {tex}Q{/tex} and {tex}{Q}^{\prime}{/tex} respectively, then {tex}\frac{{Q}^{\prime}}{{Q}}{/tex} is: {tex}\frac{1}{6}{/tex} {tex}\frac{1}{3}{/tex} 3 6 OR A slab (area A and thickness {tex}\frac{{d}}{2}{/tex} ) of dielectric constant K is inserted in a parallel plate capacitor of plate area A and plate separation d . If C and {tex}{C}_0{/tex} are the capacitances of the capacitors with and without the dielectric, then {tex}\frac{{C}}{{C}_0}{/tex} is: {tex}\frac{{K}+1}{2 {~K}}{/tex} {tex}\frac{2 {~K}}{{~K}+1}{/tex} {tex}\frac{{K}}{{K}-1}{/tex} {tex}\frac{{K}-1}{{~K}}{/tex}
explanation: (A) {tex}\left(\frac{C}{4}\right){/tex} (B) {tex}\frac{\sigma}{\sigma-\sigma_p}{/tex} (C) {tex}\frac{1}{2} \varepsilon_0 E^2 V{/tex} (D) 6 OR (B) {tex}\frac{2 K}{K+1}{/tex}
solution: (A) {tex}\left(\frac{C}{4}\right){/tex} (B) {tex}\frac{\sigma}{\sigma-\sigma_p}{/tex} (C) {tex}\frac{1}{2} \varepsilon_0 E^2 V{/tex} (D) 6 OR (B) {tex}\frac{2 K}{K+1}{/tex}
solution_html:
1. (A) {tex}\left(\frac{C}{4}\right){/tex}
  2. (B) {tex}\frac{\sigma}{\sigma-\sigma_p}{/tex}
  3. (C) {tex}\frac{1}{2} \varepsilon_0 E^2 V{/tex}
    1. (D) 6

      OR

    2. (B) {tex}\frac{2 K}{K+1}{/tex}

question_html:
Read the following paragraph and answer the questions that follow. (4 {tex}\times{/tex} 1 = 4)
A parallel plate capacitor consists of two conducting plates kept generally parallel to each other at a distance. When the capacitor is charged, the charge resides on the inner surfaces of the plates and an electric field is set up between them. Thus, electrostatic energy is stored in the capacitor. The figure shows three large square metallic plates, each of side '{tex}L{/tex}' held parallel and equidistant from each other. The space between {tex}P_1{/tex} and {tex}P_2{/tex} and {tex}P_2{/tex} and {tex}P_3{/tex} is completely filled with mica sheets of dielectric constant '{tex}K{/tex}'. The plate {tex}P_2{/tex} is connected to point {tex}A{/tex} and other plates {tex}P_1{/tex} and {tex}P_3{/tex} are connected to point B. Point A is maintained at a positive potential with respect to point B and the potential difference between A and B is V.

  1. The capacitance of the system between A and B will be:
    1. {tex}\frac{\varepsilon_0 K^2}{d}{/tex}
    2. {tex}\frac{\varepsilon_0 K L^2}{2 d}{/tex}
    3. {tex} \frac{2 \varepsilon_0 K^2}{{~d}}{/tex}
    4. {tex}\frac{2 \varepsilon_0 {Kd}}{{L}^2}{/tex}
  2. The charge on plate {tex}P_1{/tex} is:
    1. {tex}\frac{\varepsilon_0 {VKL}^2}{2 {~d}}{/tex}
    2. {tex}\frac{\varepsilon_0 V K L^2}{d}{/tex}
    3. {tex} \frac{2 \varepsilon_0 V K L^2}{{~d}}{/tex}
    4. {tex}\frac{\varepsilon_0 {VKL}^2}{4 {~d}}{/tex}
  3. The electric field in the region between {tex}P_1{/tex} and {tex}P_2{/tex} is:
    1. {tex}\frac{{V}}{{d}}{/tex}
    2. {tex}\frac{2 {~V}}{{~d}}{/tex}
    3. {tex}\frac{{V}}{2 {~d}}{/tex}
    4. {tex}\frac{{d}}{{V}}{/tex}
    1. The separation between the plates of same area {tex}\left(L^2\right){/tex} of a parallel plate air capacitor having capacitance equal to that of this system, will be:
      1. {tex}\frac{{d}}{{K}}{/tex}
      2. {tex}\frac{2 {~d}}{{~K}}{/tex}
      3. {tex}\frac{{d}}{2 {~K}}{/tex}
      4. {tex}\frac{{d}}{4 {~K}}{/tex}

        OR

    2. If the source of potential difference applied between A and B is removed, and then A and B are connected by a conducting wire, the net charge on the system will be:
      1. {tex}\frac{\varepsilon_0 {VKL}^2}{4 {~d}}{/tex}
      2. {tex}\frac{\varepsilon_0 {VKL}^2}{2 {~d}}{/tex}
      3. {tex}\frac{\varepsilon_0 {VKL}^2}{{~d}}{/tex}
      4. Zero

The capacitance of the system between A and B will be: {tex}\frac{\varepsilon_0 K^2}{d}{/tex} {tex}\frac{\varepsilon_0 K L^2}{2 d}{/tex} {tex} \frac{2 \varepsilon_0 K^2}{{~d}}{/tex} {tex}\frac{2 \varepsilon_0 {Kd}}{{L}^2}{/tex} The charge on plate {tex}P_1{/tex} is: {tex}\frac{\varepsilon_0 {VKL}^2}{2 {~d}}{/tex} {tex}\frac{\varepsilon_0 V K L^2}{d}{/tex} {tex} \frac{2 \varepsilon_0 V K L^2}{{~d}}{/tex} {tex}\frac{\varepsilon_0 {VKL}^2}{4 {~d}}{/tex} The electric field in the region between {tex}P_1{/tex} and {tex}P_2{/tex} is: {tex}\frac{{V}}{{d}}{/tex} {tex}\frac{2 {~V}}{{~d}}{/tex} {tex}\frac{{V}}{2 {~d}}{/tex} {tex}\frac{{d}}{{V}}{/tex} The separation between the plates of same area {tex}\left(L^2\right){/tex} of a parallel plate air capacitor having capacitance equal to that of this system, will be: {tex}\frac{{d}}{{K}}{/tex} {tex}\frac{2 {~d}}{{~K}}{/tex} {tex}\frac{{d}}{2 {~K}}{/tex} {tex}\frac{{d}}{4 {~K}}{/tex} OR If the source of potential difference applied between A and B is removed, and then A and B are connected by a conducting wire, the net charge on the system will be: {tex}\frac{\varepsilon_0 {VKL}^2}{4 {~d}}{/tex} {tex}\frac{\varepsilon_0 {VKL}^2}{2 {~d}}{/tex} {tex}\frac{\varepsilon_0 {VKL}^2}{{~d}}{/tex} Zero
explanation: (C) {tex}\frac{2 \varepsilon_0 K L^2}{d}{/tex} (B) {tex}\frac{\varepsilon_{{o}} {VKL}^2}{{~d}}{/tex} (A) {tex}\frac{{V}}{{d}}{/tex} (C) {tex}\frac{{d}}{2 {~K}}{/tex} OR (D) Zero
solution: (C) {tex}\frac{2 \varepsilon_0 K L^2}{d}{/tex} (B) {tex}\frac{\varepsilon_{{o}} {VKL}^2}{{~d}}{/tex} (A) {tex}\frac{{V}}{{d}}{/tex} (C) {tex}\frac{{d}}{2 {~K}}{/tex} OR (D) Zero
solution_html:
1. (C) {tex}\frac{2 \varepsilon_0 K L^2}{d}{/tex}
  2. (B) {tex}\frac{\varepsilon_{{o}} {VKL}^2}{{~d}}{/tex}
  3. (A) {tex}\frac{{V}}{{d}}{/tex}
    1. (C) {tex}\frac{{d}}{2 {~K}}{/tex}

      OR

    2. (D) Zero

question_html:
Two point charges of {tex}-5 \mu {C}{/tex} and {tex}2 \mu {C}{/tex} are located in free space at ({tex}-4 {~cm}, 0{/tex}) and ({tex}6 {~cm}, 0{/tex}) respectively.

  1. Calculate the amount of work done to separate the two charges at infinite distance.
  2. If this system of charges was initially kept in an electric field {tex}{E}=\frac{{A}}{{r}^{2}}{/tex}, where {tex}{A}=8 \times 10^{4} {~N} {C}^{-1} {m}^{2}{/tex}, calculate the electrostatic potential energy of the system.
question_text: Two point charges of {tex}-5 \mu {C}{/tex} and {tex}2 \mu {C}{/tex} are located in free space at ( {tex}-4 {~cm}, 0{/tex} ) and ( {tex}6 {~cm}, 0{/tex} ) respectively. Calculate the amount of work done to separate the two charges at infinite distance. If this system of charges was initially kept in an electric field {tex}{E}=\frac{{A}}{{r}^{2}}{/tex} , where {tex}{A}=8 \times 10^{4} {~N} {C}^{-1} {m}^{2}{/tex} , calculate the electrostatic potential energy of the system.
explanation: {tex} {U}= \frac{{kq}_1 {q}_2}{{r}} {/tex} {tex} =\frac{9 \times 10^9 \times\left(-5 \times 10^{-6}\right) \times\left(2 \times 10^{-6}\right)}{10 \times 10^{-2}} {/tex} {tex} =-0.9 {~J} {/tex} {tex} {~W}={U}(\text { infinity })-{U}({r})=0.9 {~J} {/tex} {tex} {E}= \frac{-{dV}}{{dr}} {/tex} {tex} {dV}=\frac{-{A}}{{r}^2} {dr} {/tex} {tex} {~V}= \frac{{A}}{{r}} {/tex} {tex} {U}={q}_1 {~V}_1+{q}_2 {~V}_2+\frac{{kq}_1 {q}_2}{{r}} {/tex} {tex} =\left(-5 \times 10^{-6}\right)\left(\frac{8 \times 10^4}{4 \times 10^{-2}}\right)+\left(2 \times 10^{-6}\right)\left(\frac{8 \times 10^4}{6 \times 10^{-2}}\right)-0.9 {/tex} {tex} =-10+2.67-0.9 {/tex} {tex} =-8.24 {~J} {/tex}
solution: {tex} {U}= \frac{{kq}_1 {q}_2}{{r}} {/tex} {tex} =\frac{9 \times 10^9 \times\left(-5 \times 10^{-6}\right) \times\left(2 \times 10^{-6}\right)}{10 \times 10^{-2}} {/tex} {tex} =-0.9 {~J} {/tex} {tex} {~W}={U}(\text { infinity })-{U}({r})=0.9 {~J} {/tex} {tex} {E}= \frac{-{dV}}{{dr}} {/tex} {tex} {dV}=\frac{-{A}}{{r}^2} {dr} {/tex} {tex} {~V}= \frac{{A}}{{r}} {/tex} {tex} {U}={q}_1 {~V}_1+{q}_2 {~V}_2+\frac{{kq}_1 {q}_2}{{r}} {/tex} {tex} =\left(-5 \times 10^{-6}\right)\left(\frac{8 \times 10^4}{4 \times 10^{-2}}\right)+\left(2 \times 10^{-6}\right)\left(\frac{8 \times 10^4}{6 \times 10^{-2}}\right)-0.9 {/tex} {tex} =-10+2.67-0.9 {/tex} {tex} =-8.24 {~J} {/tex}
solution_html:
1. {tex} {U}= \frac{{kq}_1 {q}_2}{{r}} {/tex}
    {tex} =\frac{9 \times 10^9 \times\left(-5 \times 10^{-6}\right) \times\left(2 \times 10^{-6}\right)}{10 \times 10^{-2}} {/tex}
    {tex} =-0.9 {~J} {/tex}
    {tex} {~W}={U}(\text { infinity })-{U}({r})=0.9 {~J} {/tex}
  2. {tex} {E}= \frac{-{dV}}{{dr}} {/tex}
    {tex} {dV}=\frac{-{A}}{{r}^2} {dr} {/tex}
    {tex} {~V}= \frac{{A}}{{r}} {/tex}
    {tex} {U}={q}_1 {~V}_1+{q}_2 {~V}_2+\frac{{kq}_1 {q}_2}{{r}} {/tex}
    {tex} =\left(-5 \times 10^{-6}\right)\left(\frac{8 \times 10^4}{4 \times 10^{-2}}\right)+\left(2 \times 10^{-6}\right)\left(\frac{8 \times 10^4}{6 \times 10^{-2}}\right)-0.9 {/tex}
    {tex} =-10+2.67-0.9 {/tex}
    {tex} =-8.24 {~J} {/tex}

question_html:
Read the following paragraph and answer the questions that follow.  ({tex}4 \times 1=4{/tex})
A parallel plate capacitor has two parallel plates which are separated by an insulating medium like air, mica, etc. When the plates are connected to the terminals of a battery, they get equal and opposite charges and an electric field is set up in between them. This electric field between the two plates depends upon the potential difference applied, the separation of the plates and nature of the medium between the plates.

  1. The electric field between the plates of a parallel plate capacitor is E. Now the separation between the plates is doubled and simultaneously the applied potential difference between the plates is reduced to half of its initial value. The new value of the electric field between the plates will be:
    1. E
    2. 2 E
    3. {tex}\frac{{E}}{4}{/tex}
    4. {tex}\frac{E}{2}{/tex}
  2. A constant electric field is to be maintained between the two plates of a capacitor whose separation d changes with time. Which of the graphs correctly depict the potential difference ({tex}V{/tex}) to be applied between the plates as a function of separation between the plates to maintain the constant electric field?

  3. In the above figure {tex}{P}, {Q}{/tex} are the two parallel plates of a capacitor. Plate {tex}Q{/tex} is at positive potential with respect to plate {tex}P{/tex}. MN is an imaginary line drawn perpendicular to the plates. Which of the graphs shows correctly the variations of the magnitude of electric field strength E along the line MN?
    1. Three parallel plates are placed above each other with equal displacement {tex}\vec{d}{/tex} between neighbouring plates. The electric field between the first pair of the plates is {tex}\overrightarrow{{E}}_1{/tex} and the electric field between the second pair of the plates is {tex}\overrightarrow{{E}}_2{/tex}. The potential difference between the third and the first plate is -
      1. {tex}\left(\overrightarrow{{E}}_1+\overrightarrow{{E}}_2\right) \cdot \overrightarrow{{d}}{/tex}
      2. {tex}\left(\overrightarrow{{E}}_1-\overrightarrow{{E}}_2\right) \cdot \overrightarrow{{d}}{/tex}
      3. {tex}\left(\vec{E}_2-\vec{E}_1\right) \cdot \vec{d}{/tex}
      4. {tex}\frac{d\left(E_1+E_2\right)}{2}{/tex}

        OR

    2. (iv) A material of dielectric constant K is filled in a parallel plate capacitor of capacitance C. The new value of its capacitance becomes
      1. C
      2. {tex}\frac{{C}}{{K}}{/tex}
      3. CK
      4. (D) {tex}{C}\left(1+\frac{1}{{~K}}\right){/tex}

This electric field between the two plates depends upon the potential difference applied, the separation of the plates and nature of the medium between the plates. The electric field between the plates of a parallel plate capacitor is E. Now the separation between the plates is doubled and simultaneously the applied potential difference between the plates is reduced to half of its initial value. The new value of the electric field between the plates will be: E 2 E {tex}\frac{{E}}{4}{/tex} {tex}\frac{E}{2}{/tex} A constant electric field is to be maintained between the two plates of a capacitor whose separation d changes with time. Which of the graphs correctly depict the potential difference ( {tex}V{/tex} ) to be applied between the plates as a function of separation between the plates to maintain the constant electric field? In the above figure {tex}{P}, {Q}{/tex} are the two parallel plates of a capacitor. Plate {tex}Q{/tex} is at positive potential with respect to plate {tex}P{/tex} . Three parallel plates are placed above each other with equal displacement {tex}\vec{d}{/tex} between neighbouring plates. The electric field between the first pair of the plates is {tex}\overrightarrow{{E}}_1{/tex} and the electric field between the second pair of the plates is {tex}\overrightarrow{{E}}_2{/tex} . The potential difference between the third and the first plate is - {tex}\left(\overrightarrow{{E}}_1+\overrightarrow{{E}}_2\right) \cdot \overrightarrow{{d}}{/tex} {tex}\left(\overrightarrow{{E}}_1-\overrightarrow{{E}}_2\right) \cdot \overrightarrow{{d}}{/tex} {tex}\left(\vec{E}_2-\vec{E}_1\right) \cdot \vec{d}{/tex} {tex}\frac{d\left(E_1+E_2\right)}{2}{/tex} OR (iv) A material of dielectric constant K is filled in a parallel plate capacitor of capacitance C. question_html: Three point charges {tex}{Q}_1, {Q}_2{/tex} and {tex}{Q}_3{/tex} are located in {tex}{x}-{y}{/tex} plane at points {tex}(-{d}, 0),(0,0){/tex} and {tex}(d, 0){/tex} respectively. {tex}Q_1{/tex} and {tex}Q_3{/tex} are identical and {tex}Q_2{/tex} is positive. What will be the nature and value of {tex}Q_1{/tex} so that the potential energy of the system is zero?
question_text: Three point charges {tex}{Q}_1, {Q}_2{/tex} and {tex}{Q}_3{/tex} are located in {tex}{x}-{y}{/tex} plane at points {tex}(-{d}, 0),(0,0){/tex} and {tex}(d, 0){/tex} respectively. {tex}Q_1{/tex} and {tex}Q_3{/tex} are identical and {tex}Q_2{/tex} is positive. What will be the nature and value of {tex}Q_1{/tex} so that the potential energy of the system is zero?
explanation: Nature of {tex}{Q}_1{/tex} will be negative. Let, {tex}{Q}_1={Q}_3={q}{/tex} {tex} \frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0}\left[\frac{q Q_2}{d}+\frac{q q}{2 d}+\frac{Q_2 q}{d}\right]=0 {/tex} {tex} \frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0 d}\left[q Q_2+\frac{q^2}{2}+Q_2 q\right]=0 {/tex} {tex} 2 q Q_2+\frac{q^2}{2}=0 {/tex} {tex} 2 q Q_2=-\frac{q^2}{2} {/tex} {tex} {Q}_1=q=-4 Q_2 {/tex}
solution: Nature of {tex}{Q}_1{/tex} will be negative. Let, {tex}{Q}_1={Q}_3={q}{/tex} {tex} \frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0}\left[\frac{q Q_2}{d}+\frac{q q}{2 d}+\frac{Q_2 q}{d}\right]=0 {/tex} {tex} \frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0 d}\left[q Q_2+\frac{q^2}{2}+Q_2 q\right]=0 {/tex} {tex} 2 q Q_2+\frac{q^2}{2}=0 {/tex} {tex} 2 q Q_2=-\frac{q^2}{2} {/tex} {tex} {Q}_1=q=-4 Q_2 {/tex}
solution_html:
Nature of {tex}{Q}_1{/tex} will be negative.
Let, {tex}{Q}_1={Q}_3={q}{/tex}
{tex} \frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0}\left[\frac{q Q_2}{d}+\frac{q q}{2 d}+\frac{Q_2 q}{d}\right]=0 {/tex}
{tex} \frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0 d}\left[q Q_2+\frac{q^2}{2}+Q_2 q\right]=0 {/tex}
{tex} 2 q Q_2+\frac{q^2}{2}=0 {/tex}
{tex} 2 q Q_2=-\frac{q^2}{2} {/tex}
{tex} {Q}_1=q=-4 Q_2 {/tex}

subject: Physics
question_html: An air-filled parallel plate capacitor with plate separation 1 mm has a capacitance of 20 pF. It is charged to {tex}4.0 \mu {C}{/tex}. Calculate the amount of work done to pull its plates to a separation of 5 mm. Assume the charge on the plates remains the same.
question_text: An air-filled parallel plate capacitor with plate separation 1 mm has a capacitance of 20 pF. It is charged to {tex}4.0 \mu {C}{/tex} . Calculate the amount of work done to pull its plates to a separation of 5 mm. Assume the charge on the plates remains the same.
explanation: To calculate the work done in pulling the plates of an air-filled parallel plate capacitor apart while keeping the charge constant, use the formula: {tex} W=\frac{1}{2} Q\left(V_f-V_i\right){/tex} Initially, the capacitance is {tex}C_i=20 {pF}{/tex} and charge {tex}Q=4.0 \mu {C}{/tex} . The initial voltage {tex}V_i{/tex} is: {tex} V_i=\frac{Q}{C_i}=\frac{4.0 \times 10^{-6}}{20 \times 10^{-12}}=200 {~V}{/tex} When the separation increases to 5 mm , the new capacitance {tex}C_f={/tex} 4.0 pF , and the final voltage {tex}V_f{/tex} is: {tex} V_f=\frac{Q}{C_f}=\frac{4.0 \times 10^{-6}}{4.0 \times 10^{-12}}=1000 {~V}{/tex} Thus, the work done is: {tex} W=\frac{1}{2} \times 4.0 \times 10^{-6} \times(1000-200)=1.6 {~mJ}{/tex} So, the work done is 1.6 mJ.
solution: To calculate the work done in pulling the plates of an air-filled parallel plate capacitor apart while keeping the charge constant, use the formula: {tex} W=\frac{1}{2} Q\left(V_f-V_i\right){/tex} Initially, the capacitance is {tex}C_i=20 {pF}{/tex} and charge {tex}Q=4.0 \mu {C}{/tex} . The initial voltage {tex}V_i{/tex} is: {tex} V_i=\frac{Q}{C_i}=\frac{4.0 \times 10^{-6}}{20 \times 10^{-12}}=200 {~V}{/tex} When the separation increases to 5 mm , the new capacitance {tex}C_f={/tex} 4.0 pF , and the final voltage {tex}V_f{/tex} is: {tex} V_f=\frac{Q}{C_f}=\frac{4.0 \times 10^{-6}}{4.0 \times 10^{-12}}=1000 {~V}{/tex} Thus, the work done is: {tex} W=\frac{1}{2} \times 4.0 \times 10^{-6} \times(1000-200)=1.6 {~mJ}{/tex} So, the work done is 1.6 mJ.
solution_html:
To calculate the work done in pulling the plates of an air-filled parallel plate capacitor apart while keeping the charge constant, use the formula:
{tex} W=\frac{1}{2} Q\left(V_f-V_i\right){/tex}
Initially, the capacitance is {tex}C_i=20 {pF}{/tex} and charge {tex}Q=4.0 \mu {C}{/tex}. The initial voltage {tex}V_i{/tex} is:
{tex} V_i=\frac{Q}{C_i}=\frac{4.0 \times 10^{-6}}{20 \times 10^{-12}}=200 {~V}{/tex}
When the separation increases to 5 mm , the new capacitance {tex}C_f={/tex} 4.0 pF , and the final voltage {tex}V_f{/tex} is:
{tex} V_f=\frac{Q}{C_f}=\frac{4.0 \times 10^{-6}}{4.0 \times 10^{-12}}=1000 {~V}{/tex}
Thus, the work done is:
{tex} W=\frac{1}{2} \times 4.0 \times 10^{-6} \times(1000-200)=1.6 {~mJ}{/tex}
So, the work done is 1.6 mJ.

question_html:
1. Derive an expression for potential energy of an electric dipole {tex}\vec{p}{/tex} in an external uniform electric field {tex}\vec{E}{/tex}. When is the potential energy of the dipole (1) maximum, and (2) minimum?
  2. An electric dipole consists of point charges -1.0 pC and +1.0 pC located at {tex}(0,0){/tex} and (3 mm, 4 mm) respectively in x - y plane. An electric field {tex}\vec{E}=\left(\frac{1000 V}{m}\right) \hat{i}{/tex} is switched on in the region. Find the torque {tex}\vec{\tau}{/tex} acting on the dipole.
question_text: Derive an expression for potential energy of an electric dipole {tex}\vec{p}{/tex} in an external uniform electric field {tex}\vec{E}{/tex} . When is the potential energy of the dipole (1) maximum, and (2) minimum? An electric dipole consists of point charges -1.0 pC and +1.0 pC located at {tex}(0,0){/tex} and (3 mm, 4 mm) respectively in x - y plane. An electric field {tex}\vec{E}=\left(\frac{1000 V}{m}\right) \hat{i}{/tex} is switched on in the region. Find the torque {tex}\vec{\tau}{/tex} acting on the dipole.
explanation: The amount of work done in rotating the dipole from {tex}\theta=\theta_0{/tex} to {tex}\theta=\theta_1{/tex} by the external torque {tex}{W}=\int_\limits{e_0}^{\theta_1} \tau_{e x t} d \theta{/tex} {tex}=\int_\limits{\theta_0}^a p E \sin \theta d \theta{/tex} {tex}{W}=p E\left(\cos \theta_0-\cos \theta_1\right){/tex} For {tex}\theta_0=\frac{\pi}{2}{/tex} and {tex}\theta_1=\theta{/tex} {tex}=p E\left(\cos \frac{\pi}{2}-\cos \theta\right){/tex} {tex}U(\theta)=-p E \cos \theta{/tex} {tex}=-\vec{p} \cdot \vec{E}{/tex} Potential energy is maximum when: {tex}\vec{p}{/tex} is antiparallel to {tex}\vec{E}{/tex} Potential energy is minimum when: {tex}\vec{p}{/tex} is along to {tex}\vec{E}{/tex} Dipole Moment Calculation: The charge q = 1.0 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -12 C. Separation vector {tex}\vec{d}{/tex} = (3 mm, 4 mm) = (3 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -3 m, 4 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -3 m) Dipole moment {tex}\vec{p}=q \vec{d}{/tex} = (1.0 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -12 {tex}\times{/tex} 3 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -3 , 1.0 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -12 {tex}\times{/tex} 4 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -3 ) = (3.0 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -15 , 4.0 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -15 ) C.m. Torque Calculation: Electric field {tex}\vec{E}=1000 \hat{i} \mathrm{~V} / \mathrm{m}{/tex} Torque {tex}\vec{\tau}=\vec{p} \times \vec{E}{/tex} {tex}\vec{\tau}=\left(3.0 \times 10^{-15} \hat{i}+4.0 \times 10^{-15} \hat{j}\right) \times 1000 \hat{i}=\left(4.0 \times 10^{-15} \times 1000\right) \hat{k}{/tex} {tex}\vec{\tau}=4.0 \times 10^{-12} \mathrm{~N} \cdot \mathrm{m} \hat{k}{/tex} The torque is {tex}\vec{\tau}=4.0 \times 10^{-12} \mathrm{~N} \cdot \mathrm{m} \hat{k}{/tex}
solution: The amount of work done in rotating the dipole from {tex}\theta=\theta_0{/tex} to {tex}\theta=\theta_1{/tex} by the external torque {tex}{W}=\int_\limits{e_0}^{\theta_1} \tau_{e x t} d \theta{/tex} {tex}=\int_\limits{\theta_0}^a p E \sin \theta d \theta{/tex} {tex}{W}=p E\left(\cos \theta_0-\cos \theta_1\right){/tex} For {tex}\theta_0=\frac{\pi}{2}{/tex} and {tex}\theta_1=\theta{/tex} {tex}=p E\left(\cos \frac{\pi}{2}-\cos \theta\right){/tex} {tex}U(\theta)=-p E \cos \theta{/tex} {tex}=-\vec{p} \cdot \vec{E}{/tex} Potential energy is maximum when: {tex}\vec{p}{/tex} is antiparallel to {tex}\vec{E}{/tex} Potential energy is minimum when: {tex}\vec{p}{/tex} is along to {tex}\vec{E}{/tex} Dipole Moment Calculation: The charge q = 1.0 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -12 C. Separation vector {tex}\vec{d}{/tex} = (3 mm, 4 mm) = (3 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -3 m, 4 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -3 m) Dipole moment {tex}\vec{p}=q \vec{d}{/tex} = (1.0 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -12 {tex}\times{/tex} 3 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -3 , 1.0 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -12 {tex}\times{/tex} 4 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -3 ) = (3.0 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -15 , 4.0 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -15 ) C.m. Torque Calculation: Electric field {tex}\vec{E}=1000 \hat{i} \mathrm{~V} / \mathrm{m}{/tex} Torque {tex}\vec{\tau}=\vec{p} \times \vec{E}{/tex} {tex}\vec{\tau}=\left(3.0 \times 10^{-15} \hat{i}+4.0 \times 10^{-15} \hat{j}\right) \times 1000 \hat{i}=\left(4.0 \times 10^{-15} \times 1000\right) \hat{k}{/tex} {tex}\vec{\tau}=4.0 \times 10^{-12} \mathrm{~N} \cdot \mathrm{m} \hat{k}{/tex} The torque is {tex}\vec{\tau}=4.0 \times 10^{-12} \mathrm{~N} \cdot \mathrm{m} \hat{k}{/tex}
solution_html:
1. The amount of work done in rotating the dipole from {tex}\theta=\theta_0{/tex} to {tex}\theta=\theta_1{/tex} by the external torque
    {tex}{W}=\int_\limits{e_0}^{\theta_1} \tau_{e x t} d \theta{/tex}
    {tex}=\int_\limits{\theta_0}^a p E \sin \theta d \theta{/tex}
    {tex}{W}=p E\left(\cos \theta_0-\cos \theta_1\right){/tex}
    For {tex}\theta_0=\frac{\pi}{2}{/tex} and {tex}\theta_1=\theta{/tex}
    {tex}=p E\left(\cos \frac{\pi}{2}-\cos \theta\right){/tex}
    {tex}U(\theta)=-p E \cos \theta{/tex}{tex}=-\vec{p} \cdot \vec{E}{/tex}
    1. Potential energy is maximum when:
      {tex}\vec{p}{/tex} is antiparallel to {tex}\vec{E}{/tex}
    2. Potential energy is minimum when:
      {tex}\vec{p}{/tex} is along to {tex}\vec{E}{/tex}
  2. Dipole Moment Calculation:
    The charge q = 1.0 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-12C.
    Separation vector {tex}\vec{d}{/tex} = (3 mm, 4 mm) = (3 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-3 m, 4 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-3 m)
    Dipole moment {tex}\vec{p}=q \vec{d}{/tex} = (1.0 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-12 {tex}\times{/tex} 3 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-3, 1.0 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-12 {tex}\times{/tex} 4 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-3) = (3.0 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-15, 4.0 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-15) C.m.
    Torque Calculation:
    Electric field {tex}\vec{E}=1000 \hat{i} \mathrm{~V} / \mathrm{m}{/tex}
    Torque {tex}\vec{\tau}=\vec{p} \times \vec{E}{/tex}
    {tex}\vec{\tau}=\left(3.0 \times 10^{-15} \hat{i}+4.0 \times 10^{-15} \hat{j}\right) \times 1000 \hat{i}=\left(4.0 \times 10^{-15} \times 1000\right) \hat{k}{/tex}
    {tex}\vec{\tau}=4.0 \times 10^{-12} \mathrm{~N} \cdot \mathrm{m} \hat{k}{/tex}
    The torque is {tex}\vec{\tau}=4.0 \times 10^{-12} \mathrm{~N} \cdot \mathrm{m} \hat{k}{/tex}

question_html:
1. An electric dipole (dipole moment {tex}\overrightarrow{{p}}={p} \hat{{i}}{/tex} ), consisting of charges {tex}-q{/tex} and {tex}q{/tex}, separated by distance {tex}2 a{/tex}, is placed along the {tex}x{/tex}-axis, with its centre at the origin. Show that the potential V, due to this dipole, at a point {tex}x,(x>>a){/tex} is equal to {tex}\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0} \cdot \frac{\overrightarrow{{p}} \cdot \hat{{i}}}{{x}^2}{/tex}.
  2. Two isolated metallic spheres {tex}S_1{/tex} and {tex}S_2{/tex} of radii 1 cm and 3 cm respectively are charged such that both have the same charge density {tex}\left(\frac{2}{\pi} \times 10^{-9}\right) {C} / {m}^2{/tex}. They are placed far away from each other and connected by a thin wire. Calculate the new charge on sphere {tex}S_1{/tex}.
question_text: An electric dipole (dipole moment {tex}\overrightarrow{{p}}={p} \hat{{i}}{/tex} ), consisting of charges {tex}-q{/tex} and {tex}q{/tex} , separated by distance {tex}2 a{/tex} , is placed along the {tex}x{/tex} -axis, with its centre at the origin. Show that the potential V, due to this dipole, at a point {tex}x,(x>>a){/tex} is equal to {tex}\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0} \cdot \frac{\overrightarrow{{p}} \cdot \hat{{i}}}{{x}^2}{/tex} . Two isolated metallic spheres {tex}S_1{/tex} and {tex}S_2{/tex} of radii 1 cm and 3 cm respectively are charged such that both have the same charge density {tex}\left(\frac{2}{\pi} \times 10^{-9}\right) {C} / {m}^2{/tex} . They are placed far away from each other and connected by a thin wire. Calculate the new charge on sphere {tex}S_1{/tex} .
explanation: {tex} V=\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0} \frac{q}{r} {/tex} {tex} V=V_{+q}-V_{-q}{/tex} {tex} V =\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0}\left[\frac{q}{(x-a)}-\frac{q}{(x+a)}\right] {/tex} {tex} =\frac{q}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0}\left[\frac{x+a-x+a}{\left(x^2-a^2\right)}\right] {/tex} {tex}V =\frac{q}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0} \frac{2 a}{\left(x^2-a^2\right)}=\frac{p}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0\left(x^2-a^2\right)}{/tex} As p is along x-axis, so {tex}V=\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0} \frac{\vec{p} \cdot \hat{i}}{\left(x^2-a^2\right)}{/tex} If x >> a {tex}V=\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0} \frac{\vec{p} \cdot \hat{i}}{x^2}{/tex} Charge on sphere S 1 : Q 1 = surface charge density {tex}\times{/tex} surface Area {tex}=\left(\frac{2}{\pi} \times 10^{-9}\right) \times 4 \pi\left(1 \times 10^{-2}\right)^2{/tex} = 8 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -13 C Charge on sphere S 2 : Q 2 = surface charge density {tex}\times{/tex} surface Area {tex}=\left(\frac{2}{\pi} \times 10^{-9}\right) \times 4 \pi\left(3 \times 10^{-2}\right)^2{/tex} = 72 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -13 C When connected by a thin wire they acquire a common potential V and the charge remains conserved. {tex}Q_1+Q_2=Q_1^{\prime}+Q_2^{\prime}{/tex} = C 1 V + C 2 V Common potential(V) {tex}=\frac{Q_1+Q_2}{C_1+C_2}{/tex} {tex}C_1=4 \pi \varepsilon_0 r_1=\frac{1}{9 \times 10^9} \times 10^{-2}=\frac{1}{9} \times 10^{-11} \mathrm{~F}{/tex} {tex}C_2=4 \pi \varepsilon_0 r_2=\frac{1}{9 \times 10^9} \times 3 \times 10^{-2}=\frac{1}{3} \times 10^{-11} \mathrm{~F}{/tex} {tex}V=\frac{80 \times 10^{-13}}{\left(\frac{1}{9}+\frac{1}{3}\right) \times 10^{-11}}=1.8 \mathrm{~V}{/tex} {tex}Q_1^{\prime}=C_1 V=\frac{1}{9} \times 10^{-11} \times 1.8{/tex} {tex}Q_1^{\prime}=2 \times 10^{-12} C{/tex}
solution: {tex} V=\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0} \frac{q}{r} {/tex} {tex} V=V_{+q}-V_{-q}{/tex} {tex} V =\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0}\left[\frac{q}{(x-a)}-\frac{q}{(x+a)}\right] {/tex} {tex} =\frac{q}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0}\left[\frac{x+a-x+a}{\left(x^2-a^2\right)}\right] {/tex} {tex}V =\frac{q}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0} \frac{2 a}{\left(x^2-a^2\right)}=\frac{p}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0\left(x^2-a^2\right)}{/tex} As p is along x-axis, so {tex}V=\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0} \frac{\vec{p} \cdot \hat{i}}{\left(x^2-a^2\right)}{/tex} If x >> a {tex}V=\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0} \frac{\vec{p} \cdot \hat{i}}{x^2}{/tex} Charge on sphere S 1 : Q 1 = surface charge density {tex}\times{/tex} surface Area {tex}=\left(\frac{2}{\pi} \times 10^{-9}\right) \times 4 \pi\left(1 \times 10^{-2}\right)^2{/tex} = 8 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -13 C Charge on sphere S 2 : Q 2 = surface charge density {tex}\times{/tex} surface Area {tex}=\left(\frac{2}{\pi} \times 10^{-9}\right) \times 4 \pi\left(3 \times 10^{-2}\right)^2{/tex} = 72 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -13 C When connected by a thin wire they acquire a common potential V and the charge remains conserved. {tex}Q_1+Q_2=Q_1^{\prime}+Q_2^{\prime}{/tex} = C 1 V + C 2 V Common potential(V) {tex}=\frac{Q_1+Q_2}{C_1+C_2}{/tex} {tex}C_1=4 \pi \varepsilon_0 r_1=\frac{1}{9 \times 10^9} \times 10^{-2}=\frac{1}{9} \times 10^{-11} \mathrm{~F}{/tex} {tex}C_2=4 \pi \varepsilon_0 r_2=\frac{1}{9 \times 10^9} \times 3 \times 10^{-2}=\frac{1}{3} \times 10^{-11} \mathrm{~F}{/tex} {tex}V=\frac{80 \times 10^{-13}}{\left(\frac{1}{9}+\frac{1}{3}\right) \times 10^{-11}}=1.8 \mathrm{~V}{/tex} {tex}Q_1^{\prime}=C_1 V=\frac{1}{9} \times 10^{-11} \times 1.8{/tex} {tex}Q_1^{\prime}=2 \times 10^{-12} C{/tex}
solution_html:
1. {tex} V=\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0} \frac{q}{r} {/tex}
    {tex} V=V_{+q}-V_{-q}{/tex}
    {tex} V =\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0}\left[\frac{q}{(x-a)}-\frac{q}{(x+a)}\right] {/tex}
    {tex} =\frac{q}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0}\left[\frac{x+a-x+a}{\left(x^2-a^2\right)}\right] {/tex}
    {tex}V =\frac{q}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0} \frac{2 a}{\left(x^2-a^2\right)}=\frac{p}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0\left(x^2-a^2\right)}{/tex}
    As p is along x-axis, so
    {tex}V=\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0} \frac{\vec{p} \cdot \hat{i}}{\left(x^2-a^2\right)}{/tex}
    If x >> a
    {tex}V=\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0} \frac{\vec{p} \cdot \hat{i}}{x^2}{/tex}
  2. Charge on sphere S1:
    Q1 = surface charge density {tex}\times{/tex} surface Area
    {tex}=\left(\frac{2}{\pi} \times 10^{-9}\right) \times 4 \pi\left(1 \times 10^{-2}\right)^2{/tex}
    = 8 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-13 C
    Charge on sphere S2:
    Q2 = surface charge density {tex}\times{/tex} surface Area
    {tex}=\left(\frac{2}{\pi} \times 10^{-9}\right) \times 4 \pi\left(3 \times 10^{-2}\right)^2{/tex}
    = 72 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-13 C
    When connected by a thin wire they acquire a common potential V and the charge remains conserved.
    {tex}Q_1+Q_2=Q_1^{\prime}+Q_2^{\prime}{/tex}
    = C1V + C2V
    Common potential(V) {tex}=\frac{Q_1+Q_2}{C_1+C_2}{/tex}
    {tex}C_1=4 \pi \varepsilon_0 r_1=\frac{1}{9 \times 10^9} \times 10^{-2}=\frac{1}{9} \times 10^{-11} \mathrm{~F}{/tex}
    {tex}C_2=4 \pi \varepsilon_0 r_2=\frac{1}{9 \times 10^9} \times 3 \times 10^{-2}=\frac{1}{3} \times 10^{-11} \mathrm{~F}{/tex}
    {tex}V=\frac{80 \times 10^{-13}}{\left(\frac{1}{9}+\frac{1}{3}\right) \times 10^{-11}}=1.8 \mathrm{~V}{/tex}
    {tex}Q_1^{\prime}=C_1 V=\frac{1}{9} \times 10^{-11} \times 1.8{/tex}
    {tex}Q_1^{\prime}=2 \times 10^{-12} C{/tex}
