question_html: Consider a group of charges {tex}q_1, q_2, q_3 \ldots{/tex} such that {tex}\Sigma q \neq 0{/tex}. Then equipotentials at a large distance, due to this group are approximately:
question_text: Consider a group of charges {tex}q_1, q_2, q_3 \ldots{/tex} such that {tex}\Sigma q \neq 0{/tex} . Then equipotentials at a large distance, due to this group are approximately:

options.explanation: For a group of point charges {tex}q_1, q_2, q_3{/tex} such that the net charge is zero {tex}\left(q_1+q_2+q_3=0\right){/tex} , the equipotential surfaces at a large distance from this configuration will behave similarly to the electric field of a single point charge with a net charge of zero. At a large distance, the influence of the individual charges diminishes, and the equipotentials approximate those of a point charge. Thus, they form spherical surfaces centered around the point where the net charge is zero.

explanation: For a group of point charges {tex}q_1, q_2, q_3{/tex} such that the net charge is zero {tex}\left(q_1+q_2+q_3=0\right){/tex} , the equipotential surfaces at a large distance from this configuration will behave similarly to the electric field of a single point charge with a net charge of zero. At a large distance, the influence of the individual charges diminishes, and the equipotentials approximate those of a point charge. Thus, they form spherical surfaces centered around the point where the net charge is zero.

question_html:
1. Draw equipotential surfaces for an electric dipole.
  2. Two point charges {tex}q_{1}{/tex} and {tex}q_{2}{/tex} are located at {tex}\vec{r}_{1}{/tex} and {tex}\vec{r}_{2}{/tex} respectively in an external electric field {tex}\vec{E}{/tex}. Obtain an expression for the potential energy of the system.
  3. The dipole moment of a molecule is {tex}10^{-30} {Cm}{/tex}. It is placed in an electric field {tex}\overrightarrow{{E}}{/tex} of {tex}10^{5} {~V} / {m}{/tex} such that its axis is along the electric field. The direction of {tex}\vec{E}{/tex} is suddenly changed by {tex}60^{\circ}{/tex} at an instant. Find the change in the potential energy of the dipole, at that instant.
question_text: Draw equipotential surfaces for an electric dipole. Two point charges {tex}q_{1}{/tex} and {tex}q_{2}{/tex} are located at {tex}\vec{r}_{1}{/tex} and {tex}\vec{r}_{2}{/tex} respectively in an external electric field {tex}\vec{E}{/tex} . Obtain an expression for the potential energy of the system. The dipole moment of a molecule is {tex}10^{-30} {Cm}{/tex} . It is placed in an electric field {tex}\overrightarrow{{E}}{/tex} of {tex}10^{5} {~V} / {m}{/tex} such that its axis is along the electric field. The direction of {tex}\vec{E}{/tex} is suddenly changed by {tex}60^{\circ}{/tex} at an instant. Find the change in the potential energy of the dipole, at that instant.
explanation: Work done in bringing a charge {tex}{q}_{1}{/tex} from infinity to {tex}\overrightarrow{{r}}_{1}{/tex} : {tex}{W}_{1}={q}_{1} {~V}\left(\overrightarrow{{r}}_{1}\right){/tex} ...(1) Work done in bringing a charge {tex}{q}_{2}{/tex} from infinity to {tex}\overrightarrow{{r}}_{2}{/tex} against the external field : {tex}{W}_{2}={q}_{2} {~V}\left(\overrightarrow{{r}}_{2}\right){/tex} ...(2) Work done on {tex}{q}_{2}{/tex} against the field due to {tex}{q}_{1}{/tex} : {tex}{W}_{12}=\frac{{q}_{1} {q}_{2}}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0} {r}_{12}}{/tex} ...(3) Potential energy of the system {tex}={/tex} Total work done {tex}={q}_{1} {~V}\left(\overrightarrow{{r}}_{1}\right)+{q}_{2} {~V}\left(\overrightarrow{{r}}_{2}\right)+\frac{{q}_{1} {q}_{2}}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0} {r}_{12}}{/tex} Change in Potential energy {tex}={/tex} Work done {tex}{W}= {pE}\left[\cos \theta_{0}-\cos \theta_{1}\right]{/tex} {tex}{W}=10^{-30} \times 10^{5}\left[\cos 0^{0}-\cos 60^{0}\right]{/tex} {tex}{W}=5.0 \times 10^{-26} {~J}{/tex}
solution: Work done in bringing a charge {tex}{q}_{1}{/tex} from infinity to {tex}\overrightarrow{{r}}_{1}{/tex} : {tex}{W}_{1}={q}_{1} {~V}\left(\overrightarrow{{r}}_{1}\right){/tex} ...(1) Work done in bringing a charge {tex}{q}_{2}{/tex} from infinity to {tex}\overrightarrow{{r}}_{2}{/tex} against the external field : {tex}{W}_{2}={q}_{2} {~V}\left(\overrightarrow{{r}}_{2}\right){/tex} ...(2) Work done on {tex}{q}_{2}{/tex} against the field due to {tex}{q}_{1}{/tex} : {tex}{W}_{12}=\frac{{q}_{1} {q}_{2}}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0} {r}_{12}}{/tex} ...(3) Potential energy of the system {tex}={/tex} Total work done {tex}={q}_{1} {~V}\left(\overrightarrow{{r}}_{1}\right)+{q}_{2} {~V}\left(\overrightarrow{{r}}_{2}\right)+\frac{{q}_{1} {q}_{2}}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0} {r}_{12}}{/tex} Change in Potential energy {tex}={/tex} Work done {tex}{W}= {pE}\left[\cos \theta_{0}-\cos \theta_{1}\right]{/tex} {tex}{W}=10^{-30} \times 10^{5}\left[\cos 0^{0}-\cos 60^{0}\right]{/tex} {tex}{W}=5.0 \times 10^{-26} {~J}{/tex}
solution_html:
2. Work done in bringing a charge {tex}{q}_{1}{/tex} from infinity to {tex}\overrightarrow{{r}}_{1}{/tex}:
    {tex}{W}_{1}={q}_{1} {~V}\left(\overrightarrow{{r}}_{1}\right){/tex} ...(1)
    Work done in bringing a charge {tex}{q}_{2}{/tex} from infinity to {tex}\overrightarrow{{r}}_{2}{/tex} against the external field :
    {tex}{W}_{2}={q}_{2} {~V}\left(\overrightarrow{{r}}_{2}\right){/tex} ...(2)
    Work done on {tex}{q}_{2}{/tex} against the field due to {tex}{q}_{1}{/tex} :
    {tex}{W}_{12}=\frac{{q}_{1} {q}_{2}}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0} {r}_{12}}{/tex}...(3)
    Potential energy of the system {tex}={/tex} Total work done
    {tex}={q}_{1} {~V}\left(\overrightarrow{{r}}_{1}\right)+{q}_{2} {~V}\left(\overrightarrow{{r}}_{2}\right)+\frac{{q}_{1} {q}_{2}}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0} {r}_{12}}{/tex}
  3. Change in Potential energy {tex}={/tex} Work done
    {tex}{W}= {pE}\left[\cos \theta_{0}-\cos \theta_{1}\right]{/tex}
    {tex}{W}=10^{-30} \times 10^{5}\left[\cos 0^{0}-\cos 60^{0}\right]{/tex}
    {tex}{W}=5.0 \times 10^{-26} {~J}{/tex}

question_html: A thin spherical conducting shell of radius {tex}R{/tex} has a charge {tex}q{/tex}. A point charge Q is placed at the centre of the shell. Find (i) The charge density on the outer surface of the shell and (ii) the potential at a distance of (R/2) from the centre of the shell.
question_text: A thin spherical conducting shell of radius {tex}R{/tex} has a charge {tex}q{/tex} . A point charge Q is placed at the centre of the shell. Find (i) The charge density on the outer surface of the shell and (ii) the potential at a distance of (R/2) from the centre of the shell.
explanation: When a point charge Q is placed at the centre of the shell, a charge (-Q) is induced at its inner surface, consequently a net charge on outer surface of the shell {tex}={q}+{Q}{/tex} Charge density on outer surface of the shell {tex}\sigma =\frac{\text { charge }}{\text { Area }}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{{q}+{Q}}{4 \pi {R}^{2}}{/tex} Potential due to shell at a distance of ( {tex}{R} / 2{/tex} ) from the centre of the shell {tex}{V}_{1}=\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}} \frac{{q}}{{R}}{/tex} Potential due to charge Q at a distance of {tex}({R} / 2){/tex} from the centre of the shell {tex}{V}_{2}=\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}} \cdot \frac{{Q}}{{R} / 2}{/tex} Net potential at a distance of {tex}({R} / 2){/tex} from the centre of the shell {tex}{V}={V}_{2}+{V}_{2}{/tex} {tex}{~V}=\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0} {R}}({q}+2 {Q}){/tex}
solution: When a point charge Q is placed at the centre of the shell, a charge (-Q) is induced at its inner surface, consequently a net charge on outer surface of the shell {tex}={q}+{Q}{/tex} Charge density on outer surface of the shell {tex}\sigma =\frac{\text { charge }}{\text { Area }}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{{q}+{Q}}{4 \pi {R}^{2}}{/tex} Potential due to shell at a distance of ( {tex}{R} / 2{/tex} ) from the centre of the shell {tex}{V}_{1}=\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}} \frac{{q}}{{R}}{/tex} Potential due to charge Q at a distance of {tex}({R} / 2){/tex} from the centre of the shell {tex}{V}_{2}=\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}} \cdot \frac{{Q}}{{R} / 2}{/tex} Net potential at a distance of {tex}({R} / 2){/tex} from the centre of the shell {tex}{V}={V}_{2}+{V}_{2}{/tex} {tex}{~V}=\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0} {R}}({q}+2 {Q}){/tex}
solution_html:
1. When a point charge Q is placed at the centre of the shell, a charge (-Q) is induced at its inner surface, consequently a net charge on outer surface of the shell {tex}={q}+{Q}{/tex}
    Charge density on outer surface of the shell
    {tex}\sigma =\frac{\text { charge }}{\text { Area }}{/tex}
    {tex}=\frac{{q}+{Q}}{4 \pi {R}^{2}}{/tex}
  2. Potential due to shell at a distance of ({tex}{R} / 2{/tex}) from the centre of the shell
    {tex}{V}_{1}=\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}} \frac{{q}}{{R}}{/tex}
    Potential due to charge Q at a distance of {tex}({R} / 2){/tex} from the centre of the shell
    {tex}{V}_{2}=\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}} \cdot \frac{{Q}}{{R} / 2}{/tex}
    Net potential at a distance of {tex}({R} / 2){/tex} from the centre of the shell
    {tex}{V}={V}_{2}+{V}_{2}{/tex}
    {tex}{~V}=\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0} {R}}({q}+2 {Q}){/tex}

subject: Physics
question_html:
Dielectrics play an important role in design of capacitors. The molecules of a dielectric may be polar or non-polar. When a dielectric slab is placed in an external electric field, opposite charges appear on the two surfaces of the slab perpendicular to electric field. Due to this an electric field is established inside the dielectric.
The capacitance of a capacitor is determined by the dielectric constant of the material that fills the space between the plates. Consequently, the energy storage capacity of a capacitor is also affected. Like resistors, capacitors can also be arranged in series and/or parallel.

  1. Which of the following is a polar molecule?
    1. {tex}{O}_{2}{/tex}
    2. {tex}{H}_{2}{/tex}
    3. {tex}{N}_{2}{/tex}
    4. HCl
  2. Which of the following statements about dielectrics is correct?
    1. A polar dielectric has a net dipole moment in absence of an external electric field which gets modified due to the induced dipoles.
    2. The net dipole moments of induced dipoles is along the direction of the applied electric field.
    3. Dielectrics contain free charges.
    4. The electric field produced due to induced surface charges inside a dielectric is along the external electric field.
  3. When a dielectric slab is inserted between the plates of an isolated charged capacitor, the energy stored in it:
    1. increases and the electric field inside it also increases.
    2. decreases and the electric field also decreases.
    3. decreases and the electric field increases.
    4. increases and the electric field decreases.
    1. An air-filled capacitor with plate area A and plate separation d has capacitance {tex}C_{0}{/tex}. A slab of dielectric constant {tex}K{/tex}, area {tex}A{/tex} and thickness {tex}\left(\frac{{d}}{5}\right){/tex} is inserted between the plates. The capacitance of the capacitor will become
      1. {tex}\left[\frac{4 {~K}}{5 {~K}+1}\right] {C}_{0}{/tex}
      2. {tex}\left[\frac{{K}+5}{4}\right] {C}_{0}{/tex}
      3. {tex}\left[\frac{5 {~K}}{4 {~K}+1}\right] {C}_{0}{/tex}
      4. {tex}\left[\frac{{K}+4}{5 {~K}}\right] {C}_{0}{/tex}

        OR

    2. Two capacitors of capacitances {tex}2 {C}_{0}{/tex} and {tex}6 {C}_{0}{/tex} are first connected in series and then in parallel across the same battery. The ratio of energies stored in series combination to that in parallel is
      1. {tex}\frac{1}{4}{/tex}
      2. {tex}\frac{1}{6}{/tex}
      3. {tex}\frac{2}{15}{/tex}
      4. {tex}\frac{3}{16}{/tex}
question_text: Dielectrics play an important role in design of capacitors. The molecules of a dielectric may be polar or non-polar. When a dielectric slab is placed in an external electric field, opposite charges appear on the two surfaces of the slab perpendicular to electric field. Due to this an electric field is established inside the dielectric. The capacitance of a capacitor is determined by the dielectric constant of the material that fills the space between the plates. Consequently, the energy storage capacity of a capacitor is also affected. Like resistors, capacitors can also be arranged in series and/or parallel. Which of the following is a polar molecule? {tex}{O}_{2}{/tex} {tex}{H}_{2}{/tex} {tex}{N}_{2}{/tex} HCl Which of the following statements about dielectrics is correct? A polar dielectric has a net dipole moment in absence of an external electric field which gets modified due to the induced dipoles. The net dipole moments of induced dipoles is along the direction of the applied electric field. Dielectrics contain free charges. The electric field produced due to induced surface charges inside a dielectric is along the external electric field. When a dielectric slab is inserted between the plates of an isolated charged capacitor, the energy stored in it: increases and the electric field inside it also increases. decreases and the electric field also decreases. decreases and the electric field increases. increases and the electric field decreases. An air-filled capacitor with plate area A and plate separation d has capacitance {tex}C_{0}{/tex} . A slab of dielectric constant {tex}K{/tex} , area {tex}A{/tex} and thickness {tex}\left(\frac{{d}}{5}\right){/tex} is inserted between the plates. The capacitance of the capacitor will become {tex}\left[\frac{4 {~K}}{5 {~K}+1}\right] {C}_{0}{/tex} {tex}\left[\frac{{K}+5}{4}\right] {C}_{0}{/tex} {tex}\left[\frac{5 {~K}}{4 {~K}+1}\right] {C}_{0}{/tex} {tex}\left[\frac{{K}+4}{5 {~K}}\right] {C}_{0}{/tex} OR Two capacitors of capacitances {tex}2 {C}_{0}{/tex} and {tex}6 {C}_{0}{/tex} are first connected in series and then in parallel across the same battery. The ratio of energies stored in series combination to that in parallel is {tex}\frac{1}{4}{/tex} {tex}\frac{1}{6}{/tex} {tex}\frac{2}{15}{/tex} {tex}\frac{3}{16}{/tex}
explanation: (D) HCl (B) The net dipole moment of induced dipoles is along the direction of the applied electric field. (B) decreases and the electric field also decreases. (C) {tex}\left[\frac{5 {~K}}{4 {~K}+1}\right] {C}_{0}{/tex} . OR (D) {tex}\frac{3}{16}{/tex}
solution: (D) HCl (B) The net dipole moment of induced dipoles is along the direction of the applied electric field. (B) decreases and the electric field also decreases. (C) {tex}\left[\frac{5 {~K}}{4 {~K}+1}\right] {C}_{0}{/tex} . OR (D) {tex}\frac{3}{16}{/tex}
solution_html:
1. (D) HCl
  2. (B) The net dipole moment of induced dipoles is along the direction of the applied electric field.
  3. (B) decreases and the electric field also decreases.
    1. (C) {tex}\left[\frac{5 {~K}}{4 {~K}+1}\right] {C}_{0}{/tex}.
      OR
    2. (D) {tex}\frac{3}{16}{/tex}

subject: Physics
question_html: Two point charges of {tex}10 \mu {C}{/tex} and {tex}20 \mu {C}{/tex} are located at points {tex}(-4 {~cm}, 0,0){/tex} and {tex}(5 {~cm}, 0,0){/tex} respectively, in a region with electric field {tex}{E}=\frac{{A}}{{r}^{2}}{/tex}, where {tex}{A}=2 \times 10^{6} {NC}^{-1} {~m}^{2}{/tex} and {tex}\vec{{r}}{/tex} is the position vector of the point under consideration. Calculate the electrostatic potential energy of the system.
question_text: Two point charges of {tex}10 \mu {C}{/tex} and {tex}20 \mu {C}{/tex} are located at points {tex}(-4 {~cm}, 0,0){/tex} and {tex}(5 {~cm}, 0,0){/tex} respectively, in a region with electric field {tex}{E}=\frac{{A}}{{r}^{2}}{/tex} , where {tex}{A}=2 \times 10^{6} {NC}^{-1} {~m}^{2}{/tex} and {tex}\vec{{r}}{/tex} is the position vector of the point under consideration. Calculate the electrostatic potential energy of the system.
solution_html:
Electrostatic potential energy of the system:
{tex}{U} = \frac{{kq}_{1} {q}_{2}}{{r}_{12}}+{q}_{1} {~V}_{1}+{q}_{2} {~V}_{2}{/tex}
{tex}\frac{{kq}_{1} {q}_{2}}{{r}_{12}} =\frac{9 \times 10^{9} \times 10 \times 10^{-6} \times 20 \times 10^{-6}}{9 \times 10^{-2}}=20 {~J}{/tex}
{tex}{q}_{1} {~V}_{1} ={q}_{1} \frac{{~A}}{{r}_{1}}=\frac{10 \times 10^{-6} \times 2 \times 10^{6}}{4 \times 10^{-2}}=500 {~J}{/tex}
{tex}{q}_{2} {~V}_{2} ={q}_{2} \frac{{~A}}{{r}_{2}}=\frac{20 \times 10^{-6} \times 2 \times 10^{6}}{5 \times 10^{-2}}=800 {~J}{/tex}
{tex}{U} =(20+500+800) {J}{/tex}
{tex}{U} =1320 {~J}{/tex}

subject: Physics
question_html: Two conducting spherical shells {tex}A{/tex} and {tex}B{/tex} of radii {tex}R{/tex} and {tex}2 R{/tex} are kept far apart and charged to the same charge density {tex}\sigma{/tex}. They are connected by a wire. Obtain an expression for final potential of shell A.
question_text: Two conducting spherical shells {tex}A{/tex} and {tex}B{/tex} of radii {tex}R{/tex} and {tex}2 R{/tex} are kept far apart and charged to the same charge density {tex}\sigma{/tex} . They are connected by a wire. Obtain an expression for final potential of shell A.
explanation: Charge on spherical Shell A {tex}{q}_{{A}}=4 \pi {R}^{2} \sigma{/tex} Charge on speherical shell {tex}B{/tex} {tex}q_{B}=4 \pi(2 R)^{2} \sigma=16 \pi R^{2} \sigma{/tex} After connecting by a wire, their potentials will become equal after sharing of charge. {tex}\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}} \frac{{q}_{{A}}^{\prime}}{{R}} =\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}} \frac{{q}_{{B}}^{\prime}}{2 {R}}{/tex} {tex}{q}_{{B}}^{\prime} =2 {q}_{{A}}^{\prime}{/tex} From conservation of charge {tex}{q}_{{A}}+{q}_{{B}}={q}_{{A}}^{\prime}+{q}_{{B}}^{\prime}{/tex} {tex}4 \pi {R}^{2} \sigma+16 \pi {R}^{2} \sigma=3 {q}_{{A}}^{\prime}{/tex} {tex}{q}_{{A}}^{\prime}=\frac{20 \pi {R}^{2} \sigma}{3}{/tex} Final potential of Shell A {tex}{V}_{{A}} =\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}} \frac{{q}_{{A}}^{\prime}}{{R}}{/tex} {tex}{~V}_{{A}} =\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}} \frac{20 \pi {R}^{2} \sigma}{3 {R}}{/tex} {tex}{~V}_{{A}} =\frac{5 \sigma {R}}{3 \varepsilon_{0}}{/tex}
solution: Charge on spherical Shell A {tex}{q}_{{A}}=4 \pi {R}^{2} \sigma{/tex} Charge on speherical shell {tex}B{/tex} {tex}q_{B}=4 \pi(2 R)^{2} \sigma=16 \pi R^{2} \sigma{/tex} After connecting by a wire, their potentials will become equal after sharing of charge. {tex}\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}} \frac{{q}_{{A}}^{\prime}}{{R}} =\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}} \frac{{q}_{{B}}^{\prime}}{2 {R}}{/tex} {tex}{q}_{{B}}^{\prime} =2 {q}_{{A}}^{\prime}{/tex} From conservation of charge {tex}{q}_{{A}}+{q}_{{B}}={q}_{{A}}^{\prime}+{q}_{{B}}^{\prime}{/tex} {tex}4 \pi {R}^{2} \sigma+16 \pi {R}^{2} \sigma=3 {q}_{{A}}^{\prime}{/tex} {tex}{q}_{{A}}^{\prime}=\frac{20 \pi {R}^{2} \sigma}{3}{/tex} Final potential of Shell A {tex}{V}_{{A}} =\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}} \frac{{q}_{{A}}^{\prime}}{{R}}{/tex} {tex}{~V}_{{A}} =\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}} \frac{20 \pi {R}^{2} \sigma}{3 {R}}{/tex} {tex}{~V}_{{A}} =\frac{5 \sigma {R}}{3 \varepsilon_{0}}{/tex}
solution_html:
Charge on spherical Shell A
{tex}{q}_{{A}}=4 \pi {R}^{2} \sigma{/tex}
Charge on speherical shell {tex}B{/tex}
{tex}q_{B}=4 \pi(2 R)^{2} \sigma=16 \pi R^{2} \sigma{/tex}
After connecting by a wire, their potentials will become equal after sharing of charge.
{tex}\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}} \frac{{q}_{{A}}^{\prime}}{{R}} =\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}} \frac{{q}_{{B}}^{\prime}}{2 {R}}{/tex}
{tex}{q}_{{B}}^{\prime} =2 {q}_{{A}}^{\prime}{/tex}
From conservation of charge
{tex}{q}_{{A}}+{q}_{{B}}={q}_{{A}}^{\prime}+{q}_{{B}}^{\prime}{/tex}
{tex}4 \pi {R}^{2} \sigma+16 \pi {R}^{2} \sigma=3 {q}_{{A}}^{\prime}{/tex}
{tex}{q}_{{A}}^{\prime}=\frac{20 \pi {R}^{2} \sigma}{3}{/tex}
Final potential of Shell A
{tex}{V}_{{A}} =\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}} \frac{{q}_{{A}}^{\prime}}{{R}}{/tex}
{tex}{~V}_{{A}} =\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}} \frac{20 \pi {R}^{2} \sigma}{3 {R}}{/tex}
{tex}{~V}_{{A}} =\frac{5 \sigma {R}}{3 \varepsilon_{0}}{/tex}

subject: Physics
question_html:
A capacitor is a system of two conductors separated by an insulator. The two conductors have equal and opposite charges with a potential difference between them. The capacitance of a capacitor depends on the geometrical configuration (shape, size and separation) of the system and also on the nature of the insulator separating the two conductors. They are used to store charges. Like resistors, capacitors can be arranged in series or parallel or a combination of both to obtain desired value of capacitance.


Find the equivalent capacitance between points A and B in the given diagram.
question_text: A capacitor is a system of two conductors separated by an insulator. The two conductors have equal and opposite charges with a potential difference between them. The capacitance of a capacitor depends on the geometrical configuration (shape, size and separation) of the system and also on the nature of the insulator separating the two conductors. They are used to store charges. Like resistors, capacitors can be arranged in series or parallel or a combination of both to obtain desired value of capacitance. Find the equivalent capacitance between points A and B in the given diagram.
explanation: C net = C + C = 2C
solution: C net = C + C = 2C
solution_html:
Cnet = C + C
= 2C

subject: Physics
question_html:
A capacitor is a system of two conductors separated by an insulator. The two conductors have equal and opposite charges with a potential difference between them. The capacitance of a capacitor depends on the geometrical configuration (shape, size and separation) of the system and also on the nature of the insulator separating the two conductors. They are used to store charges. Like resistors, capacitors can be arranged in series or parallel or a combination of both to obtain desired value of capacitance.


A capacitor A of capacitance C, having charge Q is connected across another uncharged capacitor B of capacitance 2C. Find an expression for (a) the potential difference across the combination and (b) the charge lost by capacitor A.
question_text: A capacitor is a system of two conductors separated by an insulator. The two conductors have equal and opposite charges with a potential difference between them. The capacitance of a capacitor depends on the geometrical configuration (shape, size and separation) of the system and also on the nature of the insulator separating the two conductors. They are used to store charges. Like resistors, capacitors can be arranged in series or parallel or a combination of both to obtain desired value of capacitance. A capacitor A of capacitance C, having charge Q is connected across another uncharged capacitor B of capacitance 2C. Find an expression for (a) the potential difference across the combination and (b) the charge lost by capacitor A.
explanation: {tex}V^{\prime}=\frac{Q_{\text {Total }}}{C_{\text {eqi }}}=\frac{Q}{3 C}{/tex} {tex}V^{\prime}=\frac{Q}{3 C}=\frac{V}{3}{/tex} {tex}Q_A^{\prime}=C \times \frac{V}{3}=\frac{Q}{3}{/tex} Q A = CV = Q Charge lost by capacitor A is {tex}\Delta Q=Q-\frac{Q}{3}=\frac{2 Q}{3}{/tex}
solution: {tex}V^{\prime}=\frac{Q_{\text {Total }}}{C_{\text {eqi }}}=\frac{Q}{3 C}{/tex} {tex}V^{\prime}=\frac{Q}{3 C}=\frac{V}{3}{/tex} {tex}Q_A^{\prime}=C \times \frac{V}{3}=\frac{Q}{3}{/tex} Q A = CV = Q Charge lost by capacitor A is {tex}\Delta Q=Q-\frac{Q}{3}=\frac{2 Q}{3}{/tex}
solution_html:
1. {tex}V^{\prime}=\frac{Q_{\text {Total }}}{C_{\text {eqi }}}=\frac{Q}{3 C}{/tex}
    {tex}V^{\prime}=\frac{Q}{3 C}=\frac{V}{3}{/tex}
  2. {tex}Q_A^{\prime}=C \times \frac{V}{3}=\frac{Q}{3}{/tex}
    QA = CV = Q
    Charge lost by capacitor A is
    {tex}\Delta Q=Q-\frac{Q}{3}=\frac{2 Q}{3}{/tex}

subject: Physics
question_html:
A capacitor is a system of two conductors separated by an insulator. The two conductors have equal and opposite charges with a potential difference between them. The capacitance of a capacitor depends on the geometrical configuration (shape, size and separation) of the system and also on the nature of the insulator separating the two conductors. They are used to store charges. Like resistors, capacitors can be arranged in series or parallel or a combination of both to obtain desired value of capacitance.


Two slabs of dielectric constants 2K and K fill the space between the plates of a parallel plate capacitor of plate area A and plate separation d as shown in figure. Find an expression for capacitance of the system.
question_text: A capacitor is a system of two conductors separated by an insulator. The two conductors have equal and opposite charges with a potential difference between them. The capacitance of a capacitor depends on the geometrical configuration (shape, size and separation) of the system and also on the nature of the insulator separating the two conductors. They are used to store charges. Like resistors, capacitors can be arranged in series or parallel or a combination of both to obtain desired value of capacitance. Two slabs of dielectric constants 2K and K fill the space between the plates of a parallel plate capacitor of plate area A and plate separation d as shown in figure. Find an expression for capacitance of the system.
explanation: Capacitance of left portion, C 1 = {tex}\frac{6 K \in_0 A}{d}{/tex} Capacitance of right portion, C 2 = {tex}\frac{3 K \in_0 A}{2 d}{/tex} As the capacitors are in series {tex}\frac{1}{C_{e q i}}=\frac{1}{C_1}+\frac{1}{C_2}{/tex} {tex}\frac{1}{C_{e q i}}=\frac{d}{6 K \in_O A}+\frac{2 d}{3 K \in_o A}=\frac{5 d}{6 K A \in_o}{/tex} {tex}C_{e q i}=\frac{6 K A \in_o}{5 d}{/tex}
solution: Capacitance of left portion, C 1 = {tex}\frac{6 K \in_0 A}{d}{/tex} Capacitance of right portion, C 2 = {tex}\frac{3 K \in_0 A}{2 d}{/tex} As the capacitors are in series {tex}\frac{1}{C_{e q i}}=\frac{1}{C_1}+\frac{1}{C_2}{/tex} {tex}\frac{1}{C_{e q i}}=\frac{d}{6 K \in_O A}+\frac{2 d}{3 K \in_o A}=\frac{5 d}{6 K A \in_o}{/tex} {tex}C_{e q i}=\frac{6 K A \in_o}{5 d}{/tex}
solution_html:
Capacitance of left portion, C1 = {tex}\frac{6 K \in_0 A}{d}{/tex}
Capacitance of right portion, C2 = {tex}\frac{3 K \in_0 A}{2 d}{/tex}
As the capacitors are in series
{tex}\frac{1}{C_{e q i}}=\frac{1}{C_1}+\frac{1}{C_2}{/tex}
{tex}\frac{1}{C_{e q i}}=\frac{d}{6 K \in_O A}+\frac{2 d}{3 K \in_o A}=\frac{5 d}{6 K A \in_o}{/tex}
{tex}C_{e q i}=\frac{6 K A \in_o}{5 d}{/tex}

question_html:
A 100 {tex}\mu \mathrm{F}{/tex} capacitor is charged by a 12 V battery.

  1. How much electrostatic energy is stored by the capacitor?
  2. The capacitor is disconnected from the battery and connected in parallel to another uncharged 100 {tex}\mu \mathrm{F}{/tex} capacitor. What is the electrostatic energy stored by the system?
question_text: A 100 {tex}\mu \mathrm{F}{/tex} capacitor is charged by a 12 V battery. How much electrostatic energy is stored by the capacitor? The capacitor is disconnected from the battery and connected in parallel to another uncharged 100 {tex}\mu \mathrm{F}{/tex} capacitor. What is the electrostatic energy stored by the system?
explanation: C = 100 {tex}\mu F{/tex} = 100 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -6 F, V = 12V Electrostatic energy U = {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} CV 2 = {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} {tex}\times{/tex} 100 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -6 {tex}\times{/tex} (12) 2 = {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -4 {tex}\times{/tex} 144 = 72 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -4 {tex}\times{/tex} 144 = 72 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -4 J = 7.2 mJ Equivalent capacitance C eq = C 1 + C 2 = 200 {tex}\mu F{/tex} Q = CV = 100 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -6 {tex}\times{/tex} 12 = 12 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -4 C {tex}U=\frac{Q^2}{2 C_{e q}}=\frac{\left(12 \times 10^{-4}\right)^2}{2 \times 200 \times 10^{-6}}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{144}{4} \times{/tex} 10 -4 = 36 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -4 J = 3.6 mJ
solution: C = 100 {tex}\mu F{/tex} = 100 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -6 F, V = 12V Electrostatic energy U = {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} CV 2 = {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} {tex}\times{/tex} 100 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -6 {tex}\times{/tex} (12) 2 = {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -4 {tex}\times{/tex} 144 = 72 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -4 {tex}\times{/tex} 144 = 72 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -4 J = 7.2 mJ Equivalent capacitance C eq = C 1 + C 2 = 200 {tex}\mu F{/tex} Q = CV = 100 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -6 {tex}\times{/tex} 12 = 12 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -4 C {tex}U=\frac{Q^2}{2 C_{e q}}=\frac{\left(12 \times 10^{-4}\right)^2}{2 \times 200 \times 10^{-6}}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{144}{4} \times{/tex} 10 -4 = 36 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -4 J = 3.6 mJ
solution_html:
C = 100 {tex}\mu F{/tex} = 100 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-6 F, V = 12V

  1. Electrostatic energy U = {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex}CV2
    = {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} {tex}\times{/tex} 100 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-6 {tex}\times{/tex} (12)2
    = {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} {tex}\times{/tex} 10-4 {tex}\times{/tex} 144
    = 72 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-4 {tex}\times{/tex} 144
    = 72 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-4 J = 7.2 mJ
  2. Equivalent capacitance Ceq = C1 + C2
    = 200 {tex}\mu F{/tex}
    Q = CV
    = 100 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-6 {tex}\times{/tex} 12
    = 12 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-4 C
    {tex}U=\frac{Q^2}{2 C_{e q}}=\frac{\left(12 \times 10^{-4}\right)^2}{2 \times 200 \times 10^{-6}}{/tex}
    {tex}=\frac{144}{4} \times{/tex} 10-4
    = 36 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-4 J = 3.6 mJ

subject: Physics
question_html: Three point charges 1 {tex}\mu{/tex}C, -1 {tex}\mu{/tex}C and 2 {tex}\mu{/tex}C are kept at the vertices A, B and C respectively of an equilateral triangle of side 1 m. A1, B1 and C1 are the midpoints of the sides AB, BC and CA respectively. Calculate the net amount of work done in displacing the charge from A to A1, from B to B1 and from C to C1.
question_text: Three point charges 1 {tex}\mu{/tex} C, -1 {tex}\mu{/tex} C and 2 {tex}\mu{/tex} C are kept at the vertices A, B and C respectively of an equilateral triangle of side 1 m. A 1 , B 1 and C 1 are the midpoints of the sides AB, BC and CA respectively. Calculate the net amount of work done in displacing the charge from A to A 1 , from B to B 1 and from C to C 1 .
explanation: Initial electrostatic potential energy of the system U i = {tex}\frac{k}{r}{/tex} [1 {tex}\times{/tex} (-1) + (-1) {tex}\times{/tex} 2 + (1) {tex}\times{/tex} (2)] {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -12 {tex}=\frac{9 \times 10^9}{1}{/tex} [-1 - 2 + 2] {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -12 = -9 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -3 J Now A 1 B 1 = B 1 C 1 = A 1 C 1 = {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} m Final electrostatic potential energy of the system {tex}U_f=\frac{-9 \times 10^{-9}}{1 / 2}{/tex} = -18 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -3 J Amount of work done W = U f - U i W = 18 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -3 + 9 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -3 = -9 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -3 J
solution: Initial electrostatic potential energy of the system U i = {tex}\frac{k}{r}{/tex} [1 {tex}\times{/tex} (-1) + (-1) {tex}\times{/tex} 2 + (1) {tex}\times{/tex} (2)] {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -12 {tex}=\frac{9 \times 10^9}{1}{/tex} [-1 - 2 + 2] {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -12 = -9 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -3 J Now A 1 B 1 = B 1 C 1 = A 1 C 1 = {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} m Final electrostatic potential energy of the system {tex}U_f=\frac{-9 \times 10^{-9}}{1 / 2}{/tex} = -18 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -3 J Amount of work done W = U f - U i W = 18 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -3 + 9 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -3 = -9 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -3 J
solution_html:
Initial electrostatic potential energy of the system
Ui = {tex}\frac{k}{r}{/tex} [1 {tex}\times{/tex} (-1) + (-1) {tex}\times{/tex} 2 + (1) {tex}\times{/tex} (2)] {tex}\times{/tex} 10-12
{tex}=\frac{9 \times 10^9}{1}{/tex} [-1 - 2 + 2] {tex}\times{/tex} 10-12
= -9 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-3 J
Now A1B1 = B1C1 = A1C1 = {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex}m
Final electrostatic potential energy of the system
{tex}U_f=\frac{-9 \times 10^{-9}}{1 / 2}{/tex} = -18 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-3 J
Amount of work done W = Uf - Ui
W = 18 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-3 + 9 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-3 = -9 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-3 J

subject: Physics
question_html:
A parallel plate capacitor is an arrangement of two identical metal plates kept parallel, a small distance apart. The capacitance of a capacitor depends on the size and separation of the two plates and also on the dielectric constant of the medium between the plates. Like resistors, capacitors can also be arranged in series or parallel or a combination of both. By virtue of electric field between the plates, charged capacitors store energy.


The capacitance of a parallel plate capacitor increases from 10 {tex}\mu{/tex}F to 80 {tex}\mu{/tex}F on introducing a dielectric medium between the plates. Find the dielectric constant of the medium.
question_text: A parallel plate capacitor is an arrangement of two identical metal plates kept parallel, a small distance apart. The capacitance of a capacitor depends on the size and separation of the two plates and also on the dielectric constant of the medium between the plates. Like resistors, capacitors can also be arranged in series or parallel or a combination of both. By virtue of electric field between the plates, charged capacitors store energy. The capacitance of a parallel plate capacitor increases from 10 {tex}\mu{/tex} F to 80 {tex}\mu{/tex} F on introducing a dielectric medium between the plates. Find the dielectric constant of the medium.
explanation: {tex}K=\frac{C}{C_0}{/tex} {tex}K=\frac{80 \mu F}{10 \mu F}=8{/tex}
solution: {tex}K=\frac{C}{C_0}{/tex} {tex}K=\frac{80 \mu F}{10 \mu F}=8{/tex}
solution_html:
{tex}K=\frac{C}{C_0}{/tex}
{tex}K=\frac{80 \mu F}{10 \mu F}=8{/tex}

subject: Physics
question_html:
A parallel plate capacitor is an arrangement of two identical metal plates kept parallel, a small distance apart. The capacitance of a capacitor depends on the size and separation of the two plates and also on the dielectric constant of the medium between the plates. Like resistors, capacitors can also be arranged in series or parallel or a combination of both. By virtue of electric field between the plates, charged capacitors store energy.


n capacitors, each of capacitance C, are connected in series. Find the equivalent capacitance of the combination.
question_text: A parallel plate capacitor is an arrangement of two identical metal plates kept parallel, a small distance apart. The capacitance of a capacitor depends on the size and separation of the two plates and also on the dielectric constant of the medium between the plates. Like resistors, capacitors can also be arranged in series or parallel or a combination of both. By virtue of electric field between the plates, charged capacitors store energy. n capacitors, each of capacitance C, are connected in series. Find the equivalent capacitance of the combination.
explanation: {tex}\frac{1}{C_S}=\frac{1}{C_1}+\frac{1}{C_2}+\ldots \ldots \ldots+\frac{1}{C_n}{/tex} {tex}\frac{1}{C_S}=\frac{n}{C}{/tex} {tex}C_S=\frac{C}{n}{/tex}
solution: {tex}\frac{1}{C_S}=\frac{1}{C_1}+\frac{1}{C_2}+\ldots \ldots \ldots+\frac{1}{C_n}{/tex} {tex}\frac{1}{C_S}=\frac{n}{C}{/tex} {tex}C_S=\frac{C}{n}{/tex}
solution_html:
{tex}\frac{1}{C_S}=\frac{1}{C_1}+\frac{1}{C_2}+\ldots \ldots \ldots+\frac{1}{C_n}{/tex}
{tex}\frac{1}{C_S}=\frac{n}{C}{/tex}
{tex}C_S=\frac{C}{n}{/tex}

subject: Physics
question_html:
A parallel plate capacitor is an arrangement of two identical metal plates kept parallel, a small distance apart. The capacitance of a capacitor depends on the size and separation of the two plates and also on the dielectric constant of the medium between the plates. Like resistors, capacitors can also be arranged in series or parallel or a combination of both. By virtue of electric field between the plates, charged capacitors store energy.


A capacitor is charged to a potential (V) by connecting it to a battery. After some time, the battery is disconnected and a dielectric is introduced between the plates. How will the potential difference between the plates, and the energy stored in it be affected? Justify your answer.
question_text: A parallel plate capacitor is an arrangement of two identical metal plates kept parallel, a small distance apart. The capacitance of a capacitor depends on the size and separation of the two plates and also on the dielectric constant of the medium between the plates. Like resistors, capacitors can also be arranged in series or parallel or a combination of both. By virtue of electric field between the plates, charged capacitors store energy. A capacitor is charged to a potential (V) by connecting it to a battery. After some time, the battery is disconnected and a dielectric is introduced between the plates. How will the potential difference between the plates, and the energy stored in it be affected? Justify your answer.
explanation: Charge is constant Q 1 = Q 2 C 2 = KC 1 C 1 V 1 = KC 1 V 2 {tex}V_2=\frac{V_1}{K}{/tex} Potential diff decreases by a factor {tex}(\frac{1}{K}){/tex} {tex}U_2=\frac{1}{2} \frac{Q^2}{C_2}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{1}{2} \frac{Q^2}{k C_1}=\frac{1}{k}\left(\frac{Q^2}{2 C_2}\right){/tex} {tex}U_2=\frac{U_1}{K}{/tex} Energy reduces by a factor of {tex}\frac{1}{K}{/tex} .
solution: Charge is constant Q 1 = Q 2 C 2 = KC 1 C 1 V 1 = KC 1 V 2 {tex}V_2=\frac{V_1}{K}{/tex} Potential diff decreases by a factor {tex}(\frac{1}{K}){/tex} {tex}U_2=\frac{1}{2} \frac{Q^2}{C_2}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{1}{2} \frac{Q^2}{k C_1}=\frac{1}{k}\left(\frac{Q^2}{2 C_2}\right){/tex} {tex}U_2=\frac{U_1}{K}{/tex} Energy reduces by a factor of {tex}\frac{1}{K}{/tex} .
solution_html:
Charge is constant
Q1 = Q2
C2 = KC1
C1V1 = KC1V2
{tex}V_2=\frac{V_1}{K}{/tex} Potential diff decreases by a factor {tex}(\frac{1}{K}){/tex}
{tex}U_2=\frac{1}{2} \frac{Q^2}{C_2}{/tex}
{tex}=\frac{1}{2} \frac{Q^2}{k C_1}=\frac{1}{k}\left(\frac{Q^2}{2 C_2}\right){/tex}
{tex}U_2=\frac{U_1}{K}{/tex}
Energy reduces by a factor of {tex}\frac{1}{K}{/tex}.

subject: Physics
question_html:
A parallel plate capacitor (A) of capacitance C is charged by a battery to voltage V. The battery is disconnected and an uncharged capacitor (B) of capacitance 2C is connected across A. Find the ratio of

  1. final charges on A and B.
  2. total electrostatic energy stored in A and B finally and that stored in A initially.
question_text: A parallel plate capacitor (A) of capacitance C is charged by a battery to voltage V. The battery is disconnected and an uncharged capacitor (B) of capacitance 2C is connected across A. Find the ratio of final charges on A and B. total electrostatic energy stored in A and B finally and that stored in A initially.
explanation: Initially Q = CV Finally q A = C A V 1 & q B = C B V 1 {tex}\frac{q_A}{q_B}=\frac{C_A}{C_B}=\frac{1}{2}{/tex} q A + q b = Q {tex}\therefore q_A=\frac{Q}{3} \& \quad q_B=\frac{2 Q}{3}{/tex} {tex}\frac{U_f}{U_i}=\frac{U_A+U_B}{U_{A i}}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{\frac{q_A^2}{2 C_A}+\frac{q_B^2}{2 C_B}}{\frac{Q^2}{2 C_A}}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{1}{3}{/tex}
solution: Initially Q = CV Finally q A = C A V 1 & q B = C B V 1 {tex}\frac{q_A}{q_B}=\frac{C_A}{C_B}=\frac{1}{2}{/tex} q A + q b = Q {tex}\therefore q_A=\frac{Q}{3} \& \quad q_B=\frac{2 Q}{3}{/tex} {tex}\frac{U_f}{U_i}=\frac{U_A+U_B}{U_{A i}}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{\frac{q_A^2}{2 C_A}+\frac{q_B^2}{2 C_B}}{\frac{Q^2}{2 C_A}}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{1}{3}{/tex}
solution_html:
1. Initially Q = CV
    Finally qA = CAV1 & qB = CBV1
    {tex}\frac{q_A}{q_B}=\frac{C_A}{C_B}=\frac{1}{2}{/tex}
  2. qA + qb = Q
    {tex}\therefore q_A=\frac{Q}{3} \& \quad q_B=\frac{2 Q}{3}{/tex}
    {tex}\frac{U_f}{U_i}=\frac{U_A+U_B}{U_{A i}}{/tex}
    {tex}=\frac{\frac{q_A^2}{2 C_A}+\frac{q_B^2}{2 C_B}}{\frac{Q^2}{2 C_A}}{/tex}
    {tex}=\frac{1}{3}{/tex}

question_html: Three point charges Q1 (-15 {tex}\mu{/tex}C), Q2 (10 {tex}\mu{/tex}C) and Q3 (16 {tex}\mu{/tex}C) are located at (0 cm, 0 cm), (0 cm, 3 cm) and (4 cm, 3 cm) respectively. Calculate the electrostatic potential energy of this system of charges.
question_text: Three point charges Q 1 (-15 {tex}\mu{/tex} C), Q 2 (10 {tex}\mu{/tex} C) and Q 3 (16 {tex}\mu{/tex} C) are located at (0 cm, 0 cm), (0 cm, 3 cm) and (4 cm, 3 cm) respectively. Calculate the electrostatic potential energy of this system of charges.
explanation: {tex}\mathrm{U}_{\mathrm{Q} 1 \mathrm{Q} 2}=\frac{1}{4 \pi \epsilon_o} \frac{Q_1 Q_2}{r_{12}}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{9 \times 10^9 \times\left(-15 \times 10^{-6}\right) \times\left(10 \times 10^{-6}\right)}{3 \times 10^{-2}}{/tex} = -45 J {tex}\mathrm{U}_{\mathrm{Q} 2 \mathrm{Q} 3}=\frac{1}{4 \pi \epsilon_o} \frac{Q_2 Q_3}{r_{23}}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{9 \times 10^9 \times\left(10 \times 10^{-6}\right) \times\left(16 \times 10^{-6}\right)}{4 \times 10^{-2}}{/tex} = 36 J {tex}\mathrm{U}_{\mathrm{Q} 1 \mathrm{Q} 3}=\frac{1}{4 \pi \epsilon_o} \frac{Q_1 Q_3}{r_{13}}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{9 \times 10^9 \times\left(-15 \times 10^{-6}\right) \times\left(16 \times 10^{-6}\right)}{5 \times 10^{-2}}{/tex} = -43.2 J {tex}\mathrm{U}_{\mathrm{net}}=\mathrm{U}_{\mathrm{Q} 1 \mathrm{Q} 2}+\mathrm{U}_{\mathrm{Q} 2 \mathrm{Q} 3}+\mathrm{U}_{\mathrm{Q} 1 \mathrm{Q} 3}{/tex} = -45 + 36 - 43.2 = 52.2 J
solution: {tex}\mathrm{U}_{\mathrm{Q} 1 \mathrm{Q} 2}=\frac{1}{4 \pi \epsilon_o} \frac{Q_1 Q_2}{r_{12}}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{9 \times 10^9 \times\left(-15 \times 10^{-6}\right) \times\left(10 \times 10^{-6}\right)}{3 \times 10^{-2}}{/tex} = -45 J {tex}\mathrm{U}_{\mathrm{Q} 2 \mathrm{Q} 3}=\frac{1}{4 \pi \epsilon_o} \frac{Q_2 Q_3}{r_{23}}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{9 \times 10^9 \times\left(10 \times 10^{-6}\right) \times\left(16 \times 10^{-6}\right)}{4 \times 10^{-2}}{/tex} = 36 J {tex}\mathrm{U}_{\mathrm{Q} 1 \mathrm{Q} 3}=\frac{1}{4 \pi \epsilon_o} \frac{Q_1 Q_3}{r_{13}}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{9 \times 10^9 \times\left(-15 \times 10^{-6}\right) \times\left(16 \times 10^{-6}\right)}{5 \times 10^{-2}}{/tex} = -43.2 J {tex}\mathrm{U}_{\mathrm{net}}=\mathrm{U}_{\mathrm{Q} 1 \mathrm{Q} 2}+\mathrm{U}_{\mathrm{Q} 2 \mathrm{Q} 3}+\mathrm{U}_{\mathrm{Q} 1 \mathrm{Q} 3}{/tex} = -45 + 36 - 43.2 = 52.2 J
solution_html:
{tex}\mathrm{U}_{\mathrm{Q} 1 \mathrm{Q} 2}=\frac{1}{4 \pi \epsilon_o} \frac{Q_1 Q_2}{r_{12}}{/tex}
{tex}=\frac{9 \times 10^9 \times\left(-15 \times 10^{-6}\right) \times\left(10 \times 10^{-6}\right)}{3 \times 10^{-2}}{/tex}
= -45 J
{tex}\mathrm{U}_{\mathrm{Q} 2 \mathrm{Q} 3}=\frac{1}{4 \pi \epsilon_o} \frac{Q_2 Q_3}{r_{23}}{/tex}
{tex}=\frac{9 \times 10^9 \times\left(10 \times 10^{-6}\right) \times\left(16 \times 10^{-6}\right)}{4 \times 10^{-2}}{/tex}
= 36 J
{tex}\mathrm{U}_{\mathrm{Q} 1 \mathrm{Q} 3}=\frac{1}{4 \pi \epsilon_o} \frac{Q_1 Q_3}{r_{13}}{/tex}
{tex}=\frac{9 \times 10^9 \times\left(-15 \times 10^{-6}\right) \times\left(16 \times 10^{-6}\right)}{5 \times 10^{-2}}{/tex}
= -43.2 J
{tex}\mathrm{U}_{\mathrm{net}}=\mathrm{U}_{\mathrm{Q} 1 \mathrm{Q} 2}+\mathrm{U}_{\mathrm{Q} 2 \mathrm{Q} 3}+\mathrm{U}_{\mathrm{Q} 1 \mathrm{Q} 3}{/tex}
= -45 + 36 - 43.2
= 52.2 J

subject: Physics
question_html:
Assertion (A): Work done in moving a charge around a closed path, in an electric field is always zero.
Reason (R): Electrostatic force is a conservative force.
question_text: Assertion (A): Work done in moving a charge around a closed path, in an electric field is always zero. Reason (R): Electrostatic force is a conservative force.

options.text: Both Assertion (A) and Reason (R) are true and Reason (R) is the correct explanation of Assertion (A).
options.html: Both Assertion (A) and Reason (R) are true and Reason (R) is the correct explanation of Assertion (A).
options.explanation: The assertion is correct because in electrostatics, the work done in moving a charge around a closed path is always zero. This happens because the electrostatic force is a conservative force, as stated in the reason. A conservative force means the work done depends only on the initial and final positions, not the path taken, and is zero for a closed path.

options.text: Both Assertion (A) and Reason (R) are true and Reason (R) is NOT the correct explanation of Assertion (A).
options.html: Both Assertion (A) and Reason (R) are true and Reason (R) is NOT the correct explanation of Assertion (A).

options.text: Assertion (A) is true but Reason (R) is false.

options.text: Assertion (A) is false but Reason (R) is also false.
options.html: Assertion (A) is false but Reason (R) is also false.

This happens because the electrostatic force is a conservative force, as stated in the reason. A conservative force means the work done depends only on the initial and final positions, not the path taken, and is zero for a closed path.

question_html: Equipotentials at a great distance from a collection of charges whose total sum is not zero are approximately
question_text: Equipotentials at a great distance from a collection of charges whose total sum is not zero are approximately

options.explanation: Here we have to find out the shape of the equipotential surface. These surfaces are perpendicular to the field lines. So there must be an electric field which cannot be without charge. So the algebraic sum of all charges must not be zero. Equipotential surface at a great distance means that the space of charge is negligible as compared to distance. So the collection of charges is considered as a point charge. The electric potential due to point charge is given by V=1/4 {tex}\pi\in{/tex} 0 r. It means that potential due to a point charge is same for all equidistant points, which are at the same potential form spherical shape. The lines of the field from point charges are radial. So the equipotential surface (perpendicular to the field lines) form a sphere.

subject: Physics
question_html: A variable capacitor is connected to a 200 V battery. If its capacitance is changed from {tex}2 \mu \mathrm{F}{/tex} to {tex}X \mu \mathrm{F}{/tex}, the decrease in energy of the capacitor is
question_text: A variable capacitor is connected to a 200 V battery. If its capacitance is changed from {tex}2 \mu \mathrm{F}{/tex} to {tex}X \mu \mathrm{F}{/tex} , the decrease in energy of the capacitor is

options.explanation: Energy = {tex}\frac{1}{2} C V^2{/tex} In 1 st case: E 1 = {tex}\frac{1}{2} \times 2 \times 10^{-6} \times 200^2{/tex} {tex}=4 \times 10^{-2} \mathrm{~J}{/tex} In 2 nd case: E 2 = {tex}\frac{1}{2} \times(\mathrm{X}) \times 10^{-6} \times 200^2 \mathrm{~J}{/tex} Decrease in energy = E 1 - E 2 or, 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -2 = 4 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -2 -2 {tex}\times{/tex} (X) {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -2 or, 2 = 4 - 2X {tex}\therefore{/tex} X = 1 {tex}\mu \mathrm{F}{/tex}

explanation: Energy = {tex}\frac{1}{2} C V^2{/tex} In 1 st case: E 1 = {tex}\frac{1}{2} \times 2 \times 10^{-6} \times 200^2{/tex} {tex}=4 \times 10^{-2} \mathrm{~J}{/tex} In 2 nd case: E 2 = {tex}\frac{1}{2} \times(\mathrm{X}) \times 10^{-6} \times 200^2 \mathrm{~J}{/tex} Decrease in energy = E 1 - E 2 or, 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -2 = 4 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -2 -2 {tex}\times{/tex} (X) {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -2 or, 2 = 4 - 2X {tex}\therefore{/tex} X = 1 {tex}\mu \mathrm{F}{/tex}

question_text: Four charges -q, -q, +q and +q are placed at the corners of a square of side 2 L is shown in figure. The electric potential at point A midway between the two charges +q and +q is

options.explanation: Electric potential due to two +q charges {tex}= \frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0} \times \frac{2 q}{L}{/tex} Electric potential due to two -q charges {tex}=\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0} \times \frac{-2 q}{\sqrt{5} L}{/tex} Total potential at A = {tex}\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0} \times \frac{2 q}{L}\left(1-\frac{1}{\sqrt{5}}\right){/tex}

subject: Physics
question_html:
1. Why does the electric field inside a dielectric slab decrease when kept in an external electric field?
    2. Derive an expression for the capacitance of a parallel plate capacitor filled with a medium of dielectric constant K.
  2. A charge q = 2 {tex}\mu{/tex}C is placed at the centre of a sphere of radius 20 cm. What is the amount of work done in moving 4 {tex}\mu{/tex}C from one point to another point on its surface?
  3. Write a relation for polarisation {tex}\overrightarrow{\mathrm{P}}{/tex} of a dielectric material in the presence of an external electric field.
question_text: Why does the electric field inside a dielectric slab decrease when kept in an external electric field? Derive an expression for the capacitance of a parallel plate capacitor filled with a medium of dielectric constant K. A charge q = 2 {tex}\mu{/tex} C is placed at the centre of a sphere of radius 20 cm. What is the amount of work done in moving 4 {tex}\mu{/tex} C from one point to another point on its surface? Write a relation for polarisation {tex}\overrightarrow{\mathrm{P}}{/tex} of a dielectric material in the presence of an external electric field.
explanation: A dielectric material gets polarized when it is placed in an external electric field. The field produced due to the polarization of material reduces the effect of external electric field. Hence, the electric field inside a dielectric decreases. Electric field in vacuum between the plates = E0 = {tex}\frac{\sigma}{\varepsilon_o}{/tex} Electric field in dielectric between the plates, E = {tex}\frac{E_0}{K}{/tex} Potential difference between the capacitor plates V = Et + E 0 (d - t) where 't' is the thickness of dielectric slab. V = {tex}\frac{E_0}{K} \mathrm{t}+\mathrm{E}_0(\mathrm{~d}-\mathrm{t}){/tex} V = {tex}\frac{\sigma}{\varepsilon_o}\left[\frac{t}{K}+(\mathrm{d}-\mathrm{t})\right]{/tex} V = {tex}\frac{\sigma}{\varepsilon_o}\left[\frac{t+K(d-t)}{K}\right]{/tex} As C = {tex}\frac{Q}{V}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow {/tex} C = {tex}\frac{\varepsilon_0 A K}{t+K(d-t)}{/tex} The surface of the sphere is equipotential. So, the work done in moving the charge from one point to the other is zero. W = q {tex}\Delta{/tex} V ( {tex}\because{/tex} {tex}\Delta{/tex} V = 0) = 0 P = {tex}\chi{/tex} E
solution: A dielectric material gets polarized when it is placed in an external electric field. The field produced due to the polarization of material reduces the effect of external electric field. Hence, the electric field inside a dielectric decreases. Electric field in vacuum between the plates = E0 = {tex}\frac{\sigma}{\varepsilon_o}{/tex} Electric field in dielectric between the plates, E = {tex}\frac{E_0}{K}{/tex} Potential difference between the capacitor plates V = Et + E 0 (d - t) where 't' is the thickness of dielectric slab. V = {tex}\frac{E_0}{K} \mathrm{t}+\mathrm{E}_0(\mathrm{~d}-\mathrm{t}){/tex} V = {tex}\frac{\sigma}{\varepsilon_o}\left[\frac{t}{K}+(\mathrm{d}-\mathrm{t})\right]{/tex} V = {tex}\frac{\sigma}{\varepsilon_o}\left[\frac{t+K(d-t)}{K}\right]{/tex} As C = {tex}\frac{Q}{V}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow {/tex} C = {tex}\frac{\varepsilon_0 A K}{t+K(d-t)}{/tex} The surface of the sphere is equipotential. So, the work done in moving the charge from one point to the other is zero. W = q {tex}\Delta{/tex} V ( {tex}\because{/tex} {tex}\Delta{/tex} V = 0) = 0 P = {tex}\chi{/tex} E
solution_html:
1. A dielectric material gets polarized when it is placed in an external electric field. The field produced due to the polarization of material reduces the effect of external electric field. Hence, the electric field inside a dielectric decreases.
    2. Electric field in vacuum between the plates = E0 = {tex}\frac{\sigma}{\varepsilon_o}{/tex}
      Electric field in dielectric between the plates, E = {tex}\frac{E_0}{K}{/tex}
      Potential difference between the capacitor plates
      V = Et + E0(d - t)
      where 't' is the thickness of dielectric slab.
      V =  {tex}\frac{E_0}{K} \mathrm{t}+\mathrm{E}_0(\mathrm{~d}-\mathrm{t}){/tex}

      V = {tex}\frac{\sigma}{\varepsilon_o}\left[\frac{t}{K}+(\mathrm{d}-\mathrm{t})\right]{/tex}
      V = {tex}\frac{\sigma}{\varepsilon_o}\left[\frac{t+K(d-t)}{K}\right]{/tex}
      As C = {tex}\frac{Q}{V}{/tex}
      {tex}\Rightarrow {/tex} C = {tex}\frac{\varepsilon_0 A K}{t+K(d-t)}{/tex}

  2. The surface of the sphere is equipotential. So, the work done in moving the charge from one point to the other is zero.
    W = q {tex}\Delta{/tex} V ({tex}\because{/tex} {tex}\Delta{/tex} V = 0)
     = 0
  3. P =  {tex}\chi{/tex} E

subject: Physics
question_html:
1. Obtain an expression for the potential energy of an electric dipole placed in a uniform electric field.
  2. Three capacitors of capacitance C1, C2 and C3 are connected in series to a source of V volt. Show that the total energy stored in the combination of capacitors is equal to sum of the energy stored in individual capacitors.
  3. A capacitor of capacitance C is connected across a battery. After charging, the battery is disconnected and the separation between the plates is doubled. How will (i) the capacitance of the capacitor, and (ii) the electric field between the plates be affected? Justify your answer.
question_text: Obtain an expression for the potential energy of an electric dipole placed in a uniform electric field. Three capacitors of capacitance C 1 , C 2 and C 3 are connected in series to a source of V volt. Show that the total energy stored in the combination of capacitors is equal to sum of the energy stored in individual capacitors. A capacitor of capacitance C is connected across a battery. After charging, the battery is disconnected and the separation between the plates is doubled. How will (i) the capacitance of the capacitor, and (ii) the electric field between the plates be affected? Justify your answer.
explanation: Consider a dipole with charges q1 = +q and q2 = -q is placed in a uniform electric field E, as shown in Fig The dipole experiences no net force; but experiences a torque {tex}\boldsymbol{\tau}{/tex} given by {tex}\boldsymbol{\tau}{/tex} = p {tex}\times{/tex} E which will tend to rotate it (unless p is parallel or antiparallel to E). Suppose an external torque {tex}\boldsymbol{\tau}_{ext}{/tex} is applied in such a manner that it just neutralizes this torque and rotates it in the plane of paper from angle {tex}\theta_o{/tex} to angle {tex}\theta_1{/tex} at an infinitesimal angular speed and without angular acceleration. The amount of work done by the external torque will be given by W = {tex}\int_{\theta_0}^{\theta_1} \tau_{\text {ext }}(\theta) d \theta{/tex} = {tex}\int_{\theta_0}^{\theta_1} p E \sin \theta d \theta{/tex} W = U = pE {tex}\left(\cos \theta_0-\cos \theta_1\right){/tex} U = {tex}\frac{1}{2} \frac{Q^2}{C_{\text {eff }}}{/tex} {tex}\frac{1}{C_{\text {eff }}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1}{C_1}+\frac{1}{C_2}+\frac{1}{C_3}{/tex} U = {tex}\frac{1}{2} \frac{Q^2}{C_1}+\frac{1}{2} \frac{Q^2}{C_2}+\frac{1}{2} \frac{Q^2}{C_3}{/tex} U = U 1 + U 2 + U 3 When battery is disconnected then charge (q) remains constant. Capacitance is halved C' = {tex}\frac{\varepsilon_o A}{2 d}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{C}{2}{/tex} Electric field (E) is unaffected. E = {tex}\frac{\sigma}{\varepsilon}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{q}{\varepsilon_o A}{/tex} Alternatively for effect on electric field. E = {tex}\frac{V}{d}{/tex} V' = {tex}\frac{Q}{C^{\prime}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{Q}{\frac{C}{ 2}}{/tex} = 2 V E' = {tex}\frac{V^{\prime}}{d^{\prime}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{2 V}{2 d}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{V}{d}{/tex} = E
solution: Consider a dipole with charges q1 = +q and q2 = -q is placed in a uniform electric field E, as shown in Fig The dipole experiences no net force; but experiences a torque {tex}\boldsymbol{\tau}{/tex} given by {tex}\boldsymbol{\tau}{/tex} = p {tex}\times{/tex} E which will tend to rotate it (unless p is parallel or antiparallel to E). Suppose an external torque {tex}\boldsymbol{\tau}_{ext}{/tex} is applied in such a manner that it just neutralizes this torque and rotates it in the plane of paper from angle {tex}\theta_o{/tex} to angle {tex}\theta_1{/tex} at an infinitesimal angular speed and without angular acceleration. The amount of work done by the external torque will be given by W = {tex}\int_{\theta_0}^{\theta_1} \tau_{\text {ext }}(\theta) d \theta{/tex} = {tex}\int_{\theta_0}^{\theta_1} p E \sin \theta d \theta{/tex} W = U = pE {tex}\left(\cos \theta_0-\cos \theta_1\right){/tex} U = {tex}\frac{1}{2} \frac{Q^2}{C_{\text {eff }}}{/tex} {tex}\frac{1}{C_{\text {eff }}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1}{C_1}+\frac{1}{C_2}+\frac{1}{C_3}{/tex} U = {tex}\frac{1}{2} \frac{Q^2}{C_1}+\frac{1}{2} \frac{Q^2}{C_2}+\frac{1}{2} \frac{Q^2}{C_3}{/tex} U = U 1 + U 2 + U 3 When battery is disconnected then charge (q) remains constant. Capacitance is halved C' = {tex}\frac{\varepsilon_o A}{2 d}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{C}{2}{/tex} Electric field (E) is unaffected. E = {tex}\frac{\sigma}{\varepsilon}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{q}{\varepsilon_o A}{/tex} Alternatively for effect on electric field. E = {tex}\frac{V}{d}{/tex} V' = {tex}\frac{Q}{C^{\prime}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{Q}{\frac{C}{ 2}}{/tex} = 2 V E' = {tex}\frac{V^{\prime}}{d^{\prime}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{2 V}{2 d}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{V}{d}{/tex} = E
solution_html:
1. Consider a dipole with charges q1 = +q and q2 = -q is placed in a uniform electric field E, as shown in Fig The dipole experiences no net force; but experiences a torque {tex}\boldsymbol{\tau}{/tex} given by {tex}\boldsymbol{\tau}{/tex} = p {tex}\times{/tex} E 
    which will tend to rotate it (unless p is parallel or antiparallel to E). Suppose an external torque {tex}\boldsymbol{\tau}_{ext}{/tex} is applied in such a manner that it just neutralizes this torque and rotates it in the plane of paper from angle {tex}\theta_o{/tex} to angle {tex}\theta_1{/tex} at an infinitesimal angular speed and without angular acceleration. The amount of work done by the external torque will be given by
    W = {tex}\int_{\theta_0}^{\theta_1} \tau_{\text {ext }}(\theta) d \theta{/tex} = {tex}\int_{\theta_0}^{\theta_1} p E \sin \theta d \theta{/tex}
    W = U = pE {tex}\left(\cos \theta_0-\cos \theta_1\right){/tex}
  2. U = {tex}\frac{1}{2} \frac{Q^2}{C_{\text {eff }}}{/tex}
    {tex}\frac{1}{C_{\text {eff }}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1}{C_1}+\frac{1}{C_2}+\frac{1}{C_3}{/tex}
    U = {tex}\frac{1}{2} \frac{Q^2}{C_1}+\frac{1}{2} \frac{Q^2}{C_2}+\frac{1}{2} \frac{Q^2}{C_3}{/tex}
    U = U1 + U2 + U3

  3. When battery is disconnected then charge (q) remains constant.
    Capacitance is halved
    C' = {tex}\frac{\varepsilon_o A}{2 d}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{C}{2}{/tex}
    Electric field (E) is unaffected.
    E = {tex}\frac{\sigma}{\varepsilon}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{q}{\varepsilon_o A}{/tex}
    Alternatively for effect on electric field.
    E = {tex}\frac{V}{d}{/tex}
    V' = {tex}\frac{Q}{C^{\prime}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{Q}{\frac{C}{ 2}}{/tex} = 2 V
    E' = {tex}\frac{V^{\prime}}{d^{\prime}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{2 V}{2 d}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{V}{d}{/tex} = E

subject: Physics
question_html:
1. Two-point charges q1 and q2 are kept r distance apart in a uniform external electric field {tex}\vec E{/tex}. Find the amount of work done in assembling this system of charges.
  2. A cube of side 20 cm is kept in a region as shown in the figure. An electric field {tex}\vec E{/tex} exists in the region such that the potential at a point is given by V = 10x + 5, where V is in volt and x is in m.

    Find the
    1. electric field {tex}\vec E{/tex}, and
    2. total electric flux through the cube.
question_text: Two-point charges q 1 and q 2 are kept r distance apart in a uniform external electric field {tex}\vec E{/tex} . Find the amount of work done in assembling this system of charges. A cube of side 20 cm is kept in a region as shown in the figure. An electric field {tex}\vec E{/tex} exists in the region such that the potential at a point is given by V = 10x + 5, where V is in volt and x is in m. Find the electric field {tex}\vec E{/tex} , and total electric flux through the cube.
explanation: Let the charge q 1 travels r 1 distance. The work done in bringing the charge q1 in the field is:- W 1 = F 1 {tex}\times{/tex} r 1 = q 1 E {tex}\times{/tex} r 1 the work done in bringing the second charge W 2 = F 2 {tex}\times{/tex} r 2 = q 2 E {tex}\times{/tex} r 2 and the work is also done to overcome the force of the charge on one-another. W 3 = {tex}\frac{1}{4 \pi \in_0} \cdot \frac{\mathrm{q}_{1} \mathrm{q}_{2}}{\mathrm{r}^{2}}{/tex} So, total work = q 1 Er 1 + q 2 Er 2 + {tex}\frac{1}{4 \pi \in_0} \cdot \frac{\mathrm{q}_{1} \mathrm{q}_{2}}{\mathrm{r}^{2}}{/tex} Given that, V = 10x + 5 We know E= - {tex}\frac{\mathrm{dv}}{\mathrm{dx}}{/tex} v = 10x + 5 {tex}\frac{\mathrm{dv}}{\mathrm{dx}}=\frac{\mathrm{d}}{\mathrm{dx}}(10 \mathrm{x}+5){/tex} = {tex}10 \frac{\mathrm{d}}{\mathrm{dx}} \mathrm{x}+0{/tex} = 10 electric field is given by E = -10 N/C Since electric field is constant in negative x-direction as the flux enter in the cube will be same as flux come out through the cube so flux {tex}\phi_{\mathrm{in}}=\phi_{\text {out }}{/tex} hence, Net flux from the cube = 0, so total electric flux is given by: {tex}\phi_{\text {net }}{/tex} = 0
solution: Let the charge q 1 travels r 1 distance. The work done in bringing the charge q1 in the field is:- W 1 = F 1 {tex}\times{/tex} r 1 = q 1 E {tex}\times{/tex} r 1 the work done in bringing the second charge W 2 = F 2 {tex}\times{/tex} r 2 = q 2 E {tex}\times{/tex} r 2 and the work is also done to overcome the force of the charge on one-another. W 3 = {tex}\frac{1}{4 \pi \in_0} \cdot \frac{\mathrm{q}_{1} \mathrm{q}_{2}}{\mathrm{r}^{2}}{/tex} So, total work = q 1 Er 1 + q 2 Er 2 + {tex}\frac{1}{4 \pi \in_0} \cdot \frac{\mathrm{q}_{1} \mathrm{q}_{2}}{\mathrm{r}^{2}}{/tex} Given that, V = 10x + 5 We know E= - {tex}\frac{\mathrm{dv}}{\mathrm{dx}}{/tex} v = 10x + 5 {tex}\frac{\mathrm{dv}}{\mathrm{dx}}=\frac{\mathrm{d}}{\mathrm{dx}}(10 \mathrm{x}+5){/tex} = {tex}10 \frac{\mathrm{d}}{\mathrm{dx}} \mathrm{x}+0{/tex} = 10 electric field is given by E = -10 N/C Since electric field is constant in negative x-direction as the flux enter in the cube will be same as flux come out through the cube so flux {tex}\phi_{\mathrm{in}}=\phi_{\text {out }}{/tex} hence, Net flux from the cube = 0, so total electric flux is given by: {tex}\phi_{\text {net }}{/tex} = 0
solution_html:
1. Let the charge q1 travels r1 distance.
    The work done in bringing the charge q1 in the field is:-
    W1 = F1 {tex}\times{/tex} r1
    = q1E {tex}\times{/tex} r1
    the work done in bringing the second charge
    W2 = F2 {tex}\times{/tex} r2
    = q2E {tex}\times{/tex} r2
    and the work is also done to overcome the force of the charge on one-another.
    W3 = {tex}\frac{1}{4 \pi \in_0} \cdot \frac{\mathrm{q}_{1} \mathrm{q}_{2}}{\mathrm{r}^{2}}{/tex} 
    So, total work = q1Er1 + q2Er2 + {tex}\frac{1}{4 \pi \in_0} \cdot \frac{\mathrm{q}_{1} \mathrm{q}_{2}}{\mathrm{r}^{2}}{/tex} 
    1. Given that, V = 10x + 5
      We know
      E= -{tex}\frac{\mathrm{dv}}{\mathrm{dx}}{/tex} 
      v = 10x + 5
      {tex}\frac{\mathrm{dv}}{\mathrm{dx}}=\frac{\mathrm{d}}{\mathrm{dx}}(10 \mathrm{x}+5){/tex} 
      = {tex}10 \frac{\mathrm{d}}{\mathrm{dx}} \mathrm{x}+0{/tex} 
      = 10
       electric field is given by E = -10 N/C
    2. Since electric field is constant in negative x-direction
      as the  flux enter in the cube will be same as flux come out through the cube so flux
      {tex}\phi_{\mathrm{in}}=\phi_{\text {out }}{/tex} hence,
      Net flux from the cube = 0, so total electric flux is given by:
      {tex}\phi_{\text {net }}{/tex} = 0

question_text: Two capacitors of capacitances C 1 and C 2 are connected in parallel. If a charge Q is given to the combination, the ratio of the charge on the capacitor C 1 to the charge on C 2 will be

options.explanation: As C 1 and C 2 are connected in parallel, so the potential V= {tex}Q\over C_1+C_2{/tex} will be same for both capacitors. thus, Q 1 = {tex}QC_1\over C_1+C_2{/tex} and Q 2 = {tex}QC_2\over C_1+C_2{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\frac{\mathrm{Q}_{1}}{\mathrm{Q}_{2}}=\frac{\mathrm{C}_{1}}{\mathrm{C}_{2}}{/tex}

explanation: As C 1 and C 2 are connected in parallel, so the potential V= {tex}Q\over C_1+C_2{/tex} will be same for both capacitors. thus, Q 1 = {tex}QC_1\over C_1+C_2{/tex} and Q 2 = {tex}QC_2\over C_1+C_2{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\frac{\mathrm{Q}_{1}}{\mathrm{Q}_{2}}=\frac{\mathrm{C}_{1}}{\mathrm{C}_{2}}{/tex}

question_text: An electric dipole consisting of charges + q and - q separated by a distance L is in stable equilibrium in a uniform electric field {tex}\vec E{/tex} . The electrostatic potential energy of the dipole is

options.explanation: When the dipole is in the direction of the field then net force is qE + (-qE) = 0 and its potential energy is minimum P= -pE Here, p is an electric dipole defined by the product of charge and distance between them: p = qL thus P= -qLE

subject: Physics
question_html:
You are given three capacitors of {tex}2 \mu \mathrm{F}, 3 \mu \mathrm{F}{/tex} and {tex}4 \mu \mathrm{F}{/tex}, respectively.

  1. Form a combination of all these capacitors of equivalent capacitance {tex}\frac{13}{3} \mu \mathrm{F}{/tex}.
  2. What is the maximum and minimum value of the equivalent capacitance that can be obtained by connecting these capacitors?
question_text: You are given three capacitors of {tex}2 \mu \mathrm{F}, 3 \mu \mathrm{F}{/tex} and {tex}4 \mu \mathrm{F}{/tex} , respectively. Form a combination of all these capacitors of equivalent capacitance {tex}\frac{13}{3} \mu \mathrm{F}{/tex} . What is the maximum and minimum value of the equivalent capacitance that can be obtained by connecting these capacitors?
explanation: {tex}2 \mu \mathrm{F}{/tex} and {tex}4 \mu \mathrm{F}{/tex} capacitors are to be connected in series. So, the effective capacitor will be {tex} C_{\text {eff }}=\frac{2 \times 4}{2+4}=\frac{4}{3} \mu \mathrm{F} {/tex} With {tex}\mathrm{C}_{\text {eff }}{/tex} , the {tex}3 \mu \mathrm{F}{/tex} capacitor is to be connected in parallel. So, the equivalent capacitor will be {tex}C_{\text {eqv }}=\frac{4}{3}+3=\frac{13}{3} \mu \mathrm{F}{/tex} Maximum value of capacitance may be obtained by connecting the capacitors in parallel. In that case, the equivalent capacitance will be, (2 + 3 + 4) = {tex}9 \mu \mathrm{F}{/tex} Minimum value of capacitance may be obtained by connecting the capacitors in series. In that case, the equivalent capacitance will be C S . {tex}\frac{1}{C_S}=\frac{1}{2}+\frac{1}{3}+\frac{1}{4}=\frac{13}{12}{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} C S = {tex}\frac{12}{13} \mu \mathrm{F}{/tex}
solution: {tex}2 \mu \mathrm{F}{/tex} and {tex}4 \mu \mathrm{F}{/tex} capacitors are to be connected in series. So, the effective capacitor will be {tex} C_{\text {eff }}=\frac{2 \times 4}{2+4}=\frac{4}{3} \mu \mathrm{F} {/tex} With {tex}\mathrm{C}_{\text {eff }}{/tex} , the {tex}3 \mu \mathrm{F}{/tex} capacitor is to be connected in parallel. So, the equivalent capacitor will be {tex}C_{\text {eqv }}=\frac{4}{3}+3=\frac{13}{3} \mu \mathrm{F}{/tex} Maximum value of capacitance may be obtained by connecting the capacitors in parallel. In that case, the equivalent capacitance will be, (2 + 3 + 4) = {tex}9 \mu \mathrm{F}{/tex} Minimum value of capacitance may be obtained by connecting the capacitors in series. In that case, the equivalent capacitance will be C S . {tex}\frac{1}{C_S}=\frac{1}{2}+\frac{1}{3}+\frac{1}{4}=\frac{13}{12}{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} C S = {tex}\frac{12}{13} \mu \mathrm{F}{/tex}
solution_html:
1. {tex}2 \mu \mathrm{F}{/tex} and {tex}4 \mu \mathrm{F}{/tex} capacitors are to be connected in series. So, the effective capacitor will be {tex} C_{\text {eff }}=\frac{2 \times 4}{2+4}=\frac{4}{3} \mu \mathrm{F} {/tex}
    With {tex}\mathrm{C}_{\text {eff }}{/tex}, the {tex}3 \mu \mathrm{F}{/tex} capacitor is to be connected in parallel. So, the equivalent capacitor will be {tex}C_{\text {eqv }}=\frac{4}{3}+3=\frac{13}{3} \mu \mathrm{F}{/tex}
  2. Maximum value of capacitance may be obtained by connecting the capacitors in parallel. In that case, the equivalent capacitance will be, (2 + 3 + 4) = {tex}9 \mu \mathrm{F}{/tex}
    Minimum value of capacitance may be obtained by connecting the capacitors in series. In that case, the equivalent capacitance will be CS.
    {tex}\frac{1}{C_S}=\frac{1}{2}+\frac{1}{3}+\frac{1}{4}=\frac{13}{12}{/tex}
    {tex}\therefore{/tex} CS = {tex}\frac{12}{13} \mu \mathrm{F}{/tex}

explanation: 6 {tex}\mu{/tex} F and 3 {tex}\mu{/tex} F are connected in series. Hence their equivalent capacitance is 2 {tex}\mu{/tex} F. 4 {tex}\mu{/tex} F and 2 {tex}\mu{/tex} F are connected in series. Hence their equivalent capacitance is {tex}\frac{4}{3}{/tex} {tex}\mu{/tex} F. Again, 2 {tex}\mu{/tex} F and {tex}\frac{4}{3}{/tex} {tex}\mu{/tex} F capacitors are connected in parallel. So, the equivalent capacitance {tex}=\frac{10}{3}{/tex} {tex}\mu{/tex} F = {tex}\frac{10}{3}{/tex} {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -6 F Since, Q = CV Given: C {tex}=\frac{10}{3} \times 10^{-6} \mathrm{~F} {/tex} and V = 3 V {tex}\therefore{/tex} Q = {tex}\frac{10}{3} \times 10^{-6} \times 3=10^{-5} \mathrm{C}{/tex} = 10 {tex}\mu{/tex} C
solution: 6 {tex}\mu{/tex} F and 3 {tex}\mu{/tex} F are connected in series. Hence their equivalent capacitance is 2 {tex}\mu{/tex} F. 4 {tex}\mu{/tex} F and 2 {tex}\mu{/tex} F are connected in series. Hence their equivalent capacitance is {tex}\frac{4}{3}{/tex} {tex}\mu{/tex} F. Again, 2 {tex}\mu{/tex} F and {tex}\frac{4}{3}{/tex} {tex}\mu{/tex} F capacitors are connected in parallel. So, the equivalent capacitance {tex}=\frac{10}{3}{/tex} {tex}\mu{/tex} F = {tex}\frac{10}{3}{/tex} {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -6 F Since, Q = CV Given: C {tex}=\frac{10}{3} \times 10^{-6} \mathrm{~F} {/tex} and V = 3 V {tex}\therefore{/tex} Q = {tex}\frac{10}{3} \times 10^{-6} \times 3=10^{-5} \mathrm{C}{/tex} = 10 {tex}\mu{/tex} C
solution_html:
6 {tex}\mu{/tex}F and 3 {tex}\mu{/tex}F are connected in series. Hence their equivalent capacitance is 2 {tex}\mu{/tex}F.
4 {tex}\mu{/tex}F and 2 {tex}\mu{/tex}F are connected in series. Hence their equivalent capacitance is {tex}\frac{4}{3}{/tex} {tex}\mu{/tex}F.
 Again, 2 {tex}\mu{/tex}F and {tex}\frac{4}{3}{/tex} {tex}\mu{/tex}F capacitors are connected in parallel. So, the equivalent capacitance {tex}=\frac{10}{3}{/tex} {tex}\mu{/tex}F = {tex}\frac{10}{3}{/tex} {tex}\times{/tex} 10-6 F
Since, Q = CV
Given: C {tex}=\frac{10}{3} \times 10^{-6} \mathrm{~F} {/tex} and V = 3 V
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Q = {tex}\frac{10}{3} \times 10^{-6} \times 3=10^{-5} \mathrm{C}{/tex}
= 10 {tex}\mu{/tex}C

In the figure given below, find the

question_text: In the figure given below, find the equivalent capacitance of the network between points A and B. Given: C 1 = C 5 = 8 {tex}\mu F{/tex} , C 2 = C 3 = C 4 = 4 {tex}\mu{/tex} F. maximum charge supplied by the battery, and total energy stored in the network.
explanation: In the network capacitors C 1 and C 2 as well as C 4 and C 5 are short circuited so its contribution in the equivalent capacitance of the circuit is zero. {tex}\therefore{/tex} Equivalent capacitance C = C 3 = 4 {tex}\mu{/tex} Net Charge supplied by the battery Q = CV = (4 {tex}\mu{/tex} F) {tex}\times{/tex} 7V = 28 {tex}\mu{/tex} C Total energy stored in the network of capacitors U = {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} CV 2 = {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} (4F) {tex}\times{/tex} (7V) 2 = 98 {tex}\mu{/tex} J
solution: In the network capacitors C 1 and C 2 as well as C 4 and C 5 are short circuited so its contribution in the equivalent capacitance of the circuit is zero. {tex}\therefore{/tex} Equivalent capacitance C = C 3 = 4 {tex}\mu{/tex} Net Charge supplied by the battery Q = CV = (4 {tex}\mu{/tex} F) {tex}\times{/tex} 7V = 28 {tex}\mu{/tex} C Total energy stored in the network of capacitors U = {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} CV 2 = {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} (4F) {tex}\times{/tex} (7V) 2 = 98 {tex}\mu{/tex} J
solution_html:
1. In the network capacitors C1 and C2 as well as C4 and C5 are short circuited so its contribution in the equivalent capacitance of the circuit is zero.
    {tex}\therefore{/tex} Equivalent capacitance C = C3= 4{tex}\mu{/tex}
  2. Net Charge supplied by the battery Q = CV = (4{tex}\mu{/tex}F) {tex}\times{/tex} 7V = 28{tex}\mu{/tex}C
  3. Total energy stored in the network of capacitors  U = {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex}CV2 = {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex}(4F) {tex}\times{/tex} (7V)2 = 98{tex}\mu{/tex}J

question_html: A capacitor of 4 {tex}\mu{/tex}F is charged by a battery of 12 V. The battery is disconnected and a dielectric slab of dielectric constant 8 is inserted in between the plates of the capacitor to fill the space completely. Find the change in the (a) charge stored in the capacitor, (b) potential difference between the plates of the capacitor, and (c) energy stored in the capacitor.
question_text: A capacitor of 4 {tex}\mu{/tex} F is charged by a battery of 12 V. The battery is disconnected and a dielectric slab of dielectric constant 8 is inserted in between the plates of the capacitor to fill the space completely. Find the change in the (a) charge stored in the capacitor, (b) potential difference between the plates of the capacitor, and (c) energy stored in the capacitor.
explanation: q = CV = 48 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -6 coulomb Charge stored in the capacitor remains unchanged. Initial potential difference V o = 12 volt After battery is removed V = {tex}\frac{V_o}{K}=\frac{12}{8}{/tex} = 1.5 volt Decrease in potential difference = 12 - 1.5 = 10.5 V Energy stored initially U o = {tex}\frac {1} {2}{/tex} C o V o 2 = {tex}\frac {1} {2}{/tex} {tex}\times{/tex} 4 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 −6 {tex}\times{/tex} 12 {tex}\times{/tex} 12 = 288 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 −6 J Final U = {tex}\frac{1}{2} \mathrm{CV}^2=\frac{1}{2} \mathrm{KC}_o \frac{\mathrm{Vo}^2}{K^2}{/tex} {tex}\frac{1}{2} \mathrm{C}_{\mathrm{o}} \frac{\mathrm{Vo}^2}{\mathrm{~K}}=\frac{1}{2} \times \frac{4 \times 10^{-6} \times 12 \times 12}{8}{/tex} U = 36 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 −6 Joules Energy reduces by (288-36) {tex}\times{/tex} 10 −6 J = 252 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -6 J Even if a student, without evaluating U o and U, writes U becomes {tex}\frac {1} {K}{/tex} , award {tex}\frac {1} {2}{/tex} mark; and award {tex}\frac {1} {2}{/tex} mark for the correct formulae for U and U o
solution: q = CV = 48 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -6 coulomb Charge stored in the capacitor remains unchanged. Initial potential difference V o = 12 volt After battery is removed V = {tex}\frac{V_o}{K}=\frac{12}{8}{/tex} = 1.5 volt Decrease in potential difference = 12 - 1.5 = 10.5 V Energy stored initially U o = {tex}\frac {1} {2}{/tex} C o V o 2 = {tex}\frac {1} {2}{/tex} {tex}\times{/tex} 4 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 −6 {tex}\times{/tex} 12 {tex}\times{/tex} 12 = 288 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 −6 J Final U = {tex}\frac{1}{2} \mathrm{CV}^2=\frac{1}{2} \mathrm{KC}_o \frac{\mathrm{Vo}^2}{K^2}{/tex} {tex}\frac{1}{2} \mathrm{C}_{\mathrm{o}} \frac{\mathrm{Vo}^2}{\mathrm{~K}}=\frac{1}{2} \times \frac{4 \times 10^{-6} \times 12 \times 12}{8}{/tex} U = 36 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 −6 Joules Energy reduces by (288-36) {tex}\times{/tex} 10 −6 J = 252 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -6 J Even if a student, without evaluating U o and U, writes U becomes {tex}\frac {1} {K}{/tex} , award {tex}\frac {1} {2}{/tex} mark; and award {tex}\frac {1} {2}{/tex} mark for the correct formulae for U and U o
solution_html:
1. q = CV = 48 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-6 coulomb
    Charge stored in the capacitor remains unchanged.
  2. Initial potential difference Vo = 12 volt
    After battery is removed
    V = {tex}\frac{V_o}{K}=\frac{12}{8}{/tex} = 1.5 volt
    Decrease in potential difference = 12 - 1.5 = 10.5 V
  3. Energy stored initially
    Uo = {tex}\frac {1} {2}{/tex} CoVo2 = {tex}\frac {1} {2}{/tex} {tex}\times{/tex}4{tex}\times{/tex}10−6{tex}\times{/tex}12 {tex}\times{/tex}12 = 288 {tex}\times{/tex} 10−6 J
    Final U = {tex}\frac{1}{2} \mathrm{CV}^2=\frac{1}{2} \mathrm{KC}_o \frac{\mathrm{Vo}^2}{K^2}{/tex}
    {tex}\frac{1}{2} \mathrm{C}_{\mathrm{o}} \frac{\mathrm{Vo}^2}{\mathrm{~K}}=\frac{1}{2} \times \frac{4 \times 10^{-6} \times 12 \times 12}{8}{/tex}
    U = 36 {tex}\times{/tex} 10−6 Joules
    Energy reduces by (288-36) {tex}\times{/tex} 10−6 J = 252{tex}\times{/tex}10-6 J
  4. Even if a student, without evaluating Uo and U, writes U becomes {tex}\frac {1} {K}{/tex} , award {tex}\frac {1} {2}{/tex} mark; and award {tex}\frac {1} {2}{/tex} mark for the correct formulae for U and Uo
