subject: Physics
question_html: N small conducting liquid droplets, each of radius r, are charged to a potential V each. These droplets coalesce to form a single large drop without any charge leakage. Find the potential of the large drop.
question_text: N small conducting liquid droplets, each of radius r, are charged to a potential V each. These droplets coalesce to form a single large drop without any charge leakage. Find the potential of the large drop.
explanation: Potential of each small liquid droplet V = {tex}\frac{k_q}{r}{/tex} Volume of big drop = volume of N small drops {tex}\frac{4}{3} \pi R^3=N \cdot \frac{4}{3} \pi R^3{/tex} R = N 1 /3r Charge on big drop Q = Nq Potential on the surface of big drop V' = {tex}\frac{K Q}{R}{/tex} V' = {tex}K \frac{N q}{N^1 / 3^r}{/tex} = {tex}N^{1 / 3} \frac{k q}{r}{/tex} = N 2 /3 V
solution: Potential of each small liquid droplet V = {tex}\frac{k_q}{r}{/tex} Volume of big drop = volume of N small drops {tex}\frac{4}{3} \pi R^3=N \cdot \frac{4}{3} \pi R^3{/tex} R = N 1 /3r Charge on big drop Q = Nq Potential on the surface of big drop V' = {tex}\frac{K Q}{R}{/tex} V' = {tex}K \frac{N q}{N^1 / 3^r}{/tex} = {tex}N^{1 / 3} \frac{k q}{r}{/tex} = N 2 /3 V
solution_html:
Potential of each small liquid droplet V = {tex}\frac{k_q}{r}{/tex}
Volume of big drop = volume of N small drops
{tex}\frac{4}{3} \pi R^3=N \cdot \frac{4}{3} \pi R^3{/tex}
R = N1/3r
Charge on big drop Q = Nq
Potential on the surface of big drop V' = {tex}\frac{K Q}{R}{/tex}
V' = {tex}K \frac{N q}{N^1 / 3^r}{/tex}
= {tex}N^{1 / 3} \frac{k q}{r}{/tex} = N2/3 V

subject: Physics
question_html:
1. Derive an expression for the energy stored in a parallel plate capacitor of capacitance C when charged up to voltage V. How is this energy stored in the capacitor?
  2. A capacitor of capacitance 1 {tex}\mu{/tex}F is charged by connecting a battery of negligible internal resistance and emf 10 V across it. Calculate the amount of charge supplied by the battery in charging the capacitor fully.
question_text: Derive an expression for the energy stored in a parallel plate capacitor of capacitance C when charged up to voltage V. How is this energy stored in the capacitor? A capacitor of capacitance 1 {tex}\mu{/tex} F is charged by connecting a battery of negligible internal resistance and emf 10 V across it. Calculate the amount of charge supplied by the battery in charging the capacitor fully.
explanation: Work done in adding a charge dq = dW = Vdq = {tex}\frac{q}{c} d q{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} Total Amount of work(W) in charging a capacitor W = {tex}\int d W=\frac{1}{C} \int_0^Q q d q{/tex} W = {tex}\frac{Q^2}{2 C}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{(C V)^2}{2 C}=\frac{1}{2} C V^2{/tex} The electrostatic Energy/ potential energy is stored in the electric field between the plates. C = 1 {tex}\mu{/tex} F = 1 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -6 F; V = 10 volt Q = CV = 1 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -6 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 = 10 -5 coulomb hence, the amount of charge supplied by the battery in charging the capacitor fully is 10 -5 coulomb.
solution: Work done in adding a charge dq = dW = Vdq = {tex}\frac{q}{c} d q{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} Total Amount of work(W) in charging a capacitor W = {tex}\int d W=\frac{1}{C} \int_0^Q q d q{/tex} W = {tex}\frac{Q^2}{2 C}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{(C V)^2}{2 C}=\frac{1}{2} C V^2{/tex} The electrostatic Energy/ potential energy is stored in the electric field between the plates. C = 1 {tex}\mu{/tex} F = 1 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -6 F; V = 10 volt Q = CV = 1 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -6 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 = 10 -5 coulomb hence, the amount of charge supplied by the battery in charging the capacitor fully is 10 -5 coulomb.
solution_html:
1. Work done in adding a charge dq = dW
    = Vdq
    = {tex}\frac{q}{c} d q{/tex}
    {tex}\therefore{/tex} Total Amount of work(W) in charging a capacitor
    W = {tex}\int d W=\frac{1}{C} \int_0^Q q d q{/tex}
    W = {tex}\frac{Q^2}{2 C}{/tex}
    = {tex}\frac{(C V)^2}{2 C}=\frac{1}{2} C V^2{/tex}
    The electrostatic Energy/ potential energy is stored in the electric field between the plates.
  2. C = 1{tex}\mu{/tex}F = 1 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-6 F; V = 10 volt
    Q = CV
    = 1 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-6 {tex}\times{/tex} 10
    = 10-5 coulomb
    hence, the amount of charge supplied by the battery in charging the capacitor fully is 10-5 coulomb.

question_html: Find the total charge stored in the network of capacitors connected between A and B as shown in figure.
question_text: Find the total charge stored in the network of capacitors connected between A and B as shown in figure.
explanation: {tex}\frac{1}{C_{\text {net }}}=\frac{1}{6+12}+\frac{1}{6+12}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1}{18}+\frac{1}{18}=\frac{1}{9}{/tex} C net = 9 {tex}\mu{/tex} F Q = C net V Q = 9 {tex}\times{/tex} 3 = 27 {tex}\mu{/tex} C hence, total charge stored in the network of capacitors is 27 {tex}\mu{/tex} C.
solution: {tex}\frac{1}{C_{\text {net }}}=\frac{1}{6+12}+\frac{1}{6+12}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1}{18}+\frac{1}{18}=\frac{1}{9}{/tex} C net = 9 {tex}\mu{/tex} F Q = C net V Q = 9 {tex}\times{/tex} 3 = 27 {tex}\mu{/tex} C hence, total charge stored in the network of capacitors is 27 {tex}\mu{/tex} C.
solution_html:
{tex}\frac{1}{C_{\text {net }}}=\frac{1}{6+12}+\frac{1}{6+12}{/tex}
= {tex}\frac{1}{18}+\frac{1}{18}=\frac{1}{9}{/tex}
Cnet = 9{tex}\mu{/tex}F
Q = Cnet V
Q = 9 {tex}\times{/tex} 3
= 27 {tex}\mu{/tex}C
hence, total charge stored in the network of capacitors is 27 {tex}\mu{/tex}C.

subject: Physics
question_html: Obtain the expression for the energy stored in a capacitor connected across a dc battery. Hence define energy density of the capacitor.
question_text: Obtain the expression for the energy stored in a capacitor connected across a dc battery. Hence define energy density of the capacitor.
explanation: Let the charge on the capacitor plates at any instant, during charging process be q, amount of work done to supply further charge dq to the capacitor dW = Vdq where V is the potential difference and equals to {tex}\frac{q}{C}{/tex} Total work done to charge the capacitor upto charge Q W = {tex}\int_0^Q V d q{/tex} = {tex}\int_0^Q \frac{q}{C} d q=\frac{Q^2}{2 C}\left(\frac{1}{2} C V^2=\frac{1}{2} Q V\right){/tex} Since Energy stored = work done {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} U = {tex}\frac{Q^2}{2 C}\left(\frac{1}{2} C V^2=\frac{1}{2} Q V\right){/tex} The expression for the energy stored in a capacitor connected across a dc battery is U = {tex}\frac{Q^2}{2 C}\left(\frac{1}{2} C V^2=\frac{1}{2} Q V\right){/tex} . Energy density: Electrical energy stored per unit volume is known as energy density.
solution: Let the charge on the capacitor plates at any instant, during charging process be q, amount of work done to supply further charge dq to the capacitor dW = Vdq where V is the potential difference and equals to {tex}\frac{q}{C}{/tex} Total work done to charge the capacitor upto charge Q W = {tex}\int_0^Q V d q{/tex} = {tex}\int_0^Q \frac{q}{C} d q=\frac{Q^2}{2 C}\left(\frac{1}{2} C V^2=\frac{1}{2} Q V\right){/tex} Since Energy stored = work done {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} U = {tex}\frac{Q^2}{2 C}\left(\frac{1}{2} C V^2=\frac{1}{2} Q V\right){/tex} The expression for the energy stored in a capacitor connected across a dc battery is U = {tex}\frac{Q^2}{2 C}\left(\frac{1}{2} C V^2=\frac{1}{2} Q V\right){/tex} . Energy density: Electrical energy stored per unit volume is known as energy density.
solution_html:
Let the charge on the capacitor plates at any instant, during charging process be q, amount of work done to supply further charge dq to the capacitor
dW = Vdq
where V is the potential difference and equals to {tex}\frac{q}{C}{/tex}
Total work done to charge the capacitor upto charge Q
W = {tex}\int_0^Q V d q{/tex}
= {tex}\int_0^Q \frac{q}{C} d q=\frac{Q^2}{2 C}\left(\frac{1}{2} C V^2=\frac{1}{2} Q V\right){/tex}
Since Energy stored = work done
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} U = {tex}\frac{Q^2}{2 C}\left(\frac{1}{2} C V^2=\frac{1}{2} Q V\right){/tex}
The expression for the energy stored in a capacitor connected across a dc battery is U ={tex}\frac{Q^2}{2 C}\left(\frac{1}{2} C V^2=\frac{1}{2} Q V\right){/tex}.
Energy density: Electrical energy stored per unit volume is known as energy density.

subject: Physics
question_html:
A spherical conducting shell of inner radius r1 and outer radius r2 has a charge Q.

  1. A charge q is placed at the centre of the shell. What is the surface charge density on the inner and outer surfaces of the shell?
  2. Is the electric field inside a cavity (with no charge) zero, even if the shell is not spherical, but has any irregular shape? Explain.
question_text: A spherical conducting shell of inner radius r 1 and outer radius r 2 has a charge Q. A charge q is placed at the centre of the shell. What is the surface charge density on the inner and outer surfaces of the shell? Is the electric field inside a cavity (with no charge) zero, even if the shell is not spherical, but has any irregular shape? Explain.
explanation: As a +q charge place at the centre of the shell, it will create a '-q' charge on the inner surface of the shell. The charge on the outer surface will increase by +q due to the -q charge on the outer surface will increase by +q due to the -q charge on the inner surface by induction. Therefore, there will be total (Q+q) charge on the outer surface of the shell and -q charge on the inner surface of the shell. Now surface area of inner surface {tex} = 4\pi r_1^2{/tex} And surface area of outer surface {tex} = 4\pi r_2^2{/tex} Thus, charge density on the outer surface {tex} = \frac{{Q + q}}{{4\pi r_2^2}}{/tex} and charge density on the inner surface {tex} = \frac{{ - q}}{{4\pi r_1^2}}{/tex} Yes, whatever the shape of the shell, the electric field intensity in the cavity is zero. Take a closed loop such that it is inside the cavity and in conductor. Net work done in carrying charge along closed loop by electric field is zero. Thus, electric field is zero, whatever the shape.
solution: As a +q charge place at the centre of the shell, it will create a '-q' charge on the inner surface of the shell. The charge on the outer surface will increase by +q due to the -q charge on the outer surface will increase by +q due to the -q charge on the inner surface by induction. Therefore, there will be total (Q+q) charge on the outer surface of the shell and -q charge on the inner surface of the shell. Now surface area of inner surface {tex} = 4\pi r_1^2{/tex} And surface area of outer surface {tex} = 4\pi r_2^2{/tex} Thus, charge density on the outer surface {tex} = \frac{{Q + q}}{{4\pi r_2^2}}{/tex} and charge density on the inner surface {tex} = \frac{{ - q}}{{4\pi r_1^2}}{/tex} Yes, whatever the shape of the shell, the electric field intensity in the cavity is zero. Take a closed loop such that it is inside the cavity and in conductor. Net work done in carrying charge along closed loop by electric field is zero. Thus, electric field is zero, whatever the shape.
solution_html:
1. As a +q charge place at the centre of the shell, it will create a '-q' charge on the inner surface of the shell.

    The charge on the outer surface will increase by +q due to the -q charge on the outer surface will increase by +q due to the -q charge on the inner surface by induction. Therefore, there will be total (Q+q) charge on the outer surface of the shell and -q charge on the inner surface of the shell.
    Now surface area of inner surface {tex} = 4\pi r_1^2{/tex} 
    And surface area of outer surface {tex} = 4\pi r_2^2{/tex}
    Thus, charge density on the outer surface {tex} = \frac{{Q + q}}{{4\pi r_2^2}}{/tex} and charge density on the inner surface {tex} = \frac{{ - q}}{{4\pi r_1^2}}{/tex} 

  2. Yes, whatever the shape of the shell, the electric field intensity in the cavity is zero.
    Take a closed loop such that it is inside the cavity and in conductor. Net work done in carrying charge along closed loop by electric field is zero. Thus, electric field is zero, whatever the shape.

  2. Derive an expression for the electric potential at any point along the axial line of an electric dipole.
question_text: Draw the equipotential surfaces corresponding to a uniform electric field in the z-direction. Derive an expression for the electric potential at any point along the axial line of an electric dipole.
explanation: As the constant electric field is in the Z-direction, the equipotential surfaces are normal to the field, i.e. in XY-plane. The equipotential surfaces are equidistant from each other. Consider the figure. Potential at point P at distance r from centre of dipole is given as V p = -V -q + V +q = {tex}\frac{1}{4 \pi \epsilon_{0}} \frac{-q}{(r+a)}{/tex} + {tex}\frac{1}{4 \pi \epsilon_{0}} \frac{q}{(r-a)}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{q}{4 \pi \epsilon_{0}}{/tex} {tex}\left[\frac{1}{(r-a)}-\frac{1}{(r+a)}\right]{/tex} = {tex}\frac{q}{4 \pi \epsilon_{0}}{/tex} {tex}\left[\frac{r+a-r+a}{(r-a)(r+a)}\right]{/tex} = {tex}\frac{q}{4 \pi \epsilon_{0}} \times \frac{2 a}{\left(r^{2}-a^{2}\right)}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{q \times 2 a}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{o}\left(r^{2}-a^{2}\right.)}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1}{4 \pi \epsilon_{0}}{/tex} {tex}\frac{\mathrm{p}}{\left(\mathrm{r}^{2}-\mathrm{a}^{2}\right)}{/tex} (where p is the dipole moment, {tex}p=q\times2a{/tex} )
solution: As the constant electric field is in the Z-direction, the equipotential surfaces are normal to the field, i.e. in XY-plane. The equipotential surfaces are equidistant from each other. Consider the figure. Potential at point P at distance r from centre of dipole is given as V p = -V -q + V +q = {tex}\frac{1}{4 \pi \epsilon_{0}} \frac{-q}{(r+a)}{/tex} + {tex}\frac{1}{4 \pi \epsilon_{0}} \frac{q}{(r-a)}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{q}{4 \pi \epsilon_{0}}{/tex} {tex}\left[\frac{1}{(r-a)}-\frac{1}{(r+a)}\right]{/tex} = {tex}\frac{q}{4 \pi \epsilon_{0}}{/tex} {tex}\left[\frac{r+a-r+a}{(r-a)(r+a)}\right]{/tex} = {tex}\frac{q}{4 \pi \epsilon_{0}} \times \frac{2 a}{\left(r^{2}-a^{2}\right)}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{q \times 2 a}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{o}\left(r^{2}-a^{2}\right.)}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1}{4 \pi \epsilon_{0}}{/tex} {tex}\frac{\mathrm{p}}{\left(\mathrm{r}^{2}-\mathrm{a}^{2}\right)}{/tex} (where p is the dipole moment, {tex}p=q\times2a{/tex} )
solution_html:
1. As the constant electric field is in the Z-direction, the equipotential surfaces are normal to the field, i.e. in XY-plane. The equipotential surfaces are equidistant from each other.

  2. Consider the figure.

    Potential at point P at distance r from centre of dipole is given as
    Vp = -V-q + V+q
    = {tex}\frac{1}{4 \pi \epsilon_{0}} \frac{-q}{(r+a)}{/tex} + {tex}\frac{1}{4 \pi \epsilon_{0}} \frac{q}{(r-a)}{/tex}
    = {tex}\frac{q}{4 \pi \epsilon_{0}}{/tex} {tex}\left[\frac{1}{(r-a)}-\frac{1}{(r+a)}\right]{/tex}
    = {tex}\frac{q}{4 \pi \epsilon_{0}}{/tex} {tex}\left[\frac{r+a-r+a}{(r-a)(r+a)}\right]{/tex}
    = {tex}\frac{q}{4 \pi \epsilon_{0}} \times \frac{2 a}{\left(r^{2}-a^{2}\right)}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{q \times 2 a}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{o}\left(r^{2}-a^{2}\right.)}{/tex}
    = {tex}\frac{1}{4 \pi \epsilon_{0}}{/tex} {tex}\frac{\mathrm{p}}{\left(\mathrm{r}^{2}-\mathrm{a}^{2}\right)}{/tex}
    (where p is the dipole moment, {tex}p=q\times2a{/tex})

question_html:
1. Explain briefly, using a proper diagram, the difference in behaviour of a conductor and a dielectric in the presence of external electric field.
  2. Define the term polarization of a dielectric and write the expression for a linear isotropic dielectric in terms of electric field.
question_text: Explain briefly, using a proper diagram, the difference in behaviour of a conductor and a dielectric in the presence of external electric field. Define the term polarization of a dielectric and write the expression for a linear isotropic dielectric in terms of electric field.
explanation: When a conductor is placed in an external electric field, the free charges present inside the conductor rearrange themselves in such a manner that the electric field due to induced charges opposes the external field within the conductor. After some time a static situation is achieved when the two fields cancel each other and the net electrostatic field in the conductor becomes zero. Dielectrics are non-conducting in nature, i.e. they have no free charge carriers. Thus, in a dielectric, free movement of charges is no possible. In Dielectrics the external field induces dipole moment by stretching molecules of the dielectric. The collective effect of all the molecular dipole moments creates the net charge on the surface of the dielectric which produces a field that is the net electrostatic field in a dielectric is non-zero. The dipole moment per unit volume of a polarized dielectric is known as the polarization of a dielectric. It is denoted by the letter P for the linear isotropic dielectric. The polarisation of a linear isotropic dielectric, {tex}P=\chi_e E{/tex} Where {tex}\chi_e{/tex} is the electric susceptibility of the dielectric medium.

The polarisation of a linear isotropic dielectric, {tex}P=\chi_e E{/tex} Where {tex}\chi_e{/tex} is the electric susceptibility of the dielectric medium.

    Dielectrics are non-conducting in nature, i.e. they have no free charge carriers. Thus, in a dielectric, free movement of charges is no possible. In Dielectrics the external field induces dipole moment by stretching molecules of the dielectric. The collective effect of all the molecular dipole moments creates the net charge on the surface of the dielectric which produces a field that is the net electrostatic field in a dielectric is non-zero.
  2. The dipole moment per unit volume of a polarized dielectric is known as the polarization of a dielectric. It is denoted by the letter P for the linear isotropic dielectric.
    The polarisation of a linear isotropic dielectric, {tex}P=\chi_e E{/tex}
    Where {tex}\chi_e{/tex} is the electric susceptibility of the dielectric medium.

question_html: In a figure shows a network of five capacitors connected to a 100 V supply. Calculate the total charge and energy stored in the network.
question_text: In a figure shows a network of five capacitors connected to a 100 V supply. Calculate the total charge and energy stored in the network.
explanation: The circuit can be redrawn as shown figure. Let C 1 be the capacitance of the parallel combination of the two capacitors of 3 {tex}\mu{/tex} F each. Then, C 1 = 3 {tex}\mu{/tex} F + 3 {tex}\mu{/tex} F = 6 {tex}\mu{/tex} F Let C 2 be the capacitance of the parallel combination of the two capacitors of capacitance is given by {tex}C_{12}=\frac{C_{1} C_{2}}{C_{1}+C_{2}}=\frac{6 \times 3}{6+3}=2 \mu F{/tex} Finally, the equivalent capacitor C 12 (= 2 {tex}\mu{/tex} F) and the 2 {tex}\mu{/tex} F capacitor are in parallel. Hence, the net capacitance of the circuit, C 1 = 2 {tex}\mu{/tex} F + 2 {tex}\mu{/tex} F = 4 {tex}\mu{/tex} F = 4 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -6 F Therefore, total charge stored in the network of the capacitors, q = CV = 4 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -6 {tex}\times{/tex} 100 = 4 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -4 C Also, total energy stored, {tex}\mathrm{U}=\frac{1}{2} \mathrm{CV}^{2}=\frac{1}{2} \times 4 \times 10^{-6} \times 100^{2}=0.02 \mathrm{J}{/tex}
solution: The circuit can be redrawn as shown figure. Let C 1 be the capacitance of the parallel combination of the two capacitors of 3 {tex}\mu{/tex} F each. Then, C 1 = 3 {tex}\mu{/tex} F + 3 {tex}\mu{/tex} F = 6 {tex}\mu{/tex} F Let C 2 be the capacitance of the parallel combination of the two capacitors of capacitance is given by {tex}C_{12}=\frac{C_{1} C_{2}}{C_{1}+C_{2}}=\frac{6 \times 3}{6+3}=2 \mu F{/tex} Finally, the equivalent capacitor C 12 (= 2 {tex}\mu{/tex} F) and the 2 {tex}\mu{/tex} F capacitor are in parallel. Hence, the net capacitance of the circuit, C 1 = 2 {tex}\mu{/tex} F + 2 {tex}\mu{/tex} F = 4 {tex}\mu{/tex} F = 4 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -6 F Therefore, total charge stored in the network of the capacitors, q = CV = 4 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -6 {tex}\times{/tex} 100 = 4 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -4 C Also, total energy stored, {tex}\mathrm{U}=\frac{1}{2} \mathrm{CV}^{2}=\frac{1}{2} \times 4 \times 10^{-6} \times 100^{2}=0.02 \mathrm{J}{/tex}
solution_html:
The circuit can be redrawn as shown figure.

Let C1 be the capacitance of the parallel combination of the two capacitors of 3 {tex}\mu{/tex}F each. Then,
C1 = 3 {tex}\mu{/tex}F + 3 {tex}\mu{/tex}F = 6 {tex}\mu{/tex}F
Let C2 be the capacitance of the parallel combination of the two capacitors of capacitance is given by
{tex}C_{12}=\frac{C_{1} C_{2}}{C_{1}+C_{2}}=\frac{6 \times 3}{6+3}=2 \mu F{/tex}
Finally, the equivalent capacitor C12 (= 2 {tex}\mu{/tex}F) and the 2 {tex}\mu{/tex}F capacitor are in parallel. Hence, the net capacitance of the circuit,
C1 = 2 {tex}\mu{/tex}F + 2 {tex}\mu{/tex}F = 4 {tex}\mu{/tex}F = 4 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-6 F
Therefore, total charge stored in the network of the capacitors,
q = CV = 4 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-6 {tex}\times{/tex} 100 = 4 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-4 C
Also, total energy stored,
{tex}\mathrm{U}=\frac{1}{2} \mathrm{CV}^{2}=\frac{1}{2} \times 4 \times 10^{-6} \times 100^{2}=0.02 \mathrm{J}{/tex}

question_html: A charge Q is distributed over the surfaces of two concentric hollow spheres of radii r and R (R >> r), such that their surface charge densities are equal. Derive the expression for the potential at the common centre.
question_text: A charge Q is distributed over the surfaces of two concentric hollow spheres of radii r and R (R >> r), such that their surface charge densities are equal. Derive the expression for the potential at the common centre.
explanation: Let Q r and Q R be the charges distributed over the smaller and the larger spheres, respectively. Then Q = Q r + Q R The surface charge densities will be {tex}\sigma=\frac{Q_{R}}{4 \pi R^{2}}=\frac{Q_{r}}{4 \pi r^{2}}{/tex} or {tex}\frac{Q_{r}}{Q_{R}}=\frac{r^{2}}{R^{2}}{/tex} or {tex}\frac{Q_{r}+Q_{R}}{Q_{R}}=\frac{r^{2}+R^{2}}{R^{2}}{/tex} or {tex}\frac{Q}{Q_{R}}=\frac{r^{2}+R^{2}}{R^{2}}{/tex} or {tex}Q_{R}=\left(\frac{R^{2}}{R^{2}+r^{2}}\right) Q{/tex} Similarly, {tex}Q_{r}=\left(\frac{r^{2}}{R^{2}+r^{2}}\right) Q{/tex} Potential due to the charge on the smaller sphere is {tex}V_{r}=\frac{Q_{r}}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0} r}=\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0} r} \cdot\left(\frac{r^{2}}{R^{2}+r^{2}}\right) Q=\frac{Q \cdot r}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}\left(R^{2}+r^{2}\right)}{/tex} Potential due to the charge on larger sphere is {tex}V_{R}=\frac{Q_{R}}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0} R}=\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0} R} \cdot\left(\frac{R^{2}}{R^{2}+r^{2}}\right) Q=\frac{Q \cdot R}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}\left(R^{2}+r^{2}\right)}{/tex} The total potential at the centre, {tex}V=V_{r}+V_{R}=\frac{Q(r+R)}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}\left(R^{2}+r^{2}\right)}{/tex}
solution: Let Q r and Q R be the charges distributed over the smaller and the larger spheres, respectively. Then Q = Q r + Q R The surface charge densities will be {tex}\sigma=\frac{Q_{R}}{4 \pi R^{2}}=\frac{Q_{r}}{4 \pi r^{2}}{/tex} or {tex}\frac{Q_{r}}{Q_{R}}=\frac{r^{2}}{R^{2}}{/tex} or {tex}\frac{Q_{r}+Q_{R}}{Q_{R}}=\frac{r^{2}+R^{2}}{R^{2}}{/tex} or {tex}\frac{Q}{Q_{R}}=\frac{r^{2}+R^{2}}{R^{2}}{/tex} or {tex}Q_{R}=\left(\frac{R^{2}}{R^{2}+r^{2}}\right) Q{/tex} Similarly, {tex}Q_{r}=\left(\frac{r^{2}}{R^{2}+r^{2}}\right) Q{/tex} Potential due to the charge on the smaller sphere is {tex}V_{r}=\frac{Q_{r}}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0} r}=\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0} r} \cdot\left(\frac{r^{2}}{R^{2}+r^{2}}\right) Q=\frac{Q \cdot r}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}\left(R^{2}+r^{2}\right)}{/tex} Potential due to the charge on larger sphere is {tex}V_{R}=\frac{Q_{R}}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0} R}=\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0} R} \cdot\left(\frac{R^{2}}{R^{2}+r^{2}}\right) Q=\frac{Q \cdot R}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}\left(R^{2}+r^{2}\right)}{/tex} The total potential at the centre, {tex}V=V_{r}+V_{R}=\frac{Q(r+R)}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}\left(R^{2}+r^{2}\right)}{/tex}
solution_html:
Let Qr and QR be the charges distributed over the smaller and the larger spheres, respectively.
Then Q = Qr + QR
The surface charge densities will be
{tex}\sigma=\frac{Q_{R}}{4 \pi R^{2}}=\frac{Q_{r}}{4 \pi r^{2}}{/tex}
or {tex}\frac{Q_{r}}{Q_{R}}=\frac{r^{2}}{R^{2}}{/tex} or {tex}\frac{Q_{r}+Q_{R}}{Q_{R}}=\frac{r^{2}+R^{2}}{R^{2}}{/tex}
or {tex}\frac{Q}{Q_{R}}=\frac{r^{2}+R^{2}}{R^{2}}{/tex} or {tex}Q_{R}=\left(\frac{R^{2}}{R^{2}+r^{2}}\right) Q{/tex}
Similarly, {tex}Q_{r}=\left(\frac{r^{2}}{R^{2}+r^{2}}\right) Q{/tex}
Potential due to the charge on the smaller sphere is
{tex}V_{r}=\frac{Q_{r}}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0} r}=\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0} r} \cdot\left(\frac{r^{2}}{R^{2}+r^{2}}\right) Q=\frac{Q \cdot r}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}\left(R^{2}+r^{2}\right)}{/tex}
Potential due to the charge on larger sphere is
{tex}V_{R}=\frac{Q_{R}}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0} R}=\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0} R} \cdot\left(\frac{R^{2}}{R^{2}+r^{2}}\right) Q=\frac{Q \cdot R}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}\left(R^{2}+r^{2}\right)}{/tex}
The total potential at the centre,
{tex}V=V_{r}+V_{R}=\frac{Q(r+R)}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}\left(R^{2}+r^{2}\right)}{/tex}

subject: Physics
question_html:
1. Describe briefly the process of transferring the charge between the two plates of a parallel plate capacitor when connected to a battery. Derive an expression for the energy stored in a capacitor.
  2. A parallel plate capacitor is charged by a battery to a potential difference V. It is disconnected from the battery and then connected to another uncharged capacitor of the same capacitance. Calculate the ratio of the energy stored in the combination to the initial energy on the single capacitor.
question_text: Describe briefly the process of transferring the charge between the two plates of a parallel plate capacitor when connected to a battery. Derive an expression for the energy stored in a capacitor. A parallel plate capacitor is charged by a battery to a potential difference V. It is disconnected from the battery and then connected to another uncharged capacitor of the same capacitance. Calculate the ratio of the energy stored in the combination to the initial energy on the single capacitor.
explanation: Consider a parallel plate capacitor which is connected across a battery. The electrons are transferred from the negative terminal of the battery to the metallic plate connected to the negative terminal and acquires a negative charge. Similarly, the electrons move from the second plate to the positive terminal of the battery and acquire a positive charge. This process continues until the potential difference between the two plates becomes equal to the potential difference between the terminals of the battery. Thus, the charge is developed on the capacitor. Let ‘dW’ be the work done by the battery in increasing the charge on the capacitor is given by, having the charge q and potential V is: dW = V dq where V = {tex}\frac{q}{C}{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} dW = {tex}\frac{q}{C}{/tex} dq Total work done in charging up the capacitor is given by, W = {tex}\int{/tex} dW = {tex}\int\limits_0^Q {\frac{q}{c}} {/tex} dq {tex}\therefore{/tex} W = {tex}\frac{Q^{2}}{2 C}{/tex} Hence total energy stored in the plates of the capacitor is given by, W = {tex}\frac{Q^{2}}{2 C}=\frac{1}{2} C V^{2}=\frac{1}{2} Q V{/tex} Charge on the plates of the capacitor is given by q = CV When uncharged capacitor of same capacitance is connected to the charged capacitor, sharing of charges takes place between the two capacitors till both the capacitors acquire same potential {tex}\frac{V}{2}{/tex} Energy stored in the combination of capacitors is given by, {tex}U_2{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} C( {tex}\frac{V}{2}{/tex} ) 2 + {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} C( {tex}\frac{V}{2}{/tex} ) 2 = {tex}\frac{CV^2}{4}{/tex} Energy stored by a single capacitor before connecting is given by, U 1 = {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} CV 2 Ratio of energy stored in the combination to that in the single capacitor is given by, {tex}\frac{\mathrm{U}_{2}}{\mathrm{U}_{1}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{\mathrm{CV}^{2} / 4}{C V^{2} / 2}{/tex} = 1 : 2 , Hence these are required results.
solution: Consider a parallel plate capacitor which is connected across a battery. The electrons are transferred from the negative terminal of the battery to the metallic plate connected to the negative terminal and acquires a negative charge. Similarly, the electrons move from the second plate to the positive terminal of the battery and acquire a positive charge. This process continues until the potential difference between the two plates becomes equal to the potential difference between the terminals of the battery. Thus, the charge is developed on the capacitor. Let ‘dW’ be the work done by the battery in increasing the charge on the capacitor is given by, having the charge q and potential V is: dW = V dq where V = {tex}\frac{q}{C}{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} dW = {tex}\frac{q}{C}{/tex} dq Total work done in charging up the capacitor is given by, W = {tex}\int{/tex} dW = {tex}\int\limits_0^Q {\frac{q}{c}} {/tex} dq {tex}\therefore{/tex} W = {tex}\frac{Q^{2}}{2 C}{/tex} Hence total energy stored in the plates of the capacitor is given by, W = {tex}\frac{Q^{2}}{2 C}=\frac{1}{2} C V^{2}=\frac{1}{2} Q V{/tex} Charge on the plates of the capacitor is given by q = CV When uncharged capacitor of same capacitance is connected to the charged capacitor, sharing of charges takes place between the two capacitors till both the capacitors acquire same potential {tex}\frac{V}{2}{/tex} Energy stored in the combination of capacitors is given by, {tex}U_2{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} C( {tex}\frac{V}{2}{/tex} ) 2 + {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} C( {tex}\frac{V}{2}{/tex} ) 2 = {tex}\frac{CV^2}{4}{/tex} Energy stored by a single capacitor before connecting is given by, U 1 = {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} CV 2 Ratio of energy stored in the combination to that in the single capacitor is given by, {tex}\frac{\mathrm{U}_{2}}{\mathrm{U}_{1}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{\mathrm{CV}^{2} / 4}{C V^{2} / 2}{/tex} = 1 : 2 , Hence these are required results.
solution_html:
1. Consider a parallel plate capacitor which is connected across a battery. The electrons are transferred from the negative terminal of the battery to the metallic plate connected to the negative terminal and acquires a negative charge. Similarly, the electrons move from the second plate to the positive terminal of the battery and acquire a positive charge. This process continues until the potential difference between the two plates becomes equal to the potential difference between the terminals of the battery. Thus, the charge is developed on the capacitor.
    Let ‘dW’ be the work done by the battery in increasing the charge on the capacitor is given by, having the charge q and potential V is:
    dW = V dq
    where V = {tex}\frac{q}{C}{/tex}
    {tex}\therefore{/tex}  dW = {tex}\frac{q}{C}{/tex}dq
    Total work done in charging up the capacitor is given by,
    W = {tex}\int{/tex} dW = {tex}\int\limits_0^Q {\frac{q}{c}} {/tex}dq
    {tex}\therefore{/tex} W = {tex}\frac{Q^{2}}{2 C}{/tex}
    Hence total energy stored in the plates of the capacitor is given by, W = {tex}\frac{Q^{2}}{2 C}=\frac{1}{2} C V^{2}=\frac{1}{2} Q V{/tex}
  2. Charge on the plates of the capacitor is given by q = CV
    When uncharged capacitor of same capacitance is connected to the charged capacitor, sharing of charges takes place between the two capacitors till both the capacitors acquire same potential {tex}\frac{V}{2}{/tex}
    Energy stored in the combination of capacitors is given by,
    {tex}U_2{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex}C({tex}\frac{V}{2}{/tex})2 + {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex}C({tex}\frac{V}{2}{/tex})2 = {tex}\frac{CV^2}{4}{/tex}
    Energy stored by a single capacitor before connecting is given by, U1 = {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex}CV2 
    Ratio of energy stored in the combination to that in the single capacitor is given by,
    {tex}\frac{\mathrm{U}_{2}}{\mathrm{U}_{1}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{\mathrm{CV}^{2} / 4}{C V^{2} / 2}{/tex} = 1 : 2  , Hence these are required results.

question_html: A particle, having a charge +5 {tex}\mu{/tex}C, is initially at rest at the point x = 30 cm on the x-axis. The particle begins to move due to the presence of a charge Q that is kept fixed at the origin. Find the kinetic energy of the particle at the instant it has moved 15 cm from its initial position if Q = +15 {tex}\mu{/tex}C and  Q = -15 {tex}\mu{/tex}C.
question_text: A particle, having a charge +5 {tex}\mu{/tex} C, is initially at rest at the point x = 30 cm on the x-axis. The particle begins to move due to the presence of a charge Q that is kept fixed at the origin. Find the kinetic energy of the particle at the instant it has moved 15 cm from its initial position if Q = +15 {tex}\mu{/tex} C and Q = -15 {tex}\mu{/tex} C.
explanation: From energy conservation, U i + K i = U f + K f (K f is the final kinetic energy ,K i is the initial kinetic energy similarly for the potential energy) When Q is +15 {tex}\mu{/tex} C, q will move 15 cm away from it. Hence r f = 45 cm K f =E-E'= 9 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 9 {tex}\times{/tex} 15 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -6 {tex}\times{/tex} 5 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -6 {tex}\times{/tex} ( {tex}\frac 1{30\times10^{-3}} {/tex} ) - {tex}\frac{1}{45\times10^{-4}}{/tex} ] = 0.75 J When Q is - 15 {tex}\mu{/tex} C, q will move 15 cm towards it. Hence r f = 15 cm K f = 9 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 9 {tex}\times{/tex} (-15 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -6 ) {tex}\times{/tex} 5 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -6 [( {tex}\frac 1{30} \times{/tex} 10 -2 ) - {tex}\frac{1}{15\times10^{-2}}{/tex} ] = 2.25 J
solution: From energy conservation, U i + K i = U f + K f (K f is the final kinetic energy ,K i is the initial kinetic energy similarly for the potential energy) When Q is +15 {tex}\mu{/tex} C, q will move 15 cm away from it. Hence r f = 45 cm K f =E-E'= 9 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 9 {tex}\times{/tex} 15 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -6 {tex}\times{/tex} 5 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -6 {tex}\times{/tex} ( {tex}\frac 1{30\times10^{-3}} {/tex} ) - {tex}\frac{1}{45\times10^{-4}}{/tex} ] = 0.75 J When Q is - 15 {tex}\mu{/tex} C, q will move 15 cm towards it. Hence r f = 15 cm K f = 9 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 9 {tex}\times{/tex} (-15 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -6 ) {tex}\times{/tex} 5 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -6 [( {tex}\frac 1{30} \times{/tex} 10 -2 ) - {tex}\frac{1}{15\times10^{-2}}{/tex} ] = 2.25 J
solution_html:
From energy conservation,
Ui + Ki = Uf + Kf  (Kf  is the final kinetic energy ,Ki is the initial kinetic energy similarly for the potential energy)
When Q is +15 {tex}\mu{/tex}C, q will move 15 cm away from it. Hence rf = 45 cm
Kf =E-E'=  9 {tex}\times{/tex} 109 {tex}\times{/tex} 15 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-6 {tex}\times{/tex} 5 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-6 {tex}\times{/tex} ({tex}\frac 1{30\times10^{-3}} {/tex}) - {tex}\frac{1}{45\times10^{-4}}{/tex}]
= 0.75 J
When Q is - 15 {tex}\mu{/tex}C, q will move 15 cm towards it.
Hence rf = 15 cm
Kf = 9 {tex}\times{/tex} 109 {tex}\times{/tex} (-15 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-6) {tex}\times{/tex} 5 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-6 [({tex}\frac 1{30} \times{/tex} 10-2) - {tex}\frac{1}{15\times10^{-2}}{/tex}]
= 2.25 J

subject: Physics
question_html:
Three concentric metallic shells A, B and C of radii a, b and c (a < b < c) have surface charge densities +{tex}\sigma,-\sigma{/tex} and +{tex}\sigma{/tex} respectively as shown.

  1. Obtain the expressions for the potential of three shells A, B and C.
  2. If shells A and C are at the same potential, obtain the relation between a, b and c.
question_text: Three concentric metallic shells A, B and C of radii a, b and c (a < b < c) have surface charge densities + {tex}\sigma,-\sigma{/tex} and + {tex}\sigma{/tex} respectively as shown. Obtain the expressions for the potential of three shells A, B and C. If shells A and C are at the same potential, obtain the relation between a, b and c.
explanation: Charges on the three shells are {tex}q_{A}=4 \pi a^{2} \sigma, q_{B}=-4 \pi b^{2} \sigma, q_{C}=4 \pi c^{2} \sigma{/tex} The potential at every point inside a spherical shell is constant and is equal to that on its surface. Potential of shell A. Any point on the surface of shell A lies inside the shells B and C. {tex}\therefore V_{A}=\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}}\left[\frac{q_{A}}{a}+\frac{q_{B}}{b}+\frac{q_{C}}{c}\right]{/tex} {tex}=\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}}\left[\frac{4 \pi a^{2} \sigma}{a}-\frac{4 \pi b^{2} \sigma}{b}+\frac{4 \pi c^{2} \sigma}{c}\right]{/tex} or {tex}V_{A}=\frac{\sigma}{\varepsilon_{0}}(a-b+c){/tex} Potential of shell B. Any point on shell B lies outside the shell A and inside the shell C. {tex}\therefore V_{B}=\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}}\left[\frac{q_{A}}{b}+\frac{q_{B}}{b}+\frac{q_{C}}{c}\right]{/tex} {tex}=\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}}\left[\frac{4 \pi a^{2} \sigma}{b}-\frac{4 \pi b^{2} \sigma}{b}+\frac{4 \pi c^{2} \sigma}{c}\right]{/tex} or {tex}V_{B}=\frac{\sigma}{\varepsilon_{0}}\left[\frac{a^{2}}{b}-b+c\right]{/tex} Potential of shell C. Any point on shell C lies outside the shells A and B. {tex}\therefore V_{C}=\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}}\left[\frac{q_{A}}{c}+\frac{q_{B}}{c}+\frac{q_{C}}{c}\right]{/tex} {tex}=\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}}\left[\frac{4 \pi a^{2} \sigma}{c}-\frac{4 \pi b^{2} \sigma}{c}+\frac{4 \pi c^{2} \sigma}{c}\right]{/tex} or {tex}V_{\mathrm{C}}=\frac{\sigma}{\varepsilon_{0}}\left[\frac{a^{2}}{c}-\frac{b^{2}}{c}+c\right]{/tex} If the shells A and C are at the same potential then V A = V C {tex}\therefore \frac{\sigma}{\varepsilon_{0}}(a-b+c)=\frac{\sigma}{\varepsilon_{0}}\left(\frac{a^{2}}{c}-\frac{b^{2}}{c}+c\right){/tex} or {tex}a-b=\frac{(a-b)(a+b)}{c}{/tex} or a + b = c
solution: Charges on the three shells are {tex}q_{A}=4 \pi a^{2} \sigma, q_{B}=-4 \pi b^{2} \sigma, q_{C}=4 \pi c^{2} \sigma{/tex} The potential at every point inside a spherical shell is constant and is equal to that on its surface. Potential of shell A. Any point on the surface of shell A lies inside the shells B and C. {tex}\therefore V_{A}=\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}}\left[\frac{q_{A}}{a}+\frac{q_{B}}{b}+\frac{q_{C}}{c}\right]{/tex} {tex}=\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}}\left[\frac{4 \pi a^{2} \sigma}{a}-\frac{4 \pi b^{2} \sigma}{b}+\frac{4 \pi c^{2} \sigma}{c}\right]{/tex} or {tex}V_{A}=\frac{\sigma}{\varepsilon_{0}}(a-b+c){/tex} Potential of shell B. Any point on shell B lies outside the shell A and inside the shell C. {tex}\therefore V_{B}=\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}}\left[\frac{q_{A}}{b}+\frac{q_{B}}{b}+\frac{q_{C}}{c}\right]{/tex} {tex}=\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}}\left[\frac{4 \pi a^{2} \sigma}{b}-\frac{4 \pi b^{2} \sigma}{b}+\frac{4 \pi c^{2} \sigma}{c}\right]{/tex} or {tex}V_{B}=\frac{\sigma}{\varepsilon_{0}}\left[\frac{a^{2}}{b}-b+c\right]{/tex} Potential of shell C. Any point on shell C lies outside the shells A and B. {tex}\therefore V_{C}=\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}}\left[\frac{q_{A}}{c}+\frac{q_{B}}{c}+\frac{q_{C}}{c}\right]{/tex} {tex}=\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}}\left[\frac{4 \pi a^{2} \sigma}{c}-\frac{4 \pi b^{2} \sigma}{c}+\frac{4 \pi c^{2} \sigma}{c}\right]{/tex} or {tex}V_{\mathrm{C}}=\frac{\sigma}{\varepsilon_{0}}\left[\frac{a^{2}}{c}-\frac{b^{2}}{c}+c\right]{/tex} If the shells A and C are at the same potential then V A = V C {tex}\therefore \frac{\sigma}{\varepsilon_{0}}(a-b+c)=\frac{\sigma}{\varepsilon_{0}}\left(\frac{a^{2}}{c}-\frac{b^{2}}{c}+c\right){/tex} or {tex}a-b=\frac{(a-b)(a+b)}{c}{/tex} or a + b = c
solution_html:
1. Charges on the three shells are
     {tex}q_{A}=4 \pi a^{2} \sigma, q_{B}=-4 \pi b^{2} \sigma, q_{C}=4 \pi c^{2} \sigma{/tex}
    The potential at every point inside a spherical shell is constant and is equal to that on its surface.
    Potential of shell A. Any point on the surface of shell A lies inside the shells B and C.
    {tex}\therefore V_{A}=\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}}\left[\frac{q_{A}}{a}+\frac{q_{B}}{b}+\frac{q_{C}}{c}\right]{/tex}
    {tex}=\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}}\left[\frac{4 \pi a^{2} \sigma}{a}-\frac{4 \pi b^{2} \sigma}{b}+\frac{4 \pi c^{2} \sigma}{c}\right]{/tex}
    or {tex}V_{A}=\frac{\sigma}{\varepsilon_{0}}(a-b+c){/tex}
    Potential of shell B. Any point on shell B lies outside the shell A and inside the shell C.
    {tex}\therefore V_{B}=\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}}\left[\frac{q_{A}}{b}+\frac{q_{B}}{b}+\frac{q_{C}}{c}\right]{/tex}
    {tex}=\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}}\left[\frac{4 \pi a^{2} \sigma}{b}-\frac{4 \pi b^{2} \sigma}{b}+\frac{4 \pi c^{2} \sigma}{c}\right]{/tex}
    or {tex}V_{B}=\frac{\sigma}{\varepsilon_{0}}\left[\frac{a^{2}}{b}-b+c\right]{/tex}
    Potential of shell C. Any point on shell C lies outside the shells A and B.
    {tex}\therefore V_{C}=\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}}\left[\frac{q_{A}}{c}+\frac{q_{B}}{c}+\frac{q_{C}}{c}\right]{/tex}
    {tex}=\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}}\left[\frac{4 \pi a^{2} \sigma}{c}-\frac{4 \pi b^{2} \sigma}{c}+\frac{4 \pi c^{2} \sigma}{c}\right]{/tex}
    or {tex}V_{\mathrm{C}}=\frac{\sigma}{\varepsilon_{0}}\left[\frac{a^{2}}{c}-\frac{b^{2}}{c}+c\right]{/tex}
  2. If the shells A and C are at the same potential then VA = VC
    {tex}\therefore \frac{\sigma}{\varepsilon_{0}}(a-b+c)=\frac{\sigma}{\varepsilon_{0}}\left(\frac{a^{2}}{c}-\frac{b^{2}}{c}+c\right){/tex}
    or {tex}a-b=\frac{(a-b)(a+b)}{c}{/tex}
    or a + b = c

question_html: The figure shows a network of five capacitors connected to a 10 V battery. Calculate the charge acquired by the 5 {tex}\mu{/tex}F capacitor.
question_text: The figure shows a network of five capacitors connected to a 10 V battery. Calculate the charge acquired by the 5 {tex}\mu{/tex} F capacitor.
explanation: Net capacitance of parallel C 1 & C 2 C 12 = 15 + 5 = 20 {tex}\mu{/tex} F Net capacitance of parallel C 4 & C 5 C 45 = 10 + 10 = 20 {tex}\mu{/tex} F C 12 , C 45 in series C 1245 = {tex}\frac{20 \times 20}{20+20}=10 \mu \mathrm{F}{/tex} C 3 in parallel with C 1245 = 10 + 20 = 30 {tex}\mu{/tex} F P.D. across C 1245 10 V P.D. across C 12 = C 45 = 5 V Charge on 5 {tex}\mu{/tex} F Q = CV = 5 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -6 {tex}\times{/tex} 5 = 25 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -6 C
solution: Net capacitance of parallel C 1 & C 2 C 12 = 15 + 5 = 20 {tex}\mu{/tex} F Net capacitance of parallel C 4 & C 5 C 45 = 10 + 10 = 20 {tex}\mu{/tex} F C 12 , C 45 in series C 1245 = {tex}\frac{20 \times 20}{20+20}=10 \mu \mathrm{F}{/tex} C 3 in parallel with C 1245 = 10 + 20 = 30 {tex}\mu{/tex} F P.D. across C 1245 10 V P.D. across C 12 = C 45 = 5 V Charge on 5 {tex}\mu{/tex} F Q = CV = 5 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -6 {tex}\times{/tex} 5 = 25 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -6 C
solution_html:
Net capacitance of parallel C1 & C2
C12 = 15 + 5 = 20{tex}\mu{/tex}F
Net capacitance of parallel C4 & C5
C45 = 10 + 10 = 20{tex}\mu{/tex}F
C12, C45 in series C1245 = {tex}\frac{20 \times 20}{20+20}=10 \mu \mathrm{F}{/tex}
C3 in parallel with C1245 = 10 + 20 = 30{tex}\mu{/tex}F
P.D. across C1245 10 V
P.D. across C12 = C45 = 5 V
Charge on 5{tex}\mu{/tex}F Q = CV
= 5 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-6 {tex}\times{/tex} 5
= 25 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-6 C

question_html:
1. Derive an expression for the potential energy of an electric dipole in a uniform electric field. Explain conditions for stable and unstable equilibrium.
  2. Is the electrostatic potential necessarily zero at a point where the electric field is zero? Give an example to support your answer.
question_text: Derive an expression for the potential energy of an electric dipole in a uniform electric field. Explain conditions for stable and unstable equilibrium. Is the electrostatic potential necessarily zero at a point where the electric field is zero? Give an example to support your answer.
explanation: Since torque acting on dipole {tex}\vec{\tau}=\vec{\mathrm{p}} \times \vec{\mathrm{E}}{/tex} {tex}\vec{\tau}=\mathrm{pE} \sin \theta \cdot \hat{\mathrm{n}}{/tex} work done {tex}\mathrm{d} \omega=\tau . \mathrm{d} \theta{/tex} {tex}=\mathrm{pE} \sin \theta \mathrm{d} \theta{/tex} {tex}\mathrm{w}=\int\limits_{\theta_1}^{\theta_2} \mathrm{dw} \quad\mathrm{pE} \int\limits_{\theta_1}^{\theta_2} \sin \theta \mathrm{d} \theta{/tex} {tex}\mathrm{w}=\mathrm{pE}[-\cos \theta]{ }_{\theta_1}^{\theta_2}{/tex} {tex}=\mathrm{pE}\left[\cos \theta_1-\cos \theta_2\right]{/tex} if {tex}\theta_1=0, \theta_2=\theta{/tex} w = pE (1 - cos {tex}\theta{/tex} ) Conditions- For stable equilibrium - When electric dipole is parallel to electric field. For unstable equilibrium - Anti Parallel to electric field. No. Inside equipotential surface
solution: Since torque acting on dipole {tex}\vec{\tau}=\vec{\mathrm{p}} \times \vec{\mathrm{E}}{/tex} {tex}\vec{\tau}=\mathrm{pE} \sin \theta \cdot \hat{\mathrm{n}}{/tex} work done {tex}\mathrm{d} \omega=\tau . \mathrm{d} \theta{/tex} {tex}=\mathrm{pE} \sin \theta \mathrm{d} \theta{/tex} {tex}\mathrm{w}=\int\limits_{\theta_1}^{\theta_2} \mathrm{dw} \quad\mathrm{pE} \int\limits_{\theta_1}^{\theta_2} \sin \theta \mathrm{d} \theta{/tex} {tex}\mathrm{w}=\mathrm{pE}[-\cos \theta]{ }_{\theta_1}^{\theta_2}{/tex} {tex}=\mathrm{pE}\left[\cos \theta_1-\cos \theta_2\right]{/tex} if {tex}\theta_1=0, \theta_2=\theta{/tex} w = pE (1 - cos {tex}\theta{/tex} ) Conditions- For stable equilibrium - When electric dipole is parallel to electric field. For unstable equilibrium - Anti Parallel to electric field. No. Inside equipotential surface
solution_html:
1. Since torque acting on dipole
    {tex}\vec{\tau}=\vec{\mathrm{p}} \times \vec{\mathrm{E}}{/tex}
    {tex}\vec{\tau}=\mathrm{pE} \sin \theta \cdot \hat{\mathrm{n}}{/tex}
    work done {tex}\mathrm{d} \omega=\tau . \mathrm{d} \theta{/tex}
    {tex}=\mathrm{pE} \sin \theta \mathrm{d} \theta{/tex}
    {tex}\mathrm{w}=\int\limits_{\theta_1}^{\theta_2} \mathrm{dw} \quad\mathrm{pE} \int\limits_{\theta_1}^{\theta_2} \sin \theta \mathrm{d} \theta{/tex}
    {tex}\mathrm{w}=\mathrm{pE}[-\cos \theta]{ }_{\theta_1}^{\theta_2}{/tex}
    {tex}=\mathrm{pE}\left[\cos \theta_1-\cos \theta_2\right]{/tex}
    if {tex}\theta_1=0, \theta_2=\theta{/tex}
    w = pE (1 - cos{tex}\theta{/tex})
    Conditions-
    For stable equilibrium - When electric dipole is parallel to electric field.
    For unstable equilibrium - Anti Parallel to electric field.
  2. No.
    Inside equipotential surface

question_html: Why is the electrostatic potential inside a charged conducting shell constant throughout the volume of the conductor?
question_text: Why is the electrostatic potential inside a charged conducting shell constant throughout the volume of the conductor?
explanation: E = 0 inside the conductor & has no tangential component on the surface. {tex}\therefore{/tex} No work is done in moving charge inside or on the surface of the conductor. & Potential is constant.
solution: E = 0 inside the conductor & has no tangential component on the surface. {tex}\therefore{/tex} No work is done in moving charge inside or on the surface of the conductor. & Potential is constant.
solution_html:
E = 0 inside the conductor & has no tangential component on the surface.
{tex}\therefore{/tex} No work is done in moving charge inside or on the surface of the conductor. & Potential is constant.

question_html:
1. Draw the equipotential surfaces due to an electric dipole.
  2. Derive an expression for the electric field due to a dipole of dipole moment {tex}\vec{p}{/tex} at a point on its perpendicular bisector.
question_text: Draw the equipotential surfaces due to an electric dipole. Derive an expression for the electric field due to a dipole of dipole moment {tex}\vec{p}{/tex} at a point on its perpendicular bisector.
explanation: The magnitude of the electric fields due to the two charges +q and -q are {tex}E_{+q}=\frac{1}{4 \pi \epsilon_0} \frac{q}{\left(r^2+a^2\right)}{/tex} {tex}E_{-q}=\frac{1}{4 \pi \epsilon_0} \frac{q}{\left(r^2+a^2\right)}{/tex} The components normal to the dipole axis cancel away and the components along the dipole axis add up Hence total Electric field = {tex}-\left(E_{+q}+E_{-q}\right) \cos\theta \ \vec{p}{/tex} {tex}E=-\frac{2 q a}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0\left(r^2+a^2\right)^{\frac{3} 2}} \vec{p}{/tex}
solution: The magnitude of the electric fields due to the two charges +q and -q are {tex}E_{+q}=\frac{1}{4 \pi \epsilon_0} \frac{q}{\left(r^2+a^2\right)}{/tex} {tex}E_{-q}=\frac{1}{4 \pi \epsilon_0} \frac{q}{\left(r^2+a^2\right)}{/tex} The components normal to the dipole axis cancel away and the components along the dipole axis add up Hence total Electric field = {tex}-\left(E_{+q}+E_{-q}\right) \cos\theta \ \vec{p}{/tex} {tex}E=-\frac{2 q a}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0\left(r^2+a^2\right)^{\frac{3} 2}} \vec{p}{/tex}
solution_html:
2. The magnitude of the electric fields due to the two charges +q and -q are
    {tex}E_{+q}=\frac{1}{4 \pi \epsilon_0} \frac{q}{\left(r^2+a^2\right)}{/tex}
    {tex}E_{-q}=\frac{1}{4 \pi \epsilon_0} \frac{q}{\left(r^2+a^2\right)}{/tex}
    The components normal to the dipole axis cancel away and the components along the dipole axis add up
    Hence total Electric field = {tex}-\left(E_{+q}+E_{-q}\right) \cos\theta \ \vec{p}{/tex}
    {tex}E=-\frac{2 q a}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0\left(r^2+a^2\right)^{\frac{3} 2}} \vec{p}{/tex}

question_html: Why can the interior of a conductor have no excess charge in the static situation?
question_text: Why can the interior of a conductor have no excess charge in the static situation?
explanation: Take any arbitrary closed surface S inside the conductor. Since electric field inside the conductor is zero, electric flux through S is also zero. Hence by Gauss’s law there is no net charge enclosed by S (Also accept: Electric field inside the conductor is zero)
solution: Take any arbitrary closed surface S inside the conductor. Since electric field inside the conductor is zero, electric flux through S is also zero. Hence by Gauss’s law there is no net charge enclosed by S (Also accept: Electric field inside the conductor is zero)

question_html: The figure shows a network of three capacitors C1 = 2 {tex}\mu{/tex}F; C2 = 6 {tex}\mu{/tex}F and C3 = 3 {tex}\mu{/tex}F connected across a battery of 10 V. If a charge of 6 {tex}\mu{/tex}C is acquired by the capacitor C3, calculate the charge acquired by C1.
question_text: The figure shows a network of three capacitors C 1 = 2 {tex}\mu{/tex} F; C 2 = 6 {tex}\mu{/tex} F and C 3 = 3 {tex}\mu{/tex} F connected across a battery of 10 V. If a charge of 6 {tex}\mu{/tex} C is acquired by the capacitor C 3 , calculate the charge acquired by C 1 .
explanation: {tex}\mathrm{C}_{23}=(6+3) \mu \mathrm{F}=9 \mu \mathrm{F}{/tex} {tex}\mathrm{C}_1=\frac{q}{V_1} \Rightarrow \mathrm{V}_1=\frac{q}{2 \mu F}{/tex} {tex}C_{23}=\frac{q}{V_2} \Rightarrow V_2=\frac{q}{9 \mu F}{/tex} V = V 1 + V 2 {tex}\Rightarrow 10=q\left(\frac{1}{2 \mu F}+\frac{1}{9 \mu F}\right){/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow q=\frac{10 \times 18}{11} \mu C=16.4 \mu C{/tex}
solution: {tex}\mathrm{C}_{23}=(6+3) \mu \mathrm{F}=9 \mu \mathrm{F}{/tex} {tex}\mathrm{C}_1=\frac{q}{V_1} \Rightarrow \mathrm{V}_1=\frac{q}{2 \mu F}{/tex} {tex}C_{23}=\frac{q}{V_2} \Rightarrow V_2=\frac{q}{9 \mu F}{/tex} V = V 1 + V 2 {tex}\Rightarrow 10=q\left(\frac{1}{2 \mu F}+\frac{1}{9 \mu F}\right){/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow q=\frac{10 \times 18}{11} \mu C=16.4 \mu C{/tex}
solution_html:
{tex}\mathrm{C}_{23}=(6+3) \mu \mathrm{F}=9 \mu \mathrm{F}{/tex}
{tex}\mathrm{C}_1=\frac{q}{V_1} \Rightarrow \mathrm{V}_1=\frac{q}{2 \mu F}{/tex}
{tex}C_{23}=\frac{q}{V_2} \Rightarrow V_2=\frac{q}{9 \mu F}{/tex}
V = V1 + V2
{tex}\Rightarrow 10=q\left(\frac{1}{2 \mu F}+\frac{1}{9 \mu F}\right){/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow q=\frac{10 \times 18}{11} \mu C=16.4 \mu C{/tex}

question_html: A 100 {tex}\mu{/tex}F parallel plate capacitor having plate separation of 4 mm is charged by 200 V dc. The source is now disconnected. When the distance between the plates is doubled and a dielectric slab of thickness 4 mm and dielectric constant 5 is introduced between the plates, how will (i) its capacitance, (ii) the electric field between the plates, and (iii) energy density of the capacitor get affected? Justify your answer in each case.
question_text: A 100 {tex}\mu{/tex} F parallel plate capacitor having plate separation of 4 mm is charged by 200 V dc. The source is now disconnected. When the distance between the plates is doubled and a dielectric slab of thickness 4 mm and dielectric constant 5 is introduced between the plates, how will (i) its capacitance, (ii) the electric field between the plates, and (iii) energy density of the capacitor get affected? Justify your answer in each case.
explanation: Dielectric slab of thickness {tex}4 \mathrm{~mm}=\frac{4}{5} \mathrm{~mm}=0.8 \mathrm{~mm}{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} Effective (air)plate separation = (4 + 0.8)mm = 4.8 mm Effective new capacitance {tex}C^{\prime}=\frac{\epsilon_0 A}{4.8 \mathrm{~mm}}=\frac{4}{4.8} C{/tex} = 83.33 {tex}\mu{/tex} F The charge remains the same. Therefore new voltage {tex}V^{\prime}=\frac{Q}{C^{\prime}}=\frac{100 \times 10^{-6} \times 200}{83.33 \times 10^{-6}}=240 \mathrm{~V}{/tex} Electric field {tex}E^{\prime}=\frac{V^{\prime}}{24}{/tex} = 30N/C Energy density Energy = {tex}\frac12{/tex} CV 2 As the electric field has decreased the energy density also decreases since the energy density {tex}U=\frac{1}{2} \epsilon_0 E^2{/tex}
solution: Dielectric slab of thickness {tex}4 \mathrm{~mm}=\frac{4}{5} \mathrm{~mm}=0.8 \mathrm{~mm}{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} Effective (air)plate separation = (4 + 0.8)mm = 4.8 mm Effective new capacitance {tex}C^{\prime}=\frac{\epsilon_0 A}{4.8 \mathrm{~mm}}=\frac{4}{4.8} C{/tex} = 83.33 {tex}\mu{/tex} F The charge remains the same. Therefore new voltage {tex}V^{\prime}=\frac{Q}{C^{\prime}}=\frac{100 \times 10^{-6} \times 200}{83.33 \times 10^{-6}}=240 \mathrm{~V}{/tex} Electric field {tex}E^{\prime}=\frac{V^{\prime}}{24}{/tex} = 30N/C Energy density Energy = {tex}\frac12{/tex} CV 2 As the electric field has decreased the energy density also decreases since the energy density {tex}U=\frac{1}{2} \epsilon_0 E^2{/tex}
solution_html:
Dielectric slab of thickness {tex}4 \mathrm{~mm}=\frac{4}{5} \mathrm{~mm}=0.8 \mathrm{~mm}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Effective (air)plate separation
= (4 + 0.8)mm
= 4.8 mm

  1. Effective new capacitance
    {tex}C^{\prime}=\frac{\epsilon_0 A}{4.8 \mathrm{~mm}}=\frac{4}{4.8} C{/tex}
    = 83.33 {tex}\mu{/tex}F
  2. The charge remains the same. Therefore new voltage
    {tex}V^{\prime}=\frac{Q}{C^{\prime}}=\frac{100 \times 10^{-6} \times 200}{83.33 \times 10^{-6}}=240 \mathrm{~V}{/tex}
    Electric field
    {tex}E^{\prime}=\frac{V^{\prime}}{24}{/tex} = 30N/C
  3. Energy density
    Energy = {tex}\frac12{/tex}CV2
    As the electric field has decreased the energy density also decreases since the energy density
    {tex}U=\frac{1}{2} \epsilon_0 E^2{/tex}

question_html:
In a network, four capacitors C1, C2, C3 and C4 are connected as shown in the figure.

  1. Calculate the net capacitance in the circuit.
  2. If the charge on the capacitor C1 is 6 {tex}\mu{/tex}C, (i) calculate the charge on the capacitors C3 and C4, and (ii) net energy stored in the capacitors C3 and C4 connected in series.
question_text: In a network, four capacitors C 1 , C 2 , C 3 and C 4 are connected as shown in the figure. Calculate the net capacitance in the circuit. If the charge on the capacitor C 1 is 6 {tex}\mu{/tex} C, (i) calculate the charge on the capacitors C 3 and C 4 , and (ii) net energy stored in the capacitors C 3 and C 4 connected in series.
explanation: Capacitance across C 3 & C 4 {tex}C_{3,4}=\frac{12 \times 4}{16}=3 \mu F{/tex} Capacitance across C 2 & C 1 C 12 = 6 + 3 = 9 {tex}\mu{/tex} F Equivalent capacitance {tex}C_{e q}=\frac{9 \times 3}{12}=\frac{9 \mu F}{4}{/tex} The total charge flowing through the circuit {tex}C_{n e t} V=\frac{9}{4} \times 8=18 \mu C{/tex} As C 3 & C 4 are in series they carry a charge of 18C each Q = 18 {tex}\mu{/tex} C C 43 = 3 {tex}\mu{/tex} F {tex}E_{3,4}=\frac{1}{2} \frac{Q^2}{C_{34}}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{1}{2} \times \frac{\left(18 \times 10^{-6}\right)^2}{3 \times 10^{-6}}{/tex} E 3, 4 = 54 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -6 Joule
solution: Capacitance across C 3 & C 4 {tex}C_{3,4}=\frac{12 \times 4}{16}=3 \mu F{/tex} Capacitance across C 2 & C 1 C 12 = 6 + 3 = 9 {tex}\mu{/tex} F Equivalent capacitance {tex}C_{e q}=\frac{9 \times 3}{12}=\frac{9 \mu F}{4}{/tex} The total charge flowing through the circuit {tex}C_{n e t} V=\frac{9}{4} \times 8=18 \mu C{/tex} As C 3 & C 4 are in series they carry a charge of 18C each Q = 18 {tex}\mu{/tex} C C 43 = 3 {tex}\mu{/tex} F {tex}E_{3,4}=\frac{1}{2} \frac{Q^2}{C_{34}}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{1}{2} \times \frac{\left(18 \times 10^{-6}\right)^2}{3 \times 10^{-6}}{/tex} E 3, 4 = 54 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -6 Joule
solution_html:
1. Capacitance across C3 & C4
    {tex}C_{3,4}=\frac{12 \times 4}{16}=3 \mu F{/tex}
    Capacitance across C2 & C1
    C12 = 6 + 3 = 9 {tex}\mu{/tex}F
    Equivalent capacitance
    {tex}C_{e q}=\frac{9 \times 3}{12}=\frac{9 \mu F}{4}{/tex}
    1. The total charge flowing through the circuit
      {tex}C_{n e t} V=\frac{9}{4} \times 8=18 \mu C{/tex}
      As C3 & C4 are in series they carry a charge of 18C each
    2. Q = 18{tex}\mu{/tex}C
      C43 = 3{tex}\mu{/tex}F
      {tex}E_{3,4}=\frac{1}{2} \frac{Q^2}{C_{34}}{/tex}
      {tex}=\frac{1}{2} \times \frac{\left(18 \times 10^{-6}\right)^2}{3 \times 10^{-6}}{/tex}
      E3, 4 = 54 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-6 Joule

question_html: Two identical capacitors of 10 pF each are connected in turn (i) in series, and (ii) in parallel across a 20 V battery. Calculate the potential difference across each capacitor in the first case and charge acquired by each capacitor in the second case.
question_text: Two identical capacitors of 10 pF each are connected in turn (i) in series, and (ii) in parallel across a 20 V battery. Calculate the potential difference across each capacitor in the first case and charge acquired by each capacitor in the second case.
explanation: given, capacitance of capacitor = 10pF, Voltage V = 20 V Arrangement is In series connection charges are the same Q = Q 1 = Q 2 we know, Q = CV V = {tex}\frac{Q}{C}{/tex} Here, capacitance is equal so the potential difference across each capacitor is equal which is 10V In parallel connection potential differences across the capacitor are the same. That is, V = V 1 = V 2 = 20 V Therefore, Q = CV = 10pF {tex}\times{/tex} 20 V = 200pC The Charge acquired by each capacitor is 200 pC.
solution: given, capacitance of capacitor = 10pF, Voltage V = 20 V Arrangement is In series connection charges are the same Q = Q 1 = Q 2 we know, Q = CV V = {tex}\frac{Q}{C}{/tex} Here, capacitance is equal so the potential difference across each capacitor is equal which is 10V In parallel connection potential differences across the capacitor are the same. That is, V = V 1 = V 2 = 20 V Therefore, Q = CV = 10pF {tex}\times{/tex} 20 V = 200pC The Charge acquired by each capacitor is 200 pC.
solution_html:
1. given, capacitance of capacitor = 10pF, Voltage V = 20 V
    Arrangement is

    In series connection charges are the same
    Q = Q1 = Q2
    we know, Q = CV
    V = {tex}\frac{Q}{C}{/tex}
    Here, capacitance is equal so the potential difference across each capacitor is equal which is 10V

  2. In parallel connection potential differences across the capacitor are the same.
    That is, V = V1 = V2 = 20 V
    Therefore, Q = CV
    = 10pF {tex}\times{/tex} 20 V = 200pC
    The Charge acquired by each capacitor is 200 pC.

question_text: Obtain the expression for potential energy of an electric dipole placed with its axis at an angle {tex}(\theta){/tex} to an external electric field {tex}(\vec{\mathrm{E}}){/tex} . What is the minimum value of the potential energy?
explanation: Torque {tex}\tau=p E \sin \theta{/tex} P.E. {tex}=W=\int_{\theta_0}^\theta p E \sin \theta d \theta{/tex} {tex}=-p E\left(\cos \theta-\cos \theta_0\right){/tex} {tex}=-p E \cos \theta \left(\right.{/tex} for {tex}\left.\theta_0=\pi / 2\right){/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} Minimum value of P.E. = - p E
solution: Torque {tex}\tau=p E \sin \theta{/tex} P.E. {tex}=W=\int_{\theta_0}^\theta p E \sin \theta d \theta{/tex} {tex}=-p E\left(\cos \theta-\cos \theta_0\right){/tex} {tex}=-p E \cos \theta \left(\right.{/tex} for {tex}\left.\theta_0=\pi / 2\right){/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} Minimum value of P.E. = - p E
solution_html:
Torque {tex}\tau=p E \sin \theta{/tex}
P.E. {tex}=W=\int_{\theta_0}^\theta p E \sin \theta d \theta{/tex}
{tex}=-p E\left(\cos \theta-\cos \theta_0\right){/tex}
{tex}=-p E \cos \theta \left(\right.{/tex} for {tex}\left.\theta_0=\pi / 2\right){/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Minimum value of P.E. = - p E

question_html: Derive an expression for the electric field at a point on the axis of an electric dipole of dipole moment {tex}\vec{p}{/tex}. Also write its expression when the distance r >> the length a of the dipole.
question_text: Derive an expression for the electric field at a point on the axis of an electric dipole of dipole moment {tex}\vec{p}{/tex} . Also write its expression when the distance r >> the length a of the dipole.
explanation: Let the point P be at distance r from the centre of the dipole on the side of the charge q, as shown in Fig. (a) Then {tex} {E}_{-q}=-\frac{q}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0(r+a)^2} \hat{{p}}{/tex} where p is the unit vector along the dipole axis (from {tex}-q{/tex} to {tex}q{/tex} ). Also {tex} {E}_{+q}=\frac{q}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0(r-a)^2} \hat{{p}}{/tex} The total field at {tex}P{/tex} is {tex} {E}={E}_{+q}+{E}_{-q}=\frac{q}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0}\left[\frac{1}{(r-a)^2}-\frac{1}{(r+a)^2}\right] \dot{{p}} {/tex} {tex} =\frac{q}{4 \pi \varepsilon_o} \frac{4 a r}{\left(r^2-a^2\right)^2} \hat{{p}}{/tex} For {tex}r \gg a{/tex} {tex} {E}=\frac{4 q a}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0 r^3} \hat{{p}} (r \gg a){/tex}
solution: Let the point P be at distance r from the centre of the dipole on the side of the charge q, as shown in Fig. (a) Then {tex} {E}_{-q}=-\frac{q}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0(r+a)^2} \hat{{p}}{/tex} where p is the unit vector along the dipole axis (from {tex}-q{/tex} to {tex}q{/tex} ). Also {tex} {E}_{+q}=\frac{q}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0(r-a)^2} \hat{{p}}{/tex} The total field at {tex}P{/tex} is {tex} {E}={E}_{+q}+{E}_{-q}=\frac{q}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0}\left[\frac{1}{(r-a)^2}-\frac{1}{(r+a)^2}\right] \dot{{p}} {/tex} {tex} =\frac{q}{4 \pi \varepsilon_o} \frac{4 a r}{\left(r^2-a^2\right)^2} \hat{{p}}{/tex} For {tex}r \gg a{/tex} {tex} {E}=\frac{4 q a}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0 r^3} \hat{{p}} (r \gg a){/tex}
solution_html:
Let the point P be at distance r from the centre of the dipole on the side of the charge q, as shown in Fig. (a)

Then
{tex} {E}_{-q}=-\frac{q}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0(r+a)^2} \hat{{p}}{/tex}
where p is the unit vector along the dipole axis (from {tex}-q{/tex} to {tex}q{/tex} ). Also
{tex} {E}_{+q}=\frac{q}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0(r-a)^2} \hat{{p}}{/tex}
The total field at {tex}P{/tex} is
{tex} {E}={E}_{+q}+{E}_{-q}=\frac{q}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0}\left[\frac{1}{(r-a)^2}-\frac{1}{(r+a)^2}\right] \dot{{p}} {/tex}
{tex} =\frac{q}{4 \pi \varepsilon_o} \frac{4 a r}{\left(r^2-a^2\right)^2} \hat{{p}}{/tex}
For {tex}r \gg a{/tex}
{tex} {E}=\frac{4 q a}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0 r^3} \hat{{p}} (r \gg a){/tex}

question_html:
A point charge Q is placed at point O as shown in the figure. Is the potential at point A, i.e., VA, greater, smaller or equal to potential, VB at point B, when Q is
 

  1. positive, and
  2. negative charge?
question_text: A point charge Q is placed at point O as shown in the figure. Is the potential at point A, i.e., V A , greater, smaller or equal to potential, V B at point B, when Q is positive, and negative charge?
explanation: V A > V B V A < V B Detailed Answer: Let r A is the distance of point A from point charge Q and r B is the distance of point B from point charge Q. Potential at point A : {tex}V\left(r_{A}\right)=\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}} \cdot \frac{Q}{r_{A}}{/tex} Potential at point B : {tex}V\left(r_{B}\right)=\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}} \cdot \frac{Q}{r_{B}}{/tex} Since r A < r B , so when : charge Q is positive; V A >V B so that v A -v B . >0 charge Q is negative; V A <V B
solution: V A > V B V A < V B Detailed Answer: Let r A is the distance of point A from point charge Q and r B is the distance of point B from point charge Q. Potential at point A : {tex}V\left(r_{A}\right)=\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}} \cdot \frac{Q}{r_{A}}{/tex} Potential at point B : {tex}V\left(r_{B}\right)=\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}} \cdot \frac{Q}{r_{B}}{/tex} Since r A < r B , so when : charge Q is positive; V A >V B so that v A -v B . >0 charge Q is negative; V A <V B
solution_html:
1. VA > VB
  2. VA < VB

Detailed Answer:
Let rA is the distance of point A from point charge Q
and rB is the distance of point B from point charge Q.

Potential at point A :
{tex}V\left(r_{A}\right)=\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}} \cdot \frac{Q}{r_{A}}{/tex}
Potential at point B :
{tex}V\left(r_{B}\right)=\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}} \cdot \frac{Q}{r_{B}}{/tex}
Since rA< rB, so when :
charge Q is positive; VA>VB so that vA-vB .>0
charge Q is negative; VA<VB

subject: Physics
question_html:
A capacitor of capacitance C1 is charged to a potential V1 while another capacitor of capacitance C2 is charged to a potential difference V2. The capacitors are now disconnected from their respective charging batteries and connected in parallel to each other.

  1. Find the total energy stored in the two capacitors before they are connected.
  2. Find the total energy stored in the parallel combination of two capacitors.
  3. Explain the reason for the difference of energy in parallel combination in comparison to the total energy before they are connected
question_text: A capacitor of capacitance C 1 is charged to a potential V 1 while another capacitor of capacitance C 2 is charged to a potential difference V 2 . The capacitors are now disconnected from their respective charging batteries and connected in parallel to each other. Find the total energy stored in the two capacitors before they are connected. Find the total energy stored in the parallel combination of two capacitors. Explain the reason for the difference of energy in parallel combination in comparison to the total energy before they are connected
explanation: Total energy stored in the two capacitors before they are connected, {tex}u_{i}=\frac{1}{2} C_{1} V_{1}^{2}+\frac{1}{2} C_{2} V_{2}^{2}{/tex} After the two capacitors are connected in parallel, the common potential is {tex}V=\frac{\text { Total charge }}{\text { Total capacitance }}=\frac{q_{1}+q_{2}}{C_{1}+C_{2}}=\frac{C_{1} V_{1}+C_{2} V_{2}}{C_{1}+C_{2}}{/tex} Total energy stored in the parallel combination, {tex}U_{f}=\frac{1}{2}\left(C_{1}+C_{2}\right) V^{2}=\frac{1}{2}\left(C_{1}+C_{2}\right)\left(\frac{C_{1} V_{1}+C_{2} V_{2}}{C_{1}+C_{2}}\right)^{2}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{1}{2} \frac{\left(C_{1} V_{1}+C_{2} V_{2}\right)^{2}}{C_{1}+C_{2}}{/tex} Clearly, U f < U i . Thus the total energy of the parallel combination is less than the sum of the energies stored in the two capacitors before they are connected. During sharing of charges, some energy is lost as heat due to the flow of charges in connecting wires.
solution: Total energy stored in the two capacitors before they are connected, {tex}u_{i}=\frac{1}{2} C_{1} V_{1}^{2}+\frac{1}{2} C_{2} V_{2}^{2}{/tex} After the two capacitors are connected in parallel, the common potential is {tex}V=\frac{\text { Total charge }}{\text { Total capacitance }}=\frac{q_{1}+q_{2}}{C_{1}+C_{2}}=\frac{C_{1} V_{1}+C_{2} V_{2}}{C_{1}+C_{2}}{/tex} Total energy stored in the parallel combination, {tex}U_{f}=\frac{1}{2}\left(C_{1}+C_{2}\right) V^{2}=\frac{1}{2}\left(C_{1}+C_{2}\right)\left(\frac{C_{1} V_{1}+C_{2} V_{2}}{C_{1}+C_{2}}\right)^{2}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{1}{2} \frac{\left(C_{1} V_{1}+C_{2} V_{2}\right)^{2}}{C_{1}+C_{2}}{/tex} Clearly, U f < U i . Thus the total energy of the parallel combination is less than the sum of the energies stored in the two capacitors before they are connected. During sharing of charges, some energy is lost as heat due to the flow of charges in connecting wires.
solution_html:
1. Total energy stored in the two capacitors before they are connected,
    {tex}u_{i}=\frac{1}{2} C_{1} V_{1}^{2}+\frac{1}{2} C_{2} V_{2}^{2}{/tex}
  2. After the two capacitors are connected in parallel, the common potential is
    {tex}V=\frac{\text { Total charge }}{\text { Total capacitance }}=\frac{q_{1}+q_{2}}{C_{1}+C_{2}}=\frac{C_{1} V_{1}+C_{2} V_{2}}{C_{1}+C_{2}}{/tex}
    Total energy stored in the parallel combination,
    {tex}U_{f}=\frac{1}{2}\left(C_{1}+C_{2}\right) V^{2}=\frac{1}{2}\left(C_{1}+C_{2}\right)\left(\frac{C_{1} V_{1}+C_{2} V_{2}}{C_{1}+C_{2}}\right)^{2}{/tex}
    {tex}=\frac{1}{2} \frac{\left(C_{1} V_{1}+C_{2} V_{2}\right)^{2}}{C_{1}+C_{2}}{/tex}
  3. Clearly, Uf < Ui. Thus the total energy of the parallel combination is less than the sum of the energies stored in the two capacitors before they are connected. During sharing of charges, some energy is lost as heat due to the flow of charges in connecting wires.

question_html:
Four point charges Q, q, Q and q are placed at the corners of a square of side a as shown in the figure.

Find the 

  1. Resultant electric force on a charge Q, and
  2. Potential energy of this system.
question_text: Four point charges Q, q, Q and q are placed at the corners of a square of side a as shown in the figure. Find the Resultant electric force on a charge Q, and Potential energy of this system.
explanation: Finding the resultant force on a charge Q. Let us find the force on the charge Q at the point C Force due to the other charge Q(at A), {tex}F_1=\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0} \cdot \frac{Q^2}{(a \sqrt{2})^2}{/tex} {tex}\vec{F}_1=\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0}{/tex} {tex}\left(\frac{Q^2}{2 a^2}\right){/tex} (along AC) Force due to the charge q(at B), {tex}\vec{F_2}=\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0} \cdot \frac{q Q}{a^2}{/tex} along BC Force due to the charge q (at D), {tex}\vec{F_3}=\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0} \cdot \frac{q Q}{a^2}{/tex} along DC Resultant of these two equal forces {tex}\vec{F}_2{/tex} & {tex}\vec{F}_3{/tex} , {tex}\vec{F_{23}}=\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0} \cdot \frac{q Q(\sqrt{2})}{a^2}{/tex} (along AC) {tex}\therefore{/tex} Net force on charge Q(at point C) {tex}\vec{F}=\vec{F}_1+\vec{F}_{23}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0} \cdot \frac{Q}{a^2}\left[\frac{Q}{2}+\sqrt{2 q}\right]{/tex} This force is directed along AC. (For the charge Q, at the point A, the force will have the same magnitude but will be directed along CA) [ Note: Don't deduct marks if the student does not wite the direction of the net force, F] Potential energy of the system {tex}=\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0}\left[4 \frac{qQ}{a}+\frac{a^2}{a \sqrt{2}}+\frac{Q^2}{a \sqrt{2}}\right]{/tex} {tex} =\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0a }\left[4 qQ+\frac{q^2}{\sqrt{2}}+\frac{Q^2}{\sqrt{2}}\right] {/tex}
solution: Finding the resultant force on a charge Q. Let us find the force on the charge Q at the point C Force due to the other charge Q(at A), {tex}F_1=\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0} \cdot \frac{Q^2}{(a \sqrt{2})^2}{/tex} {tex}\vec{F}_1=\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0}{/tex} {tex}\left(\frac{Q^2}{2 a^2}\right){/tex} (along AC) Force due to the charge q(at B), {tex}\vec{F_2}=\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0} \cdot \frac{q Q}{a^2}{/tex} along BC Force due to the charge q (at D), {tex}\vec{F_3}=\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0} \cdot \frac{q Q}{a^2}{/tex} along DC Resultant of these two equal forces {tex}\vec{F}_2{/tex} & {tex}\vec{F}_3{/tex} , {tex}\vec{F_{23}}=\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0} \cdot \frac{q Q(\sqrt{2})}{a^2}{/tex} (along AC) {tex}\therefore{/tex} Net force on charge Q(at point C) {tex}\vec{F}=\vec{F}_1+\vec{F}_{23}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0} \cdot \frac{Q}{a^2}\left[\frac{Q}{2}+\sqrt{2 q}\right]{/tex} This force is directed along AC. (For the charge Q, at the point A, the force will have the same magnitude but will be directed along CA) [ Note: Don't deduct marks if the student does not wite the direction of the net force, F] Potential energy of the system {tex}=\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0}\left[4 \frac{qQ}{a}+\frac{a^2}{a \sqrt{2}}+\frac{Q^2}{a \sqrt{2}}\right]{/tex} {tex} =\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0a }\left[4 qQ+\frac{q^2}{\sqrt{2}}+\frac{Q^2}{\sqrt{2}}\right] {/tex}
solution_html:
1. Finding the resultant force on a charge Q.
    Let us find the force on the charge Q at the point C Force due to the other charge Q(at A),
    {tex}F_1=\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0} \cdot \frac{Q^2}{(a \sqrt{2})^2}{/tex}
    {tex}\vec{F}_1=\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0}{/tex}{tex}\left(\frac{Q^2}{2 a^2}\right){/tex} (along AC)
    Force due to the charge q(at B),
    {tex}\vec{F_2}=\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0} \cdot \frac{q Q}{a^2}{/tex} along BC
    Force due to the charge q (at D),
    {tex}\vec{F_3}=\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0} \cdot \frac{q Q}{a^2}{/tex} along DC
    Resultant of these two equal forces {tex}\vec{F}_2{/tex} & {tex}\vec{F}_3{/tex},
    {tex}\vec{F_{23}}=\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0} \cdot \frac{q Q(\sqrt{2})}{a^2}{/tex} (along AC)
    {tex}\therefore{/tex} Net force on charge Q(at point C)
    {tex}\vec{F}=\vec{F}_1+\vec{F}_{23}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0} \cdot \frac{Q}{a^2}\left[\frac{Q}{2}+\sqrt{2 q}\right]{/tex}
    This force is directed along AC.
    (For the charge Q, at the point A, the force will have the same magnitude but will be directed along CA)

    [Note: Don't deduct marks if the student does not wite the direction of the net force, F]
  2. Potential energy of the system
     {tex}=\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0}\left[4 \frac{qQ}{a}+\frac{a^2}{a \sqrt{2}}+\frac{Q^2}{a \sqrt{2}}\right]{/tex}
    {tex} =\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0a }\left[4 qQ+\frac{q^2}{\sqrt{2}}+\frac{Q^2}{\sqrt{2}}\right] {/tex}

subject: Physics
question_html: Derive an expression for the potential at a point along the axial line of a short electric dipole.
question_text: Derive an expression for the potential at a point along the axial line of a short electric dipole.
explanation: The figure shows an electric dipole consisting of charges -q at point A and +q at point B. Let P be any point on the axial line of the dipole at a distance r from its center. If 2a is the length of the dipole, then PA = r + a and PB = r - a The electric potential at point P due to the dipole V = {tex}\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}}{/tex} . {tex}\frac{(-q)}{\mathrm{PA}}+\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}} \cdot \frac{q}{\mathrm{PB}}{/tex} = {tex}-\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}}{/tex} . {tex}\frac{q}{(r+a)}+\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}} \cdot \frac{q}{(r-a)}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}}{/tex} .q {tex}\left(\frac{1}{r-a}-\frac{1}{r+a}\right){/tex} = {tex}\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}} \cdot q \frac{(r+a)-(r-a)}{\left(r^{2}-a^{2}\right)}{/tex} or V = {tex}\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}} \cdot \frac{p}{\left(r^{2}-a^{2}\right)}{/tex} For a dipole of short length, a 2 can be neglected as compared to r 2 . Then, V = {tex}\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}} \cdot \frac{p}{r^{2}}{/tex}
solution: The figure shows an electric dipole consisting of charges -q at point A and +q at point B. Let P be any point on the axial line of the dipole at a distance r from its center. If 2a is the length of the dipole, then PA = r + a and PB = r - a The electric potential at point P due to the dipole V = {tex}\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}}{/tex} . {tex}\frac{(-q)}{\mathrm{PA}}+\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}} \cdot \frac{q}{\mathrm{PB}}{/tex} = {tex}-\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}}{/tex} . {tex}\frac{q}{(r+a)}+\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}} \cdot \frac{q}{(r-a)}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}}{/tex} .q {tex}\left(\frac{1}{r-a}-\frac{1}{r+a}\right){/tex} = {tex}\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}} \cdot q \frac{(r+a)-(r-a)}{\left(r^{2}-a^{2}\right)}{/tex} or V = {tex}\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}} \cdot \frac{p}{\left(r^{2}-a^{2}\right)}{/tex} For a dipole of short length, a 2 can be neglected as compared to r 2 . Then, V = {tex}\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}} \cdot \frac{p}{r^{2}}{/tex}
solution_html:
The figure shows an electric dipole consisting of charges -q at point A and +q at point B. Let P be any point on the axial line of the dipole at a distance r from its center.

If 2a is the length of the dipole, then
PA = r + a and PB = r - a
The electric potential at point P due to the dipole
V = {tex}\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}}{/tex}.{tex}\frac{(-q)}{\mathrm{PA}}+\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}} \cdot \frac{q}{\mathrm{PB}}{/tex}
= {tex}-\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}}{/tex}.{tex}\frac{q}{(r+a)}+\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}} \cdot \frac{q}{(r-a)}{/tex}
= {tex}\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}}{/tex}.q{tex}\left(\frac{1}{r-a}-\frac{1}{r+a}\right){/tex}
= {tex}\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}} \cdot q \frac{(r+a)-(r-a)}{\left(r^{2}-a^{2}\right)}{/tex}
or V = {tex}\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}} \cdot \frac{p}{\left(r^{2}-a^{2}\right)}{/tex}
For a dipole of short length, a2 can be neglected as compared to r2. Then,
V = {tex}\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}} \cdot \frac{p}{r^{2}}{/tex}
