question_text: Draw the equipotential surfaces due to an electric dipole. Locate the points, where the potential due to the dipole is zero.
explanation: In a Figure (a) and Figure (b) represent the equipotential surfaces and electric lines of force for two equal positive charges and a pair of equal and opposite charges respectively. The dotted lines represent electric lines of force. The thick circles around the charges represent equipotential surfaces due to individual charges. In the two cases, the equipotential surfaces due to the two charges are obtained by adding potentials at a point due to two charges algebraically. They have been represented by thick curves or lines.
solution: In a Figure (a) and Figure (b) represent the equipotential surfaces and electric lines of force for two equal positive charges and a pair of equal and opposite charges respectively. The dotted lines represent electric lines of force. The thick circles around the charges represent equipotential surfaces due to individual charges. In the two cases, the equipotential surfaces due to the two charges are obtained by adding potentials at a point due to two charges algebraically. They have been represented by thick curves or lines.
solution_html:
In a Figure (a) and Figure (b) represent the equipotential surfaces and electric lines of force for two equal positive charges and a pair of equal and opposite charges respectively.
 
The dotted lines represent electric lines of force. The thick circles around the charges represent equipotential surfaces due to individual charges. In the two cases, the equipotential surfaces due to the two charges are obtained by adding potentials at a point due to two charges algebraically. They have been represented by thick curves or lines.

subject: Physics
question_html:
A capacitor of unknown capacitance is connected across a battery of V volt. The charge stored in it is 360µC. When potential across the capacitor is reduced by 120 V, the charge stored in it becomes 120 µC. Calculate

  1. the potential V and the unknown capacitance C.
  2. what will be the charge stored in the capacitor, if the voltage applied had increased by 120 V?
question_text: A capacitor of unknown capacitance is connected across a battery of V volt. The charge stored in it is 360µC. When potential across the capacitor is reduced by 120 V, the charge stored in it becomes 120 µC. Calculate the potential V and the unknown capacitance C. what will be the charge stored in the capacitor, if the voltage applied had increased by 120 V?
explanation: We have initial voltage is , V 1 = V volt and charge stored is , Q 1 = 360µC We know that, Q 1 = CV 1 ....(i) Now changed potential, V 2 = V 1 - 120 At that condition, stored charge is Q 2 = 120µC Again applying formula, Q 2 = CV 2 ...(ii) By dividing Eq. (i) by Eq. (ii), we get {tex}\frac { Q _ { 1 } } { Q _ { 2 } } = \frac { C V _ { 1 } } { C V _ { 2 } } \Rightarrow \frac { 360 } { 120 } = \frac { V } { V - 120 }{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} V = 180 Volt {tex}\therefore \quad C = \frac { Q _ { 1 } } { V _ { 1 } } = \frac { 360 \times 10 ^ { - 6 } } { 180 } = 2 \times 10 ^ { - 6 } \mathrm { F } = 2 \mu \mathrm { F }{/tex} Hence, the applied potential, V = 180 volt and unknown capacitance of the capacitor is 2µF. Let Q be the charge stored in the capacitor is given by :- Q = CV = 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -6 {tex}\times{/tex} (120 + 180) {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} Q = 6 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -4 C
solution: We have initial voltage is , V 1 = V volt and charge stored is , Q 1 = 360µC We know that, Q 1 = CV 1 ....(i) Now changed potential, V 2 = V 1 - 120 At that condition, stored charge is Q 2 = 120µC Again applying formula, Q 2 = CV 2 ...(ii) By dividing Eq. (i) by Eq. (ii), we get {tex}\frac { Q _ { 1 } } { Q _ { 2 } } = \frac { C V _ { 1 } } { C V _ { 2 } } \Rightarrow \frac { 360 } { 120 } = \frac { V } { V - 120 }{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} V = 180 Volt {tex}\therefore \quad C = \frac { Q _ { 1 } } { V _ { 1 } } = \frac { 360 \times 10 ^ { - 6 } } { 180 } = 2 \times 10 ^ { - 6 } \mathrm { F } = 2 \mu \mathrm { F }{/tex} Hence, the applied potential, V = 180 volt and unknown capacitance of the capacitor is 2µF. Let Q be the charge stored in the capacitor is given by :- Q = CV = 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -6 {tex}\times{/tex} (120 + 180) {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} Q = 6 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -4 C
solution_html:
1. We have initial voltage is , V1 = V volt and charge stored is , Q1 = 360µC 
    We know that, Q1 = CV1....(i)
    Now changed potential, V2 = V1 - 120
    At that condition, stored charge is  Q2 = 120µC
    Again applying formula, Q2 = CV2 ...(ii)
    By dividing Eq. (i) by Eq. (ii), we get
    {tex}\frac { Q _ { 1 } } { Q _ { 2 } } = \frac { C V _ { 1 } } { C V _ { 2 } } \Rightarrow \frac { 360 } { 120 } = \frac { V } { V - 120 }{/tex}
    {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} V = 180 Volt
    {tex}\therefore \quad C = \frac { Q _ { 1 } } { V _ { 1 } } = \frac { 360 \times 10 ^ { - 6 } } { 180 } = 2 \times 10 ^ { - 6 } \mathrm { F } = 2 \mu \mathrm { F }{/tex}
    Hence, the applied potential, V = 180 volt and unknown capacitance of the capacitor is 2µF.
  2. Let Q be the charge stored in the capacitor is given by :-
    Q = CV = 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-6{tex}\times{/tex} (120 + 180)
    {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} Q = 6 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-4 C

subject: Physics
question_html:
1. Write two properties by which electric potential is related to the electric field.
  2. Two-point charges q1 and q2, separated by a distance of r12 are kept in an external electric field. Derive an expression for the potential energy of the system of two charges in the field.
question_text: Write two properties by which electric potential is related to the electric field. Two-point charges q 1 and q 2 , separated by a distance of r 12 are kept in an external electric field. Derive an expression for the potential energy of the system of two charges in the field.
explanation: Properties (i) The magnitude of electric field intensity is numerically equal to potential gradient. (ii) Negative sign signifies the electric field intensity is in the direction of decreasing electric potential gradient. Derivation of expression of potential energy Work done in bringing the charge q 1 to a point against external electric field {tex}W_{1}=q_{1} V \vec{r}_{1}{/tex} Work done in bringing the charge q 2 against the external electric field and electric field produced due to charge q 1 {tex}W_{2}=q_{2} V(\overrightarrow{r_{2}})+\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{1}} \cdot \frac{q_{1} q_{2}}{r_{11}}{/tex} Therefore, Total work done = Electrostatic potential energy: {tex}U=q_{1} V(\overrightarrow{r_{1}})+q_{2} V(\overrightarrow{r_{2}}){/tex} {tex}+\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}} \cdot \frac{g_{1} q_{2}}{r_{12}}{/tex}
solution: Properties (i) The magnitude of electric field intensity is numerically equal to potential gradient. (ii) Negative sign signifies the electric field intensity is in the direction of decreasing electric potential gradient. Derivation of expression of potential energy Work done in bringing the charge q 1 to a point against external electric field {tex}W_{1}=q_{1} V \vec{r}_{1}{/tex} Work done in bringing the charge q 2 against the external electric field and electric field produced due to charge q 1 {tex}W_{2}=q_{2} V(\overrightarrow{r_{2}})+\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{1}} \cdot \frac{q_{1} q_{2}}{r_{11}}{/tex} Therefore, Total work done = Electrostatic potential energy: {tex}U=q_{1} V(\overrightarrow{r_{1}})+q_{2} V(\overrightarrow{r_{2}}){/tex} {tex}+\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}} \cdot \frac{g_{1} q_{2}}{r_{12}}{/tex}
solution_html:
1. Properties
    (i) The magnitude of electric field intensity is numerically equal to potential gradient.
    (ii) Negative sign signifies the electric field intensity is in the direction of decreasing electric potential gradient.
     
  2. Derivation of expression of potential energy
    Work done in bringing the charge q1 to a point against external electric field
    {tex}W_{1}=q_{1} V \vec{r}_{1}{/tex}
    Work done in bringing the charge q2 against the external electric field and electric field produced due to charge q1
    {tex}W_{2}=q_{2} V(\overrightarrow{r_{2}})+\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{1}} \cdot \frac{q_{1} q_{2}}{r_{11}}{/tex}
    Therefore, Total work done = Electrostatic potential energy:
    {tex}U=q_{1} V(\overrightarrow{r_{1}})+q_{2} V(\overrightarrow{r_{2}}){/tex} {tex}+\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}} \cdot \frac{g_{1} q_{2}}{r_{12}}{/tex}

question_text: Does the charge given to a metallic sphere depend on whether it is hollow or solid? Give reason for your answer.
explanation: In case of metallic sphere, charge given to it is mostly resides on its surface. Therefore, there is no difference whether the sphere is hollow or solid. As in both the cases, the charge that will reside will be same.
solution: In case of metallic sphere, charge given to it is mostly resides on its surface. Therefore, there is no difference whether the sphere is hollow or solid. As in both the cases, the charge that will reside will be same.
solution_html: In case of metallic sphere, charge given to it is mostly resides on its surface. Therefore, there is no difference whether the sphere is hollow or solid. As in both the cases, the charge that will reside will be same.

subject: Physics
question_html: Why does current in steady state not flow in a capacitor connected across a battery? However, momentary current does flow during charging or discharging of the capacitor. Explain.
question_text: Why does current in steady state not flow in a capacitor connected across a battery? However, momentary current does flow during charging or discharging of the capacitor. Explain.
explanation: In steady state, electric flux between plates of a capacitor is constant. So, displacement current is {tex}i _ { d } = \varepsilon _ { 0 } \frac { d \phi _ { E } } { d t } \;and\; \frac { d \phi _ { E } } { d t } = 0{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad i _ { d } = 0{/tex} But during charging or discharging a capacitor, magnetic flux becomes time dependent, not a constant one. Hence, a displacement current exists in the capacitor which is {tex}i _ { d } = \varepsilon _ { 0 } \frac { d \phi _ { E } } { d t }{/tex} .
solution: In steady state, electric flux between plates of a capacitor is constant. So, displacement current is {tex}i _ { d } = \varepsilon _ { 0 } \frac { d \phi _ { E } } { d t } \;and\; \frac { d \phi _ { E } } { d t } = 0{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad i _ { d } = 0{/tex} But during charging or discharging a capacitor, magnetic flux becomes time dependent, not a constant one. Hence, a displacement current exists in the capacitor which is {tex}i _ { d } = \varepsilon _ { 0 } \frac { d \phi _ { E } } { d t }{/tex} .
solution_html:
In steady state, electric flux between plates of a capacitor is constant.
So, displacement current is
{tex}i _ { d } = \varepsilon _ { 0 } \frac { d \phi _ { E } } { d t } \;and\; \frac { d \phi _ { E } } { d t } = 0{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad i _ { d } = 0{/tex}
But during charging or discharging a capacitor, magnetic flux becomes time dependent, not a constant one. Hence, a displacement current exists in the capacitor which is {tex}i _ { d } = \varepsilon _ { 0 } \frac { d \phi _ { E } } { d t }{/tex}.

subject: Physics
question_html: Two identical parallel plate capacitors A and B are connected to a battery of emf E volts with the switch S is closed. The switch is now opened and the free space between the plates of the capacitors is filled with a dielectric of dielectric constant K. Find the ratio of the total electrostatic energy stored in both capacitors before and after the introduction of the dielectric.
question_text: Two identical parallel plate capacitors A and B are connected to a battery of emf E volts with the switch S is closed. The switch is now opened and the free space between the plates of the capacitors is filled with a dielectric of dielectric constant K. Find the ratio of the total electrostatic energy stored in both capacitors before and after the introduction of the dielectric.
explanation: When switch S is closed, the potential difference across capacitors A and B are same i.e., {tex}E = \frac { Q _ { A } } { C } = \frac { Q _ { B } } { C }{/tex} , capacitance of both capacitors A and B is equal to C. Initial charges on capacitors Q A = Q B = CE When the dielectric is introduced, the new capacitance of either capacitor C' = KC As switch S is opened, the potential difference across capacitor 'A' remains the same (E Volts). Let potential difference across capacitor B be E'. When dielectric is introduced in between the plates of both the capacitors with switch S opened (i.e. battery disconnected from capacitor B), the charges on capacitor B remains unchanged, so Q B = CE = C'E' {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}E' = \frac {CE}{C'} = \frac {E}{K}{/tex} Initial total energy of both the capacitors, {tex}U _ {i } = \frac { 1 } { 2 } C E ^ { 2 } + \frac { 1 } { 2 } C E ^ { 2 } = C E ^ { 2 }{/tex} Final total energy of both capacitors, {tex}U _ { f } = \frac { 1 } { 2 } C ^ { \prime } E ^ { 2 } + \frac { 1 } { 2 } C ^ { \prime } E' ^ { 2 } = \frac { 1 } { 2 } ( K C ) E ^ { 2 } + \frac { 1 } { 2 } ( K C ) \left( \frac { E } { K } \right) ^ { 2 }{/tex} {tex}= \frac { 1 } { 2 } C E ^ { 2 } \left[ K + \frac { 1 } { K } \right] = \frac { 1 } { 2 } C E ^ { 2 } \left( \frac { K ^ { 2 } + 1 } { K } \right){/tex} Hence, ratio of initial total stored energy to the final total stored energy, {tex}\frac { U _ { i } } { U _ { f } } = \frac { C E ^ { 2 } } { \frac { 1 } { 2 } C E ^ { 2 } \left( \frac { K ^ { 2 } + 1 } { K } \right) } = \frac { 2 K } { K ^ { 2 } + 1 }{/tex}
solution: When switch S is closed, the potential difference across capacitors A and B are same i.e., {tex}E = \frac { Q _ { A } } { C } = \frac { Q _ { B } } { C }{/tex} , capacitance of both capacitors A and B is equal to C. Initial charges on capacitors Q A = Q B = CE When the dielectric is introduced, the new capacitance of either capacitor C' = KC As switch S is opened, the potential difference across capacitor 'A' remains the same (E Volts). Let potential difference across capacitor B be E'. When dielectric is introduced in between the plates of both the capacitors with switch S opened (i.e. battery disconnected from capacitor B), the charges on capacitor B remains unchanged, so Q B = CE = C'E' {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}E' = \frac {CE}{C'} = \frac {E}{K}{/tex} Initial total energy of both the capacitors, {tex}U _ {i } = \frac { 1 } { 2 } C E ^ { 2 } + \frac { 1 } { 2 } C E ^ { 2 } = C E ^ { 2 }{/tex} Final total energy of both capacitors, {tex}U _ { f } = \frac { 1 } { 2 } C ^ { \prime } E ^ { 2 } + \frac { 1 } { 2 } C ^ { \prime } E' ^ { 2 } = \frac { 1 } { 2 } ( K C ) E ^ { 2 } + \frac { 1 } { 2 } ( K C ) \left( \frac { E } { K } \right) ^ { 2 }{/tex} {tex}= \frac { 1 } { 2 } C E ^ { 2 } \left[ K + \frac { 1 } { K } \right] = \frac { 1 } { 2 } C E ^ { 2 } \left( \frac { K ^ { 2 } + 1 } { K } \right){/tex} Hence, ratio of initial total stored energy to the final total stored energy, {tex}\frac { U _ { i } } { U _ { f } } = \frac { C E ^ { 2 } } { \frac { 1 } { 2 } C E ^ { 2 } \left( \frac { K ^ { 2 } + 1 } { K } \right) } = \frac { 2 K } { K ^ { 2 } + 1 }{/tex}
solution_html:
When switch S is closed, the potential difference across capacitors A and B are same
i.e., {tex}E = \frac { Q _ { A } } { C } = \frac { Q _ { B } } { C }{/tex}, capacitance of both capacitors A and B is equal to C.
Initial charges on capacitors
 QA = QB = CE
When the dielectric is introduced, the new capacitance of either capacitor
C' = KC
As switch S is opened, the potential difference across capacitor 'A' remains the same (E Volts).
Let potential difference across capacitor B be E'. When dielectric is introduced in between the plates of both the capacitors with switch S opened (i.e. battery disconnected from capacitor B), the charges on capacitor B remains unchanged, so
QB = CE = C'E' {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex}  {tex}E' = \frac {CE}{C'} = \frac {E}{K}{/tex}
Initial total energy of both the capacitors,
{tex}U _ {i } = \frac { 1 } { 2 } C E ^ { 2 } + \frac { 1 } { 2 } C E ^ { 2 } = C E ^ { 2 }{/tex}
Final total energy of both capacitors,
{tex}U _ { f } = \frac { 1 } { 2 } C ^ { \prime } E ^ { 2 } + \frac { 1 } { 2 } C ^ { \prime } E' ^ { 2 } = \frac { 1 } { 2 } ( K C ) E ^ { 2 } + \frac { 1 } { 2 } ( K C ) \left( \frac { E } { K } \right) ^ { 2 }{/tex}
{tex}= \frac { 1 } { 2 } C E ^ { 2 } \left[ K + \frac { 1 } { K } \right] = \frac { 1 } { 2 } C E ^ { 2 } \left( \frac { K ^ { 2 } + 1 } { K } \right){/tex}
Hence, ratio of initial total stored energy to the final total stored energy,
{tex}\frac { U _ { i } } { U _ { f } } = \frac { C E ^ { 2 } } { \frac { 1 } { 2 } C E ^ { 2 } \left( \frac { K ^ { 2 } + 1 } { K } \right) } = \frac { 2 K } { K ^ { 2 } + 1 }{/tex}

subject: Physics
question_html: A 12 pF capacitor is connected to a 50 V battery. How much electrostatic energy is stored in the capacitor? If another capacitor of 6 pF is connected in series with it with the same battery connected across the combination, find the charge stored and potential difference across each capacitor.
question_text: A 12 pF capacitor is connected to a 50 V battery. How much electrostatic energy is stored in the capacitor? If another capacitor of 6 pF is connected in series with it with the same battery connected across the combination, find the charge stored and potential difference across each capacitor.
explanation: Energy stored in the capacitor of capacitance 12 pF = {tex}\frac { 1 } { 2 } C _ { 1 } V ^ { 2 }{/tex} {tex}= \frac { 1 } { 2 } \times 12 \times 10 ^ { - 12 } \times ( 50 ) ^ { 2 } \mathrm { J } = 6 \times 25 \times 10 ^ { - 10 } \mathrm { J } = 15 \times 10 ^ { - 9 } \mathrm { J }{/tex} With another capacitor of capacitance 6 pF in series with the first one, we get equivalent capacitance, C = {tex}\frac { C _ { 1 } \times C _ { 2 } } { C _ { 1 } + C _ { 2 } } = \frac { 6 \times 12 } { 6 + 12 } \mathrm { pF }{/tex} = {tex}\frac { 12 \times 6 } { 18 }{/tex} = 4 pF Charge stored in each capacitor is same and given by Q = CV = 4 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -12 {tex}\times{/tex} 50 C = 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -10 C Each of the two capacitors will get same charge and equal to Q = 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -10 C Potential on capacitors with capacitance 12pF is {tex}= \frac { Q } { C _ { 1 } } = \frac { 2 \times 10 ^ { - 10 } } { 12 \times 10 ^ { - 12 } } \mathrm { V } = 16.67 \mathrm { V }{/tex} Potential on capacitor with capacitance 6 pF is {tex}= \frac { 2 \times 10 ^ { - 10 } } { 6 \times 10 ^ { - 12 } } \mathrm { V } = 33.33 \mathrm { V }{/tex}
solution: Energy stored in the capacitor of capacitance 12 pF = {tex}\frac { 1 } { 2 } C _ { 1 } V ^ { 2 }{/tex} {tex}= \frac { 1 } { 2 } \times 12 \times 10 ^ { - 12 } \times ( 50 ) ^ { 2 } \mathrm { J } = 6 \times 25 \times 10 ^ { - 10 } \mathrm { J } = 15 \times 10 ^ { - 9 } \mathrm { J }{/tex} With another capacitor of capacitance 6 pF in series with the first one, we get equivalent capacitance, C = {tex}\frac { C _ { 1 } \times C _ { 2 } } { C _ { 1 } + C _ { 2 } } = \frac { 6 \times 12 } { 6 + 12 } \mathrm { pF }{/tex} = {tex}\frac { 12 \times 6 } { 18 }{/tex} = 4 pF Charge stored in each capacitor is same and given by Q = CV = 4 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -12 {tex}\times{/tex} 50 C = 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -10 C Each of the two capacitors will get same charge and equal to Q = 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -10 C Potential on capacitors with capacitance 12pF is {tex}= \frac { Q } { C _ { 1 } } = \frac { 2 \times 10 ^ { - 10 } } { 12 \times 10 ^ { - 12 } } \mathrm { V } = 16.67 \mathrm { V }{/tex} Potential on capacitor with capacitance 6 pF is {tex}= \frac { 2 \times 10 ^ { - 10 } } { 6 \times 10 ^ { - 12 } } \mathrm { V } = 33.33 \mathrm { V }{/tex}
solution_html:
Energy stored in the capacitor of capacitance 12 pF = {tex}\frac { 1 } { 2 } C _ { 1 } V ^ { 2 }{/tex}
{tex}= \frac { 1 } { 2 } \times 12 \times 10 ^ { - 12 } \times ( 50 ) ^ { 2 } \mathrm { J } = 6 \times 25 \times 10 ^ { - 10 } \mathrm { J } = 15 \times 10 ^ { - 9 } \mathrm { J }{/tex}
With another capacitor of capacitance 6 pF in series with the first one, we get equivalent capacitance,
C = {tex}\frac { C _ { 1 } \times C _ { 2 } } { C _ { 1 } + C _ { 2 } } = \frac { 6 \times 12 } { 6 + 12 } \mathrm { pF }{/tex} = {tex}\frac { 12 \times 6 } { 18 }{/tex} = 4 pF
Charge stored in each capacitor is same and given by
Q = CV = 4 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-12{tex}\times{/tex} 50 C = 2{tex}\times{/tex}10-10C
Each of the two capacitors will get same charge and equal to Q = 2 {tex}\times{/tex}10-10C
Potential on capacitors with capacitance 12pF is {tex}= \frac { Q } { C _ { 1 } } = \frac { 2 \times 10 ^ { - 10 } } { 12 \times 10 ^ { - 12 } } \mathrm { V } = 16.67 \mathrm { V }{/tex}
Potential on capacitor with capacitance 6 pF is {tex}= \frac { 2 \times 10 ^ { - 10 } } { 6 \times 10 ^ { - 12 } } \mathrm { V } = 33.33 \mathrm { V }{/tex}

question_html:
1. Derive the expression for the electric potential due to an electric dipole at a point on its axial line.
  2. Depict the equipotential surfaces due to an electric dipole.
question_text: Derive the expression for the electric potential due to an electric dipole at a point on its axial line. Depict the equipotential surfaces due to an electric dipole.
explanation: Let electric potential is to be determined at a point P lying on the axis of an electric dipole of dipole length 2l at a distance d from the centre of the dipole as shown in the figure. Potential at P due to +q charge of the dipole {tex}= \frac { 1 } { 4 \pi \varepsilon _ { 0 } } \cdot \frac { q } { ( d - l) }{/tex} Potential at P due to -q charge of the dipole {tex}= \frac { 1 } { 4 \pi \varepsilon _ { 0 } } \frac { - q } { ( d + l ) }{/tex} Total potential at P due to both the charges of the dipole {tex}= \frac { q } { 4 \pi \varepsilon _ { 0 } } \left[ \frac { 1 } { ( d - l) } - \frac { 1 } { ( d + l ) } \right]{/tex} {tex}= q \times \frac {2l} {4 \pi \varepsilon _ { 0 }} × \frac {1}{\left( d ^ { 2 } - l ^ { 2 } \right)} = \frac {p} { 4 \pi \varepsilon _ { 0 }}× \frac {1}{ \left( d ^ { 2 } - l ^ { 2 } \right)}{/tex} where, the scalar value of dipole moment (p) = q × 2l If l << d, then neglecting l 2 we get, the final value of the electric potential to be, {tex}V = \frac { 1 } { 4 \pi \varepsilon _ { 0 } } \frac { p } { d ^ { 2 } }{/tex} An electric dipole consists of two equal and opposite charges separated by some distance. Equipotential lines and electric field are always perpendicular to each other. Equipotential surfaces of a dipole are as shown below: Potential of points lying on the perpendicular bisector surface will be zero.
solution: Let electric potential is to be determined at a point P lying on the axis of an electric dipole of dipole length 2l at a distance d from the centre of the dipole as shown in the figure. Potential at P due to +q charge of the dipole {tex}= \frac { 1 } { 4 \pi \varepsilon _ { 0 } } \cdot \frac { q } { ( d - l) }{/tex} Potential at P due to -q charge of the dipole {tex}= \frac { 1 } { 4 \pi \varepsilon _ { 0 } } \frac { - q } { ( d + l ) }{/tex} Total potential at P due to both the charges of the dipole {tex}= \frac { q } { 4 \pi \varepsilon _ { 0 } } \left[ \frac { 1 } { ( d - l) } - \frac { 1 } { ( d + l ) } \right]{/tex} {tex}= q \times \frac {2l} {4 \pi \varepsilon _ { 0 }} × \frac {1}{\left( d ^ { 2 } - l ^ { 2 } \right)} = \frac {p} { 4 \pi \varepsilon _ { 0 }}× \frac {1}{ \left( d ^ { 2 } - l ^ { 2 } \right)}{/tex} where, the scalar value of dipole moment (p) = q × 2l If l << d, then neglecting l 2 we get, the final value of the electric potential to be, {tex}V = \frac { 1 } { 4 \pi \varepsilon _ { 0 } } \frac { p } { d ^ { 2 } }{/tex} An electric dipole consists of two equal and opposite charges separated by some distance. Equipotential lines and electric field are always perpendicular to each other. Equipotential surfaces of a dipole are as shown below: Potential of points lying on the perpendicular bisector surface will be zero.
solution_html:
1. Let electric potential is to be determined at a point P lying on the axis of an electric dipole of dipole length 2l at a distance d from the centre of the dipole as shown in the figure.

    Potential at P due to +q charge of the dipole {tex}= \frac { 1 } { 4 \pi \varepsilon _ { 0 } } \cdot \frac { q } { ( d - l) }{/tex}
    Potential at P due to -q charge of the dipole
    {tex}= \frac { 1 } { 4 \pi \varepsilon _ { 0 } } \frac { - q } { ( d + l ) }{/tex}
    Total potential at P due to both the charges of the dipole
    {tex}= \frac { q } { 4 \pi \varepsilon _ { 0 } } \left[ \frac { 1 } { ( d - l) } - \frac { 1 } { ( d + l ) } \right]{/tex}
    {tex}= q \times \frac {2l} {4 \pi \varepsilon _ { 0 }} × \frac {1}{\left( d ^ { 2 } - l ^ { 2 } \right)} = \frac {p} { 4 \pi \varepsilon _ { 0 }}× \frac {1}{ \left( d ^ { 2 } - l ^ { 2 } \right)}{/tex}
    where, the scalar value of dipole moment (p) = q × 2l
    If l << d, then neglecting l2 we get, the final value of the electric potential to be, {tex}V = \frac { 1 } { 4 \pi \varepsilon _ { 0 } } \frac { p } { d ^ { 2 } }{/tex}
  2. An electric dipole consists of two equal and opposite charges separated by some distance. Equipotential lines and electric field are always perpendicular to each other. Equipotential surfaces of a dipole are as shown below:

    Potential of points lying on the perpendicular bisector surface will be zero.

question_html:
1. How many electrons must be added to one plate and removed from other so as to store 25.0 J of energy in a 5.0 nF parallel plate capacitor?
  2. How would you modify this capacitor so that it can store 50.0 J of energy without changing the charge on its plates?
question_text: How many electrons must be added to one plate and removed from other so as to store 25.0 J of energy in a 5.0 nF parallel plate capacitor? How would you modify this capacitor so that it can store 50.0 J of energy without changing the charge on its plates?
explanation: C = 5 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -9 F, U = 25 J energy of capacitor is given by, {tex}U=\frac{Q^{2}}{2 C}{/tex} Q 2 = 2UC = 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 25 {tex}\times{/tex} 5 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -9 Q = 5.0 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -4 C Q = ne {tex}n=\frac{Q}{e}=\frac{5 \times 10^{-4} \mathrm{C}}{1.60 \times 10^{-19}}{/tex} = 3.125 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 15 electrons Without changing the charge on the plates, we can make C half. {tex}C=\frac{\varepsilon_{0} A}{d}{/tex} Double the plate separation or inserting dielectric of the dielectric constant of a value such that C will become half.
solution: C = 5 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -9 F, U = 25 J energy of capacitor is given by, {tex}U=\frac{Q^{2}}{2 C}{/tex} Q 2 = 2UC = 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 25 {tex}\times{/tex} 5 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -9 Q = 5.0 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -4 C Q = ne {tex}n=\frac{Q}{e}=\frac{5 \times 10^{-4} \mathrm{C}}{1.60 \times 10^{-19}}{/tex} = 3.125 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 15 electrons Without changing the charge on the plates, we can make C half. {tex}C=\frac{\varepsilon_{0} A}{d}{/tex} Double the plate separation or inserting dielectric of the dielectric constant of a value such that C will become half.
solution_html:
1. C = 5 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-9 F, U = 25 J 
    energy of capacitor is given by, {tex}U=\frac{Q^{2}}{2 C}{/tex}
    Q2 = 2UC
    = 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 25 {tex}\times{/tex} 5 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-9
    Q = 5.0 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-4 C
    Q = ne
    {tex}n=\frac{Q}{e}=\frac{5 \times 10^{-4} \mathrm{C}}{1.60 \times 10^{-19}}{/tex}
    = 3.125 {tex}\times{/tex} 1015 electrons
  2. Without changing the charge on the plates, we can make C half.
    {tex}C=\frac{\varepsilon_{0} A}{d}{/tex}
    Double the plate separation or inserting dielectric of the dielectric constant of a value such that C will become half.

question_html: In the given figure, charge +Q is placed at the centre of a dotted circle. Work done in taking another charge +q from A to B is W1 and from B to C is W2. Which one of the following is correct?
question_text: In the given figure, charge +Q is placed at the centre of a dotted circle. Work done in taking another charge +q from A to B is W 1 and from B to C is W 2 . Which one of the following is correct?

options.explanation: Workdone in electrostatic is given by ,W= q×(change in potential) As V A - V B = V B - V C magnitude of work done is same. Detailed Answer: As we know {tex}E=-\frac{d V}{d r}{/tex} and inside a ring there is zero electric field which means constant potential {tex}\therefore{/tex} V A - V B = V B - V C {tex}\therefore{/tex} Magnitude of work done is same.

subject: Physics
question_html:
A parallel plate capacitor of capacitance C is charged to a potential V by a battery. Without disconnecting the battery, the distance between the plates is tripled and a dielectric medium of k = 10 is introduced between the plates of the capacitor. Explain giving reasons, how will the following be affected:

  1. the capacitance of the capacitor,
  2. charge on the capacitor,
  3. the energy density of the capacitor.
question_text: A parallel plate capacitor of capacitance C is charged to a potential V by a battery. Without disconnecting the battery, the distance between the plates is tripled and a dielectric medium of k = 10 is introduced between the plates of the capacitor. Explain giving reasons, how will the following be affected: the capacitance of the capacitor, charge on the capacitor, the energy density of the capacitor.
explanation: Without the dielectric, the capacitance of a capacitor is given by, {tex}C=\frac{\varepsilon_{0} A}{d}{/tex} After dielectric introduced, {tex}C=\frac{\kappa \varepsilon_{0} A}{d^{\prime}}=10{/tex} {tex}\frac{\varepsilon_{0} A}{3 d}=\frac{10}{3} C{/tex} V remains the same since the battery is not disconnected {tex}\therefore{/tex} Q' = C'V {tex}=\frac{10}{3} C V=\frac{10}{3} Q{/tex} Energy density, U d {tex}=\frac{1}{2} \varepsilon_{0} E^{2}{/tex} {tex}E=\frac{(V)}{d}{/tex} {tex}U'_{d}=\frac{1}{2} \kappa \varepsilon_{0} E^{\prime 2}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{10}{2} \varepsilon_{0}\left(\frac{V}{d^{\prime}}\right)^{2}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{10}{9}\left(\frac{1}{2} \varepsilon_{0} E^{2}\right){/tex} (d' = 3d then V/d = E is done) {tex}=\frac{10}{9} u_{d}{/tex}
solution: Without the dielectric, the capacitance of a capacitor is given by, {tex}C=\frac{\varepsilon_{0} A}{d}{/tex} After dielectric introduced, {tex}C=\frac{\kappa \varepsilon_{0} A}{d^{\prime}}=10{/tex} {tex}\frac{\varepsilon_{0} A}{3 d}=\frac{10}{3} C{/tex} V remains the same since the battery is not disconnected {tex}\therefore{/tex} Q' = C'V {tex}=\frac{10}{3} C V=\frac{10}{3} Q{/tex} Energy density, U d {tex}=\frac{1}{2} \varepsilon_{0} E^{2}{/tex} {tex}E=\frac{(V)}{d}{/tex} {tex}U'_{d}=\frac{1}{2} \kappa \varepsilon_{0} E^{\prime 2}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{10}{2} \varepsilon_{0}\left(\frac{V}{d^{\prime}}\right)^{2}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{10}{9}\left(\frac{1}{2} \varepsilon_{0} E^{2}\right){/tex} (d' = 3d then V/d = E is done) {tex}=\frac{10}{9} u_{d}{/tex}
solution_html:
1. Without the dielectric, the capacitance of a capacitor is given by, {tex}C=\frac{\varepsilon_{0} A}{d}{/tex}
    After dielectric introduced, {tex}C=\frac{\kappa \varepsilon_{0} A}{d^{\prime}}=10{/tex} {tex}\frac{\varepsilon_{0} A}{3 d}=\frac{10}{3} C{/tex}
  2. V remains the same since the battery is not disconnected
    {tex}\therefore{/tex}Q' = C'V {tex}=\frac{10}{3} C V=\frac{10}{3} Q{/tex}
  3. Energy density, Ud {tex}=\frac{1}{2} \varepsilon_{0} E^{2}{/tex}
    {tex}E=\frac{(V)}{d}{/tex}
    {tex}U'_{d}=\frac{1}{2} \kappa \varepsilon_{0} E^{\prime 2}{/tex}
    {tex}=\frac{10}{2} \varepsilon_{0}\left(\frac{V}{d^{\prime}}\right)^{2}{/tex}
    {tex}=\frac{10}{9}\left(\frac{1}{2} \varepsilon_{0} E^{2}\right){/tex} (d' = 3d then V/d = E is done)
    {tex}=\frac{10}{9} u_{d}{/tex}

question_html: A capacitor of unknown capacitance is connected across a battery of V volt. A charge of 120 {tex}\mu{/tex}C is stored in it. When the potential across the capacitor is reduced by 40 V, the charge stored in the capacitor becomes 40 {tex}\mu{/tex}C. Calculate V and the unknown capacitance. What would be the charge in the capacitor if the voltage is increased by 40 V?
question_text: A capacitor of unknown capacitance is connected across a battery of V volt. A charge of 120 {tex}\mu{/tex} C is stored in it. When the potential across the capacitor is reduced by 40 V, the charge stored in the capacitor becomes 40 {tex}\mu{/tex} C. Calculate V and the unknown capacitance. What would be the charge in the capacitor if the voltage is increased by 40 V?
explanation: {tex}C=\frac{q}{V_{1}}=\frac{q_{2}}{V_{2}}{/tex} {tex}\therefore \frac{120 \mu C}{V}=\frac{40 \mu C}{V-40}{/tex} or 3V - 120 = V {tex}\because{/tex} V = 60 V {tex}C=\frac{q_{1}}{V_{1}}=\frac{120 \mu C}{60 V}=2 \mu {/tex} F When the voltage is increased by 40 V, {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} q = C(V + 40) {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 2 {tex}\mu{/tex} F {tex}\times{/tex} (60 + 40) V {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 200 {tex}\mu{/tex} C
solution: {tex}C=\frac{q}{V_{1}}=\frac{q_{2}}{V_{2}}{/tex} {tex}\therefore \frac{120 \mu C}{V}=\frac{40 \mu C}{V-40}{/tex} or 3V - 120 = V {tex}\because{/tex} V = 60 V {tex}C=\frac{q_{1}}{V_{1}}=\frac{120 \mu C}{60 V}=2 \mu {/tex} F When the voltage is increased by 40 V, {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} q = C(V + 40) {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 2 {tex}\mu{/tex} F {tex}\times{/tex} (60 + 40) V {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 200 {tex}\mu{/tex} C
solution_html:
{tex}C=\frac{q}{V_{1}}=\frac{q_{2}}{V_{2}}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore \frac{120 \mu C}{V}=\frac{40 \mu C}{V-40}{/tex} or 3V - 120 = V
{tex}\because{/tex} V = 60 V
{tex}C=\frac{q_{1}}{V_{1}}=\frac{120 \mu C}{60 V}=2 \mu {/tex} F
When the voltage is increased by 40 V,
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} q = C(V + 40)
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 2 {tex}\mu{/tex}F {tex}\times{/tex} (60 + 40) V
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 200 {tex}\mu{/tex}C

question_html: A large solid sphere with uniformly distributed positive charge has a smooth narrow tunnel through its centre. A small particle with a negative charge, initially at rest far from the sphere, approaches it along the line of the tunnel, reaches its surface with a speed v, and passes through the tunnel. Its speed at the centre of the sphere will be
question_text: A large solid sphere with uniformly distributed positive charge has a smooth narrow tunnel through its centre. A small particle with a negative charge, initially at rest far from the sphere, approaches it along the line of the tunnel, reaches its surface with a speed v, and passes through the tunnel. Its speed at the centre of the sphere will be

options.explanation: At infinity, {tex}V_{\infty}=0{/tex} At the surface of the sphere, {tex}V_{s}=\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}} \cdot \frac{Q}{R}{/tex} At the centre of the sphere, {tex}V_{c}=\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}} \cdot \frac{3 Q}{2 R}{/tex} Let m be the mass and -q be the charge of the small particle. Let V 0 be speed of the particle at the centre of the sphere. {tex}\frac{1}{2} m v^{2}=-q\left[V_{\infty}-V_{s}\right]=\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}} \cdot \frac{q Q}{R}{/tex} ...(i) {tex}\frac{1}{2} m v_{0}^{2}=-q\left[V_{\infty}-V_{c}\right]=\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}} \cdot \frac{3 q Q}{2 R}{/tex} ...(ii) Dividing (ii) by (i), we get {tex}\frac{v_{0}^{2}}{v^{2}}=\frac{3}{2}{/tex} = 1.5 {tex}\therefore v_{0}=\sqrt{1.5} v{/tex}

explanation: At infinity, {tex}V_{\infty}=0{/tex} At the surface of the sphere, {tex}V_{s}=\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}} \cdot \frac{Q}{R}{/tex} At the centre of the sphere, {tex}V_{c}=\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}} \cdot \frac{3 Q}{2 R}{/tex} Let m be the mass and -q be the charge of the small particle. Let V 0 be speed of the particle at the centre of the sphere. {tex}\frac{1}{2} m v^{2}=-q\left[V_{\infty}-V_{s}\right]=\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}} \cdot \frac{q Q}{R}{/tex} ...(i) {tex}\frac{1}{2} m v_{0}^{2}=-q\left[V_{\infty}-V_{c}\right]=\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}} \cdot \frac{3 q Q}{2 R}{/tex} ...(ii) Dividing (ii) by (i), we get {tex}\frac{v_{0}^{2}}{v^{2}}=\frac{3}{2}{/tex} = 1.5 {tex}\therefore v_{0}=\sqrt{1.5} v{/tex}

Reason (R): Electric field will induce dipole moment opposite to field direction.

options.text: Both A and R are true and R is the correct explanation of A.

explanation: The external field induces an electric field in the dielectric in the opposite direction which sets up a dipole moment in the same direction of the external field.

question_html: A capacitor is charged by a battery. The battery is removed and another identical uncharged capacitor is connected in parallel. The total electrostatic energy of resulting system:
question_text: A capacitor is charged by a battery. The battery is removed and another identical uncharged capacitor is connected in parallel. The total electrostatic energy of resulting system:

options.explanation: Initial energy, {tex}U_{i}=\frac{1}{2} C V^{2}{/tex} After connection, {tex}V_{C}=\frac{q_{1}+q_{2}}{C_{1}+C_{2}}=\frac{q+0}{C+C}=\frac{q}{2 C}=\frac{V}{2}{/tex} Final energy, {tex}U_{f}=\frac{1}{2} C\left(\frac{V}{2}\right)^{2}+\frac{1}{2} C\left(\frac{V}{2}\right)^{2}=\frac{1}{4} C V^{2}{/tex} Loss of energy = {tex}U_{i}-U_{f}=\frac{1}{4} C V^{2}{/tex}

explanation: Initial energy, {tex}U_{i}=\frac{1}{2} C V^{2}{/tex} After connection, {tex}V_{C}=\frac{q_{1}+q_{2}}{C_{1}+C_{2}}=\frac{q+0}{C+C}=\frac{q}{2 C}=\frac{V}{2}{/tex} Final energy, {tex}U_{f}=\frac{1}{2} C\left(\frac{V}{2}\right)^{2}+\frac{1}{2} C\left(\frac{V}{2}\right)^{2}=\frac{1}{4} C V^{2}{/tex} Loss of energy = {tex}U_{i}-U_{f}=\frac{1}{4} C V^{2}{/tex}

subject: Physics
question_html: Derive an expression for potential due to a dipole for distances large compared to the size of the dipole. How is the potential due to dipole different from that due to a single charge?
question_text: Derive an expression for potential due to a dipole for distances large compared to the size of the dipole. How is the potential due to dipole different from that due to a single charge?
explanation: Consider origin at the centre of dipole. As per superposition principle, potential due to dipole will be the sum of potentials due to charges q and -q {tex}V=\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}}\left[\frac{q}{r_{1}}-\frac{q}{r_{2}}\right]{/tex} Where, r 1 and r 2 = distances of point P from q and -q. r 1 2 = r 2 + a 2 - 2 arcos {tex}\theta{/tex} r 2 2 = r 2 + a 2 + 2 arcos {tex}\theta{/tex} If r is greater than a, and taking terms upto first order in a/r r 1 2 = r 2 {tex}\left[1-\frac{2 a \cos \theta}{r}+\frac{a^{2}}{r^{2}}\right]{/tex} {tex}=r^{2}\left[1-\frac{2 a \cos \theta}{r}\right]{/tex} Also, r 2 2 = r 2 {tex}\left[1+\frac{2 a \cos \theta}{r}\right]{/tex} With the help of Binomial theorem, keeping terms upto first order is shown below: {tex}\frac{1}{r_{1}} \equiv \frac{1}{r}\left[1-\frac{2 a \cos \theta}{r}\right]^{\frac{1}{2}}{/tex} {tex}\equiv \frac{1}{r}\left[1+\frac{a}{r} \cos \theta\right]{/tex} {tex}\frac{1}{r_{2}} \equiv \frac{1}{r}\left[1+\frac{2 a \cos \theta}{r}\right]^{\frac{1}{2}}{/tex} {tex}\equiv \frac{1}{r}\left[1-\frac{a}{r} \cos \theta\right]{/tex} As p = qa {tex}V=\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}} \frac{q(2 a) \cos \theta}{r^{2}}{/tex} {tex}V=\frac{p}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}} \cdot \frac{\cos \theta}{r^{2}}{/tex} Now, {tex}p \cos \theta=\vec{p}. \hat{r}{/tex} where {tex}\hat r{/tex} is unit vector along position vector. Hence electric potential of dipole for distances large compared to size of dipole is given as below : {tex}V=\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}} \cdot \frac{\vec p \hat r}{r^{2}}{/tex} for r >>a ---For potential at any point on axis, {tex}\theta=[0, \pi]{/tex} {tex}V=\pm \frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}} \cdot \frac{q}{r}{/tex} potential is positive when {tex}\theta{/tex} = 0 potential is negative when {tex}\theta{/tex} = {tex}\pi{/tex} Hence, electric potential falls at large distance, as {tex}\frac{1}{r^{2}}{/tex} and not as {tex}\frac{1}{r}{/tex}
solution: Consider origin at the centre of dipole. As per superposition principle, potential due to dipole will be the sum of potentials due to charges q and -q {tex}V=\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}}\left[\frac{q}{r_{1}}-\frac{q}{r_{2}}\right]{/tex} Where, r 1 and r 2 = distances of point P from q and -q. r 1 2 = r 2 + a 2 - 2 arcos {tex}\theta{/tex} r 2 2 = r 2 + a 2 + 2 arcos {tex}\theta{/tex} If r is greater than a, and taking terms upto first order in a/r r 1 2 = r 2 {tex}\left[1-\frac{2 a \cos \theta}{r}+\frac{a^{2}}{r^{2}}\right]{/tex} {tex}=r^{2}\left[1-\frac{2 a \cos \theta}{r}\right]{/tex} Also, r 2 2 = r 2 {tex}\left[1+\frac{2 a \cos \theta}{r}\right]{/tex} With the help of Binomial theorem, keeping terms upto first order is shown below: {tex}\frac{1}{r_{1}} \equiv \frac{1}{r}\left[1-\frac{2 a \cos \theta}{r}\right]^{\frac{1}{2}}{/tex} {tex}\equiv \frac{1}{r}\left[1+\frac{a}{r} \cos \theta\right]{/tex} {tex}\frac{1}{r_{2}} \equiv \frac{1}{r}\left[1+\frac{2 a \cos \theta}{r}\right]^{\frac{1}{2}}{/tex} {tex}\equiv \frac{1}{r}\left[1-\frac{a}{r} \cos \theta\right]{/tex} As p = qa {tex}V=\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}} \frac{q(2 a) \cos \theta}{r^{2}}{/tex} {tex}V=\frac{p}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}} \cdot \frac{\cos \theta}{r^{2}}{/tex} Now, {tex}p \cos \theta=\vec{p}. \hat{r}{/tex} where {tex}\hat r{/tex} is unit vector along position vector. Hence electric potential of dipole for distances large compared to size of dipole is given as below : {tex}V=\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}} \cdot \frac{\vec p \hat r}{r^{2}}{/tex} for r >>a ---For potential at any point on axis, {tex}\theta=[0, \pi]{/tex} {tex}V=\pm \frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}} \cdot \frac{q}{r}{/tex} potential is positive when {tex}\theta{/tex} = 0 potential is negative when {tex}\theta{/tex} = {tex}\pi{/tex} Hence, electric potential falls at large distance, as {tex}\frac{1}{r^{2}}{/tex} and not as {tex}\frac{1}{r}{/tex}
solution_html:
Consider origin at the centre of dipole. As per superposition principle, potential due to dipole will be the sum of potentials due to charges q and -q
{tex}V=\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}}\left[\frac{q}{r_{1}}-\frac{q}{r_{2}}\right]{/tex}
Where,
r1 and r2 = distances of point P from q and -q.
r12 = r2 + a2 - 2 arcos {tex}\theta{/tex}
r22 = r2 + a2 + 2 arcos {tex}\theta{/tex}
If r is greater than a, and taking terms upto first order in a/r
r12 = r2 {tex}\left[1-\frac{2 a \cos \theta}{r}+\frac{a^{2}}{r^{2}}\right]{/tex}
{tex}=r^{2}\left[1-\frac{2 a \cos \theta}{r}\right]{/tex}
Also, r22 = r2 {tex}\left[1+\frac{2 a \cos \theta}{r}\right]{/tex}
With the help of Binomial theorem, keeping terms upto first order is shown below:
{tex}\frac{1}{r_{1}} \equiv \frac{1}{r}\left[1-\frac{2 a \cos \theta}{r}\right]^{\frac{1}{2}}{/tex}
{tex}\equiv \frac{1}{r}\left[1+\frac{a}{r} \cos \theta\right]{/tex}
{tex}\frac{1}{r_{2}} \equiv \frac{1}{r}\left[1+\frac{2 a \cos \theta}{r}\right]^{\frac{1}{2}}{/tex}
{tex}\equiv \frac{1}{r}\left[1-\frac{a}{r} \cos \theta\right]{/tex}
As p = qa
{tex}V=\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}} \frac{q(2 a) \cos \theta}{r^{2}}{/tex}
{tex}V=\frac{p}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}} \cdot \frac{\cos \theta}{r^{2}}{/tex}
Now, {tex}p \cos \theta=\vec{p}. \hat{r}{/tex}
where {tex}\hat r{/tex} is unit vector along position vector.
Hence electric potential of dipole for distances large compared to size of dipole is given as below :
{tex}V=\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}} \cdot \frac{\vec p \hat r}{r^{2}}{/tex} for r >>a

---For potential at any point on axis, {tex}\theta=[0, \pi]{/tex}

{tex}V=\pm \frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}} \cdot \frac{q}{r}{/tex}

potential is positive when {tex}\theta{/tex} = 0
potential is negative when {tex}\theta{/tex} = {tex}\pi{/tex}
Hence, electric potential falls at large distance, as {tex}\frac{1}{r^{2}}{/tex} and not as {tex}\frac{1}{r}{/tex}

question_html: A capacitor of unknown capacitance is connected across a battery of V volt. A charge of 240 {tex}\mu{/tex}C is stored in it. When the potential across the capacitor is reduced by 80 V, the charge stored in the capacitor becomes 80 {tex}\mu{/tex}C. Calculate V and the unknown capacitance. What would have been the charge in the capacitor if the voltage were increased by 80 V?
question_text: A capacitor of unknown capacitance is connected across a battery of V volt. A charge of 240 {tex}\mu{/tex} C is stored in it. When the potential across the capacitor is reduced by 80 V, the charge stored in the capacitor becomes 80 {tex}\mu{/tex} C. Calculate V and the unknown capacitance. What would have been the charge in the capacitor if the voltage were increased by 80 V?
explanation: Given - initial voltage V 1 = V , initial charge q 1 = 240 {tex}\mu{/tex} C, therefore, capacitance C = {tex}\frac{q_1}{V_1}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{250}{V}{/tex} , ...(i) when, voltage V 2 = V − 80, charge q 2 = 80 {tex}\mu{/tex} C, therefore, capacitance C = {tex}\frac{q_2}{V_2}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{80}{(V−80)}{/tex} , ...(ii) capacitance of the capacitor remains constant as there is no change in area of the plates, medium and distance between plates, Dividing (i) by (ii), {tex}\frac{240}{V}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{80}{(V−80)}{/tex} , or 3(V − 80) = V, or V = {tex}\frac{80}{2}{/tex} = 40volt Capacitance C = {tex}\frac{240}{40}{/tex} = 6 {tex}\mu{/tex} F now the new voltage is V′ = 40 + 80 = 120V, therefore charge stored q′ = CV′ = 6 {tex}\times{/tex} 120 = 720 {tex}\mu{/tex} C
solution: Given - initial voltage V 1 = V , initial charge q 1 = 240 {tex}\mu{/tex} C, therefore, capacitance C = {tex}\frac{q_1}{V_1}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{250}{V}{/tex} , ...(i) when, voltage V 2 = V − 80, charge q 2 = 80 {tex}\mu{/tex} C, therefore, capacitance C = {tex}\frac{q_2}{V_2}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{80}{(V−80)}{/tex} , ...(ii) capacitance of the capacitor remains constant as there is no change in area of the plates, medium and distance between plates, Dividing (i) by (ii), {tex}\frac{240}{V}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{80}{(V−80)}{/tex} , or 3(V − 80) = V, or V = {tex}\frac{80}{2}{/tex} = 40volt Capacitance C = {tex}\frac{240}{40}{/tex} = 6 {tex}\mu{/tex} F now the new voltage is V′ = 40 + 80 = 120V, therefore charge stored q′ = CV′ = 6 {tex}\times{/tex} 120 = 720 {tex}\mu{/tex} C
solution_html:
Given - initial voltage V1 = V , initial charge q1 = 240{tex}\mu{/tex}C,
therefore, capacitance C = {tex}\frac{q_1}{V_1}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{250}{V}{/tex}, ...(i)
when, voltage V2 = V − 80, charge q2 = 80{tex}\mu{/tex}C,
therefore, capacitance C = {tex}\frac{q_2}{V_2}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{80}{(V−80)}{/tex}, ...(ii)
capacitance of the capacitor remains constant as there is no change in area of the plates, medium and distance between plates,
Dividing (i) by (ii),
{tex}\frac{240}{V}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{80}{(V−80)}{/tex},
or 3(V − 80) = V,
or V = {tex}\frac{80}{2}{/tex} = 40volt
Capacitance C = {tex}\frac{240}{40}{/tex} = 6{tex}\mu{/tex}F
now the new voltage is V′ = 40 + 80 = 120V,
therefore charge stored q′ = CV′ = 6 {tex}\times{/tex}120 = 720{tex}\mu{/tex}C

question_html: Two identical capacitors of 12 pF each are connected in series across a battery of 50 V. How much electrostatic energy is stored in the combination? If these were connected in parallel across the same battery, how much energy will be stored in the combination now? Also, find the charge drawn from the battery in each case.
question_text: Two identical capacitors of 12 pF each are connected in series across a battery of 50 V. How much electrostatic energy is stored in the combination? If these were connected in parallel across the same battery, how much energy will be stored in the combination now? Also, find the charge drawn from the battery in each case.
explanation: In series combination: {tex}\frac{1}{C_{s}}=\left(\frac{1}{12}+\frac{1}{12}\right)(p F)^{-1}{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} C S = 6 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -12 F {tex}U_{s}=\frac{1}{2} C V^{2}{/tex} U s = {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} {tex}\times{/tex} 6 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -12 {tex}\times{/tex} 50 {tex}\times{/tex} 50 J U s = 75 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -10 J q s = C 5 J = 6 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -12 {tex}\times{/tex} 50 C = 300 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -12 C Charge drown = 3 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -10 C In parallel combination: C p = (12 + 12) pF {tex}\therefore{/tex} C p = 24 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -12 F U p = {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} {tex}\times{/tex} 24 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -12 {tex}\times{/tex} 2500 J U p = 3 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -8 J q p = C p V = 24 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -12 {tex}\times{/tex} 50 C charge drown = 1.2 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -9 C
solution: In series combination: {tex}\frac{1}{C_{s}}=\left(\frac{1}{12}+\frac{1}{12}\right)(p F)^{-1}{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} C S = 6 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -12 F {tex}U_{s}=\frac{1}{2} C V^{2}{/tex} U s = {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} {tex}\times{/tex} 6 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -12 {tex}\times{/tex} 50 {tex}\times{/tex} 50 J U s = 75 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -10 J q s = C 5 J = 6 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -12 {tex}\times{/tex} 50 C = 300 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -12 C Charge drown = 3 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -10 C In parallel combination: C p = (12 + 12) pF {tex}\therefore{/tex} C p = 24 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -12 F U p = {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} {tex}\times{/tex} 24 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -12 {tex}\times{/tex} 2500 J U p = 3 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -8 J q p = C p V = 24 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -12 {tex}\times{/tex} 50 C charge drown = 1.2 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -9 C
solution_html:
In series combination:

{tex}\frac{1}{C_{s}}=\left(\frac{1}{12}+\frac{1}{12}\right)(p F)^{-1}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} CS = 6 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-12 F
{tex}U_{s}=\frac{1}{2} C V^{2}{/tex}
Us = {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} {tex}\times{/tex} 6 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-12 {tex}\times{/tex} 50 {tex}\times{/tex} 50 J
Us = 75 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-10 J
qs = C5 J
= 6 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-12 {tex}\times{/tex} 50 C
= 300 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-12 C
Charge drown = 3 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-10 C
In parallel combination:

Cp = (12 + 12) pF
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Cp = 24 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-12 F
Up = {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} {tex}\times{/tex} 24 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-12 {tex}\times{/tex} 2500 J
Up = 3 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-8 J
qp = CpV
= 24 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-12 {tex}\times{/tex} 50 C
charge drown = 1.2 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-9 C

question_html: A capacitor of unknown capacitance is connected across a battery of V volt. A charge of 360 {tex}\mu{/tex}C is stored in it. When the potential across the capacitor is reduced by 120 V, the charge stored in the capacitor becomes 120 {tex}\mu{/tex}C. Calculate V and the unknown capacitance. What would have been the charge on the capacitor if the voltage were increased by 120 V?
question_text: A capacitor of unknown capacitance is connected across a battery of V volt. A charge of 360 {tex}\mu{/tex} C is stored in it. When the potential across the capacitor is reduced by 120 V, the charge stored in the capacitor becomes 120 {tex}\mu{/tex} C. Calculate V and the unknown capacitance. What would have been the charge on the capacitor if the voltage were increased by 120 V?
explanation: Calculation of V and unknown capacitance Calculation of charge when voltage is increased by 120 V Capacitance, C = {tex}\frac{Q_1}{V_1}{/tex} Also, C = {tex}\frac{Q_2}{V_2}{/tex} and C = {tex}\frac{Q_3}{V_3}{/tex} {tex}\frac{360 \mu C}{V}{/tex} {tex}= \frac{120 \mu C}{(V-120)}{/tex} So, 3V - 360 V = V V = 180 V C = {tex}\frac{360 \mathrm{mV}}{180 \mathrm{~V}}{/tex} = 2 mF 2 {tex}\mu {/tex} F= {tex}\frac{Q_3}{300}{/tex} Q 3 = 600 {tex}\mu {/tex} C
solution: Calculation of V and unknown capacitance Calculation of charge when voltage is increased by 120 V Capacitance, C = {tex}\frac{Q_1}{V_1}{/tex} Also, C = {tex}\frac{Q_2}{V_2}{/tex} and C = {tex}\frac{Q_3}{V_3}{/tex} {tex}\frac{360 \mu C}{V}{/tex} {tex}= \frac{120 \mu C}{(V-120)}{/tex} So, 3V - 360 V = V V = 180 V C = {tex}\frac{360 \mathrm{mV}}{180 \mathrm{~V}}{/tex} = 2 mF 2 {tex}\mu {/tex} F= {tex}\frac{Q_3}{300}{/tex} Q 3 = 600 {tex}\mu {/tex} C
solution_html:
Calculation of V and unknown capacitance Calculation of charge when voltage is increased by 120 V
Capacitance, C = {tex}\frac{Q_1}{V_1}{/tex}
Also, C = {tex}\frac{Q_2}{V_2}{/tex} and C = {tex}\frac{Q_3}{V_3}{/tex}
{tex}\frac{360 \mu C}{V}{/tex} {tex}= \frac{120 \mu C}{(V-120)}{/tex}
So, 3V - 360 V = V
V = 180 V
C = {tex}\frac{360 \mathrm{mV}}{180 \mathrm{~V}}{/tex}= 2 mF
2 {tex}\mu {/tex}F= {tex}\frac{Q_3}{300}{/tex}
Q3 = 600 {tex}\mu {/tex}C

question_html: Derive the expression for the electric potential at any point P, at distance r from the centre of an electric dipole, making angle a, with its axis.
question_text: Derive the expression for the electric potential at any point P, at distance r from the centre of an electric dipole, making angle a, with its axis.
explanation: Electric potential at any general point due to a dipole. Consider an electric dipole of length 2a, as shown in figure. To find the potential at point P Let AP = r 1 and BP = r 2 Net potential at point P due to the dipole is V = V 1 + V 2 {tex}=\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}} \cdot \frac{-q}{r_{1}}+\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}} \cdot \frac{q}{r_{2}}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{q}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}}\left[\frac{1}{r_{2}}-\frac{1}{r_{1}}\right]{/tex} {tex}=\frac{q}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}}\left[\frac{r_{1}-r_{2}}{r_{1} r_{2}}\right]{/tex} If the point P lies far away from the dipole, then r 1 - r 2 = AB cos {tex}\theta{/tex} =2 a cos {tex}\theta{/tex} and r 1 r 2 {tex}\simeq r^{2}{/tex} {tex}\therefore V=\frac{q}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}} \cdot \frac{2 a \cos \theta}{r^{2}}{/tex} or {tex}V=\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}} \cdot \frac{p \cos \theta}{r^{2}}{/tex} or {tex}V=\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}} \frac{\vec{p} \cdot \vec{r}}{r^{3}}=\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}} \frac{\vec{p} \cdot \hat{r}}{r^{2}}{/tex} Here p = q {tex}\times{/tex} 2a ,is the dipole moment and {tex}\hat r = \frac{{\vec r}}{r}{/tex} is a unit vector along the position vector {tex}\overrightarrow{O P}=\vec{r}{/tex} .
solution: Electric potential at any general point due to a dipole. Consider an electric dipole of length 2a, as shown in figure. To find the potential at point P Let AP = r 1 and BP = r 2 Net potential at point P due to the dipole is V = V 1 + V 2 {tex}=\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}} \cdot \frac{-q}{r_{1}}+\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}} \cdot \frac{q}{r_{2}}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{q}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}}\left[\frac{1}{r_{2}}-\frac{1}{r_{1}}\right]{/tex} {tex}=\frac{q}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}}\left[\frac{r_{1}-r_{2}}{r_{1} r_{2}}\right]{/tex} If the point P lies far away from the dipole, then r 1 - r 2 = AB cos {tex}\theta{/tex} =2 a cos {tex}\theta{/tex} and r 1 r 2 {tex}\simeq r^{2}{/tex} {tex}\therefore V=\frac{q}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}} \cdot \frac{2 a \cos \theta}{r^{2}}{/tex} or {tex}V=\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}} \cdot \frac{p \cos \theta}{r^{2}}{/tex} or {tex}V=\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}} \frac{\vec{p} \cdot \vec{r}}{r^{3}}=\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}} \frac{\vec{p} \cdot \hat{r}}{r^{2}}{/tex} Here p = q {tex}\times{/tex} 2a ,is the dipole moment and {tex}\hat r = \frac{{\vec r}}{r}{/tex} is a unit vector along the position vector {tex}\overrightarrow{O P}=\vec{r}{/tex} .
solution_html:
Electric potential at any general point due to a dipole. Consider an electric dipole of length 2a, as shown in figure. To find the potential at point P
Let AP = r1 and BP = r2
Net potential at point P due to the dipole is V = V1 + V2
{tex}=\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}} \cdot \frac{-q}{r_{1}}+\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}} \cdot \frac{q}{r_{2}}{/tex}
{tex}=\frac{q}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}}\left[\frac{1}{r_{2}}-\frac{1}{r_{1}}\right]{/tex}
{tex}=\frac{q}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}}\left[\frac{r_{1}-r_{2}}{r_{1} r_{2}}\right]{/tex}

If the point P lies far away from the dipole, then
r1 - r2 = AB cos {tex}\theta{/tex} =2 a cos {tex}\theta{/tex} and r1 r2 {tex}\simeq r^{2}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore V=\frac{q}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}} \cdot \frac{2 a \cos \theta}{r^{2}}{/tex}
or {tex}V=\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}} \cdot \frac{p \cos \theta}{r^{2}}{/tex}
or {tex}V=\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}} \frac{\vec{p} \cdot \vec{r}}{r^{3}}=\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}} \frac{\vec{p} \cdot \hat{r}}{r^{2}}{/tex}
Here p = q {tex}\times{/tex} 2a ,is the dipole moment and {tex}\hat r = \frac{{\vec r}}{r}{/tex} is a unit vector along the position vector {tex}\overrightarrow{O P}=\vec{r}{/tex}.

question_html: In the given circuit, with a steady current, calculate the potential drop across the capacitor in terms of V.
question_text: In the given circuit, with a steady current, calculate the potential drop across the capacitor in terms of V.
explanation: In the steady state no current flows in the branch containing the capacitor. Thus the current say l flows in the branches containing R and 2R. Applying Kirchoff's second rule to the loop ABCDEF 5V - I(2R) - I(3R) - 10V = 0 I = {tex}\frac{5V}{5R}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{V}{R}{/tex} Potential drop across capacitor = 2V - V - l(2R) = V - v3R {tex}\times{/tex} 2R = V - 2v3 = v3
solution: In the steady state no current flows in the branch containing the capacitor. Thus the current say l flows in the branches containing R and 2R. Applying Kirchoff's second rule to the loop ABCDEF 5V - I(2R) - I(3R) - 10V = 0 I = {tex}\frac{5V}{5R}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{V}{R}{/tex} Potential drop across capacitor = 2V - V - l(2R) = V - v3R {tex}\times{/tex} 2R = V - 2v3 = v3
solution_html:
In the steady state no current flows in the branch containing the capacitor. Thus the current say l flows in the branches containing R and 2R.
Applying Kirchoff's second rule to the loop ABCDEF
5V - I(2R) - I(3R) - 10V = 0
I = {tex}\frac{5V}{5R}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{V}{R}{/tex}
Potential drop across capacitor = 2V - V - l(2R) = V - v3R {tex}\times{/tex} 2R
= V - 2v3 = v3

subject: Physics
question_html:
1. Define the capacitance of a capacitor. Obtain the expression for the capacitance of a parallel plate capacitor in vacuum in terms of plate area A and separation d between the plates.
  2. A slab of material of dielectric constant k has the same area as the plates of a parallel plate {tex}\frac{3 d}{4}{/tex} capacitor but has a thickness -. Find the ratio of the capacitance with dielectric inside it to its capacitance without the dielectric.
question_text: Define the capacitance of a capacitor. Obtain the expression for the capacitance of a parallel plate capacitor in vacuum in terms of plate area A and separation d between the plates. A slab of material of dielectric constant k has the same area as the plates of a parallel plate {tex}\frac{3 d}{4}{/tex} capacitor but has a thickness -. Find the ratio of the capacitance with dielectric inside it to its capacitance without the dielectric.
explanation: The capacitance C of a capacitor is defined as the ratio of the maximum charge Q that can be stored in a capacitor to the applied voltage V across its plates. Electric field between the plates of capacitor E = {tex}\frac{\sigma}{\varepsilon_0}=\frac{\mathrm{Q}}{\mathrm{A} \varepsilon_0}{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} V = Ed = {tex}\frac{\mathrm{Q} d}{\mathrm{~A} \varepsilon_0}{/tex} Capacitance, C = {tex}\frac{\mathrm{Q}}{\mathrm{V}}=\frac{\varepsilon_0 \mathrm{~A}}{d}{/tex} Ratio of capacitances Capacitance equals the magnitude of the charge on each plate needed to raise the potential difference between the plates by unity. Capacitance without dielectric, {tex}C=\frac{A \varepsilon_{0}}{d}{/tex} Capacitance when filled with dielectric having thickness {tex}\frac{3 d}{4}{/tex} {tex}C=\frac{A \varepsilon_{0}}{\left(d-t+\frac{t}{x}\right)}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{A E_{0}}{\left(d-\frac{3 d}{4}+\frac{3 d}{4 \kappa}\right)}{/tex} [As t {tex}=\frac{3 d}{4}{/tex} ] {tex}=\frac{4 \varepsilon_{0} k A}{d(x+3)}{/tex} Ratio {tex}\frac{C^{\prime}}{C}=\frac{A \varepsilon_{0} 4 \kappa}{d(k+3)} \times \frac{d}{A \varepsilon_{0}}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{4 \kappa}{(x+3)}{/tex}
solution: The capacitance C of a capacitor is defined as the ratio of the maximum charge Q that can be stored in a capacitor to the applied voltage V across its plates. Electric field between the plates of capacitor E = {tex}\frac{\sigma}{\varepsilon_0}=\frac{\mathrm{Q}}{\mathrm{A} \varepsilon_0}{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} V = Ed = {tex}\frac{\mathrm{Q} d}{\mathrm{~A} \varepsilon_0}{/tex} Capacitance, C = {tex}\frac{\mathrm{Q}}{\mathrm{V}}=\frac{\varepsilon_0 \mathrm{~A}}{d}{/tex} Ratio of capacitances Capacitance equals the magnitude of the charge on each plate needed to raise the potential difference between the plates by unity. Capacitance without dielectric, {tex}C=\frac{A \varepsilon_{0}}{d}{/tex} Capacitance when filled with dielectric having thickness {tex}\frac{3 d}{4}{/tex} {tex}C=\frac{A \varepsilon_{0}}{\left(d-t+\frac{t}{x}\right)}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{A E_{0}}{\left(d-\frac{3 d}{4}+\frac{3 d}{4 \kappa}\right)}{/tex} [As t {tex}=\frac{3 d}{4}{/tex} ] {tex}=\frac{4 \varepsilon_{0} k A}{d(x+3)}{/tex} Ratio {tex}\frac{C^{\prime}}{C}=\frac{A \varepsilon_{0} 4 \kappa}{d(k+3)} \times \frac{d}{A \varepsilon_{0}}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{4 \kappa}{(x+3)}{/tex}
solution_html:
1. The capacitance C of a capacitor is defined as the ratio of the maximum charge Q that can be stored in a capacitor to the applied voltage V across its plates.

    Electric field between the plates of capacitor
    E = {tex}\frac{\sigma}{\varepsilon_0}=\frac{\mathrm{Q}}{\mathrm{A} \varepsilon_0}{/tex}
    {tex}\therefore{/tex} V = Ed = {tex}\frac{\mathrm{Q} d}{\mathrm{~A} \varepsilon_0}{/tex}
    Capacitance, C = {tex}\frac{\mathrm{Q}}{\mathrm{V}}=\frac{\varepsilon_0 \mathrm{~A}}{d}{/tex}
  2. Ratio of capacitances
    1. Capacitance equals the magnitude of the charge on each plate needed to raise the potential difference between the plates by unity.
    2. Capacitance without dielectric,
      {tex}C=\frac{A \varepsilon_{0}}{d}{/tex}
      Capacitance when filled with dielectric having thickness {tex}\frac{3 d}{4}{/tex}
      {tex}C=\frac{A \varepsilon_{0}}{\left(d-t+\frac{t}{x}\right)}{/tex}
      {tex}=\frac{A E_{0}}{\left(d-\frac{3 d}{4}+\frac{3 d}{4 \kappa}\right)}{/tex} [As t {tex}=\frac{3 d}{4}{/tex}]
      {tex}=\frac{4 \varepsilon_{0} k A}{d(x+3)}{/tex}
      Ratio {tex}\frac{C^{\prime}}{C}=\frac{A \varepsilon_{0} 4 \kappa}{d(k+3)} \times \frac{d}{A \varepsilon_{0}}{/tex}
      {tex}=\frac{4 \kappa}{(x+3)}{/tex}

subject: Physics
question_html:
1. Find equivalent capacitance between A and B in the combination given below. Each capacitor is of 2 {tex}\mu{/tex}F capacitance.
  2. If a dc source of 7 V is connected across AB, how much charge is drawn from the source and what is the energy stored in the network?
question_text: Find equivalent capacitance between A and B in the combination given below. Each capacitor is of 2 {tex}\mu{/tex} F capacitance. If a dc source of 7 V is connected across AB, how much charge is drawn from the source and what is the energy stored in the network?
explanation: The equivalent network of the capacitors is shown in the figure. Capacitors C 2 , C 3 and C 4 are in parallel. {tex}\therefore{/tex} C 234 = C 2 + C 3 + C 4 = 2 + 2 + 2 = 6 {tex}\mu{/tex} F Now, C 1 , C 234 and C 5 form a series combination. {tex}\frac{1}{C_{e q}}=\frac{1}{C_{1}}+\frac{1}{C_{234}}+\frac{1}{C_{5}}=\frac{1}{2}+\frac{1}{6}+\frac{1}{2}=\frac{7}{6}{/tex} {tex}\therefore \quad C_{\text {eq }}=\frac{6}{7} \mu \mathbf{F}{/tex} Charge drawn from the 7 V source, {tex}Q=C_{e q} V=\frac{6}{7} \mu \mathrm{F} \times 7 \mathrm{~V}=6 \mu \mathrm{C}{/tex} Energy stored, {tex}U=\frac{Q^{2}}{2 C_{e q}}=\frac{\left(6 \times 10^{-6}\right)^{2} \times 7}{2 \times 6 \times 10^{-6}} \mathrm{~J}=21 \times 10^{-6} \mathrm{~J}=21 \mu \mathrm{J}{/tex}
solution: The equivalent network of the capacitors is shown in the figure. Capacitors C 2 , C 3 and C 4 are in parallel. {tex}\therefore{/tex} C 234 = C 2 + C 3 + C 4 = 2 + 2 + 2 = 6 {tex}\mu{/tex} F Now, C 1 , C 234 and C 5 form a series combination. {tex}\frac{1}{C_{e q}}=\frac{1}{C_{1}}+\frac{1}{C_{234}}+\frac{1}{C_{5}}=\frac{1}{2}+\frac{1}{6}+\frac{1}{2}=\frac{7}{6}{/tex} {tex}\therefore \quad C_{\text {eq }}=\frac{6}{7} \mu \mathbf{F}{/tex} Charge drawn from the 7 V source, {tex}Q=C_{e q} V=\frac{6}{7} \mu \mathrm{F} \times 7 \mathrm{~V}=6 \mu \mathrm{C}{/tex} Energy stored, {tex}U=\frac{Q^{2}}{2 C_{e q}}=\frac{\left(6 \times 10^{-6}\right)^{2} \times 7}{2 \times 6 \times 10^{-6}} \mathrm{~J}=21 \times 10^{-6} \mathrm{~J}=21 \mu \mathrm{J}{/tex}
solution_html:
1. The equivalent network of the capacitors is shown in the figure.

    Capacitors C2, C3 and C4 are in parallel.
    {tex}\therefore{/tex} C234 = C2 + C3 + C4 = 2 + 2 + 2 = 6 {tex}\mu{/tex}F
    Now, C1, C234 and C5 form a series combination.
    {tex}\frac{1}{C_{e q}}=\frac{1}{C_{1}}+\frac{1}{C_{234}}+\frac{1}{C_{5}}=\frac{1}{2}+\frac{1}{6}+\frac{1}{2}=\frac{7}{6}{/tex}
    {tex}\therefore \quad C_{\text {eq }}=\frac{6}{7} \mu \mathbf{F}{/tex}
  2. Charge drawn from the 7 V source,
    {tex}Q=C_{e q} V=\frac{6}{7} \mu \mathrm{F} \times 7 \mathrm{~V}=6 \mu \mathrm{C}{/tex}
    Energy stored,
    {tex}U=\frac{Q^{2}}{2 C_{e q}}=\frac{\left(6 \times 10^{-6}\right)^{2} \times 7}{2 \times 6 \times 10^{-6}} \mathrm{~J}=21 \times 10^{-6} \mathrm{~J}=21 \mu \mathrm{J}{/tex}

subject: Physics
question_html: A charge q is moved from a point A above a dipole of dipole moment p to a point B below the dipole in an equatorial plane without acceleration. Find the work done in this process.
question_text: A charge q is moved from a point A above a dipole of dipole moment p to a point B below the dipole in an equatorial plane without acceleration. Find the work done in this process.
explanation: We know work done in moving a charge from one point to another is given by the relation, W = QΔV where W is the work done in the process, Q is the charge, ΔV is the potential difference or change in potential in going from one point to another. We know electric dipole moment of a dipole is given by, p = q.d where, q is magnitude of either of charge and d is separation between them Now plane passing through the midpoint joining the two charges is called an equatorial plane, the equatorial plane is an equipotential surface, i.e. potential is same everywhere or it is constant when the charge moves from A to B, there is no change in potential as both points lie on the same equatorial plane so potential difference or change in potential is ΔV = 0 V Now the magnitude of charge is Q = q C So the work done is W = q × 0 = 0 J i.e. work done in the process is 0 J
solution: We know work done in moving a charge from one point to another is given by the relation, W = QΔV where W is the work done in the process, Q is the charge, ΔV is the potential difference or change in potential in going from one point to another. We know electric dipole moment of a dipole is given by, p = q.d where, q is magnitude of either of charge and d is separation between them Now plane passing through the midpoint joining the two charges is called an equatorial plane, the equatorial plane is an equipotential surface, i.e. potential is same everywhere or it is constant when the charge moves from A to B, there is no change in potential as both points lie on the same equatorial plane so potential difference or change in potential is ΔV = 0 V Now the magnitude of charge is Q = q C So the work done is W = q × 0 = 0 J i.e. work done in the process is 0 J
solution_html:
We know work done in moving a charge from one point to another is given by the relation,
W = QΔV
where W is the work done in the process, Q is the charge, ΔV is the potential difference or change in potential in going from one point to another.
 We know electric dipole moment of a dipole is given by,
p = q.d
where, q is magnitude of either of charge and d is separation between them
Now plane passing through the midpoint joining the two charges is called an equatorial plane, the equatorial plane is an equipotential surface, i.e. potential is same everywhere or it is constant when the charge moves from A to B, there is no change in potential as both points lie on the same equatorial plane so potential difference or change in potential is
ΔV = 0 V
Now the magnitude of charge is
Q = q C
So the work done is
W = q × 0 = 0 J
i.e. work done in the process is 0 J

question_html:
In the following arrangement of capacitors, the energy stored in the 6µF capacitor is E. Find the value of the following:

  1. energy stored in 12µF capacitor
  2. energy stored in 3µF capacitor
  3. total energy drawn from the battery
question_text: In the following arrangement of capacitors, the energy stored in the 6µF capacitor is E. Find the value of the following: energy stored in 12µF capacitor energy stored in 3µF capacitor total energy drawn from the battery
explanation: As given in the question, energy of the 6 µF capacitor is E. Let V be the potential difference along the capacitor of capacitance 6 µF. From the mathematical formula, Since, {tex}\frac { 1 } { 2 } C V ^ { 2 } = E{/tex} {tex}\frac { 1 } { 2 } \times 6 \times 10 ^ { - 6 } \times V ^ { 2 } = E \Rightarrow V ^ { 2 } = \frac { E } { 3 } \times 10 ^ { 6 }{/tex} ... (i) Since, potential is same for parallel connection of capacitors of capacitances of 6 {tex}\mu{/tex} F and 12 {tex}\mu F{/tex} . Hence the potential through 12µF capacitor is also V. Hence, energy of 12µF capacitor is {tex}E _ { 12 } = \frac { 1 } { 2 } \times 12 \times 10 ^ { - 6 } \times V ^ { 2 }{/tex} [From Eq.(i)] {tex}= \frac { 1 } { 2 } \times 12 \times 10 ^ { - 6 } \times \frac { E } { 3 } \times 10 ^ { 6 }{/tex} = 2E Since, charge remains constant in series, the charge on 6µF and 12µF capacitors combined will be equal to the net charge of the circuit and also the charge on 3 µF capacitor. Using the formula, Q = CV, we can write {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} (6 + 12) {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -6 {tex}\times{/tex} V = 3 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -6 {tex}\times{/tex} V' [as equivalent capacitance of the parallel combination of 6 {tex}\mu F{/tex} and 12 {tex}\mu F{/tex} = (6 + 12) {tex}\mu F{/tex} ] {tex}\therefore{/tex} V' = 6V Squaring the above equation on both sides, we get V' 2 = 36V 2 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} V' 2 = 12E {tex}\times{/tex} 10 6 [putting the value of V 2 from eq(i)] {tex}\therefore{/tex} E 3 = {tex}\frac { 1 } { 2 } \times 3 \times 10 ^ { - 6 } \times 12 E \times 10 ^ { 6 }{/tex} =18E [energy stored in 3 {tex}\mu F {/tex} capacitor] Total energy drawn from battery is E' = E + E 12 + E 3 = E + 2E + 18E = 21E
solution: As given in the question, energy of the 6 µF capacitor is E. Let V be the potential difference along the capacitor of capacitance 6 µF. From the mathematical formula, Since, {tex}\frac { 1 } { 2 } C V ^ { 2 } = E{/tex} {tex}\frac { 1 } { 2 } \times 6 \times 10 ^ { - 6 } \times V ^ { 2 } = E \Rightarrow V ^ { 2 } = \frac { E } { 3 } \times 10 ^ { 6 }{/tex} ... (i) Since, potential is same for parallel connection of capacitors of capacitances of 6 {tex}\mu{/tex} F and 12 {tex}\mu F{/tex} . Hence the potential through 12µF capacitor is also V. Hence, energy of 12µF capacitor is {tex}E _ { 12 } = \frac { 1 } { 2 } \times 12 \times 10 ^ { - 6 } \times V ^ { 2 }{/tex} [From Eq.(i)] {tex}= \frac { 1 } { 2 } \times 12 \times 10 ^ { - 6 } \times \frac { E } { 3 } \times 10 ^ { 6 }{/tex} = 2E Since, charge remains constant in series, the charge on 6µF and 12µF capacitors combined will be equal to the net charge of the circuit and also the charge on 3 µF capacitor. Using the formula, Q = CV, we can write {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} (6 + 12) {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -6 {tex}\times{/tex} V = 3 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -6 {tex}\times{/tex} V' [as equivalent capacitance of the parallel combination of 6 {tex}\mu F{/tex} and 12 {tex}\mu F{/tex} = (6 + 12) {tex}\mu F{/tex} ] {tex}\therefore{/tex} V' = 6V Squaring the above equation on both sides, we get V' 2 = 36V 2 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} V' 2 = 12E {tex}\times{/tex} 10 6 [putting the value of V 2 from eq(i)] {tex}\therefore{/tex} E 3 = {tex}\frac { 1 } { 2 } \times 3 \times 10 ^ { - 6 } \times 12 E \times 10 ^ { 6 }{/tex} =18E [energy stored in 3 {tex}\mu F {/tex} capacitor] Total energy drawn from battery is E' = E + E 12 + E 3 = E + 2E + 18E = 21E
solution_html:
1. As given in the question, energy of the 6 µF capacitor is E. Let V be the potential difference along the capacitor of capacitance 6 µF. From the mathematical formula,
    Since, {tex}\frac { 1 } { 2 } C V ^ { 2 } = E{/tex}
    {tex}\frac { 1 } { 2 } \times 6 \times 10 ^ { - 6 } \times V ^ { 2 } = E \Rightarrow V ^ { 2 } = \frac { E } { 3 } \times 10 ^ { 6 }{/tex} ... (i)
    Since, potential is same for parallel connection of capacitors of capacitances of 6{tex}\mu{/tex}F and 12{tex}\mu F{/tex}. Hence the potential through 12µF capacitor is also V. Hence, energy of 12µF capacitor is
    {tex}E _ { 12 } = \frac { 1 } { 2 } \times 12 \times 10 ^ { - 6 } \times V ^ { 2 }{/tex} [From Eq.(i)]
    {tex}= \frac { 1 } { 2 } \times 12 \times 10 ^ { - 6 } \times \frac { E } { 3 } \times 10 ^ { 6 }{/tex} = 2E
  2. Since, charge remains constant in series, the charge on 6µF and 12µF capacitors combined will be equal to the net charge of the circuit and also the charge on 3 µF capacitor.
    Using the formula, Q = CV, we can write
    {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} (6 + 12) {tex}\times{/tex} 10-6 {tex}\times{/tex} V = 3 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-6 {tex}\times{/tex} V'
    [as equivalent capacitance of the parallel combination of 6{tex}\mu F{/tex} and 12 {tex}\mu F{/tex}  = (6 + 12) {tex}\mu F{/tex}]
    {tex}\therefore{/tex} V' = 6V 
    Squaring the above equation on both sides, we get
    V'2 = 36V2 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} V'2 = 12E{tex}\times{/tex}106 [putting the value of V2 from eq(i)]
    {tex}\therefore{/tex} E3 = {tex}\frac { 1 } { 2 } \times 3 \times 10 ^ { - 6 } \times 12 E \times 10 ^ { 6 }{/tex} =18E [energy stored in 3{tex}\mu F {/tex} capacitor]
  3. Total energy drawn from battery is E' = E + E12 + E3 = E + 2E + 18E = 21E

question_html:
Define an equipotential surface. Draw equipotential surfaces

  1. in case of a single point charge
  2. in a constant electric field in Z-direction. Why the equipotential surfaces about a single charge are not equidistant?
  3. Can electric field exist tangential to an equipotential surface? Give reason.
question_text: Define an equipotential surface. Draw equipotential surfaces in case of a single point charge in a constant electric field in Z-direction. Why the equipotential surfaces about a single charge are not equidistant? Can electric field exist tangential to an equipotential surface? Give reason.
explanation: Any surface that has the same electric potential at every point on it and no work is done to move a point charge from one point to another on it is called an equipotential surface. Equipotential surfaces in case of single point charge are concentric spheres keeping the charge at the centre. Equipotential surfaces are the parallel planes lying in the YZ-plane along the Z axis, when the electric field is in Z-direction. The equipotential surfaces due to a single point charge is represented by concentric spherical shells of increasing radius, so they are not equidistant, the separation dr between equipotential surface will go on increasing with a decrease in the electric field. No, the electric field does not exist tangentially to an equipotential surface because no work is done in moving a charge from one point to other on an equipotential surface. This indicates that the line integral of the electric field along the equipotential surface is zero. i.e. {tex}\int \vec E . \vec {dl} =0{/tex} . Hence, the equipotential surface is always perpendicular to the electric field lines at each and every point on the surface.
solution: Any surface that has the same electric potential at every point on it and no work is done to move a point charge from one point to another on it is called an equipotential surface. Equipotential surfaces in case of single point charge are concentric spheres keeping the charge at the centre. Equipotential surfaces are the parallel planes lying in the YZ-plane along the Z axis, when the electric field is in Z-direction. The equipotential surfaces due to a single point charge is represented by concentric spherical shells of increasing radius, so they are not equidistant, the separation dr between equipotential surface will go on increasing with a decrease in the electric field. No, the electric field does not exist tangentially to an equipotential surface because no work is done in moving a charge from one point to other on an equipotential surface. This indicates that the line integral of the electric field along the equipotential surface is zero. i.e. {tex}\int \vec E . \vec {dl} =0{/tex} . Hence, the equipotential surface is always perpendicular to the electric field lines at each and every point on the surface.
solution_html:
Any surface that has the same electric potential at every point on it and no work is done to move a point charge from one point to another on it is called an equipotential surface.

  1. Equipotential surfaces in case of single point charge are concentric spheres keeping the charge at the centre.
  2. Equipotential surfaces are the parallel planes lying in the YZ-plane along the Z axis, when the electric field is in Z-direction.

    The equipotential surfaces due to a single point charge is represented by concentric spherical shells of increasing radius, so they are not equidistant, the separation dr between equipotential surface will go on increasing with a decrease in the electric field.
  3. No, the electric field does not exist tangentially to an equipotential surface because no work is done in moving a charge from one point to other on an equipotential surface. This indicates that the line integral of the electric field along the equipotential surface is zero. i.e. {tex}\int \vec E . \vec {dl} =0{/tex}. Hence, the equipotential surface is always perpendicular to the electric field lines at each and every point on the surface.
