question_html: Find the ratio of the potential differences that must be applied across the parallel and series combination of two capacitors C1 and C2 with their capacitances in the ratio 1 : 2, so that the energy stored in these two cases becomes the same.
question_text: Find the ratio of the potential differences that must be applied across the parallel and series combination of two capacitors C 1 and C 2 with their capacitances in the ratio 1 : 2, so that the energy stored in these two cases becomes the same.
explanation: Total energy stored in series or parallel combination of capacitors is equal to the stored energy in the equivalent capacitor. In parallel combination, total energy stored in both the capacitors {tex}= \frac { 1 } { 2 } C _ { 1 } V _ { 1 } ^ { 2 } + \frac { 1 } { 2 } C _ { 2 } V _ { 1 } ^ { 2 }{/tex} ..... (i) (since C = C 1 + C 2 in parallel combination of two capacitors of capacitances C 1 and C 2 ) In series combination, energy stored in the the equivalent capacitor = {tex}\frac { 1 } { 2 } \frac { C _ { 1 } C _ { 2 } } { \left( C _ { 1 } + C _ { 2 } \right) } V _ { 2 } ^ { 2 }{/tex} ...(ii) (applying the formula of equivalent capacitance for series combination of the above two capacitors) According to the question, energy in both the cases is same so, {tex}\left( \frac { 1 } { 2 } C _ { 1 } + \frac { 1 } { 2 } C _ { 2 } \right) V _ { 1 } ^ { 2 } = \frac { C _ { 1 } C _ { 2 } } {2(C _ { 1 } + C _ { 2 } ) } V _ { 2 } ^ { 2 }{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \frac { V _ { 1 } ^ { 2 } } { V _ { 2 } ^ { 2 } } = \frac { C _ { 1 } C _ { 2 } \times 2 } { 2( C _ { 1 } + C _ { 2 } ) \left( C _ { 1 } + C _ { 2 } \right) } \Rightarrow \frac { V _ { 1 } } { V _ { 2 } } = \frac { \sqrt { C _ { 1 } C _ { 2 } } } { C _ { 1 } + C _ { 2 } }{/tex} But, {tex}\frac { C _ { 1 } } { C _ { 2 } } = \frac { 1 } { 2 } \Rightarrow C _ { 2 } = 2 C _ { 1 }{/tex} So, {tex}\frac { V _ { 1 } } { V _ { 2 } } = \frac { \sqrt { C _ { 1 } \times 2 C _ { 1 } } } { C _ { 1 } + 2 C _ { 1 } } = \frac { \sqrt { 2 } C _ { 1 } } { 3 C _ { 1 } } = \frac { \sqrt { 2 } } { 3 }{/tex}
solution: Total energy stored in series or parallel combination of capacitors is equal to the stored energy in the equivalent capacitor. In parallel combination, total energy stored in both the capacitors {tex}= \frac { 1 } { 2 } C _ { 1 } V _ { 1 } ^ { 2 } + \frac { 1 } { 2 } C _ { 2 } V _ { 1 } ^ { 2 }{/tex} ..... (i) (since C = C 1 + C 2 in parallel combination of two capacitors of capacitances C 1 and C 2 ) In series combination, energy stored in the the equivalent capacitor = {tex}\frac { 1 } { 2 } \frac { C _ { 1 } C _ { 2 } } { \left( C _ { 1 } + C _ { 2 } \right) } V _ { 2 } ^ { 2 }{/tex} ...(ii) (applying the formula of equivalent capacitance for series combination of the above two capacitors) According to the question, energy in both the cases is same so, {tex}\left( \frac { 1 } { 2 } C _ { 1 } + \frac { 1 } { 2 } C _ { 2 } \right) V _ { 1 } ^ { 2 } = \frac { C _ { 1 } C _ { 2 } } {2(C _ { 1 } + C _ { 2 } ) } V _ { 2 } ^ { 2 }{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \frac { V _ { 1 } ^ { 2 } } { V _ { 2 } ^ { 2 } } = \frac { C _ { 1 } C _ { 2 } \times 2 } { 2( C _ { 1 } + C _ { 2 } ) \left( C _ { 1 } + C _ { 2 } \right) } \Rightarrow \frac { V _ { 1 } } { V _ { 2 } } = \frac { \sqrt { C _ { 1 } C _ { 2 } } } { C _ { 1 } + C _ { 2 } }{/tex} But, {tex}\frac { C _ { 1 } } { C _ { 2 } } = \frac { 1 } { 2 } \Rightarrow C _ { 2 } = 2 C _ { 1 }{/tex} So, {tex}\frac { V _ { 1 } } { V _ { 2 } } = \frac { \sqrt { C _ { 1 } \times 2 C _ { 1 } } } { C _ { 1 } + 2 C _ { 1 } } = \frac { \sqrt { 2 } C _ { 1 } } { 3 C _ { 1 } } = \frac { \sqrt { 2 } } { 3 }{/tex}
solution_html:
Total energy stored in series or parallel combination of capacitors is equal to the stored energy in the equivalent capacitor.
In parallel combination, total energy stored in both the capacitors
{tex}= \frac { 1 } { 2 } C _ { 1 } V _ { 1 } ^ { 2 } + \frac { 1 } { 2 } C _ { 2 } V _ { 1 } ^ { 2 }{/tex}..... (i) (since C = C1 + C2 in parallel combination of two capacitors of capacitances C1 and C2)
In series combination, energy stored in the the equivalent capacitor = {tex}\frac { 1 } { 2 } \frac { C _ { 1 } C _ { 2 } } { \left( C _ { 1 } + C _ { 2 } \right) } V _ { 2 } ^ { 2 }{/tex} ...(ii) (applying the formula of equivalent capacitance for series combination of the above two capacitors)
 According to the question, energy in both the cases is same so,
{tex}\left( \frac { 1 } { 2 } C _ { 1 } + \frac { 1 } { 2 } C _ { 2 } \right) V _ { 1 } ^ { 2 } = \frac { C _ { 1 } C _ { 2 } } {2(C _ { 1 } + C _ { 2 } ) } V _ { 2 } ^ { 2 }{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad \frac { V _ { 1 } ^ { 2 } } { V _ { 2 } ^ { 2 } } = \frac { C _ { 1 } C _ { 2 } \times 2 } { 2( C _ { 1 } + C _ { 2 } ) \left( C _ { 1 } + C _ { 2 } \right) } \Rightarrow \frac { V _ { 1 } } { V _ { 2 } } = \frac { \sqrt { C _ { 1 } C _ { 2 } } } { C _ { 1 } + C _ { 2 } }{/tex}
But, {tex}\frac { C _ { 1 } } { C _ { 2 } } = \frac { 1 } { 2 } \Rightarrow C _ { 2 } = 2 C _ { 1 }{/tex}
So, {tex}\frac { V _ { 1 } } { V _ { 2 } } = \frac { \sqrt { C _ { 1 } \times 2 C _ { 1 } } } { C _ { 1 } + 2 C _ { 1 } } = \frac { \sqrt { 2 } C _ { 1 } } { 3 C _ { 1 } } = \frac { \sqrt { 2 } } { 3 }{/tex}

question_html:
1. If two similar large plates, each of area A having surface charge densities +{tex}\sigma{/tex} and - {tex}\sigma{/tex} are separated by a distance d in air, then find the expression for
    1. the field at points between the two plates and on the outer side of the plates. Specify the direction of the field in each case.
    2. the potential difference between the plates.
    3. the capacitance of the capacitor so formed.
  2. Two metallic spheres of radii R and 2R are charged so that both of these have the same surface charge density {tex}\sigma{/tex}. If they are connected to each other with a conducting wire, in which direction will the charge flow and why?
question_text: If two similar large plates, each of area A having surface charge densities + {tex}\sigma{/tex} and - {tex}\sigma{/tex} are separated by a distance d in air, then find the expression for the field at points between the two plates and on the outer side of the plates. Specify the direction of the field in each case. the potential difference between the plates. the capacitance of the capacitor so formed. Two metallic spheres of radii R and 2R are charged so that both of these have the same surface charge density {tex}\sigma{/tex} . If they are connected to each other with a conducting wire, in which direction will the charge flow and why?
explanation: Consider the figure shown below: Electric field due to the plate of the positive charge of charge density {tex}+ \sigma{/tex} at point P, is given by E ​​​​​​1 = {tex}\sigma/2\varepsilon_0{/tex} Magnitude of electric field due to the other plate of negative charge density {tex}- \sigma{/tex} ,is given by E ​​​​​​2 = {tex}-\sigma/2\varepsilon_0{/tex} In , the inner region between the plates 1 and 2 , electric field due to the two charged plates add up, is given by {tex}E _ { \mathrm { net } } = E_1 + E_2 = \frac { \sigma } { 2 \varepsilon _ { 0 } } + \frac { \sigma } { 2 \varepsilon _ { 0 } } = \frac { \sigma } { \varepsilon _ { 0 } }{/tex} Outside the plate, electric field will be equal to zero because of the opposite directions of the electric fields E ​​​​​​1 and E ​​​​​​2 ​​​​​ there. Potential difference between the plates of the capacitor is given by V = Ed = {tex}\sigma d /\varepsilon_0{/tex} ( {tex}\because E = \sigma / \varepsilon _ { 0 }{/tex} ) Capacitance of the capacitor is given by ( {tex}\because{/tex} Q = CV) {tex}C = \frac { Q } { V } = \frac { \sigma A } { \sigma d } \varepsilon _ { 0 } = \frac { \varepsilon _ { 0 } A } { d }{/tex} Consider the figure shown below: Potential at the surface of the sphere of radius R, {tex}= \frac { k q } { R } \quad \left[ \because q = \sigma \times 4 {tex}\pi{/tex} R ^ { 2 } \right]{/tex} {tex}= \quad \frac { k \sigma 4 \pi R ^ { 2 } } { R } = \sigma k 4 \pi R = 4 k \sigma \pi R{/tex} Potential at the surface of the second sphere of radius twice the previous one i.e. 2R, {tex}= \frac { k q } { 2 R } \quad \left[ \because q = \sigma \times 4 \pi ( 2 R ) ^ { 2 } = 16 \sigma \pi R ^ { 2 } \right]{/tex} {tex}= \frac { k 16 \sigma \pi R ^ { 2 } } { 2 R } = 8 k \sigma \pi R{/tex} We know that charge always flows from the higher potential surface to lower potential surface. Since the potential of the bigger sphere is more, so charge will flow from sphere of radius 2R to the sphere of radius R after connecting both the spheres by a conducting wire
solution: Consider the figure shown below: Electric field due to the plate of the positive charge of charge density {tex}+ \sigma{/tex} at point P, is given by E ​​​​​​1 = {tex}\sigma/2\varepsilon_0{/tex} Magnitude of electric field due to the other plate of negative charge density {tex}- \sigma{/tex} ,is given by E ​​​​​​2 = {tex}-\sigma/2\varepsilon_0{/tex} In , the inner region between the plates 1 and 2 , electric field due to the two charged plates add up, is given by {tex}E _ { \mathrm { net } } = E_1 + E_2 = \frac { \sigma } { 2 \varepsilon _ { 0 } } + \frac { \sigma } { 2 \varepsilon _ { 0 } } = \frac { \sigma } { \varepsilon _ { 0 } }{/tex} Outside the plate, electric field will be equal to zero because of the opposite directions of the electric fields E ​​​​​​1 and E ​​​​​​2 ​​​​​ there. Potential difference between the plates of the capacitor is given by V = Ed = {tex}\sigma d /\varepsilon_0{/tex} ( {tex}\because E = \sigma / \varepsilon _ { 0 }{/tex} ) Capacitance of the capacitor is given by ( {tex}\because{/tex} Q = CV) {tex}C = \frac { Q } { V } = \frac { \sigma A } { \sigma d } \varepsilon _ { 0 } = \frac { \varepsilon _ { 0 } A } { d }{/tex} Consider the figure shown below: Potential at the surface of the sphere of radius R, {tex}= \frac { k q } { R } \quad \left[ \because q = \sigma \times 4 {tex}\pi{/tex} R ^ { 2 } \right]{/tex} {tex}= \quad \frac { k \sigma 4 \pi R ^ { 2 } } { R } = \sigma k 4 \pi R = 4 k \sigma \pi R{/tex} Potential at the surface of the second sphere of radius twice the previous one i.e. 2R, {tex}= \frac { k q } { 2 R } \quad \left[ \because q = \sigma \times 4 \pi ( 2 R ) ^ { 2 } = 16 \sigma \pi R ^ { 2 } \right]{/tex} {tex}= \frac { k 16 \sigma \pi R ^ { 2 } } { 2 R } = 8 k \sigma \pi R{/tex} We know that charge always flows from the higher potential surface to lower potential surface. Since the potential of the bigger sphere is more, so charge will flow from sphere of radius 2R to the sphere of radius R after connecting both the spheres by a conducting wire
solution_html:
1. Consider the figure shown below:
    1. Electric field due to the plate of the positive charge of charge density {tex}+ \sigma{/tex} at point P, is given by 
      E​​​​​​1 = {tex}\sigma/2\varepsilon_0{/tex}
      Magnitude of electric field due to the other plate of negative charge density {tex}- \sigma{/tex},is given by 
      E​​​​​​2 = {tex}-\sigma/2\varepsilon_0{/tex}
      In , the inner region between the plates 1 and 2 , electric field due to the two charged plates add up, is given by 
      {tex}E _ { \mathrm { net } } = E_1 + E_2 = \frac { \sigma } { 2 \varepsilon _ { 0 } } + \frac { \sigma } { 2 \varepsilon _ { 0 } } = \frac { \sigma } { \varepsilon _ { 0 } }{/tex}
      Outside the plate, electric field will be equal to zero because of the opposite directions of the electric fields E​​​​​​1 and E​​​​​​2​​​​​ there.
    2. Potential difference between the plates of the capacitor is given by
      V = Ed = {tex}\sigma d /\varepsilon_0{/tex} ({tex}\because E = \sigma / \varepsilon _ { 0 }{/tex} )
    3. Capacitance of the capacitor is given by
       ({tex}\because{/tex} Q = CV)
      {tex}C = \frac { Q } { V } = \frac { \sigma A } { \sigma d } \varepsilon _ { 0 } = \frac { \varepsilon _ { 0 } A } { d }{/tex}
  2. Consider the figure shown below:

    Potential at the surface of the sphere of radius R,
    {tex}= \frac { k q } { R } \quad \left[ \because q = \sigma \times 4{tex}\pi{/tex}R ^ { 2 } \right]{/tex}
    {tex}= \quad \frac { k \sigma 4 \pi R ^ { 2 } } { R } = \sigma k 4 \pi R = 4 k \sigma \pi R{/tex}
    Potential at the surface of the second sphere of radius twice the previous one i.e. 2R,
    {tex}= \frac { k q } { 2 R } \quad \left[ \because q = \sigma \times 4 \pi ( 2 R ) ^ { 2 } = 16 \sigma \pi R ^ { 2 } \right]{/tex}
    {tex}= \frac { k 16 \sigma \pi R ^ { 2 } } { 2 R } = 8 k \sigma \pi R{/tex}
    We know that charge always flows from the higher potential surface to lower potential surface. Since the potential of the bigger sphere is more, so charge will flow from sphere of radius 2R to the sphere of radius R after connecting both the spheres by a conducting wire

question_html:
1. Obtain the expression for the potential to show due to a point charge.
  2. Potential, due to an electric dipole (length 2a) varies as the inverse square of the distance of the 'field point' from the centre of the dipole for r > a.
question_text: Obtain the expression for the potential to show due to a point charge. Potential, due to an electric dipole (length 2a) varies as the inverse square of the distance of the 'field point' from the centre of the dipole for r > a.
explanation: Consider a point charge 'Q' kept at point O. Let P be the field point at distance r. At some intermediate point P the electrostatic force on the unit positive charge is : {tex}=\frac{Q \times 1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0} r^{2}}{/tex} Work done against this force from r' to r' + {tex}\triangle{/tex} r' is {tex}\Delta W=\frac{Q}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0} r^{2}} \Delta r^{\prime}{/tex} Total work done W by the external Force from {tex}\infty{/tex} to r {tex}W = - \int\limits_\infty ^r {\frac{Q}{{4\pi{\varepsilon_0}r{'^2}}}\triangle r'} {/tex} {tex}W=\frac{Q}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0} r}{/tex} Hence potential at this point V = W = {tex}\frac{Q}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0} r}{/tex} Electrostatic potential is zero at infinity, electrostatic potential due to a single charge is spherically symmetric. The potential at point P due to charge (-q) {tex}V_{1}=\frac{-1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}} \cdot \frac{q}{(r+a)}{/tex} Potential due to charge +q {tex}V_{2}=\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}} \cdot \frac{q}{(r-a)}{/tex} Hence total potential at point P V = V 1 + V 2 {tex}=\frac{q}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}}\left[\frac{-1}{(r+a)}+\frac{1}{(r-a)}\right]{/tex} {tex}=\frac{q \times 2 a}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}\left(r^{2}-a^{2}\right)}{/tex} {tex}V=\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}} \frac{p}{\left(r^{2}-a^{2}\right)}{/tex} where, {tex}\vec{p}=q \times 2 a{/tex} = dipole moment for r >> a {tex}\Rightarrow V=\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}} \cdot \frac{p}{r^{2}}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow V \propto \frac{1}{r^{2}}{/tex}
solution: Consider a point charge 'Q' kept at point O. Let P be the field point at distance r. At some intermediate point P the electrostatic force on the unit positive charge is : {tex}=\frac{Q \times 1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0} r^{2}}{/tex} Work done against this force from r' to r' + {tex}\triangle{/tex} r' is {tex}\Delta W=\frac{Q}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0} r^{2}} \Delta r^{\prime}{/tex} Total work done W by the external Force from {tex}\infty{/tex} to r {tex}W = - \int\limits_\infty ^r {\frac{Q}{{4\pi{\varepsilon_0}r{'^2}}}\triangle r'} {/tex} {tex}W=\frac{Q}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0} r}{/tex} Hence potential at this point V = W = {tex}\frac{Q}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0} r}{/tex} Electrostatic potential is zero at infinity, electrostatic potential due to a single charge is spherically symmetric. The potential at point P due to charge (-q) {tex}V_{1}=\frac{-1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}} \cdot \frac{q}{(r+a)}{/tex} Potential due to charge +q {tex}V_{2}=\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}} \cdot \frac{q}{(r-a)}{/tex} Hence total potential at point P V = V 1 + V 2 {tex}=\frac{q}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}}\left[\frac{-1}{(r+a)}+\frac{1}{(r-a)}\right]{/tex} {tex}=\frac{q \times 2 a}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}\left(r^{2}-a^{2}\right)}{/tex} {tex}V=\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}} \frac{p}{\left(r^{2}-a^{2}\right)}{/tex} where, {tex}\vec{p}=q \times 2 a{/tex} = dipole moment for r >> a {tex}\Rightarrow V=\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}} \cdot \frac{p}{r^{2}}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow V \propto \frac{1}{r^{2}}{/tex}
solution_html:
1. Consider a point charge 'Q' kept at point O. Let P be the field point at distance r.
    At some intermediate point P the electrostatic force on the unit positive charge is :
    {tex}=\frac{Q \times 1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0} r^{2}}{/tex}
    Work done against this force from r' to r' + {tex}\triangle{/tex}r' is
    {tex}\Delta W=\frac{Q}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0} r^{2}} \Delta r^{\prime}{/tex}
    Total work done W by the external Force from {tex}\infty{/tex} to r
    {tex}W = - \int\limits_\infty ^r {\frac{Q}{{4\pi{\varepsilon_0}r{'^2}}}\triangle r'} {/tex} 
    {tex}W=\frac{Q}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0} r}{/tex} 
    Hence potential at this point
    V = W = {tex}\frac{Q}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0} r}{/tex} Electrostatic potential is zero at infinity, electrostatic potential due to a single charge is spherically symmetric.
  2.  
    The potential at point P due to charge (-q)
    {tex}V_{1}=\frac{-1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}} \cdot \frac{q}{(r+a)}{/tex}
    Potential due to charge +q
    {tex}V_{2}=\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}} \cdot \frac{q}{(r-a)}{/tex}
    Hence total potential at point P
    V = V1 + V2 {tex}=\frac{q}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}}\left[\frac{-1}{(r+a)}+\frac{1}{(r-a)}\right]{/tex}
    {tex}=\frac{q \times 2 a}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}\left(r^{2}-a^{2}\right)}{/tex}
    {tex}V=\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}} \frac{p}{\left(r^{2}-a^{2}\right)}{/tex}
    where, {tex}\vec{p}=q \times 2 a{/tex} = dipole moment for r >> a
    {tex}\Rightarrow V=\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}} \cdot \frac{p}{r^{2}}{/tex}
    {tex}\Rightarrow V \propto \frac{1}{r^{2}}{/tex}

question_html:
A parallel plate capacitor, of capacitance 20 {tex}\mu{/tex}F, is connected to a 100 V supply. After some time, the battery is disconnected, and the space, between the plates of the capacitor, is filled with a dielectric of dielectric constant 5. Calculate the energy stored in the capacitor:

  1. Before
  2. After the dielectric has been put in between its plates.
question_text: A parallel plate capacitor, of capacitance 20 {tex}\mu{/tex} F, is connected to a 100 V supply. After some time, the battery is disconnected, and the space, between the plates of the capacitor, is filled with a dielectric of dielectric constant 5. Calculate the energy stored in the capacitor: Before After the dielectric has been put in between its plates.
explanation: Capacitance = 20×10 -6 F. Charge stored, Q = CV = 20 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -6 {tex}\times{/tex} 100 C = 2000 {tex}\mu{/tex} C New value of capacitance when dielectric is introduced = 5 {tex}\times{/tex} 20 {tex}\mu{/tex} F = 100 {tex}\mu{/tex} F Energy stored in a capacitor {tex}=\frac{1}{2} \frac{Q^{2}}{C}\left(=\frac{1}{2} C V^{2}=\frac{1}{2} Q V\right){/tex} Energy stored before dielectric {tex}=\frac{1}{2} \times \frac{\left[2000 \times 10^{-6}\right] \times\left(2000 \times 10^{-6}\right)}{20 \times 10^{-6}}{/tex} = 0.1 J Energy stored after the dielectric is introduced ( {tex}\because{/tex} there is no change in the value of Q) {tex}=\frac{1}{2} \times \frac{2000 \times 10^{-6} \times 2000 \times 10^{-6}}{100 \times 10^{-6}}{/tex} = 0.02 J
solution: Capacitance = 20×10 -6 F. Charge stored, Q = CV = 20 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -6 {tex}\times{/tex} 100 C = 2000 {tex}\mu{/tex} C New value of capacitance when dielectric is introduced = 5 {tex}\times{/tex} 20 {tex}\mu{/tex} F = 100 {tex}\mu{/tex} F Energy stored in a capacitor {tex}=\frac{1}{2} \frac{Q^{2}}{C}\left(=\frac{1}{2} C V^{2}=\frac{1}{2} Q V\right){/tex} Energy stored before dielectric {tex}=\frac{1}{2} \times \frac{\left[2000 \times 10^{-6}\right] \times\left(2000 \times 10^{-6}\right)}{20 \times 10^{-6}}{/tex} = 0.1 J Energy stored after the dielectric is introduced ( {tex}\because{/tex} there is no change in the value of Q) {tex}=\frac{1}{2} \times \frac{2000 \times 10^{-6} \times 2000 \times 10^{-6}}{100 \times 10^{-6}}{/tex} = 0.02 J
solution_html:
Capacitance = 20×10-6 F.  Charge stored, Q = CV = 20 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-6 {tex}\times{/tex} 100 C
= 2000 {tex}\mu{/tex}C
New value of capacitance when dielectric is introduced
= 5 {tex}\times{/tex} 20 {tex}\mu{/tex}F
= 100 {tex}\mu{/tex}F
Energy stored in a capacitor
{tex}=\frac{1}{2} \frac{Q^{2}}{C}\left(=\frac{1}{2} C V^{2}=\frac{1}{2} Q V\right){/tex}

  1. Energy stored before dielectric
    {tex}=\frac{1}{2} \times \frac{\left[2000 \times 10^{-6}\right] \times\left(2000 \times 10^{-6}\right)}{20 \times 10^{-6}}{/tex}
    = 0.1 J
  2. Energy stored after the dielectric is introduced
    ({tex}\because{/tex} there is no change in the value of Q)
    {tex}=\frac{1}{2} \times \frac{2000 \times 10^{-6} \times 2000 \times 10^{-6}}{100 \times 10^{-6}}{/tex}
    = 0.02 J

question_html:
1. Define the S.I. unit of capacitance.
  2. Obtain an expression for the capacitance of a parallel plate capacitor.
  3. Derive the expression for the effective capacitance of a series combination of n-capacitors.
question_text: Define the S.I. unit of capacitance. Obtain an expression for the capacitance of a parallel plate capacitor. Derive the expression for the effective capacitance of a series combination of n-capacitors.
explanation: Si unit of the capacitor is farad. When a charge of one coulomb, produces a potential difference of one volt between the plates of a capacitor, the capacitance is one farad. Q = CV {tex}\Rightarrow C=\frac{Q}{V}{/tex} When Q = 1 coulomb, V = 1 volt 1 farad {tex}=\frac{1 \text { coulomb }}{1 \text { volt }}{/tex} Electric field on the inside of the parallel plate capacitor due to one of the plates is given by:-E 1 = {tex}\sigma/2\epsilon_0{/tex} . Total Electric field between the plates of the capacitor is given by E = 2E 1 = Q/A {tex}\in{/tex} 0 {tex}\therefore{/tex} V = Ed {tex}=\frac{Q d}{A \varepsilon_{0}}{/tex} Capacitance, {tex}C=\frac{Q}{V}=\frac{\varepsilon_{0} A}{d}{/tex} In series combination, charge on each capacitor is same Let it be Q, {tex}V_{1}=\frac{Q}{C_{1}}{/tex} {tex}V_{2}=\frac{Q}{C_{2}}{/tex} {tex}V_{n}=\frac{Q}{C_{n}}{/tex} V = V 1 + V 2 + V 3 + ... + V n {tex}V=\frac{Q}{C_{1}}+\frac{Q}{C_{2}}{/tex} {tex}+\frac{Q}{C_{3}}+\ldots+\frac{Q}{C_{n}}{/tex} {tex}\frac{V}{Q}=\frac{1}{C_{1}}+\frac{1}{C_{2}}{/tex} {tex}+\frac{1}{C_{3}}+\ldots+\frac{1}{C_{n}}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{1}{C}=\frac{1}{C_{1}}+\frac{1}{C_{2}}{/tex} {tex}+\frac{1}{C_{3}}+\ldots+\frac{1}{C_{n}}{/tex}
solution: Si unit of the capacitor is farad. When a charge of one coulomb, produces a potential difference of one volt between the plates of a capacitor, the capacitance is one farad. Q = CV {tex}\Rightarrow C=\frac{Q}{V}{/tex} When Q = 1 coulomb, V = 1 volt 1 farad {tex}=\frac{1 \text { coulomb }}{1 \text { volt }}{/tex} Electric field on the inside of the parallel plate capacitor due to one of the plates is given by:-E 1 = {tex}\sigma/2\epsilon_0{/tex} . Total Electric field between the plates of the capacitor is given by E = 2E 1 = Q/A {tex}\in{/tex} 0 {tex}\therefore{/tex} V = Ed {tex}=\frac{Q d}{A \varepsilon_{0}}{/tex} Capacitance, {tex}C=\frac{Q}{V}=\frac{\varepsilon_{0} A}{d}{/tex} In series combination, charge on each capacitor is same Let it be Q, {tex}V_{1}=\frac{Q}{C_{1}}{/tex} {tex}V_{2}=\frac{Q}{C_{2}}{/tex} {tex}V_{n}=\frac{Q}{C_{n}}{/tex} V = V 1 + V 2 + V 3 + ... + V n {tex}V=\frac{Q}{C_{1}}+\frac{Q}{C_{2}}{/tex} {tex}+\frac{Q}{C_{3}}+\ldots+\frac{Q}{C_{n}}{/tex} {tex}\frac{V}{Q}=\frac{1}{C_{1}}+\frac{1}{C_{2}}{/tex} {tex}+\frac{1}{C_{3}}+\ldots+\frac{1}{C_{n}}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{1}{C}=\frac{1}{C_{1}}+\frac{1}{C_{2}}{/tex} {tex}+\frac{1}{C_{3}}+\ldots+\frac{1}{C_{n}}{/tex}
solution_html:
1. Si unit of the capacitor is farad. When a charge of one coulomb, produces a potential difference of one volt between the plates of a capacitor, the capacitance is one farad.
    Q = CV {tex}\Rightarrow C=\frac{Q}{V}{/tex}
         When Q = 1 coulomb, V = 1 volt
          1 farad {tex}=\frac{1 \text { coulomb }}{1 \text { volt }}{/tex}

  2. Electric field on the inside of the parallel plate capacitor due to one of the plates is given by:-E1={tex}\sigma/2\epsilon_0{/tex}. Total Electric field between the plates of the capacitor is given by E = 2E1 = Q/A{tex}\in{/tex}0
    {tex}\therefore{/tex}  V = Ed {tex}=\frac{Q d}{A \varepsilon_{0}}{/tex}
     Capacitance, {tex}C=\frac{Q}{V}=\frac{\varepsilon_{0} A}{d}{/tex}



  3. In series combination, charge on each capacitor is same
    Let it be Q,
    {tex}V_{1}=\frac{Q}{C_{1}}{/tex}
    {tex}V_{2}=\frac{Q}{C_{2}}{/tex}
    {tex}V_{n}=\frac{Q}{C_{n}}{/tex}
    V = V1 + V2 + V3 + ... + Vn
    {tex}V=\frac{Q}{C_{1}}+\frac{Q}{C_{2}}{/tex} {tex}+\frac{Q}{C_{3}}+\ldots+\frac{Q}{C_{n}}{/tex}
    {tex}\frac{V}{Q}=\frac{1}{C_{1}}+\frac{1}{C_{2}}{/tex} {tex}+\frac{1}{C_{3}}+\ldots+\frac{1}{C_{n}}{/tex}
    {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{1}{C}=\frac{1}{C_{1}}+\frac{1}{C_{2}}{/tex} {tex}+\frac{1}{C_{3}}+\ldots+\frac{1}{C_{n}}{/tex}

subject: Physics
question_html:
1. Obtain the expression for the potential due to a point charge.
  2. Use the above expression to show that the potential, due to an electric dipole (length 2a), varies as the inverse square of the distance r of the field point from the centre of the dipole for r >> a.
question_text: Obtain the expression for the potential due to a point charge. Use the above expression to show that the potential, due to an electric dipole (length 2a), varies as the inverse square of the distance r of the field point from the centre of the dipole for r >> a.
explanation: Electric potential due to a point charge: Consider a positive point charge q placed at the origin O. We wish to calculate its electric potential at a point P at distance r from it, as shown in the figure. By definition, the electric potential at point P will be equal to the amount of work done in bringing a unit positive charge from infinity to the point P. Suppose a test charge q 0 is placed at point A at distance x from O. By Coulomb's law, the electrostatic force acting on charge q 0 is {tex}F=\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}} \cdot \frac{q q_{0}}{x^{2}}{/tex} The force F acts away from the charge q. The small work done in moving the test charge q 0 from A to B through small-displacement {tex}d\vec{ x}{/tex} against the electrostatic force is {tex}d W=\vec{F} \cdot d\vec{ x}=F d x \cos 180^{\circ}=-F d x{/tex} The total work done in moving the charge q 0 from infinity to the point P will be {tex}W=\int d W=-\int_{\infty}^{r} F d x=-\int_{\infty}^{r} \frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}} \cdot \frac{q q_{0}}{x^{2}} d x{/tex} {tex}=-\frac{q q_{0}}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}} \int_{\infty}^{r} x^{-2} d x=-\frac{q q_{0}}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}}\left[-\frac{1}{x}\right]^{r}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{q q_{0}}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}}\left[\frac{1}{r}-\frac{1}{\infty}\right]=\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}}, \frac{q q_{0}}{r}{/tex} Hence the work done in moving a unit test charge from infinity to the point P, or the electric potential at point P is {tex}V=\frac{W}{q_{0}} \quad \text { or } \quad V=\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}} \cdot \frac{q}{r}{/tex}
solution: Electric potential due to a point charge: Consider a positive point charge q placed at the origin O. We wish to calculate its electric potential at a point P at distance r from it, as shown in the figure. By definition, the electric potential at point P will be equal to the amount of work done in bringing a unit positive charge from infinity to the point P. Suppose a test charge q 0 is placed at point A at distance x from O. By Coulomb's law, the electrostatic force acting on charge q 0 is {tex}F=\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}} \cdot \frac{q q_{0}}{x^{2}}{/tex} The force F acts away from the charge q. The small work done in moving the test charge q 0 from A to B through small-displacement {tex}d\vec{ x}{/tex} against the electrostatic force is {tex}d W=\vec{F} \cdot d\vec{ x}=F d x \cos 180^{\circ}=-F d x{/tex} The total work done in moving the charge q 0 from infinity to the point P will be {tex}W=\int d W=-\int_{\infty}^{r} F d x=-\int_{\infty}^{r} \frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}} \cdot \frac{q q_{0}}{x^{2}} d x{/tex} {tex}=-\frac{q q_{0}}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}} \int_{\infty}^{r} x^{-2} d x=-\frac{q q_{0}}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}}\left[-\frac{1}{x}\right]^{r}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{q q_{0}}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}}\left[\frac{1}{r}-\frac{1}{\infty}\right]=\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}}, \frac{q q_{0}}{r}{/tex} Hence the work done in moving a unit test charge from infinity to the point P, or the electric potential at point P is {tex}V=\frac{W}{q_{0}} \quad \text { or } \quad V=\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}} \cdot \frac{q}{r}{/tex}
solution_html:
Electric potential due to a point charge: Consider a positive point charge q placed at the origin O. We wish to calculate its electric potential at a point P at distance r from it, as shown in the figure. By definition, the electric potential at point P will be equal to the amount of work done in bringing a unit positive charge from infinity to the point P.

Suppose a test charge q0 is placed at point A at distance x from O. By Coulomb's law, the electrostatic force acting on charge q0 is 
{tex}F=\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}} \cdot \frac{q q_{0}}{x^{2}}{/tex}
The force F acts away from the charge q. The small work done in moving the test charge q0 from A to B through small-displacement {tex}d\vec{ x}{/tex} against the electrostatic force is
{tex}d W=\vec{F} \cdot d\vec{ x}=F d x \cos 180^{\circ}=-F d x{/tex}
The total work done in moving the charge q0 from infinity to the point P will be
{tex}W=\int d W=-\int_{\infty}^{r} F d x=-\int_{\infty}^{r} \frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}} \cdot \frac{q q_{0}}{x^{2}} d x{/tex}
{tex}=-\frac{q q_{0}}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}} \int_{\infty}^{r} x^{-2} d x=-\frac{q q_{0}}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}}\left[-\frac{1}{x}\right]^{r}{/tex}
{tex}=\frac{q q_{0}}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}}\left[\frac{1}{r}-\frac{1}{\infty}\right]=\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}}, \frac{q q_{0}}{r}{/tex}
Hence the work done in moving a unit test charge from infinity to the point P, or the electric potential at point P is
{tex}V=\frac{W}{q_{0}} \quad \text { or } \quad V=\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}} \cdot \frac{q}{r}{/tex}

subject: Physics
question_html:
1. Explain why, for any charge configuration, the equipotential surface through a point is normal to the electric field at that point.
    Draw a sketch of equipotential surfaces due to a single charge (-q), depicting the electric field lines due to the charge.
  2. Obtain an expression for the work done to dissociate the system of three charges placed at the vertices of an equilateral triangle of side a as shown alongside.
question_text: Explain why, for any charge configuration, the equipotential surface through a point is normal to the electric field at that point. Draw a sketch of equipotential surfaces due to a single charge (-q), depicting the electric field lines due to the charge. Obtain an expression for the work done to dissociate the system of three charges placed at the vertices of an equilateral triangle of side a as shown alongside.
explanation: The work done in moving a charge from one point to another on an equipotential surface is zero. If the field is not normal to an equipotential surface, it would have a non zero component along the surface. This would imply that work would have to be done to move a charge on the surface which is contradictory to the definition of equipotential surface. Mathematically Work done to move a charge dq, on a surface, can be expressed as {tex}d W=d q(\vec{E} \cdot \overrightarrow{d r}){/tex} But dW = 0 on an equipotential surface {tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\vec{E} \perp \overrightarrow{d r}{/tex} Equipotential surfaces for a charge -q is shown alongside. Work required to dissociate the system of three charges = - P.E. of the system {tex}=-\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}}\left[\frac{q \times(-4 q)}{a}+\frac{q \times 2 q}{a}+\frac{(-4 q) \times 2 q}{a}\right]{/tex} {tex}=-\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}}\left[-4 q^{2}+2 q^{2}-8 q^{2}\right]=+\frac{10 q^{2}}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0} a}{/tex}
solution: The work done in moving a charge from one point to another on an equipotential surface is zero. If the field is not normal to an equipotential surface, it would have a non zero component along the surface. This would imply that work would have to be done to move a charge on the surface which is contradictory to the definition of equipotential surface. Mathematically Work done to move a charge dq, on a surface, can be expressed as {tex}d W=d q(\vec{E} \cdot \overrightarrow{d r}){/tex} But dW = 0 on an equipotential surface {tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\vec{E} \perp \overrightarrow{d r}{/tex} Equipotential surfaces for a charge -q is shown alongside. Work required to dissociate the system of three charges = - P.E. of the system {tex}=-\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}}\left[\frac{q \times(-4 q)}{a}+\frac{q \times 2 q}{a}+\frac{(-4 q) \times 2 q}{a}\right]{/tex} {tex}=-\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}}\left[-4 q^{2}+2 q^{2}-8 q^{2}\right]=+\frac{10 q^{2}}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0} a}{/tex}
solution_html:
1. The work done in moving a charge from one point to another on an equipotential surface is zero. If the field is not normal to an equipotential surface, it would have a non zero component along the surface. This would imply that work would have to be done to move a charge on the surface which is contradictory to the definition of equipotential surface.
    Mathematically
    Work done to move a charge dq, on a surface, can be expressed as {tex}d W=d q(\vec{E} \cdot \overrightarrow{d r}){/tex}
    But dW = 0 on an equipotential surface
    {tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\vec{E} \perp \overrightarrow{d r}{/tex}
    Equipotential surfaces for a charge -q is shown alongside.
  2. Work required to dissociate the system of three charges
    = - P.E. of the system
    {tex}=-\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}}\left[\frac{q \times(-4 q)}{a}+\frac{q \times 2 q}{a}+\frac{(-4 q) \times 2 q}{a}\right]{/tex}
    {tex}=-\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}}\left[-4 q^{2}+2 q^{2}-8 q^{2}\right]=+\frac{10 q^{2}}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0} a}{/tex}

question_html: A parallel plate capacitor of area A, plate separation d and capacitance C is filled with four dielectric materials having dielectric constants {tex}\kappa_1{/tex}, {tex}\kappa_2{/tex}, {tex}\kappa_3{/tex} and {tex}\kappa_4{/tex} as shown in the figure below. If a single dielectric material is to be used to have the same capacitance C in this capacitor, then its dielectric constant {tex}\kappa{/tex} is given by:
question_text: A parallel plate capacitor of area A, plate separation d and capacitance C is filled with four dielectric materials having dielectric constants {tex}\kappa_1{/tex} , {tex}\kappa_2{/tex} , {tex}\kappa_3{/tex} and {tex}\kappa_4{/tex} as shown in the figure below. If a single dielectric material is to be used to have the same capacitance C in this capacitor, then its dielectric constant {tex}\kappa{/tex} is given by:

options.explanation: {tex}C_{1}=\frac{\varepsilon_{0}}{(\frac d 2)} \frac{A}{3}\left(\kappa _{1}+\kappa _{2}+\kappa _{3}\right){/tex} {tex}C_{2}=\frac{\varepsilon_{0} A}{\frac d 2)} \kappa _{4}{/tex} {tex}\frac{1}{C}=\frac{1}{C_{1}}+\frac{1}{C_{2}}{/tex} {tex}\frac{d}{\varepsilon_{0} A \kappa }=\frac{1}{\frac{\varepsilon_{0}}{(\frac d 2) 3}\left(\kappa _{1}+\kappa _{2}+\kappa _{3}\right)}+\frac{1}{\frac{\varepsilon_{0} A}{(\frac d 2)} \kappa _{4}}{/tex} {tex}\frac{2}{\kappa}=\frac{3}{\kappa_{1}+\kappa_{2}+\kappa_{3}}+\frac{1}{\kappa_{4}}{/tex}

explanation: {tex}C_{1}=\frac{\varepsilon_{0}}{(\frac d 2)} \frac{A}{3}\left(\kappa _{1}+\kappa _{2}+\kappa _{3}\right){/tex} {tex}C_{2}=\frac{\varepsilon_{0} A}{\frac d 2)} \kappa _{4}{/tex} {tex}\frac{1}{C}=\frac{1}{C_{1}}+\frac{1}{C_{2}}{/tex} {tex}\frac{d}{\varepsilon_{0} A \kappa }=\frac{1}{\frac{\varepsilon_{0}}{(\frac d 2) 3}\left(\kappa _{1}+\kappa _{2}+\kappa _{3}\right)}+\frac{1}{\frac{\varepsilon_{0} A}{(\frac d 2)} \kappa _{4}}{/tex} {tex}\frac{2}{\kappa}=\frac{3}{\kappa_{1}+\kappa_{2}+\kappa_{3}}+\frac{1}{\kappa_{4}}{/tex}

subject: Physics
question_html:
In the following arrangement of capacitors, the energy stored in the 6 {tex}\mu {/tex}F capacitor is E. Find the value of the following:

  1. Energy stored in 12 {tex}\mu{/tex}F capacitor.
  2. Energy stored in 3 {tex}\mu{/tex}F capacitor.
  3. Total energy drawn from the battery.
question_text: In the following arrangement of capacitors, the energy stored in the 6 {tex}\mu {/tex} F capacitor is E. Find the value of the following: Energy stored in 12 {tex}\mu{/tex} F capacitor. Energy stored in 3 {tex}\mu{/tex} F capacitor. Total energy drawn from the battery.
explanation: E = {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} CV 2 = {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} {tex}\times{/tex} 6 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -6 V 2 = 3 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -6 V 2 {tex}\therefore V^{2}=\frac{E}{3 \times 10^{-6}}{/tex} Energy stored in 12 {tex}\mu{/tex} F capacitor {tex}=\frac{1}{2} \times 12 \times 10^{-6} \times \frac{E}{3 \times 10^{-6}}=2 E{/tex} Capacitance of the parallel combination = 6 + 12 = 18 {tex}\mu{/tex} F Charge on parallel combination, Q = CV = 18 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -6 V Charge on 3 {tex}\mu{/tex} F capacitor = Q = 3 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -6 V 1 {tex}\therefore{/tex} 3 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -6 V 1 = 18 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -6 V {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} V 1 = 6 V Energy stored in 3 {tex}\mu{/tex} F capacitor {tex}=\frac{1}{2} C V_{1}^{2}=\frac{1}{2} \times 3 \times 10^{-6} \times(6 V)^{2}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{1}{2} \times 3 \times 10^{-6} \times \frac{36 E}{3 \times 10^{-6}}=18 E{/tex} Total energy drawn = E + 2E + 18E = 21E
solution: E = {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} CV 2 = {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} {tex}\times{/tex} 6 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -6 V 2 = 3 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -6 V 2 {tex}\therefore V^{2}=\frac{E}{3 \times 10^{-6}}{/tex} Energy stored in 12 {tex}\mu{/tex} F capacitor {tex}=\frac{1}{2} \times 12 \times 10^{-6} \times \frac{E}{3 \times 10^{-6}}=2 E{/tex} Capacitance of the parallel combination = 6 + 12 = 18 {tex}\mu{/tex} F Charge on parallel combination, Q = CV = 18 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -6 V Charge on 3 {tex}\mu{/tex} F capacitor = Q = 3 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -6 V 1 {tex}\therefore{/tex} 3 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -6 V 1 = 18 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -6 V {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} V 1 = 6 V Energy stored in 3 {tex}\mu{/tex} F capacitor {tex}=\frac{1}{2} C V_{1}^{2}=\frac{1}{2} \times 3 \times 10^{-6} \times(6 V)^{2}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{1}{2} \times 3 \times 10^{-6} \times \frac{36 E}{3 \times 10^{-6}}=18 E{/tex} Total energy drawn = E + 2E + 18E = 21E
solution_html:
1. E = {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} CV2 = {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} {tex}\times{/tex} 6 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-6 V2 = 3 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-6 V2
    {tex}\therefore V^{2}=\frac{E}{3 \times 10^{-6}}{/tex}
    Energy stored in 12 {tex}\mu{/tex}F capacitor
    {tex}=\frac{1}{2} \times 12 \times 10^{-6} \times \frac{E}{3 \times 10^{-6}}=2 E{/tex}
  2. Capacitance of the parallel combination = 6 + 12 = 18{tex}\mu{/tex}F
    Charge on parallel combination,
    Q = CV = 18 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-6V
    Charge on 3{tex}\mu{/tex}F capacitor = Q = 3 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-6 V1
    {tex}\therefore{/tex} 3 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-6 V1 = 18 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-6 V {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} V1 = 6 V
    Energy stored in 3{tex}\mu{/tex}F capacitor
    {tex}=\frac{1}{2} C V_{1}^{2}=\frac{1}{2} \times 3 \times 10^{-6} \times(6 V)^{2}{/tex}
    {tex}=\frac{1}{2} \times 3 \times 10^{-6} \times \frac{36 E}{3 \times 10^{-6}}=18 E{/tex}
  3. Total energy drawn = E + 2E + 18E = 21E

subject: Physics
question_html: Three capacitors of capacitances C1, C2 and C3 are connected in series. Find their equivalent capacitance.
question_text: Three capacitors of capacitances C 1 , C 2 and C 3 are connected in series. Find their equivalent capacitance.
explanation: Capacitors in series. When the negative plate of one capacitor is connected to the positive plate of the second, and the negative of the second to the positive of third and so on, the capacitors are said to be connected in series. The figure shows three capacitors of capacitances C 1 , C 2 , and C 3 connected in series. A potential difference V is applied across the combination. This sets up +Q and -Q charges on the plates of capacitor as shown in the figure. The potential differences across the various capacitors are {tex}V_{1}=\frac{Q}{C_{1}}{/tex} , {tex}V_{2}=\frac{Q}{C_{2}}{/tex} , {tex}V_{3}=\frac{Q}{C_{3}}{/tex} For the series circuit, the sum of these potential differences must be equal to the applied potential difference. V = {tex}V_{1}+V_{2}+V_{3}=\frac{Q}{C_{1}}+\frac{Q}{C_{2}}+\frac{Q}{C_{3}}{/tex} or {tex}\frac{V}{Q}=\frac{1}{C_{1}}+\frac{1}{C_{2}}+\frac{1}{C_{3}}{/tex} ...(i) Clearly, the combination can be regarded as an effective capacitor with charge Q and potential difference V. If Cs is the equivalent capacitance of the series combination, then {tex}C_{s}=\frac{Q}{V}{/tex} or {tex}\frac{1}{C_{s}}=\frac{V}{Q}{/tex} ...(ii) From equations (i) and (ii), we get {tex}\frac{1}{C_{s}}=\frac{1}{C_{1}}+\frac{1}{C_{2}}+\frac{1}{C_{3}}{/tex} For a series combination of n capacitors, we can write {tex}\frac{1}{C_{s}}=\frac{1}{C_{1}}+\frac{1}{C_{2}}+ ...+\frac{1}{C_{n}}{/tex}
solution: Capacitors in series. When the negative plate of one capacitor is connected to the positive plate of the second, and the negative of the second to the positive of third and so on, the capacitors are said to be connected in series. The figure shows three capacitors of capacitances C 1 , C 2 , and C 3 connected in series. A potential difference V is applied across the combination. This sets up +Q and -Q charges on the plates of capacitor as shown in the figure. The potential differences across the various capacitors are {tex}V_{1}=\frac{Q}{C_{1}}{/tex} , {tex}V_{2}=\frac{Q}{C_{2}}{/tex} , {tex}V_{3}=\frac{Q}{C_{3}}{/tex} For the series circuit, the sum of these potential differences must be equal to the applied potential difference. V = {tex}V_{1}+V_{2}+V_{3}=\frac{Q}{C_{1}}+\frac{Q}{C_{2}}+\frac{Q}{C_{3}}{/tex} or {tex}\frac{V}{Q}=\frac{1}{C_{1}}+\frac{1}{C_{2}}+\frac{1}{C_{3}}{/tex} ...(i) Clearly, the combination can be regarded as an effective capacitor with charge Q and potential difference V. If Cs is the equivalent capacitance of the series combination, then {tex}C_{s}=\frac{Q}{V}{/tex} or {tex}\frac{1}{C_{s}}=\frac{V}{Q}{/tex} ...(ii) From equations (i) and (ii), we get {tex}\frac{1}{C_{s}}=\frac{1}{C_{1}}+\frac{1}{C_{2}}+\frac{1}{C_{3}}{/tex} For a series combination of n capacitors, we can write {tex}\frac{1}{C_{s}}=\frac{1}{C_{1}}+\frac{1}{C_{2}}+ ...+\frac{1}{C_{n}}{/tex}
solution_html:
Capacitors in series. When the negative plate of one capacitor is connected to the positive plate of the second, and the negative of the second to the positive of third and so on, the capacitors are said to be connected in series.
The figure shows three capacitors of capacitances C1, C2, and C3 connected in series. A potential difference V is applied across the combination. This sets up +Q and -Q charges on the plates of capacitor as shown in the figure. 

The potential differences across the various capacitors are
{tex}V_{1}=\frac{Q}{C_{1}}{/tex}, {tex}V_{2}=\frac{Q}{C_{2}}{/tex}, {tex}V_{3}=\frac{Q}{C_{3}}{/tex}
For the series circuit, the sum of these potential differences must be equal to the applied potential difference.
V = {tex}V_{1}+V_{2}+V_{3}=\frac{Q}{C_{1}}+\frac{Q}{C_{2}}+\frac{Q}{C_{3}}{/tex}
or {tex}\frac{V}{Q}=\frac{1}{C_{1}}+\frac{1}{C_{2}}+\frac{1}{C_{3}}{/tex} ...(i)
Clearly, the combination can be regarded as an effective capacitor with charge Q and potential difference V. If Cs is the equivalent capacitance of the series combination, then
{tex}C_{s}=\frac{Q}{V}{/tex}
or {tex}\frac{1}{C_{s}}=\frac{V}{Q}{/tex} ...(ii)
From equations (i) and (ii), we get
{tex}\frac{1}{C_{s}}=\frac{1}{C_{1}}+\frac{1}{C_{2}}+\frac{1}{C_{3}}{/tex}
For a series combination of n capacitors, we can write
{tex}\frac{1}{C_{s}}=\frac{1}{C_{1}}+\frac{1}{C_{2}}+ ...+\frac{1}{C_{n}}{/tex}

subject: Physics
question_html: Two point charges q and -2q are kept d distance apart. Find the location of point relative to charge q at which potential due to this system of charges is zero.
question_text: Two point charges q and -2q are kept d distance apart. Find the location of point relative to charge q at which potential due to this system of charges is zero.
explanation: Here, q A = q and q B = -2q. Say at a distance x from the charge q A , potential will be zero. Since the potential due to system of charges is zero, therefore {tex}V _ { P A } + V _ { P B } = 0 \Rightarrow \frac { k q } { x } = \frac { 2 k q } { ( d - x ) }{/tex} {tex}\therefore d - x = 2 x \Rightarrow 3 x = d \Rightarrow x = d / 3{/tex}
solution: Here, q A = q and q B = -2q. Say at a distance x from the charge q A , potential will be zero. Since the potential due to system of charges is zero, therefore {tex}V _ { P A } + V _ { P B } = 0 \Rightarrow \frac { k q } { x } = \frac { 2 k q } { ( d - x ) }{/tex} {tex}\therefore d - x = 2 x \Rightarrow 3 x = d \Rightarrow x = d / 3{/tex}
solution_html:
Here, qA = q and qB = -2q. Say at a distance x from the charge qA, potential will be zero.

Since the potential due to system of charges is zero, therefore
{tex}V _ { P A } + V _ { P B } = 0 \Rightarrow \frac { k q } { x } = \frac { 2 k q } { ( d - x ) }{/tex}
{tex}\therefore d - x = 2 x \Rightarrow 3 x = d \Rightarrow x = d / 3{/tex}

subject: Physics
question_html:
1. Derive the expression for the energy stored in parallel plate capacitor. Hence, obtain the expression for the energy density of the electric field.
  2. A fully charged parallel plate capacitor is connected across an uncharged identical capacitor. Show that the energy stored in the combination is less than the energy stored initially in the single capacitor.
question_text: Derive the expression for the energy stored in parallel plate capacitor. Hence, obtain the expression for the energy density of the electric field. A fully charged parallel plate capacitor is connected across an uncharged identical capacitor. Show that the energy stored in the combination is less than the energy stored initially in the single capacitor.
explanation: Let the total charge on the plates of the below capacitor is +Q and -Q respectively. {tex}\therefore{/tex} The potential difference between the plates of the above capacitor of capacitance C for an infinitesimal charge q is q/C. {tex}\therefore{/tex} Potential of condenser = q/C Small amount of work done in giving an additional charge dq to the condenser, {tex}d W = \frac { q } { C } \times d q{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} Total work done in giving a charge Q to the condenser, {tex}W = \int _ { q = 0 } ^ { q = Q }{\frac {q}{C} dq} = \frac { 1 } { C } \left[ \frac { q ^ { 2 } } { 2 } \right] _ { q = 0 } ^ { q = Q } \Rightarrow W = \frac { 1 } { C } \frac { Q ^ { 2 } } { 2 }{/tex} As, an electrostatic force is conservative, this work is stored in the form of potential energy (U) of the condenser. {tex}U = W = \frac { 1 } { 2 } \frac { Q ^ { 2 } } { C }{/tex} {tex}\because \quad Q = C V \Rightarrow U = \frac { 1 } { 2 } \frac { ( C V ) ^ { 2 } } { C } = \frac { 1 } { 2 } C V ^ { 2 }{/tex} {tex}\because \quad C V = Q \Rightarrow U = \frac { 1 } { 2 } Q V{/tex} Hence, {tex}U = \frac { 1 } { 2 } \frac { Q ^ { 2 } } { C } = \frac { 1 } { 2 } C V ^ { 2 } = \frac { 1 } { 2 } Q V{/tex} Energy density (u) is defined as the total energy per unit volume of the condenser. i.e., {tex}u = \frac { \text { Total energy } ( U ) } { \text { Volume } ( V ) } = \frac { \frac { 1 } { 2 } C V ^ { 2 } } { A d }{/tex} Using, {tex}C = \frac { \varepsilon _ { 0 } A } { d }{/tex} and V = Ed (Where V is the potential difference and E is the Electric field existing between the plates) We get, {tex}u = \frac { 1 } { 2 } \left( \frac { \varepsilon _ { 0 } A } { d } \right) \left( \frac { E ^ { 2 } d ^ { 2 } } { A d } \right)= \frac { 1 } { 2 } \varepsilon _ { 0 } E ^ { 2 }{/tex} Here, Energy density between plates of capacitors is directly proportional to electric field that exists between the plates of capacitor. Initial condition : If we consider a charged capacitor of capacitance C with potential difference V, then its charge would be given, q = CV and energy stored in it is given by {tex}U _ { 1 } = \frac { 1 } { 2 } C V ^ { 2 }{/tex} .....(i) When this charged capacitor is connected to uncharged capacitor, Let the common potential be V 1 , the charge flow from first capacitor to the other capacitor unless both the capacitor attains the common potential. {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} Q 1 = CV 1 and Q 2 = CV 2 Applying conservation of charge, Q = Q 1 + Q 2 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} CV = CV 1 + CV 2 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} V = V 1 + V 2 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} V 1 = {tex}\frac { V } { 2 }{/tex} [hence voltage will be equally divided between the capacitors] Total energy stored in both the capacitor is {tex}U _ { 2 } = \frac { 1 } { 2 } C V _ { 1 } ^ { 2 } + \frac { 1 } { 2 } C V _ { 1 } ^ { 2 } \Rightarrow U _ { 2 } = \frac { 1 } { 2 } C \left( \frac { V } { 2 } \right) ^ { 2 } + \frac { 1 } { 2 } C \left( \frac { V } { 2 } \right) ^ { 2 }{/tex} {tex}U _ { 2 } = \frac { 2 C V ^ { 2 } } { 8 } = \frac { 1 } { 4 } C V ^ { 2 }{/tex} From Eqs. (i) and (ii), we get, U 2 < U 1 It means that energy stored in the combination is less than that stored initially in the single capacitor. It is due to the fact that when the charge is transferred from one capacitor to another capacitor energy is wasted in transferring the charge.
solution: Let the total charge on the plates of the below capacitor is +Q and -Q respectively. {tex}\therefore{/tex} The potential difference between the plates of the above capacitor of capacitance C for an infinitesimal charge q is q/C. {tex}\therefore{/tex} Potential of condenser = q/C Small amount of work done in giving an additional charge dq to the condenser, {tex}d W = \frac { q } { C } \times d q{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} Total work done in giving a charge Q to the condenser, {tex}W = \int _ { q = 0 } ^ { q = Q }{\frac {q}{C} dq} = \frac { 1 } { C } \left[ \frac { q ^ { 2 } } { 2 } \right] _ { q = 0 } ^ { q = Q } \Rightarrow W = \frac { 1 } { C } \frac { Q ^ { 2 } } { 2 }{/tex} As, an electrostatic force is conservative, this work is stored in the form of potential energy (U) of the condenser. {tex}U = W = \frac { 1 } { 2 } \frac { Q ^ { 2 } } { C }{/tex} {tex}\because \quad Q = C V \Rightarrow U = \frac { 1 } { 2 } \frac { ( C V ) ^ { 2 } } { C } = \frac { 1 } { 2 } C V ^ { 2 }{/tex} {tex}\because \quad C V = Q \Rightarrow U = \frac { 1 } { 2 } Q V{/tex} Hence, {tex}U = \frac { 1 } { 2 } \frac { Q ^ { 2 } } { C } = \frac { 1 } { 2 } C V ^ { 2 } = \frac { 1 } { 2 } Q V{/tex} Energy density (u) is defined as the total energy per unit volume of the condenser. i.e., {tex}u = \frac { \text { Total energy } ( U ) } { \text { Volume } ( V ) } = \frac { \frac { 1 } { 2 } C V ^ { 2 } } { A d }{/tex} Using, {tex}C = \frac { \varepsilon _ { 0 } A } { d }{/tex} and V = Ed (Where V is the potential difference and E is the Electric field existing between the plates) We get, {tex}u = \frac { 1 } { 2 } \left( \frac { \varepsilon _ { 0 } A } { d } \right) \left( \frac { E ^ { 2 } d ^ { 2 } } { A d } \right)= \frac { 1 } { 2 } \varepsilon _ { 0 } E ^ { 2 }{/tex} Here, Energy density between plates of capacitors is directly proportional to electric field that exists between the plates of capacitor. Initial condition : If we consider a charged capacitor of capacitance C with potential difference V, then its charge would be given, q = CV and energy stored in it is given by {tex}U _ { 1 } = \frac { 1 } { 2 } C V ^ { 2 }{/tex} .....(i) When this charged capacitor is connected to uncharged capacitor, Let the common potential be V 1 , the charge flow from first capacitor to the other capacitor unless both the capacitor attains the common potential. {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} Q 1 = CV 1 and Q 2 = CV 2 Applying conservation of charge, Q = Q 1 + Q 2 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} CV = CV 1 + CV 2 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} V = V 1 + V 2 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} V 1 = {tex}\frac { V } { 2 }{/tex} [hence voltage will be equally divided between the capacitors] Total energy stored in both the capacitor is {tex}U _ { 2 } = \frac { 1 } { 2 } C V _ { 1 } ^ { 2 } + \frac { 1 } { 2 } C V _ { 1 } ^ { 2 } \Rightarrow U _ { 2 } = \frac { 1 } { 2 } C \left( \frac { V } { 2 } \right) ^ { 2 } + \frac { 1 } { 2 } C \left( \frac { V } { 2 } \right) ^ { 2 }{/tex} {tex}U _ { 2 } = \frac { 2 C V ^ { 2 } } { 8 } = \frac { 1 } { 4 } C V ^ { 2 }{/tex} From Eqs. (i) and (ii), we get, U 2 < U 1 It means that energy stored in the combination is less than that stored initially in the single capacitor. It is due to the fact that when the charge is transferred from one capacitor to another capacitor energy is wasted in transferring the charge.
solution_html:
1. Let the total charge on the plates of the below capacitor is +Q and -Q respectively.

    {tex}\therefore{/tex}The potential difference between the plates of the above capacitor of capacitance C for an infinitesimal charge q is q/C.
    {tex}\therefore{/tex} Potential of condenser = q/C
    Small amount of work done in giving an additional charge dq to the condenser,
    {tex}d W = \frac { q } { C } \times d q{/tex}
    {tex}\therefore{/tex}Total work done in giving a charge Q to the condenser,
    {tex}W = \int _ { q = 0 } ^ { q = Q }{\frac {q}{C} dq} = \frac { 1 } { C } \left[ \frac { q ^ { 2 } } { 2 } \right] _ { q = 0 } ^ { q = Q } \Rightarrow W = \frac { 1 } { C } \frac { Q ^ { 2 } } { 2 }{/tex}
    As, an electrostatic force is conservative, this work is stored in the form of potential energy (U) of the condenser.
    {tex}U = W = \frac { 1 } { 2 } \frac { Q ^ { 2 } } { C }{/tex}
    {tex}\because \quad Q = C V \Rightarrow U = \frac { 1 } { 2 } \frac { ( C V ) ^ { 2 } } { C } = \frac { 1 } { 2 } C V ^ { 2 }{/tex}
    {tex}\because \quad C V = Q \Rightarrow U = \frac { 1 } { 2 } Q V{/tex}
    Hence, {tex}U = \frac { 1 } { 2 } \frac { Q ^ { 2 } } { C } = \frac { 1 } { 2 } C V ^ { 2 } = \frac { 1 } { 2 } Q V{/tex}
    Energy density (u) is defined as the total energy per unit volume of the condenser.
    i.e., {tex}u = \frac { \text { Total energy } ( U ) } { \text { Volume } ( V ) } = \frac { \frac { 1 } { 2 } C V ^ { 2 } } { A d }{/tex}
    Using, {tex}C = \frac { \varepsilon _ { 0 } A } { d }{/tex} and V = Ed (Where V is the potential difference and E is the Electric field existing between the plates)
    We get, {tex}u = \frac { 1 } { 2 } \left( \frac { \varepsilon _ { 0 } A } { d } \right) \left( \frac { E ^ { 2 } d ^ { 2 } } { A d } \right)= \frac { 1 } { 2 } \varepsilon _ { 0 } E ^ { 2 }{/tex}
    Here, Energy density between plates of capacitors is directly proportional to electric field that exists between the plates of capacitor.
  2. Initial condition :
    If we consider a charged capacitor of capacitance C with potential difference V, then its charge would be given, q = CV

    and energy stored in it is given by
    {tex}U _ { 1 } = \frac { 1 } { 2 } C V ^ { 2 }{/tex}.....(i)
    When this charged capacitor is connected to uncharged capacitor,

     Let the common potential be V1, the charge flow from first capacitor to the other capacitor unless both the capacitor attains the common potential.
    {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} Q1 = CV1 and Q2 = CV2
    Applying conservation of charge,
     Q = Q1 + Q2 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} CV = CV1 + CV2
    {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} V = V1 + V2 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} V1 = {tex}\frac { V } { 2 }{/tex} [hence voltage will be equally divided between the capacitors]
     Total energy stored in both the capacitor is
    {tex}U _ { 2 } = \frac { 1 } { 2 } C V _ { 1 } ^ { 2 } + \frac { 1 } { 2 } C V _ { 1 } ^ { 2 } \Rightarrow U _ { 2 } = \frac { 1 } { 2 } C \left( \frac { V } { 2 } \right) ^ { 2 } + \frac { 1 } { 2 } C \left( \frac { V } { 2 } \right) ^ { 2 }{/tex}
    {tex}U _ { 2 } = \frac { 2 C V ^ { 2 } } { 8 } = \frac { 1 } { 4 } C V ^ { 2 }{/tex}
    From Eqs. (i) and (ii), we get, U2 < U1
    It means that energy stored in the combination is less than that stored initially in the single capacitor. It is due to the fact that when the charge is transferred from one capacitor to another capacitor energy is wasted in transferring the charge.

question_html: Why are electric field lines perpendicular at a point on an equipotential surface of a conductor?
question_text: Why are electric field lines perpendicular at a point on an equipotential surface of a conductor?
explanation: Electric field is always normal to the equipotential surface, because no work is done to move a test charge q 0 on the equipotential surface, i.e. W = q 0 (V A - V B ) = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} V A - V B = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow - \int \vec E. \vec {dl} =0{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \vec E \perp \vec {dl}{/tex}
solution: Electric field is always normal to the equipotential surface, because no work is done to move a test charge q 0 on the equipotential surface, i.e. W = q 0 (V A - V B ) = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} V A - V B = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow - \int \vec E. \vec {dl} =0{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \vec E \perp \vec {dl}{/tex}
solution_html:
Electric field is always normal to the equipotential surface, because no work is done to move a test charge q0 on the equipotential surface,
i.e. W = q0(VA - VB) = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} VA - VB = 0
{tex}\Rightarrow - \int \vec E. \vec {dl} =0{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \vec E \perp \vec {dl}{/tex}

question_html: Calculate the potential difference and the energy stored in the capacitor C2 in the circuit shown in the figure. Given potential at A is 90 V, C1 = 20 µF, C2 = 30 µF and C3 = 15µF.
question_text: Calculate the potential difference and the energy stored in the capacitor C 2 in the circuit shown in the figure. Given potential at A is 90 V, C 1 = 20 µF, C 2 = 30 µF and C 3 = 15µF.
explanation: For a series combination of three capacitors C 1 , C 2 and C 3 , the equivalent capacitance C eq will be {tex}\frac { 1 } { C _ { \mathrm { ca } } } = \frac { 1 } { C _ { 1 } } + \frac { 1 } { C _ { 2 } } + \frac { 1 } { C _ { 3 } } \Rightarrow \frac { 1 } { C _ { e q } } = \frac { 1 } { 20 } + \frac { 1 } { 30 } + \frac { 1 } { 15 }{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac { 1 } { C _ { \mathrm { eq } } } = \frac { 3 + 2 + 4 } { 60 } \Rightarrow C _ { \mathrm { eq } } = \frac { 60 } { 9 } \mu \mathrm { F } = \frac { 20 } { 3 } \mu \mathrm { F } {/tex} Total charge of the system of three capacitors, {tex}Q = C _ { \mathrm { eq } } V = \frac { 20 } { 3 } \times 10 ^ { - 6 } \times 90{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow Q = 600 \mu C{/tex} Charge on each capacitor will remain same as they are in series with the battery. Now, potential drop across the capacitor C 2 , {tex}V _ { 2 } = \frac { Q } { C _ { 2 } } = \frac { 600 \times 10 ^ { - 6 } } { 30 \times 10 ^ { - 6 } } = 20 \mathrm { V }{/tex} Hence stored electrical potential energy of capacitor C 2 is {tex}U_2 = \frac { 1 } { 2 } C _ { 2 } V _ { 2 } ^ { 2 }{/tex} {tex}U_2 = \frac { 1 } { 2 } \times 30 \times 10 ^ { - 6 } \times ( 20 ) ^ { 2 } = 6 \times 10 ^ { - 3 } \mathrm { J }{/tex}
solution: For a series combination of three capacitors C 1 , C 2 and C 3 , the equivalent capacitance C eq will be {tex}\frac { 1 } { C _ { \mathrm { ca } } } = \frac { 1 } { C _ { 1 } } + \frac { 1 } { C _ { 2 } } + \frac { 1 } { C _ { 3 } } \Rightarrow \frac { 1 } { C _ { e q } } = \frac { 1 } { 20 } + \frac { 1 } { 30 } + \frac { 1 } { 15 }{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac { 1 } { C _ { \mathrm { eq } } } = \frac { 3 + 2 + 4 } { 60 } \Rightarrow C _ { \mathrm { eq } } = \frac { 60 } { 9 } \mu \mathrm { F } = \frac { 20 } { 3 } \mu \mathrm { F } {/tex} Total charge of the system of three capacitors, {tex}Q = C _ { \mathrm { eq } } V = \frac { 20 } { 3 } \times 10 ^ { - 6 } \times 90{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow Q = 600 \mu C{/tex} Charge on each capacitor will remain same as they are in series with the battery. Now, potential drop across the capacitor C 2 , {tex}V _ { 2 } = \frac { Q } { C _ { 2 } } = \frac { 600 \times 10 ^ { - 6 } } { 30 \times 10 ^ { - 6 } } = 20 \mathrm { V }{/tex} Hence stored electrical potential energy of capacitor C 2 is {tex}U_2 = \frac { 1 } { 2 } C _ { 2 } V _ { 2 } ^ { 2 }{/tex} {tex}U_2 = \frac { 1 } { 2 } \times 30 \times 10 ^ { - 6 } \times ( 20 ) ^ { 2 } = 6 \times 10 ^ { - 3 } \mathrm { J }{/tex}
solution_html:
For a series combination of three capacitors C1, C2 and C3, the equivalent capacitance Ceq will be 
{tex}\frac { 1 } { C _ { \mathrm { ca } } } = \frac { 1 } { C _ { 1 } } + \frac { 1 } { C _ { 2 } } + \frac { 1 } { C _ { 3 } } \Rightarrow \frac { 1 } { C _ { e q } } = \frac { 1 } { 20 } + \frac { 1 } { 30 } + \frac { 1 } { 15 }{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac { 1 } { C _ { \mathrm { eq } } } = \frac { 3 + 2 + 4 } { 60 } \Rightarrow C _ { \mathrm { eq } } = \frac { 60 } { 9 } \mu \mathrm { F } = \frac { 20 } { 3 } \mu \mathrm { F } {/tex}
Total charge of the system of three capacitors,
{tex}Q = C _ { \mathrm { eq } } V = \frac { 20 } { 3 } \times 10 ^ { - 6 } \times 90{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow Q = 600 \mu C{/tex}
Charge on each capacitor will remain same as they are in series with the battery. Now, potential drop across the capacitor C2,
{tex}V _ { 2 } = \frac { Q } { C _ { 2 } } = \frac { 600 \times 10 ^ { - 6 } } { 30 \times 10 ^ { - 6 } } = 20 \mathrm { V }{/tex}
Hence stored electrical potential energy of capacitor C2 is {tex}U_2 = \frac { 1 } { 2 } C _ { 2 } V _ { 2 } ^ { 2 }{/tex}
{tex}U_2 = \frac { 1 } { 2 } \times 30 \times 10 ^ { - 6 } \times ( 20 ) ^ { 2 } = 6 \times 10 ^ { - 3 } \mathrm { J }{/tex}

question_html: Two capacitors of unknown capacitance C1 and C2 are connected first in series and then in parallel across a battery of 100 V. If the energy stored in the two combinations is 0.045 J and 0.25 J respectively, then determine the value of C1 and C2 . Also, calculate the charge on each capacitor in parallel combination.
question_text: Two capacitors of unknown capacitance C 1 and C 2 are connected first in series and then in parallel across a battery of 100 V. If the energy stored in the two combinations is 0.045 J and 0.25 J respectively, then determine the value of C 1 and C 2 . Also, calculate the charge on each capacitor in parallel combination.
explanation: When the capacitors are connected in parallel, Equivalent capacitance, C p = C 1 + C 2 The energy stored in the parallel combination of the capacitors, {tex}E _ { P } = \frac { 1 } { 2 } C _ { P } V ^ { 2 }{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad E _ { P } = \frac { 1 } { 2 } \left( C _ { 1 } + C _ { 2 } \right) ( 100 ) ^ { 2 } = 0.25 \mathrm { J }{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad C _ { 1 } + C _ { 2 } = 5 \times 10 ^ { - 5 }{/tex} ............(i) When the capacitors are connected in series, Equivalent capacitance, C s = {tex}\frac { C _ { 1 } C _ { 2 } } { C _ { 1 } + C _ { 2 } }{/tex} The energy stored in the series combination of the capacitors, {tex}E _ { S } = \frac { 1 } { 2 } C _ { S } V ^ { 2 }{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad E _ { s } = \frac { 1 } { 2 } \cdot \frac { C _ { 1 } C _ { 2 } } { C _ { 1 } + C _ { 2 } } ( 100 ) ^ { 2 } = 0.045 \mathrm { J }{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \frac { 1 } { 2 } \times \frac { C _ { 1 } C _ { 2 } } { 5 \times 10 ^ { - 5 } } ( 100 ) ^ { 2 } = 0.045 \mathrm { J }{/tex} [putting the value of C 1 + C 2 from equation (i)] {tex}\begin{aligned} \Rightarrow \quad C _ { 1 } C _ { 2 } & = 0.045 \times 10 ^ { - 4 } \times 5 \times 10 ^ { - 5 } \times 2 = 4.5 \times 10 ^ { - 10 } \end{aligned}{/tex} Now, {tex}\left( C _ { 1 } - C _ { 2 } \right) ^ { 2 } = \left( C _ { 1 } + C _ { 2 } \right) ^ { 2 } - 4 C _ { 1 } C _ { 2 }{/tex} {tex}\begin{aligned} \Rightarrow \left( C _ { 1 } - C _ { 2 } \right) ^ { 2 } & = 25 \times 10 ^ { - 10 } - 4 \times 4.5 \times 10 ^ { - 10 } = 7 \times 10 ^ { - 10 } \end{aligned}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \left( C _ { 1 } - C _ { 2 } \right) = \sqrt { 7 \times 10 ^ { - 10 } } = 2.64 \times 10 ^ { - 5 }{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad C _ { 1 } - C _ { 2 } = 2.64 \times 10 ^ { - 5 }{/tex} .......(ii) On solving Eqs. (i) and (ii), we get C 1 = 38.2 µF and C 2 = 11.8 µF Since the capacitors are in parallel combination, the charge stored in the capacitors will be different. Hence charge stored in the capacitor C 1 , Q 1 = C 1 V = 38.2 {tex} \times {/tex} 10 -6 {tex} \times {/tex} 100 = 38.2 {tex} \times {/tex} 10 -4 C And the charge stored in the second capacitor C 2 ,Q 2 = C 2 V = 11.8 {tex} \times {/tex} 10 - 6 {tex} \times {/tex} 100 = 11.8 {tex} \times {/tex} 10 -4 C
solution: When the capacitors are connected in parallel, Equivalent capacitance, C p = C 1 + C 2 The energy stored in the parallel combination of the capacitors, {tex}E _ { P } = \frac { 1 } { 2 } C _ { P } V ^ { 2 }{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad E _ { P } = \frac { 1 } { 2 } \left( C _ { 1 } + C _ { 2 } \right) ( 100 ) ^ { 2 } = 0.25 \mathrm { J }{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad C _ { 1 } + C _ { 2 } = 5 \times 10 ^ { - 5 }{/tex} ............(i) When the capacitors are connected in series, Equivalent capacitance, C s = {tex}\frac { C _ { 1 } C _ { 2 } } { C _ { 1 } + C _ { 2 } }{/tex} The energy stored in the series combination of the capacitors, {tex}E _ { S } = \frac { 1 } { 2 } C _ { S } V ^ { 2 }{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad E _ { s } = \frac { 1 } { 2 } \cdot \frac { C _ { 1 } C _ { 2 } } { C _ { 1 } + C _ { 2 } } ( 100 ) ^ { 2 } = 0.045 \mathrm { J }{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \frac { 1 } { 2 } \times \frac { C _ { 1 } C _ { 2 } } { 5 \times 10 ^ { - 5 } } ( 100 ) ^ { 2 } = 0.045 \mathrm { J }{/tex} [putting the value of C 1 + C 2 from equation (i)] {tex}\begin{aligned} \Rightarrow \quad C _ { 1 } C _ { 2 } & = 0.045 \times 10 ^ { - 4 } \times 5 \times 10 ^ { - 5 } \times 2 = 4.5 \times 10 ^ { - 10 } \end{aligned}{/tex} Now, {tex}\left( C _ { 1 } - C _ { 2 } \right) ^ { 2 } = \left( C _ { 1 } + C _ { 2 } \right) ^ { 2 } - 4 C _ { 1 } C _ { 2 }{/tex} {tex}\begin{aligned} \Rightarrow \left( C _ { 1 } - C _ { 2 } \right) ^ { 2 } & = 25 \times 10 ^ { - 10 } - 4 \times 4.5 \times 10 ^ { - 10 } = 7 \times 10 ^ { - 10 } \end{aligned}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \left( C _ { 1 } - C _ { 2 } \right) = \sqrt { 7 \times 10 ^ { - 10 } } = 2.64 \times 10 ^ { - 5 }{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad C _ { 1 } - C _ { 2 } = 2.64 \times 10 ^ { - 5 }{/tex} .......(ii) On solving Eqs. (i) and (ii), we get C 1 = 38.2 µF and C 2 = 11.8 µF Since the capacitors are in parallel combination, the charge stored in the capacitors will be different. Hence charge stored in the capacitor C 1 , Q 1 = C 1 V = 38.2 {tex} \times {/tex} 10 -6 {tex} \times {/tex} 100 = 38.2 {tex} \times {/tex} 10 -4 C And the charge stored in the second capacitor C 2 ,Q 2 = C 2 V = 11.8 {tex} \times {/tex} 10 - 6 {tex} \times {/tex} 100 = 11.8 {tex} \times {/tex} 10 -4 C
solution_html:
When the capacitors are connected in parallel,
Equivalent capacitance, Cp = C1 + C2
The energy stored in the parallel combination of the capacitors, {tex}E _ { P } = \frac { 1 } { 2 } C _ { P } V ^ { 2 }{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad E _ { P } = \frac { 1 } { 2 } \left( C _ { 1 } + C _ { 2 } \right) ( 100 ) ^ { 2 } = 0.25 \mathrm { J }{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad C _ { 1 } + C _ { 2 } = 5 \times 10 ^ { - 5 }{/tex}............(i)
When the capacitors are connected in series,
Equivalent capacitance, Cs = {tex}\frac { C _ { 1 } C _ { 2 } } { C _ { 1 } + C _ { 2 } }{/tex}
The energy stored in the series combination of the capacitors, {tex}E _ { S } = \frac { 1 } { 2 } C _ { S } V ^ { 2 }{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad E _ { s } = \frac { 1 } { 2 } \cdot \frac { C _ { 1 } C _ { 2 } } { C _ { 1 } + C _ { 2 } } ( 100 ) ^ { 2 } = 0.045 \mathrm { J }{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad \frac { 1 } { 2 } \times \frac { C _ { 1 } C _ { 2 } } { 5 \times 10 ^ { - 5 } } ( 100 ) ^ { 2 } = 0.045 \mathrm { J }{/tex} [putting the value of C1 + C2 from equation (i)]
{tex}\begin{aligned} \Rightarrow \quad C _ { 1 } C _ { 2 } & = 0.045 \times 10 ^ { - 4 } \times 5 \times 10 ^ { - 5 } \times 2 = 4.5 \times 10 ^ { - 10 } \end{aligned}{/tex}
Now,
{tex}\left( C _ { 1 } - C _ { 2 } \right) ^ { 2 } = \left( C _ { 1 } + C _ { 2 } \right) ^ { 2 } - 4 C _ { 1 } C _ { 2 }{/tex}
{tex}\begin{aligned} \Rightarrow \left( C _ { 1 } - C _ { 2 } \right) ^ { 2 } & = 25 \times 10 ^ { - 10 } - 4 \times 4.5 \times 10 ^ { - 10 } = 7 \times 10 ^ { - 10 } \end{aligned}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad \left( C _ { 1 } - C _ { 2 } \right) = \sqrt { 7 \times 10 ^ { - 10 } } = 2.64 \times 10 ^ { - 5 }{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad C _ { 1 } - C _ { 2 } = 2.64 \times 10 ^ { - 5 }{/tex}.......(ii)
On solving Eqs. (i) and (ii), we get
C1 = 38.2 µF and C2 = 11.8 µF
Since the capacitors are in parallel combination, the charge stored in the capacitors will be different.
Hence charge stored in the capacitor C1,
Q1 = C1V = 38.2 {tex} \times {/tex} 10-6 {tex} \times {/tex} 100 = 38.2 {tex} \times {/tex} 10-4C
And the charge stored in the second capacitor C2,Q2 = C2V = 11.8 {tex} \times {/tex} 10- 6 {tex} \times {/tex} 100 = 11.8 {tex} \times {/tex} 10-4C

subject: Physics
question_html:
1. Explain using suitable diagrams, the difference in the behaviour of a
    1. conductor and
    2. dielectric in the presence of external electric field. Define the terms polarisation of a dielectric and write its relation with susceptibility.
  2. A thin metallic spherical shell of radius R carries a charge Q on its surface. A point charge Q/2 is placed at its centre C and another charge + 2Q is placed outside the shell at a distance x from the centre as shown in the figure. Find
    1. the force on the charge at the centre of shell and at the point A,
    2. the electric flux through the shell.
question_text: Explain using suitable diagrams, the difference in the behaviour of a conductor and dielectric in the presence of external electric field. Define the terms polarisation of a dielectric and write its relation with susceptibility. A thin metallic spherical shell of radius R carries a charge Q on its surface. A point charge Q/2 is placed at its centre C and another charge + 2Q is placed outside the shell at a distance x from the centre as shown in the figure. Find the force on the charge at the centre of shell and at the point A, the electric flux through the shell.
explanation: In the presence of electric field, the free charge carriers, in a conductor, move the charge distribution in the conductor re-adjusting itself so that the net electric field within the conductor becomes zero. In a dielectric, the external electric field induces a net dipole moment, by stretching/reorienting the molecules. The electric field, due to this induced dipole moment, opposes, but does not exactly cancel, the external electric field. Polarisation: Induced dipole moment, per unit volume, is called the polarization. For linear isotropic dielectrics having a susceptibility {tex}\chi{/tex} c , we have P = {tex}\chi{/tex} c E Net force on the charge {tex}\frac{\mathrm{Q}}{2}{/tex} , placed at the centre of the shell is zero. Force on charge 2Q kept at point A F = E {tex}\times{/tex} 2Q {tex}=\frac{1\left(\frac{3 Q}{2}\right) 2 Q}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0} r^{2}}=\frac{3 Q^{2}}{4 \pi r^{2}\varepsilon_{0} }{/tex} Electric flux through the shell, {tex}\phi=\frac{Q}{2\varepsilon_{0}}{/tex} (because charge enclosed is Q/2)
solution: In the presence of electric field, the free charge carriers, in a conductor, move the charge distribution in the conductor re-adjusting itself so that the net electric field within the conductor becomes zero. In a dielectric, the external electric field induces a net dipole moment, by stretching/reorienting the molecules. The electric field, due to this induced dipole moment, opposes, but does not exactly cancel, the external electric field. Polarisation: Induced dipole moment, per unit volume, is called the polarization. For linear isotropic dielectrics having a susceptibility {tex}\chi{/tex} c , we have P = {tex}\chi{/tex} c E Net force on the charge {tex}\frac{\mathrm{Q}}{2}{/tex} , placed at the centre of the shell is zero. Force on charge 2Q kept at point A F = E {tex}\times{/tex} 2Q {tex}=\frac{1\left(\frac{3 Q}{2}\right) 2 Q}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0} r^{2}}=\frac{3 Q^{2}}{4 \pi r^{2}\varepsilon_{0} }{/tex} Electric flux through the shell, {tex}\phi=\frac{Q}{2\varepsilon_{0}}{/tex} (because charge enclosed is Q/2)
solution_html:
1. In the presence of electric field, the free charge carriers, in a conductor, move the charge distribution in the conductor re-adjusting itself so that the net electric field within the conductor becomes zero.
    2. In a dielectric, the external electric field induces a net dipole moment, by stretching/reorienting the molecules. The electric field, due to this induced dipole moment, opposes, but does not exactly cancel, the external electric field.
      Polarisation:  Induced dipole moment, per unit volume, is called the polarization. For linear isotropic dielectrics having a susceptibility {tex}\chi{/tex}c, we have
      P = {tex}\chi{/tex}c E
    1. Net force on the charge {tex}\frac{\mathrm{Q}}{2}{/tex}, placed at the centre of the shell is zero.
      Force on charge 2Q kept at point A
      F = E {tex}\times{/tex} 2Q
      {tex}=\frac{1\left(\frac{3 Q}{2}\right) 2 Q}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0} r^{2}}=\frac{3 Q^{2}}{4 \pi r^{2}\varepsilon_{0} }{/tex}
    2. Electric flux through the shell,
      {tex}\phi=\frac{Q}{2\varepsilon_{0}}{/tex} (because charge enclosed is Q/2)

question_html: Two capacitors of unknown capacitances C1 and C2 are connected first in series and then in parallel across a battery of 100 V. If the energy stored in the two combinations is 0.045 J and 0.25 J respectively, determine the values of q and C2.  Also, calculate the charge on each capacitor in parallel combination.
question_text: Two capacitors of unknown capacitances C 1 and C 2 are connected first in series and then in parallel across a battery of 100 V. If the energy stored in the two combinations is 0.045 J and 0.25 J respectively, determine the values of q and C 2 . Also, calculate the charge on each capacitor in parallel combination.
explanation: For series combination, we have {tex}U=\frac{1}{2} \frac{C_{1} C_{2}}{C_{1}+C_{2}} V^{2}{/tex} {tex}\therefore 0.045=\frac{1}{2} \frac{C_{1} C_{2}}{C_{1}+C_{2}} \times(100)^{2}{/tex} ...(i) For parallel combination, we have U = {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} (C 1 + C 2 ) V 2 {tex}\therefore{/tex} 0.25 = {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} (C 1 + C 2 ) {tex}\times{/tex} (100) 2 or C 1 + C 2 = 0.5 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -4 ...(ii) From (i), {tex}0.045=\frac{1}{2} \times \frac{C_{1} C_{2}}{0.5 \times 10^{-4}} \times(100)^{2}{/tex} Now (C 1 - C 2 ) 2 = (C 1 + C 2 ) 2 - 4C 1 C 2 = (0.5 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -4 ) 2 - 4 {tex}\times{/tex} 0.045 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -8 = (0.25 - 0.180) {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -8 = 0.07 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -8 {tex}​​​​​​​\therefore{/tex} C 1 - C 2 = {tex}\sqrt{0.07} \times{/tex} 10 -4 = 0.26 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -4 ...(iii) On solving (ii) and (iii), we get C 1 = 0.38 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -4 F and C 2 = 0.12 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -4 F Charges on capacitors C 1 and C 2 in parallel combination are: Q 1 = C 1 V = 0.38 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 4 {tex}\times{/tex} 100 C = 0.38 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -2 C Q 2 = C 2 V =0.12 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -4 {tex}\times{/tex} 100 C = 0.12 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -2 C
solution: For series combination, we have {tex}U=\frac{1}{2} \frac{C_{1} C_{2}}{C_{1}+C_{2}} V^{2}{/tex} {tex}\therefore 0.045=\frac{1}{2} \frac{C_{1} C_{2}}{C_{1}+C_{2}} \times(100)^{2}{/tex} ...(i) For parallel combination, we have U = {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} (C 1 + C 2 ) V 2 {tex}\therefore{/tex} 0.25 = {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} (C 1 + C 2 ) {tex}\times{/tex} (100) 2 or C 1 + C 2 = 0.5 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -4 ...(ii) From (i), {tex}0.045=\frac{1}{2} \times \frac{C_{1} C_{2}}{0.5 \times 10^{-4}} \times(100)^{2}{/tex} Now (C 1 - C 2 ) 2 = (C 1 + C 2 ) 2 - 4C 1 C 2 = (0.5 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -4 ) 2 - 4 {tex}\times{/tex} 0.045 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -8 = (0.25 - 0.180) {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -8 = 0.07 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -8 {tex}​​​​​​​\therefore{/tex} C 1 - C 2 = {tex}\sqrt{0.07} \times{/tex} 10 -4 = 0.26 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -4 ...(iii) On solving (ii) and (iii), we get C 1 = 0.38 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -4 F and C 2 = 0.12 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -4 F Charges on capacitors C 1 and C 2 in parallel combination are: Q 1 = C 1 V = 0.38 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 4 {tex}\times{/tex} 100 C = 0.38 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -2 C Q 2 = C 2 V =0.12 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -4 {tex}\times{/tex} 100 C = 0.12 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -2 C
solution_html:
For series combination, we have
{tex}U=\frac{1}{2} \frac{C_{1} C_{2}}{C_{1}+C_{2}} V^{2}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore 0.045=\frac{1}{2} \frac{C_{1} C_{2}}{C_{1}+C_{2}} \times(100)^{2}{/tex} ...(i)
For parallel combination, we have
U = {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex}(C1 + C2) V2
{tex}\therefore{/tex} 0.25 = {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex}(C1 + C2) {tex}\times{/tex} (100)2
or C1 + C2 = 0.5 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-4 ...(ii)
From (i), {tex}0.045=\frac{1}{2} \times \frac{C_{1} C_{2}}{0.5 \times 10^{-4}} \times(100)^{2}{/tex}
Now (C1 - C2)2 = (C1 + C2)2 - 4C1C2
= (0.5 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-4 )2 - 4 {tex}\times{/tex} 0.045 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-8
= (0.25 - 0.180) {tex}\times{/tex} 10-8 = 0.07{tex}\times{/tex} 10-8
{tex}​​​​​​​\therefore{/tex} C1 - C2 = {tex}\sqrt{0.07} \times{/tex} 10-4 = 0.26 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-4 ...(iii)
On solving (ii) and (iii), we get
C1 = 0.38 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-4 F and C2 = 0.12 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-4 F
Charges on capacitors C1 and C2 in parallel combination are:
Q1 = C1V = 0.38 {tex}\times{/tex} 104 {tex}\times{/tex} 100 C = 0.38 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-2 C
Q2 = C2V =0.12 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-4 {tex}\times{/tex} 100 C = 0.12 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-2 C
