question_html: A parallel plate air capacitor of capacitance C is connected to a cell of emf V and then disconnected from it. A dielectric slab of dielectric constant k, which can just fill the air gap of the capacitor, is now inserted in it. Which of the following is incorrect?
question_text: A parallel plate air capacitor of capacitance C is connected to a cell of emf V and then disconnected from it. A dielectric slab of dielectric constant k, which can just fill the air gap of the capacitor, is now inserted in it. Which of the following is incorrect?

options.text: The charge on the capacitor is not Conserved
options.html: The charge on the capacitor is not Conserved
options.explanation: Charge on the capacitor plates remains constant, Q = CV {tex}V^{\prime}=\frac{E}{\kappa} d=\frac{V}{\kappa}{/tex} {tex}C^{\prime}=\frac{Q}{V^{\prime}}=\frac{Q}{V / \kappa}=\kappa C{/tex} {tex}U^{\prime}=\frac{1}{2} C^{\prime} V^{2}=\frac{1}{2} \kappa C \times\left(\frac{V}{\kappa}\right)^{2}=\frac{1}{\kappa} \frac{1}{2} C V^{2}=\frac{U}{\kappa}{/tex} {tex}\therefore U-U=U\left(\frac{1}{\kappa}-1\right)=\frac{1}{2} C V^{2}\left(\frac{1}{\mathrm{\kappa}}-1\right){/tex}

options.text: The change in energy stored is {tex}\frac{1}{2} C V^{2}\left(\frac{1}{k}-1\right){/tex}
options.html: The change in energy stored is {tex}\frac{1}{2} C V^{2}\left(\frac{1}{k}-1\right){/tex}

options.text: The energy stored in the capacitor decreases {tex}\kappa{/tex} times
options.html: The energy stored in the capacitor decreases {tex}\kappa{/tex} times

options.text: The potential difference between the plates decreases {tex}\kappa{/tex} times
options.html: The potential difference between the plates decreases {tex}\kappa{/tex} times

correct_option_text: The charge on the capacitor is not Conserved
explanation: Charge on the capacitor plates remains constant, Q = CV {tex}V^{\prime}=\frac{E}{\kappa} d=\frac{V}{\kappa}{/tex} {tex}C^{\prime}=\frac{Q}{V^{\prime}}=\frac{Q}{V / \kappa}=\kappa C{/tex} {tex}U^{\prime}=\frac{1}{2} C^{\prime} V^{2}=\frac{1}{2} \kappa C \times\left(\frac{V}{\kappa}\right)^{2}=\frac{1}{\kappa} \frac{1}{2} C V^{2}=\frac{U}{\kappa}{/tex} {tex}\therefore U-U=U\left(\frac{1}{\kappa}-1\right)=\frac{1}{2} C V^{2}\left(\frac{1}{\mathrm{\kappa}}-1\right){/tex}

question_html: A parallel plate air capacitor has capacity C distance of separation between plates is d and potential difference V is applied between the plates. Force of attraction between the plates of the parallel plate air capacitor is
question_text: A parallel plate air capacitor has capacity C distance of separation between plates is d and potential difference V is applied between the plates. Force of attraction between the plates of the parallel plate air capacitor is

options.explanation: Electric field due to plate 1 near plate 2 Is E = {tex}\frac{Q}{2 \mathrm{Al}_{\mathrm{i}_0}}{/tex} Force, F = -Q {tex}\times{/tex} {tex}\left(\frac{\mathrm{Q}}{2 \mathrm{~A} \bar{\sigma}_0}\right){/tex} = {tex}-\frac{-Q^2}{2 A \varepsilon_0}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{-(C V)^2}{2 A \varepsilon_0}{/tex} C = {tex}\frac{A \varepsilon_0}{d}{/tex} or A {tex}\varepsilon_0{/tex} = Cd F = {tex}\frac{-\left(C^2 V^2\right)}{2 \times C d}-\frac{-C V^2}{2 d} {/tex} |F| = {tex}\frac{C V^2}{2 d} {/tex} Negative sign indicates force is attractive in nature.

question_html:
1. Two isolated metal spheres A and B have radii R and 2R respectively, and the same charge q. Find which of the two spheres have greater:

    1. Show that the equipotential surfaces are closed together in the regions of a strong field and far apart in the regions of a weak field. Draw equipotential surfaces for an electric dipole,

question_text: Two isolated metal spheres A and B have radii R and 2R respectively, and the same charge q. Find which of the two spheres have greater: capacitance and energy density just outside the surface of the spheres Show that the equipotential surfaces are closed together in the regions of a strong field and far apart in the regions of a weak field. Draw equipotential surfaces for an electric dipole, Concentric equipotential surfaces due to a charged body placed at the centre are shown. Identify the polarity of the charge and draw the electric field lines due to it.
explanation: C A = {tex}4 \pi \varepsilon_{0} R{/tex} , C B = {tex}4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}{/tex} (2R) (in general form C = 4 {tex}\pi\in0r {/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} C B > C A {tex}U=\frac{1}{2} \varepsilon_{0} E^{2}{/tex} {tex}E=\frac{\sigma}{\varepsilon_{P}}=\frac{Q}{A \varepsilon_{0}}{/tex} {tex}\therefore U \propto \frac{1}{A^{2}}{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} U A > U B {tex}E=-\frac{d V}{d r}{/tex} For the same change in dV, E {tex}\propto \frac{1}{d r}{/tex} where, ‘dr' represents the distance between equipotential surfaces. Diagram of the equipotential surface due to a dipole The polarity of charge: negative The direction of the electric field is radially inward,(for a negative charge electric field lines are radially inward)
solution: C A = {tex}4 \pi \varepsilon_{0} R{/tex} , C B = {tex}4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}{/tex} (2R) (in general form C = 4 {tex}\pi\in0r {/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} C B > C A {tex}U=\frac{1}{2} \varepsilon_{0} E^{2}{/tex} {tex}E=\frac{\sigma}{\varepsilon_{P}}=\frac{Q}{A \varepsilon_{0}}{/tex} {tex}\therefore U \propto \frac{1}{A^{2}}{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} U A > U B {tex}E=-\frac{d V}{d r}{/tex} For the same change in dV, E {tex}\propto \frac{1}{d r}{/tex} where, ‘dr' represents the distance between equipotential surfaces. Diagram of the equipotential surface due to a dipole The polarity of charge: negative The direction of the electric field is radially inward,(for a negative charge electric field lines are radially inward)
solution_html:
1. CA = {tex}4 \pi \varepsilon_{0} R{/tex}, CB  = {tex}4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}{/tex} (2R) (in general form C = 4{tex}\pi\in0r {/tex}
    1. {tex}\therefore{/tex} CB > CA
    2. {tex}U=\frac{1}{2} \varepsilon_{0} E^{2}{/tex}
      {tex}E=\frac{\sigma}{\varepsilon_{P}}=\frac{Q}{A \varepsilon_{0}}{/tex}
      {tex}\therefore U \propto \frac{1}{A^{2}}{/tex}
      {tex}\therefore{/tex} UA > UB
    1. {tex}E=-\frac{d V}{d r}{/tex}
      For the same change in dV, E {tex}\propto \frac{1}{d r}{/tex}
      where, ‘dr' represents the distance between equipotential surfaces.
      Diagram of the equipotential surface due to a dipole
    2. The polarity of charge: negative
      The direction of the electric field is radially inward,(for a negative charge electric field lines are radially inward)

subject: Physics
question_html:
Explain briefly the process of charging a parallel plate capacitor when it is connected across a d.c. battery. A capacitor of capacitance C is charged to V volts by a battery. After some time the battery is disconnected and the distance between the plates is doubled. Now a slab of dielectric constant, 1 < {tex}\kappa{/tex} < 2, is introduced to fill the space between the plates. How will the following be affected?

  1. The electric field between the plates of the capacitor.
  2. The energy stored in the capacitor Justifies your answer by writing the necessary expressions.
question_text: Explain briefly the process of charging a parallel plate capacitor when it is connected across a d.c. battery. A capacitor of capacitance C is charged to V volts by a battery. After some time the battery is disconnected and the distance between the plates is doubled. Now a slab of dielectric constant, 1 < {tex}\kappa{/tex} < 2, is introduced to fill the space between the plates. How will the following be affected? The electric field between the plates of the capacitor. The energy stored in the capacitor Justifies your answer by writing the necessary expressions.
explanation: Electrons start flowing from one plate of the capacitor to the positive terminal of the battery and from the negative terminal to the other plate. The two plates start acquiring positive and negative charges respectively. This process of charging of the capacitor continues until the potential across its plates becomes equal to the p.d. across the terminals of the battery. Effect of dielectric: When the battery is disconnected, charges on the capacitor plates remain unchanged. Initial capacitance, {tex}C_{0}=\frac{\varepsilon_{0} A}{d}{/tex} Initial field, {tex}E_{0}=\frac{\sigma}{\varepsilon_{0}}{/tex} Final field, {tex}E=\frac{1}{\kappa} \frac{\sigma}{\varepsilon_{0}}=\frac{E_{0}}{\kappa}{/tex} Thus, the electric field decreases. Final capacitance, {tex}C=\kappa \frac{\varepsilon_{0} A}{2 d}=\frac{\kappa C_{0}}{2}{/tex} Initial energy, {tex}u_{0}=\frac{Q^{2}}{2 C_{0}}{/tex} Final energy, {tex}U=\frac{Q^{2}}{2 C}=\frac{Q^{2}}{2} \cdot \frac{2}{\kappa C_{0}}=\frac{2}{\kappa} u_{0}{/tex} As 1 < 2 < {tex}\kappa{/tex} , the energy stored increases i.e., U > U 0
solution: Electrons start flowing from one plate of the capacitor to the positive terminal of the battery and from the negative terminal to the other plate. The two plates start acquiring positive and negative charges respectively. This process of charging of the capacitor continues until the potential across its plates becomes equal to the p.d. across the terminals of the battery. Effect of dielectric: When the battery is disconnected, charges on the capacitor plates remain unchanged. Initial capacitance, {tex}C_{0}=\frac{\varepsilon_{0} A}{d}{/tex} Initial field, {tex}E_{0}=\frac{\sigma}{\varepsilon_{0}}{/tex} Final field, {tex}E=\frac{1}{\kappa} \frac{\sigma}{\varepsilon_{0}}=\frac{E_{0}}{\kappa}{/tex} Thus, the electric field decreases. Final capacitance, {tex}C=\kappa \frac{\varepsilon_{0} A}{2 d}=\frac{\kappa C_{0}}{2}{/tex} Initial energy, {tex}u_{0}=\frac{Q^{2}}{2 C_{0}}{/tex} Final energy, {tex}U=\frac{Q^{2}}{2 C}=\frac{Q^{2}}{2} \cdot \frac{2}{\kappa C_{0}}=\frac{2}{\kappa} u_{0}{/tex} As 1 < 2 < {tex}\kappa{/tex} , the energy stored increases i.e., U > U 0
solution_html:
Electrons start flowing from one plate of the capacitor to the positive terminal of the battery and from the negative terminal to the other plate. The two plates start acquiring positive and negative charges respectively. This process of charging of the capacitor continues until the potential across its plates becomes equal to the p.d. across the terminals of the battery.
Effect of dielectric: When the battery is disconnected, charges on the capacitor plates remain unchanged.

  1. Initial capacitance, {tex}C_{0}=\frac{\varepsilon_{0} A}{d}{/tex}
    Initial field, {tex}E_{0}=\frac{\sigma}{\varepsilon_{0}}{/tex}
    Final field, {tex}E=\frac{1}{\kappa} \frac{\sigma}{\varepsilon_{0}}=\frac{E_{0}}{\kappa}{/tex}
    Thus, the electric field decreases.
  2. Final capacitance, {tex}C=\kappa \frac{\varepsilon_{0} A}{2 d}=\frac{\kappa C_{0}}{2}{/tex}
    Initial energy, {tex}u_{0}=\frac{Q^{2}}{2 C_{0}}{/tex}
    Final energy, {tex}U=\frac{Q^{2}}{2 C}=\frac{Q^{2}}{2} \cdot \frac{2}{\kappa C_{0}}=\frac{2}{\kappa} u_{0}{/tex}
    As 1 < 2 < {tex}\kappa{/tex}, the energy stored increases i.e., U > U0

question_html: The figure shows the field lines of a positive point charge. What will be the sign of the potential energy difference of a small negative charge between the points Q and P? Justify your answer.
question_text: The figure shows the field lines of a positive point charge. What will be the sign of the potential energy difference of a small negative charge between the points Q and P? Justify your answer.
explanation: The sign of the potential energy difference of a small negative charge will be positive. This is because negative charge moves a point at a lower potential energy to a point at a higher potential energy.
solution: The sign of the potential energy difference of a small negative charge will be positive. This is because negative charge moves a point at a lower potential energy to a point at a higher potential energy.
solution_html: The sign of the potential energy difference of a small negative charge will be positive. This is because negative charge moves a point at a lower potential energy to a point at a higher potential energy.

subject: Physics
question_html: Explain, using suitable diagrams, the difference in the behaviour of a (i) conductor and (ii) dielectric in the presence of the external electric field. Define the term polarization of a dielectric and write its relation with susceptibility.
question_text: Explain, using suitable diagrams, the difference in the behaviour of a (i) conductor and (ii) dielectric in the presence of the external electric field. Define the term polarization of a dielectric and write its relation with susceptibility.
explanation: Dielectrics. In insulators, the electrons remain attached to the individual atoms or molecules. However, these electrons can suffer small movements within the atoms or molecules under the influence of an external electric field. The net effect of these microscopic movements gives rise to some important electric properties to such materials. In view of these electrical properties, insulators are called dielectrics. Polarisation of a dielectric. The induced dipole moment set up per unit volume of a dielectric when placed in an external electric field is called polarisation. For linear isotropic dielectrics, {tex}\vec{P}=\varepsilon_{0} \chi_{e} \vec{E}{/tex} where {tex}\chi_e{/tex} is the electric susceptibility of the dielectric medium.
solution: Dielectrics. In insulators, the electrons remain attached to the individual atoms or molecules. However, these electrons can suffer small movements within the atoms or molecules under the influence of an external electric field. The net effect of these microscopic movements gives rise to some important electric properties to such materials. In view of these electrical properties, insulators are called dielectrics. Polarisation of a dielectric. The induced dipole moment set up per unit volume of a dielectric when placed in an external electric field is called polarisation. For linear isotropic dielectrics, {tex}\vec{P}=\varepsilon_{0} \chi_{e} \vec{E}{/tex} where {tex}\chi_e{/tex} is the electric susceptibility of the dielectric medium.
solution_html:
Dielectrics. In insulators, the electrons remain attached to the individual atoms or molecules. However, these electrons can suffer small movements within the atoms or molecules under the influence of an external electric field. The net effect of these microscopic movements gives rise to some important electric properties to such materials. In view of these electrical properties, insulators are called dielectrics.
Polarisation of a dielectric. The induced dipole moment set up per unit volume of a dielectric when placed in an external electric field is called polarisation. For linear isotropic dielectrics,
{tex}\vec{P}=\varepsilon_{0} \chi_{e} \vec{E}{/tex}
where {tex}\chi_e{/tex} is the electric susceptibility of the dielectric medium.

subject: Physics
question_html:
1. Deduce the expression for the electrostatic energy stored in a capacitor of capacitance C and having charge Q.
  2. How will the (a) energy stored and (b) the electric field inside the capacitor be affected when it is completely filled with a dielectric material of dielectric constant K?
question_text: Deduce the expression for the electrostatic energy stored in a capacitor of capacitance C and having charge Q. How will the (a) energy stored and (b) the electric field inside the capacitor be affected when it is completely filled with a dielectric material of dielectric constant K?
explanation: Energy stored in a charged capacitor: Consider a capacitor of capacitance C, initially whose two plates are uncharged. The positive charge will be transferred from plate 2 to plate 1 bit by bit because of the higher potential of plate 2 than plate 1. During this process, the potential difference between the two plates will be V' = Q'/C. If a small additional charge dQ' be transferred from plate 2 to plate 1, the work done will be, {tex}d W = V ^ { \prime } × d Q' = \frac { Q' } { C } × d Q'{/tex} The total work done in transferring a charge Q starting from rest from plate 2 to plate 1 will be {tex}W = \int d W = \int\limits_0^Q {\frac{Q'}{C}} \cdot d{Q^\prime }{/tex} {tex}= \left[ \frac { Q ^ { 2 } } { 2 C } \right] _ { 0 } ^ { Q } = \frac { 1 } { 2 } \frac { Q ^ { 2 } } { C }{/tex} This work done is stored as electric potential energy U of the capacitor, {tex}U = \frac { 1 } { 2 } \frac { Q ^ { 2 } } { C } = \frac { 1 } { 2 } C V ^ { 2 }{/tex} When the capacitor is completely filled with a dielectric material of dielectric constant K, energy stored will be decreased or energy stored will become {tex}\frac { 1 } { K }{/tex} times the initial energy. and Electric field would decrease or {tex}E ^ { \prime } = \frac { E } { K }{/tex}
solution: Energy stored in a charged capacitor: Consider a capacitor of capacitance C, initially whose two plates are uncharged. The positive charge will be transferred from plate 2 to plate 1 bit by bit because of the higher potential of plate 2 than plate 1. During this process, the potential difference between the two plates will be V' = Q'/C. If a small additional charge dQ' be transferred from plate 2 to plate 1, the work done will be, {tex}d W = V ^ { \prime } × d Q' = \frac { Q' } { C } × d Q'{/tex} The total work done in transferring a charge Q starting from rest from plate 2 to plate 1 will be {tex}W = \int d W = \int\limits_0^Q {\frac{Q'}{C}} \cdot d{Q^\prime }{/tex} {tex}= \left[ \frac { Q ^ { 2 } } { 2 C } \right] _ { 0 } ^ { Q } = \frac { 1 } { 2 } \frac { Q ^ { 2 } } { C }{/tex} This work done is stored as electric potential energy U of the capacitor, {tex}U = \frac { 1 } { 2 } \frac { Q ^ { 2 } } { C } = \frac { 1 } { 2 } C V ^ { 2 }{/tex} When the capacitor is completely filled with a dielectric material of dielectric constant K, energy stored will be decreased or energy stored will become {tex}\frac { 1 } { K }{/tex} times the initial energy. and Electric field would decrease or {tex}E ^ { \prime } = \frac { E } { K }{/tex}
solution_html:
1. Energy stored in a charged capacitor: Consider a capacitor of capacitance C, initially whose two plates are uncharged. The positive charge will be transferred from plate 2 to plate 1 bit by bit because of the higher potential of plate 2 than plate 1. During this process, the potential difference between the two plates will be V' = Q'/C.
  2. If a small additional charge dQ' be transferred from plate 2 to plate 1, the work done will be,
    {tex}d W = V ^ { \prime } × d Q' = \frac { Q' } { C } × d Q'{/tex}
    The total work done in transferring a charge Q starting from rest from plate 2 to plate 1 will be
    {tex}W = \int d W = \int\limits_0^Q {\frac{Q'}{C}} \cdot d{Q^\prime }{/tex}
    {tex}= \left[ \frac { Q ^ { 2 } } { 2 C } \right] _ { 0 } ^ { Q } = \frac { 1 } { 2 } \frac { Q ^ { 2 } } { C }{/tex}
    This work done is stored as electric potential energy U of the capacitor,
    {tex}U = \frac { 1 } { 2 } \frac { Q ^ { 2 } } { C } = \frac { 1 } { 2 } C V ^ { 2 }{/tex}
    When the capacitor is completely filled with a dielectric material of dielectric constant K, energy stored will be decreased or energy stored will become {tex}\frac { 1 } { K }{/tex} times the initial energy. 
    and Electric field would decrease or {tex}E ^ { \prime } = \frac { E } { K }{/tex}

subject: Physics
question_html:
An electric dipole is held in a uniform electric field {tex}\vec{E}{/tex}.

  1. Show that the net force acting on it is zero.
  2. The dipole is aligned with its dipole moment {tex}\vec{p}{/tex} parallel to the field {tex}\vec{E}{/tex}

Find:

  1. the work done in turning the dipole till its dipole moment points in the direction opposite to {tex}\vec{E}{/tex}.
  2. the orientation of the dipole for which the torque acting on it becomes maximum.
question_text: An electric dipole is held in a uniform electric field {tex}\vec{E}{/tex} . Show that the net force acting on it is zero. The dipole is aligned with its dipole moment {tex}\vec{p}{/tex} parallel to the field {tex}\vec{E}{/tex} Find: the work done in turning the dipole till its dipole moment points in the direction opposite to {tex}\vec{E}{/tex} . the orientation of the dipole for which the torque acting on it becomes maximum.
explanation: Dipole moment of dipole is given by, | {tex}\vec{p}{/tex} | = q {tex}\times{/tex} (2a) Force on -q at A = -qE Force on +q at B = +qE So, Net force = qE - qE = 0 We have (i), W = {tex}\int_\limits{\theta_1}^{\theta_2} \tau d \theta{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} W = {tex}\int_\limits0^\pi p \mathrm{E} \sin \theta d \theta{/tex} W = pE[-cos {tex}\theta{/tex} ] {tex}\pi{/tex} v W = -2pE {tex}\because{/tex} {tex}\tau{/tex} = pE sin {tex}\theta{/tex} For {tex}\theta{/tex} = {tex}\frac{\pi}{2}{/tex} , {tex}\tau{/tex} is maximum.
solution: Dipole moment of dipole is given by, | {tex}\vec{p}{/tex} | = q {tex}\times{/tex} (2a) Force on -q at A = -qE Force on +q at B = +qE So, Net force = qE - qE = 0 We have (i), W = {tex}\int_\limits{\theta_1}^{\theta_2} \tau d \theta{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} W = {tex}\int_\limits0^\pi p \mathrm{E} \sin \theta d \theta{/tex} W = pE[-cos {tex}\theta{/tex} ] {tex}\pi{/tex} v W = -2pE {tex}\because{/tex} {tex}\tau{/tex} = pE sin {tex}\theta{/tex} For {tex}\theta{/tex} = {tex}\frac{\pi}{2}{/tex} , {tex}\tau{/tex} is maximum.
solution_html:
Dipole moment of dipole is given by,

  1. |{tex}\vec{p}{/tex}| = q {tex}\times{/tex} (2a)
    Force on -q at A = -qE
    Force on +q at B = +qE
    So,
    Net force = qE - qE = 0
    We have (i),
    W = {tex}\int_\limits{\theta_1}^{\theta_2} \tau d \theta{/tex}
    {tex}\therefore{/tex} W = {tex}\int_\limits0^\pi p \mathrm{E} \sin \theta d \theta{/tex}
    W = pE[-cos {tex}\theta{/tex}]{tex}\pi{/tex}v
    W = -2pE
  2. {tex}\because{/tex} {tex}\tau{/tex} = pE sin{tex}\theta{/tex}
    For {tex}\theta{/tex} = {tex}\frac{\pi}{2}{/tex}, {tex}\tau{/tex} is maximum.

question_html: For any charge configuration, equipotential surface through a point is a normal to the electric field. Justify.
question_text: For any charge configuration, equipotential surface through a point is a normal to the electric field. Justify.
explanation: No work is done in moving a test charge from one point on an equipotential surface to the other. {tex}W _ { B } - W _ { A } = 0 = - \int E \; dl \Rightarrow \vec E \cdot \vec {d l} = 0{/tex} , Say W B - W A is the work done to move a test charge from point A to point B on an equipotential surface. Hence, {tex}\vec E \perp \vec {d l}{/tex}
solution: No work is done in moving a test charge from one point on an equipotential surface to the other. {tex}W _ { B } - W _ { A } = 0 = - \int E \; dl \Rightarrow \vec E \cdot \vec {d l} = 0{/tex} , Say W B - W A is the work done to move a test charge from point A to point B on an equipotential surface. Hence, {tex}\vec E \perp \vec {d l}{/tex}
solution_html:
No work is done in moving a test charge from one point on an equipotential surface to the other.
{tex}W _ { B } - W _ { A } = 0 = - \int E \; dl \Rightarrow \vec E \cdot \vec {d l} = 0{/tex}, Say WB - WA is the work done to move a test charge from point A to point B on an equipotential surface.
Hence, {tex}\vec E \perp \vec {d l}{/tex}

subject: Physics
question_html: A parallel plate capacitor of capacitance C is charged to a potential V. It is then connected to another uncharged capacitor having the same capacitance. Find out the ratio of the energy stored in the combined system to that stored initially in the single capacitor.
question_text: A parallel plate capacitor of capacitance C is charged to a potential V. It is then connected to another uncharged capacitor having the same capacitance. Find out the ratio of the energy stored in the combined system to that stored initially in the single capacitor.
explanation: Let q be the charge on the charged capacitor. {tex}\therefore{/tex} Initial energy stored in it is given by, U = {tex}\frac{q^2}{2C}{/tex} When another uncharged similar capacitor is connected, then the net capacitance of the system is given by C' = 2C (using the formula for parallel combination of capacitors) From the principle of conservation of charge we can say that, the charge on the system remains constant. i.e. initial charge of the charged capacitor = final charge of the system of two capacitors = q So, the final energy stored in the system is given by {tex}U ^ { \prime } = \frac { q ^ { 2 } } { 2 C ^ { \prime } } = \frac { q ^ { 2 } } { 4 C }{/tex} Thus, the required ratio is given by = final stored energy / initial stored energy = {tex}\frac { U ^ { \prime } } { U } = \frac { q ^ { 2 } / 4 C } { q ^ { 2 } / 2 C } = \frac { 1 } { 2 }{/tex}
solution: Let q be the charge on the charged capacitor. {tex}\therefore{/tex} Initial energy stored in it is given by, U = {tex}\frac{q^2}{2C}{/tex} When another uncharged similar capacitor is connected, then the net capacitance of the system is given by C' = 2C (using the formula for parallel combination of capacitors) From the principle of conservation of charge we can say that, the charge on the system remains constant. i.e. initial charge of the charged capacitor = final charge of the system of two capacitors = q So, the final energy stored in the system is given by {tex}U ^ { \prime } = \frac { q ^ { 2 } } { 2 C ^ { \prime } } = \frac { q ^ { 2 } } { 4 C }{/tex} Thus, the required ratio is given by = final stored energy / initial stored energy = {tex}\frac { U ^ { \prime } } { U } = \frac { q ^ { 2 } / 4 C } { q ^ { 2 } / 2 C } = \frac { 1 } { 2 }{/tex}
solution_html:
Let q be the charge on the charged capacitor.
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Initial energy stored in it is given by, U = {tex}\frac{q^2}{2C}{/tex}
When another uncharged similar capacitor is connected, then the net capacitance of the system is given by C' = 2C (using the formula for parallel combination of capacitors)
From the principle of conservation of charge we can say that, the charge on the system remains constant.
i.e. initial charge of the charged capacitor = final charge of the system of two capacitors = q
So, the final energy stored in the system is given by {tex}U ^ { \prime } = \frac { q ^ { 2 } } { 2 C ^ { \prime } } = \frac { q ^ { 2 } } { 4 C }{/tex}
Thus, the required ratio is given by = final stored energy / initial stored energy = {tex}\frac { U ^ { \prime } } { U } = \frac { q ^ { 2 } / 4 C } { q ^ { 2 } / 2 C } = \frac { 1 } { 2 }{/tex}

subject: Physics
question_html: Find the charge on the capacitor as shown in the circuit.
question_text: Find the charge on the capacitor as shown in the circuit.
explanation: Total current through the circuit is given by l = {tex}\frac{V}{R}{/tex} Here, V = 2 Volt and the equivalent resistance of the give circuit, {tex} R = ( 10 + 20 )~ \Omega = 30~ \Omega{/tex} {tex}\therefore \quad I = \frac { 2 } { 30 } = \frac { 1 } { 15 } \mathrm { A }{/tex} Voltage across {tex}10 \Omega{/tex} resistor {tex}= I× ( 10 ) = \frac {10} {15} = \frac { 2 } { 3 } V{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} Charge on the capacitor is given by, {tex}Q = C V = \left( 6 \times 10 ^ { - 6 } \right) \times {\frac{2}{3}}= 4 \mu C{/tex}
solution: Total current through the circuit is given by l = {tex}\frac{V}{R}{/tex} Here, V = 2 Volt and the equivalent resistance of the give circuit, {tex} R = ( 10 + 20 )~ \Omega = 30~ \Omega{/tex} {tex}\therefore \quad I = \frac { 2 } { 30 } = \frac { 1 } { 15 } \mathrm { A }{/tex} Voltage across {tex}10 \Omega{/tex} resistor {tex}= I× ( 10 ) = \frac {10} {15} = \frac { 2 } { 3 } V{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} Charge on the capacitor is given by, {tex}Q = C V = \left( 6 \times 10 ^ { - 6 } \right) \times {\frac{2}{3}}= 4 \mu C{/tex}
solution_html:
Total current through the circuit is given by
l = {tex}\frac{V}{R}{/tex}
Here, V = 2 Volt and the equivalent resistance of the give circuit, {tex} R = ( 10 + 20 )~ \Omega = 30~ \Omega{/tex}
{tex}\therefore \quad I = \frac { 2 } { 30 } = \frac { 1 } { 15 } \mathrm { A }{/tex}
Voltage across {tex}10 \Omega{/tex} resistor {tex}= I× ( 10 ) = \frac {10} {15} = \frac { 2 } { 3 } V{/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex}Charge on the capacitor is given by,
{tex}Q = C V = \left( 6 \times 10 ^ { - 6 } \right) \times {\frac{2}{3}}= 4 \mu C{/tex}

subject: Physics
question_html: The given graph shows the variation of charge q versus potential difference for two capacitors C1and C2. The two capacitors have same plate separation, but the plate area of C2 is double that of C1. Which of the lines in the graph corresponds to C1 and C2 and why?
question_text: The given graph shows the variation of charge q versus potential difference for two capacitors C 1 and C 2 . The two capacitors have same plate separation, but the plate area of C 2 is double that of C 1 . Which of the lines in the graph corresponds to C 1 and C 2 and why?
explanation: As {tex}C = \frac{{{ \in _0}A}}{d}{/tex} , and plate area of C 2 is double than that of C 1 , therefore C 2 > C 1 . Now {tex}C = \frac{q}{V}{/tex} , which is greater for line A. Thus, the line A of the graph corresponds to C 2 and line B corresponds to C 1 .
solution: As {tex}C = \frac{{{ \in _0}A}}{d}{/tex} , and plate area of C 2 is double than that of C 1 , therefore C 2 > C 1 . Now {tex}C = \frac{q}{V}{/tex} , which is greater for line A. Thus, the line A of the graph corresponds to C 2 and line B corresponds to C 1 .
solution_html:
As {tex}C = \frac{{{ \in _0}A}}{d}{/tex}, and plate area of C2 is double than that of C1, therefore C2 > C1.
Now {tex}C = \frac{q}{V}{/tex}, which is greater for line A.
Thus, the line A of the graph corresponds to C2 and line B corresponds to C1.

subject: Physics
question_html: A network of four capacitors, each of capacitance 15µF, is connected across a battery of 100 V, as shown in the figure. Find the (i) net capacitance and (ii) the charge on the capacitor C4.
question_text: A network of four capacitors, each of capacitance 15µF, is connected across a battery of 100 V, as shown in the figure. Find the (i) net capacitance and (ii) the charge on the capacitor C 4 .
explanation: Here C 1 , C 2 and C 3 are in series, hence their equivalent capacitance C' is given by {tex}\frac { 1 } { C ^ { \prime } } = \frac { 1 } { C _ { 1 } } + \frac { 1 } { C _ { 2 } } + \frac { 1 } { C _ { 3 } }{/tex} {tex}\frac { 1 } { C ^ { \prime } } = \frac { 1 } { 15 } + \frac { 1 } { 15 } + \frac { 1 } { 15 }{/tex} {tex}\left[ C _ { 1 } = C _ { 2 } = C _ { 3 } = 15 \mu F \right]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow C ^ { \prime } = \frac { 15 } { 3 } \mu F{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow C' = 5 \mu F{/tex} Redrawing the circuit as shown below: Since, C' and C 4 are in parallel, {tex}\therefore C _ { \mathrm { net } } = C ^ { \prime } + C _ { 4 } = 5 \mu F + 15 \mu F = 20 \mu F{/tex} Since, C' and C 4 are in parallel, so the potential difference across both of them is the applied voltage i.e. 100 V. {tex}\therefore{/tex} Charge across C 4 is Q 4 and is given by {tex}Q _ { 4 } = C _ { 4 } \times V = C _ { 4 } \times 100 {/tex} {tex}= 15 \times 10 ^ { - 6 } \times 100 = 15 \times 10 ^ { - 4 } \mathrm { C }{/tex} = 1.5 mC
solution: Here C 1 , C 2 and C 3 are in series, hence their equivalent capacitance C' is given by {tex}\frac { 1 } { C ^ { \prime } } = \frac { 1 } { C _ { 1 } } + \frac { 1 } { C _ { 2 } } + \frac { 1 } { C _ { 3 } }{/tex} {tex}\frac { 1 } { C ^ { \prime } } = \frac { 1 } { 15 } + \frac { 1 } { 15 } + \frac { 1 } { 15 }{/tex} {tex}\left[ C _ { 1 } = C _ { 2 } = C _ { 3 } = 15 \mu F \right]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow C ^ { \prime } = \frac { 15 } { 3 } \mu F{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow C' = 5 \mu F{/tex} Redrawing the circuit as shown below: Since, C' and C 4 are in parallel, {tex}\therefore C _ { \mathrm { net } } = C ^ { \prime } + C _ { 4 } = 5 \mu F + 15 \mu F = 20 \mu F{/tex} Since, C' and C 4 are in parallel, so the potential difference across both of them is the applied voltage i.e. 100 V. {tex}\therefore{/tex} Charge across C 4 is Q 4 and is given by {tex}Q _ { 4 } = C _ { 4 } \times V = C _ { 4 } \times 100 {/tex} {tex}= 15 \times 10 ^ { - 6 } \times 100 = 15 \times 10 ^ { - 4 } \mathrm { C }{/tex} = 1.5 mC
solution_html:
1. Here C1, C2 and C3 are in series, hence their equivalent capacitance C' is given by
    {tex}\frac { 1 } { C ^ { \prime } } = \frac { 1 } { C _ { 1 } } + \frac { 1 } { C _ { 2 } } + \frac { 1 } { C _ { 3 } }{/tex}
    {tex}\frac { 1 } { C ^ { \prime } } = \frac { 1 } { 15 } + \frac { 1 } { 15 } + \frac { 1 } { 15 }{/tex} {tex}\left[ C _ { 1 } = C _ { 2 } = C _ { 3 } = 15 \mu F \right]{/tex}
    {tex}\Rightarrow C ^ { \prime } = \frac { 15 } { 3 } \mu F{/tex}
    {tex}\Rightarrow C' = 5 \mu F{/tex}
    Redrawing the circuit as shown below:

    Since, C' and C4 are in parallel,
    {tex}\therefore C _ { \mathrm { net } } = C ^ { \prime } + C _ { 4 } = 5 \mu F + 15 \mu F = 20 \mu F{/tex}
  2. Since, C' and C4 are in parallel, so the potential difference across both of them is the applied voltage i.e. 100 V.
    {tex}\therefore{/tex} Charge across C4 is Q4 and is given by
    {tex}Q _ { 4 } = C _ { 4 } \times V = C _ { 4 } \times 100 {/tex}
    {tex}= 15 \times 10 ^ { - 6 } \times 100 = 15 \times 10 ^ { - 4 } \mathrm { C }{/tex}
    = 1.5 mC

question_html:
Two parallel plate capacitors of capacitances C1 and C2 such that C1 = 2C2 are connected across a battery of V volts as shown in the figure. Initially the key (k, is kept closed to fully charge the capacitors. The key is now thrown open and a dielectric slab of dielectric constant V is inserted in the two capacitors to completely fill the gap between the plates. Find the ratio of

  1. the net capacitance and
  2. the energies stored in the combination, before and after the introduction of the dielectric slab.
question_text: Two parallel plate capacitors of capacitances C 1 and C 2 such that C 1 = 2C 2 are connected across a battery of V volts as shown in the figure. Initially the key (k, is kept closed to fully charge the capacitors. The key is now thrown open and a dielectric slab of dielectric constant V is inserted in the two capacitors to completely fill the gap between the plates. Find the ratio of the net capacitance and the energies stored in the combination, before and after the introduction of the dielectric slab.
explanation: Given, C 1 = 2C 2 ....(i) Hence, C initial = C 1 + C 2 = 2C 2 + C 2 = 3C 2 (using the formula of capacitance of capacitors in parallel combination)...(ii) Net capacitance after filling the gap with dielectric K is given by , {tex}C _ { \text { final } } = { 3 K C _ { 2 } } {/tex} So, {tex}\frac{C_i}{C_f}=\frac1K{/tex} Initial energy stored is given by, {tex}U _ { i } = \frac { q ^ { 2 } } { 3 C _ { 2 } } = \frac { q ^ { 2 } } { 3 C _ { 2 } }{/tex} and final energy is given by, {tex}U _ { f } = \frac { q ^ { 2 } } { 3 K C _ { 2 } } = \frac { q ^ { 2 } } { 3 K C _ { 2 } }{/tex} {tex}\frac { U _ { i } } { U _ { f } } = \frac { K } { 1 } \;or\; U _ { i } : U _ { f } = K : 1{/tex}
solution: Given, C 1 = 2C 2 ....(i) Hence, C initial = C 1 + C 2 = 2C 2 + C 2 = 3C 2 (using the formula of capacitance of capacitors in parallel combination)...(ii) Net capacitance after filling the gap with dielectric K is given by , {tex}C _ { \text { final } } = { 3 K C _ { 2 } } {/tex} So, {tex}\frac{C_i}{C_f}=\frac1K{/tex} Initial energy stored is given by, {tex}U _ { i } = \frac { q ^ { 2 } } { 3 C _ { 2 } } = \frac { q ^ { 2 } } { 3 C _ { 2 } }{/tex} and final energy is given by, {tex}U _ { f } = \frac { q ^ { 2 } } { 3 K C _ { 2 } } = \frac { q ^ { 2 } } { 3 K C _ { 2 } }{/tex} {tex}\frac { U _ { i } } { U _ { f } } = \frac { K } { 1 } \;or\; U _ { i } : U _ { f } = K : 1{/tex}
solution_html:
Given, C1 = 2C2 ....(i)
Hence, Cinitial = C1 + C2 = 2C2+ C2 = 3C2 (using the formula of capacitance of capacitors in parallel combination)...(ii)

  1. Net capacitance after filling the gap with
    dielectric K is given by , {tex}C _ { \text { final } } = { 3 K C _ { 2 } } {/tex}
    So, {tex}\frac{C_i}{C_f}=\frac1K{/tex}
  2. Initial energy stored is  given by, {tex}U _ { i } = \frac { q ^ { 2 } } { 3 C _ { 2 } } = \frac { q ^ { 2 } } { 3 C _ { 2 } }{/tex}
    and final energy is given by, {tex}U _ { f } = \frac { q ^ { 2 } } { 3 K C _ { 2 } } = \frac { q ^ { 2 } } { 3 K C _ { 2 } }{/tex}
    {tex}\frac { U _ { i } } { U _ { f } } = \frac { K } { 1 } \;or\; U _ { i } : U _ { f } = K : 1{/tex}

subject: Physics
question_html: Two point charges q1 and q2 are located at {tex}\vec{r_{1}}{/tex} and  {tex}\overrightarrow{r_{2}}{/tex} respectively in an external electric field E. Obtain the expression for the total work done in assembling this configuration.
question_text: Two point charges q 1 and q 2 are located at {tex}\vec{r_{1}}{/tex} and {tex}\overrightarrow{r_{2}}{/tex} respectively in an external electric field E. Obtain the expression for the total work done in assembling this configuration.
explanation: Total work done to assemble the charges at certain points is known as potential energy of system of charges. Work-done to bring a charge q 1 in the electric field at a distance r 1 is given by , W 1 = {tex}q_1V_{(r_1)}{/tex} ...(i) Work-done to bring a charge q 2 in the electric field at a distance r 2 is given by , W 2 = {tex}q_2V_{(r_2)}{/tex} ...(ii) Work-done on q 2 to move it against the force of charge q 1 = {tex}\frac1{4{\mathrm{ {tex}\pi{/tex} ε}}_0}{/tex} {tex}\frac{q_1q_2}{r_{12}}{/tex} ....(iii) Hence, total work done in assembling the two charges, is given by , W = {tex}q_1V_{(r_1)}{/tex} + {tex}q_2V_{(r_2)}{/tex} + {tex}\frac1{4{\mathrm{ {tex}\pi{/tex} ε}}_0}{/tex} {tex}\frac{q_1q_2}{r_{12}}{/tex}
solution: Total work done to assemble the charges at certain points is known as potential energy of system of charges. Work-done to bring a charge q 1 in the electric field at a distance r 1 is given by , W 1 = {tex}q_1V_{(r_1)}{/tex} ...(i) Work-done to bring a charge q 2 in the electric field at a distance r 2 is given by , W 2 = {tex}q_2V_{(r_2)}{/tex} ...(ii) Work-done on q 2 to move it against the force of charge q 1 = {tex}\frac1{4{\mathrm{ {tex}\pi{/tex} ε}}_0}{/tex} {tex}\frac{q_1q_2}{r_{12}}{/tex} ....(iii) Hence, total work done in assembling the two charges, is given by , W = {tex}q_1V_{(r_1)}{/tex} + {tex}q_2V_{(r_2)}{/tex} + {tex}\frac1{4{\mathrm{ {tex}\pi{/tex} ε}}_0}{/tex} {tex}\frac{q_1q_2}{r_{12}}{/tex}
solution_html:
Total work done to assemble the charges at certain points is known as potential energy of system of charges.
Work-done to bring a charge q1 in the electric field at a distance r1 is given by ,
W1 = {tex}q_1V_{(r_1)}{/tex}...(i)
Work-done to bring a charge q2  in the electric field at a distance r2 is given by ,
W2 = {tex}q_2V_{(r_2)}{/tex}...(ii)
Work-done on q2 to move it against the force of charge q1 = {tex}\frac1{4{\mathrm{{tex}\pi{/tex}ε}}_0}{/tex}{tex}\frac{q_1q_2}{r_{12}}{/tex} ....(iii)
Hence, total work done in assembling the two charges, is given by ,
W = {tex}q_1V_{(r_1)}{/tex} + {tex}q_2V_{(r_2)}{/tex} + {tex}\frac1{4{\mathrm{{tex}\pi{/tex}ε}}_0}{/tex}{tex}\frac{q_1q_2}{r_{12}}{/tex}

subject: Physics
question_html: Two charged spherical conductors of radii R1 and R2, when connected by a conducting wire acquire charges q1 and q2 respectively. Find the ratio of their surface charge densities in terms of their radii.
question_text: Two charged spherical conductors of radii R 1 and R 2 , when connected by a conducting wire acquire charges q 1 and q 2 respectively. Find the ratio of their surface charge densities in terms of their radii.
explanation: Let {tex}\sigma_1{/tex} and {tex}\sigma_2{/tex} be the surface charge densities of the two spherical conductors of radii R 1 and R 2 respectively. Then, {tex}\sigma_{1}=\frac{q_{1}}{4 \pi \mathrm{R}_{1}^{2}}{/tex} and {tex}\sigma_{2}=\frac{q_{2}}{4 \pi R_{2}^{2}}{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\frac{\sigma_{1}}{\sigma_{2}}=\frac{q_{1}}{4 \pi R_{1}^{2}} \times \frac{4 \pi R_{2}^{2}}{q_{2}}{/tex} or {tex}\frac{\sigma_{1}}{\sigma_{2}}=\frac{q_{1}}{q_{2}} \times \frac{R_{2}^{2}}{R_{1}^{2}}{/tex} .....(i) When the two conductors are connected by a conducting wire, their potentials become equal i.e. {tex}\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}} \cdot \frac{q_{1}}{R_{1}}=\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}} \cdot \frac{q_{2}}{R_{2}}{/tex} or {tex}\frac{q_{1}}{q_{2}}=\frac{R_{1}}{R_{2}}{/tex} .......(ii) From the equations (i) and (ii), we have {tex}\frac{\sigma_{1}}{\sigma_{2}}=\frac{\mathrm{R}_{1}}{\mathrm{R}_{2}} \times \frac{\mathrm{R}_{2}^{2}}{\mathrm{R}_{1}^{2}}=\frac{\mathrm{R}_{2}}{\mathrm{R}_{1}}{/tex}
solution: Let {tex}\sigma_1{/tex} and {tex}\sigma_2{/tex} be the surface charge densities of the two spherical conductors of radii R 1 and R 2 respectively. Then, {tex}\sigma_{1}=\frac{q_{1}}{4 \pi \mathrm{R}_{1}^{2}}{/tex} and {tex}\sigma_{2}=\frac{q_{2}}{4 \pi R_{2}^{2}}{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\frac{\sigma_{1}}{\sigma_{2}}=\frac{q_{1}}{4 \pi R_{1}^{2}} \times \frac{4 \pi R_{2}^{2}}{q_{2}}{/tex} or {tex}\frac{\sigma_{1}}{\sigma_{2}}=\frac{q_{1}}{q_{2}} \times \frac{R_{2}^{2}}{R_{1}^{2}}{/tex} .....(i) When the two conductors are connected by a conducting wire, their potentials become equal i.e. {tex}\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}} \cdot \frac{q_{1}}{R_{1}}=\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}} \cdot \frac{q_{2}}{R_{2}}{/tex} or {tex}\frac{q_{1}}{q_{2}}=\frac{R_{1}}{R_{2}}{/tex} .......(ii) From the equations (i) and (ii), we have {tex}\frac{\sigma_{1}}{\sigma_{2}}=\frac{\mathrm{R}_{1}}{\mathrm{R}_{2}} \times \frac{\mathrm{R}_{2}^{2}}{\mathrm{R}_{1}^{2}}=\frac{\mathrm{R}_{2}}{\mathrm{R}_{1}}{/tex}
solution_html:
Let {tex}\sigma_1{/tex} and {tex}\sigma_2{/tex} be the surface charge densities of the two spherical conductors of radii R1 and R2 respectively.
Then, {tex}\sigma_{1}=\frac{q_{1}}{4 \pi \mathrm{R}_{1}^{2}}{/tex} and {tex}\sigma_{2}=\frac{q_{2}}{4 \pi R_{2}^{2}}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\frac{\sigma_{1}}{\sigma_{2}}=\frac{q_{1}}{4 \pi R_{1}^{2}} \times \frac{4 \pi R_{2}^{2}}{q_{2}}{/tex}
or {tex}\frac{\sigma_{1}}{\sigma_{2}}=\frac{q_{1}}{q_{2}} \times \frac{R_{2}^{2}}{R_{1}^{2}}{/tex} .....(i)
When the two conductors are connected by a conducting wire, their potentials become equal i.e.
{tex}\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}} \cdot \frac{q_{1}}{R_{1}}=\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}} \cdot \frac{q_{2}}{R_{2}}{/tex}
or {tex}\frac{q_{1}}{q_{2}}=\frac{R_{1}}{R_{2}}{/tex} .......(ii)
From the equations (i) and (ii), we have
{tex}\frac{\sigma_{1}}{\sigma_{2}}=\frac{\mathrm{R}_{1}}{\mathrm{R}_{2}} \times \frac{\mathrm{R}_{2}^{2}}{\mathrm{R}_{1}^{2}}=\frac{\mathrm{R}_{2}}{\mathrm{R}_{1}}{/tex}

subject: Physics
question_html:
Eight identical spherical drops, each carrying a charge 1 nC are at a potential of 900 V each. All these drops combine together to form a single large drop. Calculate the potential of this large drop.
(Assume no wastage of any kind and take the capacitance of a sphere of radius r as proportional to r).
question_text: Eight identical spherical drops, each carrying a charge 1 nC are at a potential of 900 V each. All these drops combine together to form a single large drop. Calculate the potential of this large drop. (Assume no wastage of any kind and take the capacitance of a sphere of radius r as proportional to r).
explanation: Let the radius of each drop be r. The capacitance C of each drop is kr, where k is a constant. Also q = CV, V = 900 volt {tex}\therefore{/tex} Charge on each drop = q = (kr {tex}\times{/tex} 900) C {tex}\therefore{/tex} Total charge on all the eight drops = Q = 8q = 8×kr×900 = 7200 kr Let R be the radius of the large drop. Then by equating their volumes we get, {tex}\frac{4 \pi}{3} R^{3}=8 \times \frac{4 \pi}{3} r^{3}{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} R = (8) 1/3 r = 2r {tex}\therefore{/tex} Capacitance C of the large drop = kR = 2kr {tex}\therefore{/tex} Potential of the large drop {tex}=\frac{Q}{C^{\prime}}=\frac{7200 k r}{2 k r}{/tex} volt = 3600 V
solution: Let the radius of each drop be r. The capacitance C of each drop is kr, where k is a constant. Also q = CV, V = 900 volt {tex}\therefore{/tex} Charge on each drop = q = (kr {tex}\times{/tex} 900) C {tex}\therefore{/tex} Total charge on all the eight drops = Q = 8q = 8×kr×900 = 7200 kr Let R be the radius of the large drop. Then by equating their volumes we get, {tex}\frac{4 \pi}{3} R^{3}=8 \times \frac{4 \pi}{3} r^{3}{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} R = (8) 1/3 r = 2r {tex}\therefore{/tex} Capacitance C of the large drop = kR = 2kr {tex}\therefore{/tex} Potential of the large drop {tex}=\frac{Q}{C^{\prime}}=\frac{7200 k r}{2 k r}{/tex} volt = 3600 V
solution_html:
Let the radius of each drop be r. The capacitance C of each drop is kr, where k is a constant.
Also q = CV, V = 900 volt
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Charge on each drop = q = (kr {tex}\times{/tex} 900) C
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Total charge on all the eight drops = Q = 8q = 8×kr×900
= 7200 kr
Let R be the radius of the large drop. Then by equating their volumes we get,
{tex}\frac{4 \pi}{3} R^{3}=8 \times \frac{4 \pi}{3} r^{3}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} R = (8)1/3 r = 2r
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Capacitance C of the large drop = kR = 2kr
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Potential of the large drop {tex}=\frac{Q}{C^{\prime}}=\frac{7200 k r}{2 k r}{/tex} volt
= 3600 V

question_text: The electric field inside a parallel plate capacitor is E. Find the amount of work done in moving a charge q over a closed rectangular loop abcda.
explanation: E {tex}\perp{/tex} ab and E {tex}\perp{/tex} dc, so W ab = 0 and W d = 0. Also, W bc = -W da Total work done in moving charge q over the closed loop abcda, W = W ab + W bc + W d + W da = 0 - W da + 0 + W da = 0
solution: E {tex}\perp{/tex} ab and E {tex}\perp{/tex} dc, so W ab = 0 and W d = 0. Also, W bc = -W da Total work done in moving charge q over the closed loop abcda, W = W ab + W bc + W d + W da = 0 - W da + 0 + W da = 0
solution_html:
E {tex}\perp{/tex} ab and E {tex}\perp{/tex} dc,
so Wab = 0 and Wd = 0.
Also, Wbc = -Wda
Total work done in moving charge q over the closed loop abcda,
W = Wab + Wbc + Wd + Wda

subject: Physics
question_html:
Two closely spaced equipotential surfaces A and B with potentials V and V + {tex}\delta{/tex}V, (where {tex}\delta{/tex}V is the change in V) are kept {tex}\delta{/tex}l distance apart as shown in the figure.
Deduce the relation between the electric field and the potential gradient between them. Write the two important conclusions concerning the relation between the electric field and electric potential.
question_text: Two closely spaced equipotential surfaces A and B with potentials V and V + {tex}\delta{/tex} V, (where {tex}\delta{/tex} V is the change in V) are kept {tex}\delta{/tex} l distance apart as shown in the figure. Deduce the relation between the electric field and the potential gradient between them. Write the two important conclusions concerning the relation between the electric field and electric potential.
explanation: Work done in moving a unit positive charge along an infinitesimal distance {tex}\delta{/tex} l, {tex}\left| E _ { l } \right| \delta l = V _ { A } - V _ { B } = V - ( V + \delta V ) = - \delta V{/tex} or {tex}E = - \frac {\delta V} {\delta l} {/tex} The electric field is in the direction in which the change in electrostatic potential decreases most. (This conclusion comes due to the negative sign of the above expression) Magnitude of electric field is given by the change in the magnitude of electrostatic potential per unit displacement normal to the equipotential surface at the point.
solution: Work done in moving a unit positive charge along an infinitesimal distance {tex}\delta{/tex} l, {tex}\left| E _ { l } \right| \delta l = V _ { A } - V _ { B } = V - ( V + \delta V ) = - \delta V{/tex} or {tex}E = - \frac {\delta V} {\delta l} {/tex} The electric field is in the direction in which the change in electrostatic potential decreases most. (This conclusion comes due to the negative sign of the above expression) Magnitude of electric field is given by the change in the magnitude of electrostatic potential per unit displacement normal to the equipotential surface at the point.
solution_html:
Work done in moving a unit positive charge along an infinitesimal distance {tex}\delta{/tex}l,
{tex}\left| E _ { l } \right| \delta l = V _ { A } - V _ { B } = V - ( V + \delta V ) = - \delta V{/tex}
or {tex}E = - \frac {\delta V} {\delta l} {/tex}

  1. The electric field is in the direction in which the change in electrostatic potential decreases most. (This conclusion comes due to the negative sign of the above expression)
  2. Magnitude of electric field is given by the change in the magnitude of electrostatic potential per unit displacement normal to the equipotential surface at the point.

subject: Physics
question_html: The given graph shows variation of charge q versus potential difference V for two capacitors C1 and C2. Both the capacitors have same plate separation but plate area of C2 is greater than that of C1. Which line (A or B) corresponds to C1 and why?
question_text: The given graph shows variation of charge q versus potential difference V for two capacitors C 1 and C 2 . Both the capacitors have same plate separation but plate area of C 2 is greater than that of C 1 . Which line (A or B) corresponds to C 1 and why?
explanation: B corresponds to C 1, Since slope ( {tex}\frac qV{/tex} ) of Line B is lesser than that of Line A. Another way, C is directly proportional to area(A). In the given question, Area of plate C 2 >C 1 . And C = Q/V is greater for line A. Hence B corresponds to C 1.
solution: B corresponds to C 1, Since slope ( {tex}\frac qV{/tex} ) of Line B is lesser than that of Line A. Another way, C is directly proportional to area(A). In the given question, Area of plate C 2 >C 1 . And C = Q/V is greater for line A. Hence B corresponds to C 1.
solution_html:
B corresponds to C1, Since slope ({tex}\frac qV{/tex}) of Line B is lesser than that of Line A.
Another way, C is directly proportional to area(A). In the given question, Area of plate C2>C1. And C = Q/V is greater for line A. Hence B corresponds to C1.

subject: Physics
question_html:
1. Obtain the expression for the energy stored per unit volume in a charged parallel plate capacitor,
  2. The electric field inside a parallel plate capacitor is E. Find the amount of work done in moving a charge q over a closed rectangular loop abcda.
question_text: Obtain the expression for the energy stored per unit volume in a charged parallel plate capacitor, The electric field inside a parallel plate capacitor is E. Find the amount of work done in moving a charge q over a closed rectangular loop abcda.
explanation: Work done by the source of potential, in storing an additional charge (dq), is dW = V.dq But {tex}V=\frac{q}{C}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow d W=\frac{q}{C} d q{/tex} Total work done in storing the charge q, {tex}\int d W=\int_{0}^{q} \frac{q}{C} d q{/tex} {tex}W=\frac{1}{C}\left(\frac{q^{2}}{2}\right)_{0}^{q}=\frac{q^{2}}{2 C}{/tex} This work is stored as electrostatic energy in the capacitor. {tex}\therefore U=\frac{1}{2} C V^{2}{/tex} ( {tex}\because{/tex} q = CV) Energy stored per unit volume {tex}=\frac{\frac{1}{2} C V^{2}}{A d}{/tex} , the potential difference between the plates is V = ED and C = {tex}\in{/tex} 0 A/d, by putting these value we get, {tex}U=\frac{\frac{1}{2}\left(\frac{\varepsilon_{0} A}{d}\right)(E d)^{2}}{A d}{/tex} {tex}U=\frac{1}{2} \varepsilon_{0} E^{2}{/tex} Work done in moving the charge q from a to b, and from c to d is zero because the electric field is perpendicular to the displacement. Work done from b to c = - Work done from d to a {tex}\therefore{/tex} Total work done in moving a charge q over the closed-loop = 0
solution: Work done by the source of potential, in storing an additional charge (dq), is dW = V.dq But {tex}V=\frac{q}{C}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow d W=\frac{q}{C} d q{/tex} Total work done in storing the charge q, {tex}\int d W=\int_{0}^{q} \frac{q}{C} d q{/tex} {tex}W=\frac{1}{C}\left(\frac{q^{2}}{2}\right)_{0}^{q}=\frac{q^{2}}{2 C}{/tex} This work is stored as electrostatic energy in the capacitor. {tex}\therefore U=\frac{1}{2} C V^{2}{/tex} ( {tex}\because{/tex} q = CV) Energy stored per unit volume {tex}=\frac{\frac{1}{2} C V^{2}}{A d}{/tex} , the potential difference between the plates is V = ED and C = {tex}\in{/tex} 0 A/d, by putting these value we get, {tex}U=\frac{\frac{1}{2}\left(\frac{\varepsilon_{0} A}{d}\right)(E d)^{2}}{A d}{/tex} {tex}U=\frac{1}{2} \varepsilon_{0} E^{2}{/tex} Work done in moving the charge q from a to b, and from c to d is zero because the electric field is perpendicular to the displacement. Work done from b to c = - Work done from d to a {tex}\therefore{/tex} Total work done in moving a charge q over the closed-loop = 0
solution_html:
1. Work done by the source of potential, in storing an additional charge (dq), is
    dW = V.dq
    But {tex}V=\frac{q}{C}{/tex}
    {tex}\Rightarrow d W=\frac{q}{C} d q{/tex}
    Total work done in storing the charge q,
    {tex}\int d W=\int_{0}^{q} \frac{q}{C} d q{/tex}
    {tex}W=\frac{1}{C}\left(\frac{q^{2}}{2}\right)_{0}^{q}=\frac{q^{2}}{2 C}{/tex}
    This work is stored as electrostatic energy in the capacitor.
    {tex}\therefore U=\frac{1}{2} C V^{2}{/tex} ({tex}\because{/tex} q = CV)
    Energy stored per unit volume {tex}=\frac{\frac{1}{2} C V^{2}}{A d}{/tex} , the potential difference between the plates is V = ED and C = {tex}\in{/tex}0A/d, by putting these value we get,
    {tex}U=\frac{\frac{1}{2}\left(\frac{\varepsilon_{0} A}{d}\right)(E d)^{2}}{A d}{/tex}
    {tex}U=\frac{1}{2} \varepsilon_{0} E^{2}{/tex}
  2. Work done in moving the charge q from a to b, and from c to d is zero because the electric field is perpendicular to the displacement.
    Work done from b to c = - Work done from d to a
    {tex}\therefore{/tex} Total work done in moving a charge q over the closed-loop = 0

question_html:
Assertion (A): When charges are shared between any two bodies, no charge is really lost and some loss of energy does occur.
Reason (R): Some energy disappears in the form of heat, sparking, etc.
question_text: Assertion (A): When charges are shared between any two bodies, no charge is really lost and some loss of energy does occur. Reason (R): Some energy disappears in the form of heat, sparking, etc.

options.text: Both A and R are true and R is the correct explanation of A.
options.html: Both A and R are true and R is the correct explanation of A.
options.explanation: A charge is always conserved but some energy is lost in the form of heat.

options.text: Both A and R are true but R is not the correct explanation of A.
options.html: Both A and R are true but R is not the correct explanation of A.

options.text: A is true but R is false.
options.html: A is true but R is false.

options.text: A is false but R is true.
options.html: A is false but R is true.

correct_option_text: Both A and R are true and R is the correct explanation of A.
explanation: A charge is always conserved but some energy is lost in the form of heat.

subject: Physics
question_html: The plates in a parallel plate capacitor are separated by a distance d with air as the medium between the plates. In order to increase the capacity by 66% a dielectric slab of dielectric constant 5 is introduced between the plates. What is the thickness of the dielectric slab?
question_text: The plates in a parallel plate capacitor are separated by a distance d with air as the medium between the plates. In order to increase the capacity by 66% a dielectric slab of dielectric constant 5 is introduced between the plates. What is the thickness of the dielectric slab?

options.explanation: {tex}C_{0}=\frac{\varepsilon_{0} A}{d}{/tex} With a dielectric slab of {tex}\kappa{/tex} = 5, {tex}C=\frac{\varepsilon_{0} A}{d-t+\frac{t}{5}}{/tex} {tex}\therefore \frac{C_{0}}{C}=\frac{d-t+\frac{t}{5}}{d}{/tex} or {tex}\frac{100}{166}=\frac{d-\frac{4 t}{5}}{d}{/tex} {tex}166 \times \frac{4 t}{5}=66 d{/tex} {tex}t=\frac{66 d \times 5}{166 \times 4} \approx \frac{d}{2}{/tex}

explanation: {tex}C_{0}=\frac{\varepsilon_{0} A}{d}{/tex} With a dielectric slab of {tex}\kappa{/tex} = 5, {tex}C=\frac{\varepsilon_{0} A}{d-t+\frac{t}{5}}{/tex} {tex}\therefore \frac{C_{0}}{C}=\frac{d-t+\frac{t}{5}}{d}{/tex} or {tex}\frac{100}{166}=\frac{d-\frac{4 t}{5}}{d}{/tex} {tex}166 \times \frac{4 t}{5}=66 d{/tex} {tex}t=\frac{66 d \times 5}{166 \times 4} \approx \frac{d}{2}{/tex}

question_html: In a region, the potential is represented by V(x, y, z) = 6x - 8xy - 8y + 6yz, where V is N volts and x, y, z are in metres. The electric force experienced by a charge of 2 coulomb situated at point (1, 1, 1) is:
question_text: In a region, the potential is represented by V(x, y, z) = 6x - 8xy - 8y + 6yz, where V is N volts and x, y, z are in metres. The electric force experienced by a charge of 2 coulomb situated at point (1, 1, 1) is:

options.explanation: V = 6x - 8xy - 8y + 6yz At the point (1, 1, 1), we have {tex}E_{x}=-\frac{\partial V}{\partial x}{/tex} = -(6 - 8y) = 2 {tex}E_{y}=-\frac{\partial V}{\partial y} {/tex} = -(-8x - 8 + 6z) = 10 {tex}E_{2}=-\frac{\partial V}{\partial z}{/tex} = -6y = -6 {tex}E=\sqrt{E_{x}^{2}+E_{y}^{2}+E_{z}^{2}}=\sqrt{4+100+36}=\sqrt{140}{/tex} {tex}=2 \sqrt{35} \mathrm{NC}^{-1}{/tex} {tex}F=q E=2 \times 2 \sqrt{35}=4 \sqrt{35} \mathrm{~N}{/tex}

explanation: V = 6x - 8xy - 8y + 6yz At the point (1, 1, 1), we have {tex}E_{x}=-\frac{\partial V}{\partial x}{/tex} = -(6 - 8y) = 2 {tex}E_{y}=-\frac{\partial V}{\partial y} {/tex} = -(-8x - 8 + 6z) = 10 {tex}E_{2}=-\frac{\partial V}{\partial z}{/tex} = -6y = -6 {tex}E=\sqrt{E_{x}^{2}+E_{y}^{2}+E_{z}^{2}}=\sqrt{4+100+36}=\sqrt{140}{/tex} {tex}=2 \sqrt{35} \mathrm{NC}^{-1}{/tex} {tex}F=q E=2 \times 2 \sqrt{35}=4 \sqrt{35} \mathrm{~N}{/tex}

subject: Physics
question_html: For any charge configuration, equipotential surface through a point is normal to the electric field. Justify.
question_text: For any charge configuration, equipotential surface through a point is normal to the electric field. Justify.
explanation: In an electric field {tex}\vec E{/tex} , small work done in moving a test charge q 0 through a small displacement {tex}\overrightarrow{\mathrm{dr}}{/tex} along an equipotential surface is given by dW = {tex}q_{0} \overrightarrow{\mathrm{E}} \cdot \overrightarrow{d r}{/tex} As all the points on an equipotential surface are at the same potential, the is zero, the work done in moving the test charge must be zero i.e. {tex}\overrightarrow{\mathrm{E}} \cdot \overrightarrow{d r}{/tex} = or {tex}\overrightarrow{\mathrm{E}} \perp \overrightarrow{d r}{/tex} Since displacement is along the equipotential surface, the electric field must be at right angles to the equipotential surface.
solution: In an electric field {tex}\vec E{/tex} , small work done in moving a test charge q 0 through a small displacement {tex}\overrightarrow{\mathrm{dr}}{/tex} along an equipotential surface is given by dW = {tex}q_{0} \overrightarrow{\mathrm{E}} \cdot \overrightarrow{d r}{/tex} As all the points on an equipotential surface are at the same potential, the is zero, the work done in moving the test charge must be zero i.e. {tex}\overrightarrow{\mathrm{E}} \cdot \overrightarrow{d r}{/tex} = or {tex}\overrightarrow{\mathrm{E}} \perp \overrightarrow{d r}{/tex} Since displacement is along the equipotential surface, the electric field must be at right angles to the equipotential surface.
solution_html:
In an electric field {tex}\vec E{/tex}, small work done in moving a test charge q0 through a small displacement {tex}\overrightarrow{\mathrm{dr}}{/tex} along an equipotential surface is given by
dW = {tex}q_{0} \overrightarrow{\mathrm{E}} \cdot \overrightarrow{d r}{/tex}
As all the points on an equipotential surface are at the same potential, the is zero, the work done in moving the test charge must be zero i.e.
{tex}\overrightarrow{\mathrm{E}} \cdot \overrightarrow{d r}{/tex} = or {tex}\overrightarrow{\mathrm{E}} \perp \overrightarrow{d r}{/tex}
Since displacement is along the equipotential surface, the electric field must be at right angles to the equipotential surface.

subject: Physics
question_html:
1. Obtain the expression for the energy stored per unit volume in a charged parallel plate capacitor.
  2. The electric field inside a parallel plate capacitor is E. Find the amount of work done in moving a charge q over a closed rectangular loop.
question_text: Obtain the expression for the energy stored per unit volume in a charged parallel plate capacitor. The electric field inside a parallel plate capacitor is E. Find the amount of work done in moving a charge q over a closed rectangular loop.
explanation: Let us assume that initially both the plates are uncharged. Now, we have to repeatedly remove small positive charges from one plate and transfer them to other plate. Now, when an additional small charge (dq) is transferred from one plate to another, the small work done is given by {tex}d W = V d q = \frac { q } { C } d q{/tex} The total work done in transferring charge Q is given by {tex}W = \int _ { 0 } ^ { Q } \frac { q } { C } d q = \frac { 1 } { C } \int _ { 0 } ^ { Q } q d q = \frac { 1 } { C } \left[ \frac { ( q ) ^ { 2 } } { 2 } \right] _ { 0 } ^ { Q } = \frac { Q ^ { 2 } } { 2 C }{/tex} This work is stored as electrostatic potential energy U in the capacitor. {tex}U = \frac { Q ^ { 2 } } { 2 C } = \frac { ( C V ) ^ { 2 } } { 2 C }{/tex} {tex}U = \frac { 1 } { 2 } C V ^ { 2 }{/tex} The energy stored per unit volume of space in a capacitor is called energy density. {tex}u = \frac { \frac { 1 } { 2 } C V ^ { 2 } } { A d } \Rightarrow u = \frac { 1 } { 2 } \frac { \varepsilon _ { 0 } A V ^ { 2 } } { d ^ { 2 } A }{/tex} Energy density, {tex}u = \frac { 1 } { 2 } \varepsilon _ { 0 } E ^ { 2 }{/tex} Work done, W = F.d where, F is the force exerted on electrical charge and d is the displacement. Since, the charge is moved along a closed path, net displacement is zero. Therefore, work done = 0
solution: Let us assume that initially both the plates are uncharged. Now, we have to repeatedly remove small positive charges from one plate and transfer them to other plate. Now, when an additional small charge (dq) is transferred from one plate to another, the small work done is given by {tex}d W = V d q = \frac { q } { C } d q{/tex} The total work done in transferring charge Q is given by {tex}W = \int _ { 0 } ^ { Q } \frac { q } { C } d q = \frac { 1 } { C } \int _ { 0 } ^ { Q } q d q = \frac { 1 } { C } \left[ \frac { ( q ) ^ { 2 } } { 2 } \right] _ { 0 } ^ { Q } = \frac { Q ^ { 2 } } { 2 C }{/tex} This work is stored as electrostatic potential energy U in the capacitor. {tex}U = \frac { Q ^ { 2 } } { 2 C } = \frac { ( C V ) ^ { 2 } } { 2 C }{/tex} {tex}U = \frac { 1 } { 2 } C V ^ { 2 }{/tex} The energy stored per unit volume of space in a capacitor is called energy density. {tex}u = \frac { \frac { 1 } { 2 } C V ^ { 2 } } { A d } \Rightarrow u = \frac { 1 } { 2 } \frac { \varepsilon _ { 0 } A V ^ { 2 } } { d ^ { 2 } A }{/tex} Energy density, {tex}u = \frac { 1 } { 2 } \varepsilon _ { 0 } E ^ { 2 }{/tex} Work done, W = F.d where, F is the force exerted on electrical charge and d is the displacement. Since, the charge is moved along a closed path, net displacement is zero. Therefore, work done = 0
solution_html:
1. Let us assume that initially both the plates are uncharged. Now, we have to repeatedly remove small positive charges from one plate and transfer them to other plate.
    Now, when an additional small charge (dq) is transferred from one plate to another, the small work done is given by
    {tex}d W = V d q = \frac { q } { C } d q{/tex}
    The total work done in transferring charge Q is given by
    {tex}W = \int _ { 0 } ^ { Q } \frac { q } { C } d q = \frac { 1 } { C } \int _ { 0 } ^ { Q } q d q = \frac { 1 } { C } \left[ \frac { ( q ) ^ { 2 } } { 2 } \right] _ { 0 } ^ { Q } = \frac { Q ^ { 2 } } { 2 C }{/tex}
    This work is stored as electrostatic potential energy U in the capacitor.
    {tex}U = \frac { Q ^ { 2 } } { 2 C } = \frac { ( C V ) ^ { 2 } } { 2 C }{/tex}
    {tex}U = \frac { 1 } { 2 } C V ^ { 2 }{/tex}
    The energy stored per unit volume of space in a capacitor is called energy density.
    {tex}u = \frac { \frac { 1 } { 2 } C V ^ { 2 } } { A d } \Rightarrow u = \frac { 1 } { 2 } \frac { \varepsilon _ { 0 } A V ^ { 2 } } { d ^ { 2 } A }{/tex}
    Energy density, {tex}u = \frac { 1 } { 2 } \varepsilon _ { 0 } E ^ { 2 }{/tex}
     
  2. Work done, W = F.d
    where, F is the force exerted on electrical charge and d is the displacement.
    Since, the charge is moved along a closed path, net displacement is zero.
    Therefore, work done = 0

subject: Physics
question_html:
Two thin dielectric slabs of dielectric constants {tex}\kappa_1{/tex} and {tex}\kappa_2{/tex}({tex}\kappa_1{/tex} < {tex}\kappa_2{/tex}) are inserted between plates of a parallel plate capacitor, as shown in the figure. The variation of electric field E between the plates with distance d as
measured from plate P is correctly shown by:
question_text: Two thin dielectric slabs of dielectric constants {tex}\kappa_1{/tex} and {tex}\kappa_2{/tex} ( {tex}\kappa_1{/tex} < {tex}\kappa_2{/tex} ) are inserted between plates of a parallel plate capacitor, as shown in the figure. The variation of electric field E between the plates with distance d as measured from plate P is correctly shown by:

options.explanation: In a region of vacuum, {tex}E=\frac{Q}{\varepsilon_{0} A}{/tex} In a region where the dielectric is present, {tex}E=\frac{Q}{\kappa \varepsilon_{0} A}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow E \propto \frac{1}{\kappa}{/tex} As {tex}\kappa_{1}<\kappa_{2}{/tex} , decrease in E is greater for {tex}\kappa_{2}{/tex} than for {tex}\kappa_{1}{/tex}

explanation: In a region of vacuum, {tex}E=\frac{Q}{\varepsilon_{0} A}{/tex} In a region where the dielectric is present, {tex}E=\frac{Q}{\kappa \varepsilon_{0} A}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow E \propto \frac{1}{\kappa}{/tex} As {tex}\kappa_{1}<\kappa_{2}{/tex} , decrease in E is greater for {tex}\kappa_{2}{/tex} than for {tex}\kappa_{1}{/tex}

question_html: What is the geometrical shape of equipotential surfaces due to a single isolated charge?
question_text: What is the geometrical shape of equipotential surfaces due to a single isolated charge?
explanation: The equipotential surfaces produced by a single point charge are spherically symmetrical surfaces which are concentric. In concentric spheres which are as shown below in the figure, the lines of force point radially outwards, so they are perpendicular to the equipotential surfaces at all points.
solution: The equipotential surfaces produced by a single point charge are spherically symmetrical surfaces which are concentric. In concentric spheres which are as shown below in the figure, the lines of force point radially outwards, so they are perpendicular to the equipotential surfaces at all points.
solution_html: The equipotential surfaces produced by a single point charge are spherically symmetrical surfaces which are concentric. In concentric spheres which are as shown below in the figure, the lines of force point radially outwards, so they are perpendicular to the equipotential surfaces at all points.

solution_html: The amount of work done in carrying a charge on the equipotential surface is always zero since the potential difference is zero for every point on the surface.

question_html: Calculate the amount of work done to dissociate a system of three charges 1 {tex}\mu \mathrm{C}{/tex}, 1 {tex}\mu \mathrm{C}{/tex} and - 4 {tex}\mu \mathrm{C}{/tex} placed on the vertices of an equilateral triangle of side 10 cm.
question_text: Calculate the amount of work done to dissociate a system of three charges 1 {tex}\mu \mathrm{C}{/tex} , 1 {tex}\mu \mathrm{C}{/tex} and - 4 {tex}\mu \mathrm{C}{/tex} placed on the vertices of an equilateral triangle of side 10 cm.
explanation: Given: q 1 = 1 µC, q 2 = 1 µC and q 3 = -4 µC Each side of equilateral triangle, r = 10 cm = 0.1 m Now, potential energy, U = Total work done to dissociate the system of the given three charges , is given by :- {tex}\therefore{/tex} U = W 1 + W 2 + W 3 {tex}\Rightarrow\;U=\;\frac{kq_1q_2}{r_1}+\frac{kq_2q_3}{r_2}+\frac{kq_3q_1}{r_3}{/tex} {tex}{where,\;k = {1 \over {4\pi {\varepsilon _0}}}}{/tex} {tex}= 9 \times {{10}^{9\;}}N{m^2}{C^{ - 2}}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow U = \frac{1}{{4\pi {\varepsilon _0}}}\left[ {\frac{{1 \times {{10}^{ - 6}}\times\left( { - 4 \times {{10}^{ - 6}}} \right)}}{{0.10}} + } \right.{/tex} {tex}\left. {\frac{{1 \times {{10}^{ - 6}}\times \left( {1 \times {{10}^{ - 6}}} \right)}}{{0.10}} + \frac{{ - 4 \times {{10}^{ - 6}}\times\left( {1 \times {{10}^{ - 6}}} \right)}}{{0.10}}} \right]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow U = \left( 1 / 4 \pi \varepsilon _ { 0 } \right) \times 10 ^ { - 12 } ( - 4 \times 10+ 10 - 4 \times 10 ){/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow U = - 9 \times 10 ^ { 9 } \times 10 ^ { - 12 } \times 70{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} U = -0.63 J Hence the net work done to dissociate the system of the given three charges is given by , W = -U = 0.63 J
solution: Given: q 1 = 1 µC, q 2 = 1 µC and q 3 = -4 µC Each side of equilateral triangle, r = 10 cm = 0.1 m Now, potential energy, U = Total work done to dissociate the system of the given three charges , is given by :- {tex}\therefore{/tex} U = W 1 + W 2 + W 3 {tex}\Rightarrow\;U=\;\frac{kq_1q_2}{r_1}+\frac{kq_2q_3}{r_2}+\frac{kq_3q_1}{r_3}{/tex} {tex}{where,\;k = {1 \over {4\pi {\varepsilon _0}}}}{/tex} {tex}= 9 \times {{10}^{9\;}}N{m^2}{C^{ - 2}}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow U = \frac{1}{{4\pi {\varepsilon _0}}}\left[ {\frac{{1 \times {{10}^{ - 6}}\times\left( { - 4 \times {{10}^{ - 6}}} \right)}}{{0.10}} + } \right.{/tex} {tex}\left. {\frac{{1 \times {{10}^{ - 6}}\times \left( {1 \times {{10}^{ - 6}}} \right)}}{{0.10}} + \frac{{ - 4 \times {{10}^{ - 6}}\times\left( {1 \times {{10}^{ - 6}}} \right)}}{{0.10}}} \right]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow U = \left( 1 / 4 \pi \varepsilon _ { 0 } \right) \times 10 ^ { - 12 } ( - 4 \times 10+ 10 - 4 \times 10 ){/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow U = - 9 \times 10 ^ { 9 } \times 10 ^ { - 12 } \times 70{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} U = -0.63 J Hence the net work done to dissociate the system of the given three charges is given by , W = -U = 0.63 J
solution_html:
Given: q1 = 1 µC, q2 = 1 µC and q3 = -4 µC
Each side of equilateral triangle, r = 10 cm = 0.1 m

Now, potential energy, U = Total work done to dissociate the system of the given three charges , is given by :-
{tex}\therefore{/tex} U = W1 + W2 + W3
{tex}\Rightarrow\;U=\;\frac{kq_1q_2}{r_1}+\frac{kq_2q_3}{r_2}+\frac{kq_3q_1}{r_3}{/tex}
{tex}{where,\;k = {1 \over {4\pi {\varepsilon _0}}}}{/tex} {tex}= 9 \times {{10}^{9\;}}N{m^2}{C^{ - 2}}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow U = \frac{1}{{4\pi {\varepsilon _0}}}\left[ {\frac{{1 \times {{10}^{ - 6}}\times\left( { - 4 \times {{10}^{ - 6}}} \right)}}{{0.10}} + } \right.{/tex} {tex}\left. {\frac{{1 \times {{10}^{ - 6}}\times \left( {1 \times {{10}^{ - 6}}} \right)}}{{0.10}} + \frac{{ - 4 \times {{10}^{ - 6}}\times\left( {1 \times {{10}^{ - 6}}} \right)}}{{0.10}}} \right]{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow U = \left( 1 / 4 \pi \varepsilon _ { 0 } \right) \times 10 ^ { - 12 } ( - 4 \times 10+ 10 - 4 \times 10 ){/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow U = - 9 \times 10 ^ { 9 } \times 10 ^ { - 12 } \times 70{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} U = -0.63 J
Hence the net work done to dissociate the system of the given three charges is given by ,
W = -U = 0.63 J

subject: Physics
question_html: Determine the potential difference across the plates of the capacitor C1 of the network shown in the figure. (assume, E2 > E1)
question_text: Determine the potential difference across the plates of the capacitor C 1 of the network shown in the figure. (assume, E 2 > E 1 )
explanation: {tex}-E_{2}-\frac{q}{C_{2}}+E_{1}-\frac{q}{C_{1}}=0{/tex} {tex}\frac{q}{C_{1}}+\frac{q}{C_{2}}=E_{2}-E_{1}{/tex} {tex}q\left(\frac{1}{C_{1}}+\frac{1}{C_{2}}\right)=E_{2}-E_{1}{/tex} {tex}q \times\left(\frac{C_{1}+C_{2}}{C_{1} \times C_{2}}\right)=E_{2}-E_{1}{/tex} {tex}q=\frac{\left(C_{1} \times C_{2}\right)\left(E_{2}-E_{1}\right)}{C_{1}+C_{2}}{/tex} Thus, potential difference across C 1 is {tex}V_{1}=\frac{q}{C_{1}}=\frac{C_{2}\left(E_{2}-E_{1}\right)}{C_{1}+C_{2}}{/tex}
solution: {tex}-E_{2}-\frac{q}{C_{2}}+E_{1}-\frac{q}{C_{1}}=0{/tex} {tex}\frac{q}{C_{1}}+\frac{q}{C_{2}}=E_{2}-E_{1}{/tex} {tex}q\left(\frac{1}{C_{1}}+\frac{1}{C_{2}}\right)=E_{2}-E_{1}{/tex} {tex}q \times\left(\frac{C_{1}+C_{2}}{C_{1} \times C_{2}}\right)=E_{2}-E_{1}{/tex} {tex}q=\frac{\left(C_{1} \times C_{2}\right)\left(E_{2}-E_{1}\right)}{C_{1}+C_{2}}{/tex} Thus, potential difference across C 1 is {tex}V_{1}=\frac{q}{C_{1}}=\frac{C_{2}\left(E_{2}-E_{1}\right)}{C_{1}+C_{2}}{/tex}
solution_html:
{tex}-E_{2}-\frac{q}{C_{2}}+E_{1}-\frac{q}{C_{1}}=0{/tex}
{tex}\frac{q}{C_{1}}+\frac{q}{C_{2}}=E_{2}-E_{1}{/tex}
{tex}q\left(\frac{1}{C_{1}}+\frac{1}{C_{2}}\right)=E_{2}-E_{1}{/tex}
{tex}q \times\left(\frac{C_{1}+C_{2}}{C_{1} \times C_{2}}\right)=E_{2}-E_{1}{/tex}
{tex}q=\frac{\left(C_{1} \times C_{2}\right)\left(E_{2}-E_{1}\right)}{C_{1}+C_{2}}{/tex}
Thus, potential difference across C1 is
{tex}V_{1}=\frac{q}{C_{1}}=\frac{C_{2}\left(E_{2}-E_{1}\right)}{C_{1}+C_{2}}{/tex}

question_html: A parallel plate capacitor, each of plate area A and separation 'd' between the two plates, is charged with charges +Q and -Q on the two plates. Deduce the expression for the energy stored in capacitor.
question_text: A parallel plate capacitor, each of plate area A and separation 'd' between the two plates, is charged with charges +Q and -Q on the two plates. Deduce the expression for the energy stored in capacitor.
explanation: The work done in charging a capacitor is stored as its electrical potential energy. Now considering a capacitor of capacitance C, initially whose two plates are uncharged, let Q and -Q are charges on the two plates and produces a uniform electric field, {tex}E = \frac { \sigma } { \varepsilon _ { 0 } }{/tex} at any point between the plates( {tex}\sigma {/tex} being surface charge density) and a potential difference {tex}V = \frac { q } { C }{/tex} ...(i) If an infinitesimal charge dq is transported in steps from negative charged plate to positive charged plate, till charges rises to +Q and -Q, then work done, dW = dq × V ...(ii) From Eqs. (i) and (ii), we get {tex}d W = d q \left( \frac { q } { C } \right) \Rightarrow W = \int d W{/tex} To charge the capacitor from 0 to Q, work done is stored as electrical potential energy U in the capacitor {tex}\therefore U = W = \int\limits_0^Q {\frac{q}{C}} ×dq = \frac{{{Q^2}}}{{2C}}{/tex} {tex}\therefore U = \frac { Q ^ { 2 } } { { 2C } } = \frac { 1 } { 2 } C V ^ { 2 } = \frac { 1 } { 2 } Q V{/tex} {tex}[ \because Q = C V ]{/tex}
solution: The work done in charging a capacitor is stored as its electrical potential energy. Now considering a capacitor of capacitance C, initially whose two plates are uncharged, let Q and -Q are charges on the two plates and produces a uniform electric field, {tex}E = \frac { \sigma } { \varepsilon _ { 0 } }{/tex} at any point between the plates( {tex}\sigma {/tex} being surface charge density) and a potential difference {tex}V = \frac { q } { C }{/tex} ...(i) If an infinitesimal charge dq is transported in steps from negative charged plate to positive charged plate, till charges rises to +Q and -Q, then work done, dW = dq × V ...(ii) From Eqs. (i) and (ii), we get {tex}d W = d q \left( \frac { q } { C } \right) \Rightarrow W = \int d W{/tex} To charge the capacitor from 0 to Q, work done is stored as electrical potential energy U in the capacitor {tex}\therefore U = W = \int\limits_0^Q {\frac{q}{C}} ×dq = \frac{{{Q^2}}}{{2C}}{/tex} {tex}\therefore U = \frac { Q ^ { 2 } } { { 2C } } = \frac { 1 } { 2 } C V ^ { 2 } = \frac { 1 } { 2 } Q V{/tex} {tex}[ \because Q = C V ]{/tex}
solution_html:
The work done in charging a capacitor is stored as its electrical potential energy.
Now considering a capacitor of capacitance C, initially whose two plates are uncharged, let Q and -Q are charges on the two plates and produces a uniform electric field,
{tex}E = \frac { \sigma } { \varepsilon _ { 0 } }{/tex} at any point between the plates({tex}\sigma {/tex} being surface charge density) and a potential difference
{tex}V = \frac { q } { C }{/tex} ...(i)
If an infinitesimal charge dq is transported in steps from negative charged plate to positive charged plate, till charges rises to +Q and -Q, then
work done, dW = dq × V ...(ii)
From Eqs. (i) and (ii), we get
{tex}d W = d q \left( \frac { q } { C } \right) \Rightarrow W = \int d W{/tex}
To charge the capacitor from 0 to Q, work done is stored as electrical potential energy U in the capacitor
{tex}\therefore U = W = \int\limits_0^Q {\frac{q}{C}} ×dq = \frac{{{Q^2}}}{{2C}}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore U = \frac { Q ^ { 2 } } { { 2C } } = \frac { 1 } { 2 } C V ^ { 2 } = \frac { 1 } { 2 } Q V{/tex} {tex}[ \because Q = C V ]{/tex}

subject: Physics
question_html: N spherical droplets, each of radius r, have been charged to have a potential V each. If all these droplets were to coalesce to form a single large drop, what would be the potential of this large drop?
question_text: N spherical droplets, each of radius r, have been charged to have a potential V each. If all these droplets were to coalesce to form a single large drop, what would be the potential of this large drop?
explanation: Let us consider r is the radius of the small droplet, R is the radius of the new droplet. In normal form, charge is given by, q = 4 {tex}\pi\in{/tex} 0 rV. Total (initial) charge on all the droplets {tex}=N \times\left(4 \pi \varepsilon_{0} k r V\right){/tex} Also {tex}N \times \frac{4}{3} \pi r^{3}=\frac{4}{3} \pi R^{3}{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} R = N 1/3 r. If V' is the potential of the large drop, we have {tex}4 \pi \varepsilon_{0} k R \times V'{/tex} {tex}=N \times 4 \pi \varepsilon_{0} k r \times V{/tex} {tex}\therefore V'=\frac{N r}{R} \cdot V=N^{2 / 3} V{/tex}
solution: Let us consider r is the radius of the small droplet, R is the radius of the new droplet. In normal form, charge is given by, q = 4 {tex}\pi\in{/tex} 0 rV. Total (initial) charge on all the droplets {tex}=N \times\left(4 \pi \varepsilon_{0} k r V\right){/tex} Also {tex}N \times \frac{4}{3} \pi r^{3}=\frac{4}{3} \pi R^{3}{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} R = N 1/3 r. If V' is the potential of the large drop, we have {tex}4 \pi \varepsilon_{0} k R \times V'{/tex} {tex}=N \times 4 \pi \varepsilon_{0} k r \times V{/tex} {tex}\therefore V'=\frac{N r}{R} \cdot V=N^{2 / 3} V{/tex}
solution_html:
Let us consider r is the radius of the small droplet, R is the radius of the new droplet. In normal form, charge is given by, q = 4{tex}\pi\in{/tex}0rV. Total (initial) charge on all the droplets
{tex}=N \times\left(4 \pi \varepsilon_{0} k r V\right){/tex}
Also {tex}N \times \frac{4}{3} \pi r^{3}=\frac{4}{3} \pi R^{3}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} R = N1/3r.
If V' is the potential of the large drop, we have
{tex}4 \pi \varepsilon_{0} k R \times V'{/tex} {tex}=N \times 4 \pi \varepsilon_{0} k r \times V{/tex}
{tex}\therefore V'=\frac{N r}{R} \cdot V=N^{2 / 3} V{/tex}
