question_html: Find the amount of work done in arranging the three point charges, on the vertices of an equilateral triangle ABC, of side 10 cm, as shown in the figure.
question_text: Find the amount of work done in arranging the three point charges, on the vertices of an equilateral triangle ABC, of side 10 cm, as shown in the figure.
explanation: Given q A =6 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -6 C = q B qC = -6 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -6 C r = 10 cm = 0.1 m W = W AB + W BC + W AC (in general form W= q 1 q 2/4 {tex}\pi\in{/tex} 0 r) {tex}W=\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0} r}{/tex} [q A q B + q B q C + q C q A ] {tex}W=9 \times 10^{9} \times \frac{1}{0.1}{/tex} [36-36-36] {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -12 W = 9 × 10 9 × (-36) × 10 -11 W = -3.24 J
solution: Given q A =6 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -6 C = q B qC = -6 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -6 C r = 10 cm = 0.1 m W = W AB + W BC + W AC (in general form W= q 1 q 2/4 {tex}\pi\in{/tex} 0 r) {tex}W=\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0} r}{/tex} [q A q B + q B q C + q C q A ] {tex}W=9 \times 10^{9} \times \frac{1}{0.1}{/tex} [36-36-36] {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -12 W = 9 × 10 9 × (-36) × 10 -11 W = -3.24 J
solution_html:
Given qA =6{tex}\times{/tex} 10-6 C = qB 
qC = -6{tex}\times{/tex} 10-6C
r = 10 cm = 0.1 m
W = WAB + WBC + WAC (in general form W= q1q2/4{tex}\pi\in{/tex}0r)
{tex}W=\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0} r}{/tex} [qAqB + qBqC + qCqA]
{tex}W=9 \times 10^{9} \times \frac{1}{0.1}{/tex} [36-36-36] {tex}\times{/tex} 10-12
W = 9 × 109 × (-36) × 10-11
W = -3.24 J

subject: Physics
question_html:
1. Obtain the expression for the potential due to an electric dipole of dipole moment P at a point r on the axial line.
  2. Two identical capacitors of plate dimensions l x b and plate separation d have dielectric slabs filled in between the space of the plates as shown in the figure. Find out the relation between K, K​​​​​​1​​​​​ and K​​​​​​2.
question_text: Obtain the expression for the potential due to an electric dipole of dipole moment P at a point r on the axial line. Two identical capacitors of plate dimensions l x b and plate separation d have dielectric slabs filled in between the space of the plates as shown in the figure. Find out the relation between K, K ​​​​​​1 ​​​​​ and K ​​​​​​2 .
explanation: Let P be an axial point at a distance r from the centre of the dipole of dipole length 2a. Electric potential at point P will be {tex}V = V _ { 1 } + V _ { 2 } = \frac { 1 } { 4 \pi \varepsilon _ { 0 } } \cdot \frac { ( - q ) } { r + a } + \frac { 1 } { 4 \pi \varepsilon _ { 0 } } \cdot \frac { q } { r - a }{/tex} (V ​​​​​​1 and V ​​​​​​2 are the potentials due to charges -q and +q respectively of the dipole) {tex}= \frac { q } { 4 \pi \varepsilon _ { 0 } } \left[ \frac { 1 } { r - a } - \frac { 1 } { r + a } \right] = \frac { q } { 4 \pi \varepsilon _ { 0 } } \cdot \frac { 2 a } { r ^ { 2 } - a ^ { 2 } }{/tex} {tex}= \frac { 1 } { 4 \pi \varepsilon _ { 0 } } \cdot \frac { p } { r ^ { 2 } - a ^ { 2 } } \quad \quad \quad [ \because p = q ( 2 a ) {/tex} ] For a far away point, r >>a {tex}\therefore V = \frac { 1 } { 4 \pi \varepsilon _ { 0 } } \cdot \frac { p } { r ^ { 2 } } \;or\; V \propto \frac { 1 } { r ^ { 2 } }{/tex} Thus, due to an electric dipole, potential at an axial point is V {tex}\propto \frac { 1 } { r ^ { 2 } }{/tex} Let A is area of each plate and C 1 and C 2 are capacitances of the slabs given in the figures above. Let intially, {tex}C _ { 1 }' = C = \frac { \varepsilon _ { 0 } A } { d } = C _ { 2 }{/tex} ..........(i) After inserting respective dielectric slabs. {tex}C _ { 2 } ^ { \prime } = K C{/tex} and {tex}C _ { 2 } ^ { \prime } = K _ { 1 } \frac { \varepsilon _ { 0 } ( A / 2 ) } { d } + K _ { 2 } \frac { \varepsilon _ { 0 } ( A / 2 ) } { d }{/tex} {tex}= \frac { \varepsilon _ { 0 } A } { 2 d } \left( K _ { 1 } + K _ { 2 } \right){/tex} {tex}C _ { 2 } ^ { \prime } = \frac { C } { 2 } \left( K _ { 1 } + K _ { 2 } \right){/tex} ............(ii) From Eqs. (i) and (ii), we get {tex}C _ { 1 } ^ { \prime } = C _ { 2 } ^ { \prime } \Rightarrow K C = \frac { C } { 2 } \left( K _ { 1 } + K _ { 2 } \right) \Rightarrow K = \frac { 1 } { 2 } \left( K _ { 1 } + K _ { 2 } \right){/tex}
solution: Let P be an axial point at a distance r from the centre of the dipole of dipole length 2a. Electric potential at point P will be {tex}V = V _ { 1 } + V _ { 2 } = \frac { 1 } { 4 \pi \varepsilon _ { 0 } } \cdot \frac { ( - q ) } { r + a } + \frac { 1 } { 4 \pi \varepsilon _ { 0 } } \cdot \frac { q } { r - a }{/tex} (V ​​​​​​1 and V ​​​​​​2 are the potentials due to charges -q and +q respectively of the dipole) {tex}= \frac { q } { 4 \pi \varepsilon _ { 0 } } \left[ \frac { 1 } { r - a } - \frac { 1 } { r + a } \right] = \frac { q } { 4 \pi \varepsilon _ { 0 } } \cdot \frac { 2 a } { r ^ { 2 } - a ^ { 2 } }{/tex} {tex}= \frac { 1 } { 4 \pi \varepsilon _ { 0 } } \cdot \frac { p } { r ^ { 2 } - a ^ { 2 } } \quad \quad \quad [ \because p = q ( 2 a ) {/tex} ] For a far away point, r >>a {tex}\therefore V = \frac { 1 } { 4 \pi \varepsilon _ { 0 } } \cdot \frac { p } { r ^ { 2 } } \;or\; V \propto \frac { 1 } { r ^ { 2 } }{/tex} Thus, due to an electric dipole, potential at an axial point is V {tex}\propto \frac { 1 } { r ^ { 2 } }{/tex} Let A is area of each plate and C 1 and C 2 are capacitances of the slabs given in the figures above. Let intially, {tex}C _ { 1 }' = C = \frac { \varepsilon _ { 0 } A } { d } = C _ { 2 }{/tex} ..........(i) After inserting respective dielectric slabs. {tex}C _ { 2 } ^ { \prime } = K C{/tex} and {tex}C _ { 2 } ^ { \prime } = K _ { 1 } \frac { \varepsilon _ { 0 } ( A / 2 ) } { d } + K _ { 2 } \frac { \varepsilon _ { 0 } ( A / 2 ) } { d }{/tex} {tex}= \frac { \varepsilon _ { 0 } A } { 2 d } \left( K _ { 1 } + K _ { 2 } \right){/tex} {tex}C _ { 2 } ^ { \prime } = \frac { C } { 2 } \left( K _ { 1 } + K _ { 2 } \right){/tex} ............(ii) From Eqs. (i) and (ii), we get {tex}C _ { 1 } ^ { \prime } = C _ { 2 } ^ { \prime } \Rightarrow K C = \frac { C } { 2 } \left( K _ { 1 } + K _ { 2 } \right) \Rightarrow K = \frac { 1 } { 2 } \left( K _ { 1 } + K _ { 2 } \right){/tex}
solution_html:
1. Let P be an axial point at a distance r from the centre of the dipole of dipole length 2a.
    Electric potential at point P will be
    {tex}V = V _ { 1 } + V _ { 2 } = \frac { 1 } { 4 \pi \varepsilon _ { 0 } } \cdot \frac { ( - q ) } { r + a } + \frac { 1 } { 4 \pi \varepsilon _ { 0 } } \cdot \frac { q } { r - a }{/tex}(V​​​​​​1 and V​​​​​​2 are the potentials due to charges -q and +q respectively of the dipole)
    {tex}= \frac { q } { 4 \pi \varepsilon _ { 0 } } \left[ \frac { 1 } { r - a } - \frac { 1 } { r + a } \right] = \frac { q } { 4 \pi \varepsilon _ { 0 } } \cdot \frac { 2 a } { r ^ { 2 } - a ^ { 2 } }{/tex}
    {tex}= \frac { 1 } { 4 \pi \varepsilon _ { 0 } } \cdot \frac { p } { r ^ { 2 } - a ^ { 2 } } \quad \quad \quad [ \because p = q ( 2 a ) {/tex}]
     For a far away point, r >>a
    {tex}\therefore V = \frac { 1 } { 4 \pi \varepsilon _ { 0 } } \cdot \frac { p } { r ^ { 2 } } \;or\; V \propto \frac { 1 } { r ^ { 2 } }{/tex}
    Thus, due to an electric dipole, potential at an axial point is V {tex}\propto \frac { 1 } { r ^ { 2 } }{/tex}
  2. Let A is area of each plate and C1 and C2 are capacitances of the slabs given in the figures above.
    Let intially, {tex}C _ { 1 }' = C = \frac { \varepsilon _ { 0 } A } { d } = C _ { 2 }{/tex}..........(i)
    After inserting respective dielectric slabs.
    {tex}C _ { 2 } ^ { \prime } = K C{/tex}
    and {tex}C _ { 2 } ^ { \prime } = K _ { 1 } \frac { \varepsilon _ { 0 } ( A / 2 ) } { d } + K _ { 2 } \frac { \varepsilon _ { 0 } ( A / 2 ) } { d }{/tex}
    {tex}= \frac { \varepsilon _ { 0 } A } { 2 d } \left( K _ { 1 } + K _ { 2 } \right){/tex}
    {tex}C _ { 2 } ^ { \prime } = \frac { C } { 2 } \left( K _ { 1 } + K _ { 2 } \right){/tex}............(ii)
    From Eqs. (i) and (ii), we get
    {tex}C _ { 1 } ^ { \prime } = C _ { 2 } ^ { \prime } \Rightarrow K C = \frac { C } { 2 } \left( K _ { 1 } + K _ { 2 } \right) \Rightarrow K = \frac { 1 } { 2 } \left( K _ { 1 } + K _ { 2 } \right){/tex}

subject: Physics
question_html:
Two identical capacitors of plate dimension l {tex}\times{/tex} b and plate separation d have dielectric slabs filled in between the space of the plates as shown in the figures.

Obtain the relation between the dielectric constants K, K1 and K2.
question_text: Two identical capacitors of plate dimension l {tex}\times{/tex} b and plate separation d have dielectric slabs filled in between the space of the plates as shown in the figures. Obtain the relation between the dielectric constants K, K 1 and K 2 .
explanation: The capacitor can be considered as split into two capacitors connected in parallel. Area of the capacitor becomes half of its original value. Here {tex}c_{1}=\frac{\kappa_{1} \varepsilon_{0} A / 2}{d}{/tex} , {tex}C_{2}=\frac{\kappa_{2} \varepsilon_{0} A / 2}{d}{/tex} In parallel combination, C eq = C 1 + C 2 {tex}C_{e q}=\frac{\kappa_{1} \varepsilon_{0} A}{2 d}+\frac{\kappa_{2} \varepsilon_{0} A}{2 d}{/tex} {tex}C_{e q}=\frac{\varepsilon_{0} A}{2 d}{/tex} (K 1 + K 2 ) ...(i) From this figure {tex}C_{e q}=\frac{K \varepsilon_{0} A}{d}{/tex} ...(ii) From (i) and (ii) {tex}\frac{\ K\varepsilon_{0} A}{ d}=\frac{\varepsilon_{0} A}{2 d}{/tex} (K 1 + K 2 ) K = (K 1 + K 2 ) /2
solution: The capacitor can be considered as split into two capacitors connected in parallel. Area of the capacitor becomes half of its original value. Here {tex}c_{1}=\frac{\kappa_{1} \varepsilon_{0} A / 2}{d}{/tex} , {tex}C_{2}=\frac{\kappa_{2} \varepsilon_{0} A / 2}{d}{/tex} In parallel combination, C eq = C 1 + C 2 {tex}C_{e q}=\frac{\kappa_{1} \varepsilon_{0} A}{2 d}+\frac{\kappa_{2} \varepsilon_{0} A}{2 d}{/tex} {tex}C_{e q}=\frac{\varepsilon_{0} A}{2 d}{/tex} (K 1 + K 2 ) ...(i) From this figure {tex}C_{e q}=\frac{K \varepsilon_{0} A}{d}{/tex} ...(ii) From (i) and (ii) {tex}\frac{\ K\varepsilon_{0} A}{ d}=\frac{\varepsilon_{0} A}{2 d}{/tex} (K 1 + K 2 ) K = (K 1 + K 2 ) /2
solution_html:
The capacitor can be considered as split into two capacitors connected in parallel. Area of the capacitor becomes half of its original value.
Here {tex}c_{1}=\frac{\kappa_{1} \varepsilon_{0} A / 2}{d}{/tex}, {tex}C_{2}=\frac{\kappa_{2} \varepsilon_{0} A / 2}{d}{/tex}

In parallel combination,
Ceq = C1 + C2
{tex}C_{e q}=\frac{\kappa_{1} \varepsilon_{0} A}{2 d}+\frac{\kappa_{2} \varepsilon_{0} A}{2 d}{/tex}
{tex}C_{e q}=\frac{\varepsilon_{0} A}{2 d}{/tex} (K1 + K2) ...(i)
From this figure

{tex}C_{e q}=\frac{K \varepsilon_{0} A}{d}{/tex} ...(ii)
From (i) and (ii)
{tex}\frac{\ K\varepsilon_{0} A}{ d}=\frac{\varepsilon_{0} A}{2 d}{/tex} (K1 + K2)
K = (K1 + K2) /2

question_html: Two thin concentric shells of radii r1 and r2 (r2 > r1 ) have charges q1 and q2. Write the expression for the potential at the surface of inner and outer shells.
question_text: Two thin concentric shells of radii r 1 and r 2 (r 2 > r 1 ) have charges q 1 and q 2 . Write the expression for the potential at the surface of inner and outer shells.
explanation: The potential at the surface of the inner shell, V 1 = Potential due to its own charge q 1 + Potential due to charge q 2 on the outer shell {tex}=\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}}\left(\frac{q_{1}}{r_{1}}+\frac{q_{2}}{r_{2}}\right){/tex} The potential at the surface of the outer shell V 2 = Potential due to charge q 1 on inner shell + Potential due to charge q 2 on the outer shell {tex}=\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}}\left(\frac{q_{1}}{r_{2}}+\frac{q_{2}}{r_{2}}\right){/tex}
solution: The potential at the surface of the inner shell, V 1 = Potential due to its own charge q 1 + Potential due to charge q 2 on the outer shell {tex}=\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}}\left(\frac{q_{1}}{r_{1}}+\frac{q_{2}}{r_{2}}\right){/tex} The potential at the surface of the outer shell V 2 = Potential due to charge q 1 on inner shell + Potential due to charge q 2 on the outer shell {tex}=\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}}\left(\frac{q_{1}}{r_{2}}+\frac{q_{2}}{r_{2}}\right){/tex}
solution_html:
The potential at the surface of the inner shell,
V1 = Potential due to its own charge q1 + Potential due to charge q2 on the outer shell
{tex}=\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}}\left(\frac{q_{1}}{r_{1}}+\frac{q_{2}}{r_{2}}\right){/tex}
The potential at the surface of the outer shell
V2 = Potential due to charge q1 on inner shell + Potential due to charge q2 on the outer shell 
{tex}=\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}}\left(\frac{q_{1}}{r_{2}}+\frac{q_{2}}{r_{2}}\right){/tex}

question_html: Two charges {tex}2 \mu \mathrm{C}{/tex} and {tex}-2 \mu \mathrm{C}{/tex} are placed at points a and b, 5 cm apart. Depict an equipotential surface of the system.
question_text: Two charges {tex}2 \mu \mathrm{C}{/tex} and {tex}-2 \mu \mathrm{C}{/tex} are placed at points a and b, 5 cm apart. Depict an equipotential surface of the system.
explanation: Given, {tex} q_A=2 \mu \mathrm{C}=2 \times 10^{-6} \mathrm{C} {/tex} {tex}q_B=-2 \mu C=-2 \times 10^{-6} \mathrm{C} \text { and } r=5 \mathrm{~cm}{/tex} As, potential at point 0 is zero. {tex}V=\frac{2 \times 10^{-6}}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0 x \times 10^{-2}}+\frac{-2 \times 10^{-6}}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0(5-x) \times 10^{-2}}=0{/tex} or {tex}\frac{2 \times 10^{-6}}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0 x \times 10^{-2}}=\frac{2 \times 10^{-6}}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0(5-x) \times 10^{-2}}{/tex} or x = 5 - x {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x = 2.5 cm
solution: Given, {tex} q_A=2 \mu \mathrm{C}=2 \times 10^{-6} \mathrm{C} {/tex} {tex}q_B=-2 \mu C=-2 \times 10^{-6} \mathrm{C} \text { and } r=5 \mathrm{~cm}{/tex} As, potential at point 0 is zero. {tex}V=\frac{2 \times 10^{-6}}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0 x \times 10^{-2}}+\frac{-2 \times 10^{-6}}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0(5-x) \times 10^{-2}}=0{/tex} or {tex}\frac{2 \times 10^{-6}}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0 x \times 10^{-2}}=\frac{2 \times 10^{-6}}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0(5-x) \times 10^{-2}}{/tex} or x = 5 - x {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x = 2.5 cm
solution_html:
Given, {tex} q_A=2 \mu \mathrm{C}=2 \times 10^{-6} \mathrm{C} {/tex}
{tex}q_B=-2 \mu C=-2 \times 10^{-6} \mathrm{C} \text { and } r=5 \mathrm{~cm}{/tex}

As, potential at point 0 is zero.
{tex}V=\frac{2 \times 10^{-6}}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0 x \times 10^{-2}}+\frac{-2 \times 10^{-6}}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0(5-x) \times 10^{-2}}=0{/tex}
or {tex}\frac{2 \times 10^{-6}}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0 x \times 10^{-2}}=\frac{2 \times 10^{-6}}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0(5-x) \times 10^{-2}}{/tex}
or x = 5 - x {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex}x = 2.5 cm

subject: Physics
question_html: Why there is no work done in moving a charge from one point to another on an equipotential surface?
question_text: Why there is no work done in moving a charge from one point to another on an equipotential surface?
explanation: An equipotential surface is a surface at which electric potential at each and every point on the surface is same. As, work done in moving a charged particle having charge +q from one point to another on any surface is defined as {tex}W = q × (V_A-V_B) {/tex} (say, V A and V B are potentials at two points A and B on the surface) On an equipotential surface, the potential everywhere always remains constant. So, change in potential {tex}V_A - V_B=0{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow {/tex} Work done, {tex}W = 0{/tex}
solution: An equipotential surface is a surface at which electric potential at each and every point on the surface is same. As, work done in moving a charged particle having charge +q from one point to another on any surface is defined as {tex}W = q × (V_A-V_B) {/tex} (say, V A and V B are potentials at two points A and B on the surface) On an equipotential surface, the potential everywhere always remains constant. So, change in potential {tex}V_A - V_B=0{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow {/tex} Work done, {tex}W = 0{/tex}
solution_html:
An equipotential surface is a surface at which electric potential at each and every point on the surface is same.
As, work done in moving a charged particle having charge +q from one point to another on any surface is defined as
{tex}W = q × (V_A-V_B) {/tex} (say, VA and VB are potentials at two points A and B on the surface)
On an equipotential surface, the potential everywhere always remains constant. So, change in potential {tex}V_A - V_B=0{/tex} 
{tex}\Rightarrow {/tex} Work done, {tex}W = 0{/tex}

subject: Physics
question_html: Why the potential inside a hollow spherical charged conductor is constant and has the same value as on its surface?
question_text: Why the potential inside a hollow spherical charged conductor is constant and has the same value as on its surface?
explanation: The electric field inside the hollow spherical charged conductor is zero. And the field is related to the potential as {tex}\vec E = - \frac {dV}{dr} \hat n{/tex} . Hence dV = 0. Integrating this we get V = constant. Thus, potential is constant and therefore equal to its value at the surface, i.e {tex}V = \frac {q}{4 {tex}\pi{/tex} \epsilon_0 R}{/tex} , R being the radius of the sphere.
solution: The electric field inside the hollow spherical charged conductor is zero. And the field is related to the potential as {tex}\vec E = - \frac {dV}{dr} \hat n{/tex} . Hence dV = 0. Integrating this we get V = constant. Thus, potential is constant and therefore equal to its value at the surface, i.e {tex}V = \frac {q}{4 {tex}\pi{/tex} \epsilon_0 R}{/tex} , R being the radius of the sphere.
solution_html:
The electric field inside the hollow spherical charged conductor is zero. And the field is related to the potential as {tex}\vec E = - \frac {dV}{dr} \hat n{/tex}. Hence dV = 0.
Integrating this we get V = constant.
Thus, potential is constant and therefore equal to its value at the surface, i.e {tex}V = \frac {q}{4{tex}\pi{/tex} \epsilon_0 R}{/tex}, R being the radius of the sphere.

subject: Physics
question_html: A capacitor of 200 pF is charged by a 300 V battery. The battery is then disconnected and the charged capacitor is connected to another uncharged capacitor of 100 pF. Calculate the difference between the final energy stored in the combined system and the initial energy stored in the single capacitor.
question_text: A capacitor of 200 pF is charged by a 300 V battery. The battery is then disconnected and the charged capacitor is connected to another uncharged capacitor of 100 pF. Calculate the difference between the final energy stored in the combined system and the initial energy stored in the single capacitor.
explanation: Given, C = 200 pF = 200 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -12 F and V = 300V The energy (initially) stored in the capacitor is {tex}\begin{aligned} U _ { i } & = \frac { 1 } { 2 } C V ^ { 2 } = \frac { 1 } { 2 } \times 200 \times 10 ^ { - 12 } \times 300 \times 300 = 9 \times 10 ^ { - 6 } \mathrm { J } \end{aligned}{/tex} The charge stored in the capacitor when charged through 300 V battery is Q = CV = 200 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -12 {tex}\times{/tex} 300 = 6 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -8 = 60 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -9 C = 60 nC After removing the battery when two capacitors are connected, they have their positive plates at the same potential and negative plates also at the same potential. Let V be the common potential difference. By the law of conservation of charge, charge would distribute but total charge would remain constant. Thus, Q = q + q', q and q' are the charges stored in the two capacitors (say) {tex}\frac { q } { C } = \frac { q ^ { \prime } } { C ^ { \prime } }{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac { q } { 200 } = \frac { q ^ { \prime } } { 100 }{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} q = 2q' Thus, net charge, Q = 2q' + q' = 3q' So, {tex}q ^ { \prime } = \frac { Q } { 3 } = \frac { 60 \mathrm { nC } } { 3 } = 20 \mathrm { nc }{/tex} and q = 2q' = 40nC Now the final energy stored in the system of two capacitors, {tex}U _ { f } = \frac { q ^ { 2 } } { 2 C } + \frac { q' ^ { 2 } } { 2 C' }{/tex} {tex}= \frac { 1 } { 2 } \times \frac { \left( 40 \times 10 ^ { - 9 } \right) ^ { 2 } } { 200 \times 10 ^ { - 12 } } + \frac { 1 } { 2 } \times \frac { \left( 20 \times 10 ^ { - 9 } \right) ^ { 2 } } { 100 \times 10 ^ { - 12 } }{/tex} {tex}= 4 \times 10 ^ { - 6 } + 2 \times 10 ^ { - 6 } = 6 \times 10 ^ { - 6 } \mathrm { J }{/tex} Difference in energy = final energy - initial energy = U f - U i {tex}= 6 \times 10 ^ { - 6 } - 9 \times 10 ^ { - 6 } = - 3 \times 10 ^ { - 6 }{/tex} Joules (negative sign indicates that the energy is lost in the process) Thus, difference in energy is {tex}- 3 \times 10 ^ { - 6 }{/tex} J.
solution: Given, C = 200 pF = 200 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -12 F and V = 300V The energy (initially) stored in the capacitor is {tex}\begin{aligned} U _ { i } & = \frac { 1 } { 2 } C V ^ { 2 } = \frac { 1 } { 2 } \times 200 \times 10 ^ { - 12 } \times 300 \times 300 = 9 \times 10 ^ { - 6 } \mathrm { J } \end{aligned}{/tex} The charge stored in the capacitor when charged through 300 V battery is Q = CV = 200 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -12 {tex}\times{/tex} 300 = 6 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -8 = 60 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -9 C = 60 nC After removing the battery when two capacitors are connected, they have their positive plates at the same potential and negative plates also at the same potential. Let V be the common potential difference. By the law of conservation of charge, charge would distribute but total charge would remain constant. Thus, Q = q + q', q and q' are the charges stored in the two capacitors (say) {tex}\frac { q } { C } = \frac { q ^ { \prime } } { C ^ { \prime } }{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac { q } { 200 } = \frac { q ^ { \prime } } { 100 }{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} q = 2q' Thus, net charge, Q = 2q' + q' = 3q' So, {tex}q ^ { \prime } = \frac { Q } { 3 } = \frac { 60 \mathrm { nC } } { 3 } = 20 \mathrm { nc }{/tex} and q = 2q' = 40nC Now the final energy stored in the system of two capacitors, {tex}U _ { f } = \frac { q ^ { 2 } } { 2 C } + \frac { q' ^ { 2 } } { 2 C' }{/tex} {tex}= \frac { 1 } { 2 } \times \frac { \left( 40 \times 10 ^ { - 9 } \right) ^ { 2 } } { 200 \times 10 ^ { - 12 } } + \frac { 1 } { 2 } \times \frac { \left( 20 \times 10 ^ { - 9 } \right) ^ { 2 } } { 100 \times 10 ^ { - 12 } }{/tex} {tex}= 4 \times 10 ^ { - 6 } + 2 \times 10 ^ { - 6 } = 6 \times 10 ^ { - 6 } \mathrm { J }{/tex} Difference in energy = final energy - initial energy = U f - U i {tex}= 6 \times 10 ^ { - 6 } - 9 \times 10 ^ { - 6 } = - 3 \times 10 ^ { - 6 }{/tex} Joules (negative sign indicates that the energy is lost in the process) Thus, difference in energy is {tex}- 3 \times 10 ^ { - 6 }{/tex} J.
solution_html:
Given, C = 200 pF = 200 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-12 F and V = 300V
The energy (initially) stored in the capacitor is
{tex}\begin{aligned} U _ { i } & = \frac { 1 } { 2 } C V ^ { 2 } = \frac { 1 } { 2 } \times 200 \times 10 ^ { - 12 } \times 300 \times 300 = 9 \times 10 ^ { - 6 } \mathrm { J } \end{aligned}{/tex}
The charge stored in the capacitor when charged through 300 V battery is
 Q = CV = 200 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-12 {tex}\times{/tex} 300 = 6 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-8 = 60 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-9 C = 60 nC
After removing the battery when two capacitors are connected, they have their positive plates at the same potential and negative plates also at the same potential. Let V be the common potential difference.
By the law of conservation of charge, charge would distribute but total charge would remain constant.
Thus,
Q = q + q', q and q' are the charges stored in the two capacitors (say)
{tex}\frac { q } { C } = \frac { q ^ { \prime } } { C ^ { \prime } }{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac { q } { 200 } = \frac { q ^ { \prime } } { 100 }{/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} q = 2q'
Thus, net charge, Q = 2q' + q' = 3q'
So, {tex}q ^ { \prime } = \frac { Q } { 3 } = \frac { 60 \mathrm { nC } } { 3 } = 20 \mathrm { nc }{/tex}
and q = 2q' = 40nC
Now the final energy stored in the system of two capacitors,
{tex}U _ { f } = \frac { q ^ { 2 } } { 2 C } + \frac { q' ^ { 2 } } { 2 C' }{/tex}
{tex}= \frac { 1 } { 2 } \times \frac { \left( 40 \times 10 ^ { - 9 } \right) ^ { 2 } } { 200 \times 10 ^ { - 12 } } + \frac { 1 } { 2 } \times \frac { \left( 20 \times 10 ^ { - 9 } \right) ^ { 2 } } { 100 \times 10 ^ { - 12 } }{/tex}
{tex}= 4 \times 10 ^ { - 6 } + 2 \times 10 ^ { - 6 } = 6 \times 10 ^ { - 6 } \mathrm { J }{/tex}
Difference in energy = final energy - initial energy
= Uf - Ui {tex}= 6 \times 10 ^ { - 6 } - 9 \times 10 ^ { - 6 } = - 3 \times 10 ^ { - 6 }{/tex} Joules (negative sign indicates that the energy is lost in the process)
Thus, difference in energy is {tex}- 3 \times 10 ^ { - 6 }{/tex} J.

question_html: Why electrostatic potential is constant throughout the volume of the conductor and has the same value as on its surface?
question_text: Why electrostatic potential is constant throughout the volume of the conductor and has the same value as on its surface?
explanation: Since, electric field intensity inside the conductor is zero. So, electrostatic potential is constant. Mathematically, E = - {tex}\frac {dV}{dr}{/tex} = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} dV = 0 ....(i) Integrating the equation (i) we get V = constant. Thus, the potentials at every point inside the conductor as well as on the surface of the conductor are same.

Thus, the potentials at every point inside the conductor as well as on the surface of the conductor are same.

Integrating the equation (i) we get V = constant.
Thus, the potentials at every point inside the conductor as well as on the surface of the conductor are same.

subject: Physics
question_html: Why should the electrostatic field be zero inside a conductor?
question_text: Why should the electrostatic field be zero inside a conductor?
explanation: In case the electrostatic field had been non-zero inside the conductor, the free electrons in the conductor will experience force and will not remain static. It would lead to a flow of current in the interior of the conductor. The fact that the net electric field inside the conductor is zero implies that there can be no net charge anywhere in the interior of the conductor.
solution: In case the electrostatic field had been non-zero inside the conductor, the free electrons in the conductor will experience force and will not remain static. It would lead to a flow of current in the interior of the conductor. The fact that the net electric field inside the conductor is zero implies that there can be no net charge anywhere in the interior of the conductor.
solution_html: In case the electrostatic field had been non-zero inside the conductor, the free electrons in the conductor will experience force and will not remain static. It would lead to a flow of current in the interior of the conductor. The fact that the net electric field inside the conductor is zero implies that there can be no net charge anywhere in the interior of the conductor.

question_html: A capacitor of capacitance C is being charged by connecting it across a d.c. source along with an ammeter. Will the ammeter show a momentary deflection during the process of charging? If so, how would you explain the momentary deflection and the resulting continuity of current in the circuit? Write the expression for the current inside the capacitor.
question_text: A capacitor of capacitance C is being charged by connecting it across a d.c. source along with an ammeter. Will the ammeter show a momentary deflection during the process of charging? If so, how would you explain the momentary deflection and the resulting continuity of current in the circuit? Write the expression for the current inside the capacitor.
explanation: Yes, the ammeter will show a momentary deflection. The momentary deflection is due to the flow of electrons in the circuit during the charging process. During this process, the electric field between the capacitor plates is changing and hence a displacement current flows in the gap. Hence we can say that there is a continuity of current in the circuit. Conduction current is equal to the displacement current. i.e. {tex}\mathrm{I}_{\mathrm{C}}=\mathrm{I}_{\mathrm{D}}=\frac{\epsilon_{0} \mathrm{d} \phi_{\mathrm{E}}}{\mathrm{d} \mathrm{t}}{/tex} Resulting continuity of current becomes zero because, is maximum when, capacitor is fully charged.
solution: Yes, the ammeter will show a momentary deflection. The momentary deflection is due to the flow of electrons in the circuit during the charging process. During this process, the electric field between the capacitor plates is changing and hence a displacement current flows in the gap. Hence we can say that there is a continuity of current in the circuit. Conduction current is equal to the displacement current. i.e. {tex}\mathrm{I}_{\mathrm{C}}=\mathrm{I}_{\mathrm{D}}=\frac{\epsilon_{0} \mathrm{d} \phi_{\mathrm{E}}}{\mathrm{d} \mathrm{t}}{/tex} Resulting continuity of current becomes zero because, is maximum when, capacitor is fully charged.
solution_html:
Yes, the ammeter will show a momentary deflection.
The momentary deflection is due to the flow of electrons in the circuit during the charging process. During this process, the electric field between the capacitor plates is changing and hence a displacement current flows in the gap.
Hence we can say that there is a continuity of current in the circuit.
Conduction current is equal to the displacement current.
i.e. {tex}\mathrm{I}_{\mathrm{C}}=\mathrm{I}_{\mathrm{D}}=\frac{\epsilon_{0} \mathrm{d} \phi_{\mathrm{E}}}{\mathrm{d} \mathrm{t}}{/tex}
Resulting continuity of current becomes zero because, is maximum when, capacitor is fully charged.

subject: Physics
question_html: A hollow metal sphere of radius 5 cm is charged such that potential on its surface is 10V. What is the potential at the centre of the sphere?
question_text: A hollow metal sphere of radius 5 cm is charged such that potential on its surface is 10V. What is the potential at the centre of the sphere?
explanation: Electric field inside the hollow spherical charged conductor is zero, as no charge resides inside the hollow portion where there is no material of the body. The relation between electric field intensity and potential is {tex}\vec E = - \frac {dV}{dr} \hat n{/tex} So if {tex}\vec E =0, ~then ~ dV =0{/tex} . Thus, the potential(V) is constant throughout the whole spehere and equal to the value at the surface, i.e. equal to its value at the surface = 10V.
solution: Electric field inside the hollow spherical charged conductor is zero, as no charge resides inside the hollow portion where there is no material of the body. The relation between electric field intensity and potential is {tex}\vec E = - \frac {dV}{dr} \hat n{/tex} So if {tex}\vec E =0, ~then ~ dV =0{/tex} . Thus, the potential(V) is constant throughout the whole spehere and equal to the value at the surface, i.e. equal to its value at the surface = 10V.
solution_html:
Electric field inside the hollow spherical charged conductor is zero, as no charge resides inside the hollow portion where there is no material of the body.
The relation between electric field intensity and potential is {tex}\vec E = - \frac {dV}{dr} \hat n{/tex}
So if {tex}\vec E =0, ~then ~ dV =0{/tex}.
Thus, the potential(V) is constant throughout the whole spehere and equal to the value at the surface, i.e. equal to its value at the surface = 10V.

subject: Physics
question_html: Can two equipotential surfaces intersect each other? Justify your answer.
question_text: Can two equipotential surfaces intersect each other? Justify your answer.
explanation: Tangent drawn on an equipotential surface gives the direction of electric field intensity at that point. Now, when two equipotential surfaces intersect each other, two tangents can be drawn at the point of intersection. That means, we can get two different directions of same electric field intensity at that point, which is not possible. That's why no two equipotential surfaces intersect each other.
solution: Tangent drawn on an equipotential surface gives the direction of electric field intensity at that point. subject: Physics
question_html: Define the dielectric constant of a medium. What is its unit?
question_text: Define the dielectric constant of a medium. What is its unit?
explanation: Dielectric constant of a medium is defined as the ratio of capacitance of a capacitor when the medium is present between the plates of it to the capacitance of the capacitor when air/vacuum is present between the plates of it. More the dielectric constant more is the capacitance of a parallel plate capacitor when the medium present between its two plates. i.e, {tex}K=\frac{C_{dielectric}}{C_{vacuum}}{/tex} It is unitless quantity and it is a dimensionless quantity.
solution: Dielectric constant of a medium is defined as the ratio of capacitance of a capacitor when the medium is present between the plates of it to the capacitance of the capacitor when air/vacuum is present between the plates of it. More the dielectric constant more is the capacitance of a parallel plate capacitor when the medium present between its two plates. i.e, {tex}K=\frac{C_{dielectric}}{C_{vacuum}}{/tex} It is unitless quantity and it is a dimensionless quantity.
solution_html:
Dielectric constant of a medium is defined as the ratio of capacitance of a capacitor when the medium is present between the plates of it to the capacitance of the capacitor when air/vacuum is present between the plates of it. More the dielectric constant more is the capacitance of a parallel plate capacitor when the medium present between its two plates.
i.e, {tex}K=\frac{C_{dielectric}}{C_{vacuum}}{/tex}
It is unitless quantity and it is a dimensionless quantity.

question_text: Two point charges 3 µC and -3 µC are placed at points A and B, 5 cm apart. Draw the equipotential surfaces of the system. Why do equipotential surfaces get close to each other near the point charge?
explanation: Equipotential surfaces of the system of the equal and opposite charges separated by a small distance i.e for the dipole are shown by the dotted lines, Equipotential surfaces get closer to each other near the point charges as strong electric field is produced there {tex}E=-\frac{\Delta V}{\Delta r}\;and\;E\propto-\frac1{\Delta r}{/tex} For a given equipotential surface, small value of distance, {tex}\Delta r{/tex} represents strong value of electric field, E and vice-versa.
solution: Equipotential surfaces of the system of the equal and opposite charges separated by a small distance i.e for the dipole are shown by the dotted lines, Equipotential surfaces get closer to each other near the point charges as strong electric field is produced there {tex}E=-\frac{\Delta V}{\Delta r}\;and\;E\propto-\frac1{\Delta r}{/tex} For a given equipotential surface, small value of distance, {tex}\Delta r{/tex} represents strong value of electric field, E and vice-versa.

1. Equipotential surfaces of the system of the equal and opposite charges separated by a small distance i.e for the dipole are shown by the dotted lines,
  2. Equipotential surfaces get closer to each other near the point charges as strong electric field is produced there
    {tex}E=-\frac{\Delta V}{\Delta r}\;and\;E\propto-\frac1{\Delta r}{/tex}

subject: Physics
question_html:
Net capacitance of three identical capacitors in series is 1 µF. What will be their net capacitance, if connected in parallel?
Find the ratio of energy stored in these two configurations, if they are both connected to the same source.
question_text: Net capacitance of three identical capacitors in series is 1 µF. What will be their net capacitance, if connected in parallel? Find the ratio of energy stored in these two configurations, if they are both connected to the same source.
explanation: If n identical capacitors, each of capacitance C are connected in series combination then equivalent capacitance, {tex}C _ { s} = \frac { C } { n }{/tex} and when connected in n parallel combination, then equivalent capacitance, C p = nC According to the problem, {tex}C = n C _ { s } = 3 \times 1 \mu F = 3 \mu F{/tex} i.e. the capacitance of each capacitor is 3 {tex}\mu F{/tex} . Now for the parallel combination, {tex}C _ { p } = n C = 3 \times 3 = 9 \mu F{/tex} This is the net capacitance when the capacitors are connected in parallel. Also, for same voltage, energy stored in the capacitor is given by {tex}U = \frac { 1 } { 2 } C V ^ { 2 }{/tex} [for constant] For same voltage, {tex}U \propto C{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \frac { U _ { s } } { U _ { p } } = \frac { C _ { s } } { C _ { p } } \Rightarrow \frac { U _ { s } } { U _ { p } } = \frac { C / n } { n C } = \frac { 1 } { n ^ { 2 } }{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \frac { U _ { \mathrm { s } } } { U _ { p } } = \frac { 1 } { ( 3 ) ^ { 2 } } = \frac { 1 } { 9 } \Rightarrow \quad \frac { U _ { \mathrm { s } } } { U _ { p } } = \frac { 1 } { 9 }{/tex} or U s : U p = 1 : 9
solution: If n identical capacitors, each of capacitance C are connected in series combination then equivalent capacitance, {tex}C _ { s} = \frac { C } { n }{/tex} and when connected in n parallel combination, then equivalent capacitance, C p = nC According to the problem, {tex}C = n C _ { s } = 3 \times 1 \mu F = 3 \mu F{/tex} i.e. the capacitance of each capacitor is 3 {tex}\mu F{/tex} . Now for the parallel combination, {tex}C _ { p } = n C = 3 \times 3 = 9 \mu F{/tex} This is the net capacitance when the capacitors are connected in parallel. Also, for same voltage, energy stored in the capacitor is given by {tex}U = \frac { 1 } { 2 } C V ^ { 2 }{/tex} [for constant] For same voltage, {tex}U \propto C{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \frac { U _ { s } } { U _ { p } } = \frac { C _ { s } } { C _ { p } } \Rightarrow \frac { U _ { s } } { U _ { p } } = \frac { C / n } { n C } = \frac { 1 } { n ^ { 2 } }{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \frac { U _ { \mathrm { s } } } { U _ { p } } = \frac { 1 } { ( 3 ) ^ { 2 } } = \frac { 1 } { 9 } \Rightarrow \quad \frac { U _ { \mathrm { s } } } { U _ { p } } = \frac { 1 } { 9 }{/tex} or U s : U p = 1 : 9
solution_html:
If n identical capacitors, each of capacitance C are connected in series combination then equivalent capacitance, {tex}C _ { s} = \frac { C } { n }{/tex} and when connected in n parallel combination, then equivalent capacitance, Cp = nC
According to the problem,
{tex}C = n C _ { s } = 3 \times 1 \mu F = 3 \mu F{/tex} i.e. the capacitance of each capacitor is 3{tex}\mu F{/tex}.
Now for the parallel combination,
{tex}C _ { p } = n C = 3 \times 3 = 9 \mu F{/tex}
This is the net capacitance when the capacitors are connected in parallel.           

Also, for same voltage, energy stored in the capacitor is given by
{tex}U = \frac { 1 } { 2 } C V ^ { 2 }{/tex} [for constant]
For same voltage, {tex}U \propto C{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad \frac { U _ { s } } { U _ { p } } = \frac { C _ { s } } { C _ { p } } \Rightarrow \frac { U _ { s } } { U _ { p } } = \frac { C / n } { n C } = \frac { 1 } { n ^ { 2 } }{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad \frac { U _ { \mathrm { s } } } { U _ { p } } = \frac { 1 } { ( 3 ) ^ { 2 } } = \frac { 1 } { 9 } \Rightarrow \quad \frac { U _ { \mathrm { s } } } { U _ { p } } = \frac { 1 } { 9 }{/tex}
or Us : Up = 1 : 9

subject: Physics
question_html: Two charged conducting spheres of radii r1 and r2 connected to each other by a wire. Find the ratio of electric fields at the surfaces of the two spheres.
question_text: Two charged conducting spheres of radii r 1 and r 2 connected to each other by a wire. Find the ratio of electric fields at the surfaces of the two spheres.
explanation: When two charged conducting spheres are connected, then charge flows between the two from higher potential to lower potential, till their potentials become same. Electric potential on the surface of connected charged conducting spheres would be equal. i.e., {tex}V _ { 1 } = V _ { 2 } \Rightarrow \frac { 1 } { 4 \pi \varepsilon _ { 0 } } \frac { q _ { 1 } } { r _ { 1 } } = \frac { 1 } { 4 \pi \varepsilon _ { 0 } } \frac { q _ { 2 } } { r _ { 2 } }{/tex} [Assuming q 1 and q 2 are charges on the surfaces of the spheres connected to each other, V 1 and V 2 are potentials and r 1 , r 2 are their radii.] {tex}\frac { q _ { 1 } } { r _ { 1 } } = \frac { q _ { 2 } } { r _ { 2 } } \Rightarrow \frac { q _ { 1 } } { q _ { 2 } } = \frac { r _ { 1 } } { r _ { 2 } }{/tex} ....(i) Now, ratio of electric field intensities due to charges q 1 and q 2 of the spheres, {tex}\frac { E _ { 1 } } { E _ { 2 } } = \frac { 1 / 4 \pi \varepsilon _ { 0 } } { 1 / 4 \pi \varepsilon _ { 0 } }× \frac { q _ { 1 } / r _ { 1 } ^ { 2 } } { q _ { 2 } / r _ { 2 } ^ { 2 } } = \frac { q _ { 1 } } { q _ { 2 } } \times \frac { r _ { 2 } ^ { 2 } } { r _ { 1 } ^ { 2 } }{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac { E _ { 1 } } { E _ { 2 } } = \left( \frac { q _ { 1 } } { q _ { 2 } } \right) \times \frac { r _ { 2 } ^ { 2 } } { r _ { 1 } ^ { 2 } } = \frac { r _ { 1 } } { r _ { 2 } } \times \frac { r _ { 2 } ^ { 2 } } { r _ { 1 } ^ { 2 } }{/tex} [From Eq (i)] {tex}\Rightarrow \frac { E _ { 1 } } { E _ { 2 } } = \frac { r _ { 2 } } { r _ { 1 } }{/tex} , this is the required ratio.
solution: When two charged conducting spheres are connected, then charge flows between the two from higher potential to lower potential, till their potentials become same. Electric potential on the surface of connected charged conducting spheres would be equal. i.e., {tex}V _ { 1 } = V _ { 2 } \Rightarrow \frac { 1 } { 4 \pi \varepsilon _ { 0 } } \frac { q _ { 1 } } { r _ { 1 } } = \frac { 1 } { 4 \pi \varepsilon _ { 0 } } \frac { q _ { 2 } } { r _ { 2 } }{/tex} [Assuming q 1 and q 2 are charges on the surfaces of the spheres connected to each other, V 1 and V 2 are potentials and r 1 , r 2 are their radii.] {tex}\frac { q _ { 1 } } { r _ { 1 } } = \frac { q _ { 2 } } { r _ { 2 } } \Rightarrow \frac { q _ { 1 } } { q _ { 2 } } = \frac { r _ { 1 } } { r _ { 2 } }{/tex} ....(i) Now, ratio of electric field intensities due to charges q 1 and q 2 of the spheres, {tex}\frac { E _ { 1 } } { E _ { 2 } } = \frac { 1 / 4 \pi \varepsilon _ { 0 } } { 1 / 4 \pi \varepsilon _ { 0 } }× \frac { q _ { 1 } / r _ { 1 } ^ { 2 } } { q _ { 2 } / r _ { 2 } ^ { 2 } } = \frac { q _ { 1 } } { q _ { 2 } } \times \frac { r _ { 2 } ^ { 2 } } { r _ { 1 } ^ { 2 } }{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac { E _ { 1 } } { E _ { 2 } } = \left( \frac { q _ { 1 } } { q _ { 2 } } \right) \times \frac { r _ { 2 } ^ { 2 } } { r _ { 1 } ^ { 2 } } = \frac { r _ { 1 } } { r _ { 2 } } \times \frac { r _ { 2 } ^ { 2 } } { r _ { 1 } ^ { 2 } }{/tex} [From Eq (i)] {tex}\Rightarrow \frac { E _ { 1 } } { E _ { 2 } } = \frac { r _ { 2 } } { r _ { 1 } }{/tex} , this is the required ratio.
solution_html:
When two charged conducting spheres are connected, then charge flows between the two from higher potential to lower potential, till their potentials become same.
Electric potential on the surface of connected charged conducting spheres would be equal.
i.e., {tex}V _ { 1 } = V _ { 2 } \Rightarrow \frac { 1 } { 4 \pi \varepsilon _ { 0 } } \frac { q _ { 1 } } { r _ { 1 } } = \frac { 1 } { 4 \pi \varepsilon _ { 0 } } \frac { q _ { 2 } } { r _ { 2 } }{/tex}
[Assuming q1 and q2 are charges on the surfaces of the spheres connected to each other, V1 and V2 are potentials and r1, r2 are their radii.]
{tex}\frac { q _ { 1 } } { r _ { 1 } } = \frac { q _ { 2 } } { r _ { 2 } } \Rightarrow \frac { q _ { 1 } } { q _ { 2 } } = \frac { r _ { 1 } } { r _ { 2 } }{/tex} ....(i)
Now, ratio of electric field intensities due to charges q1 and q2 of the spheres,
{tex}\frac { E _ { 1 } } { E _ { 2 } } = \frac { 1 / 4 \pi \varepsilon _ { 0 } } { 1 / 4 \pi \varepsilon _ { 0 } }× \frac { q _ { 1 } / r _ { 1 } ^ { 2 } } { q _ { 2 } / r _ { 2 } ^ { 2 } } = \frac { q _ { 1 } } { q _ { 2 } } \times \frac { r _ { 2 } ^ { 2 } } { r _ { 1 } ^ { 2 } }{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac { E _ { 1 } } { E _ { 2 } } = \left( \frac { q _ { 1 } } { q _ { 2 } } \right) \times \frac { r _ { 2 } ^ { 2 } } { r _ { 1 } ^ { 2 } } = \frac { r _ { 1 } } { r _ { 2 } } \times \frac { r _ { 2 } ^ { 2 } } { r _ { 1 } ^ { 2 } }{/tex} [From Eq (i)]
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac { E _ { 1 } } { E _ { 2 } } = \frac { r _ { 2 } } { r _ { 1 } }{/tex}, this is the required ratio.

question_html: Three charges, each + q, are placed at the corners of an isosceles triangle ABC of sides BC and AC, each equal to 2a. D and E are the midpoints of BC and CA The work done in taking a charge Q from D to E is:
question_text: Three charges, each + q, are placed at the corners of an isosceles triangle ABC of sides BC and AC, each equal to 2a. D and E are the midpoints of BC and CA The work done in taking a charge Q from D to E is:

options.explanation: {tex}V_{E}-V_{D}=\frac{q}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}}\left[\left(\frac{1}{E A}+\frac{1}{E B}+\frac{1}{E C}\right)-\left(\frac{1}{D A}+\frac{1}{D B}+\frac{1}{D C}\right)\right]{/tex} {tex}=\frac{q}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}}\left[\left(\frac{1}{E A}+\frac{1}{E B}+\frac{1}{E C}\right)-\left(\frac{1}{E B}+\frac{1}{E A}+\frac{1}{E C}\right)\right]=0{/tex} W = Q(V E - V D ) = 0

explanation: {tex}V_{E}-V_{D}=\frac{q}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}}\left[\left(\frac{1}{E A}+\frac{1}{E B}+\frac{1}{E C}\right)-\left(\frac{1}{D A}+\frac{1}{D B}+\frac{1}{D C}\right)\right]{/tex} {tex}=\frac{q}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}}\left[\left(\frac{1}{E A}+\frac{1}{E B}+\frac{1}{E C}\right)-\left(\frac{1}{E B}+\frac{1}{E A}+\frac{1}{E C}\right)\right]=0{/tex} W = Q(V E - V D ) = 0

subject: Physics
question_html:
Two capacitors of 1 µF capacitance are connected to a battery of 6 V. Initially switch S is closed. After sometime S is left open and dielectric slab of dielectric constant K = 3 are inserted to fill completely the space between the plates of the two capacitors.

How will the charge and potential difference between the plates of the capacitors be affected after the slabs are inserted?
question_text: Two capacitors of 1 µF capacitance are connected to a battery of 6 V. Initially switch S is closed. After sometime S is left open and dielectric slab of dielectric constant K = 3 are inserted to fill completely the space between the plates of the two capacitors. How will the charge and potential difference between the plates of the capacitors be affected after the slabs are inserted?
explanation: When the switch S is closed, potential difference across capacitor is 6V. Potential across the battery, V 1 = V 2 = 6V Capacitance of the capacitor, C 1 = C 2 = 1µC Charge on each capacitor, q 1 = q 2 = C 1 V 1 = 1 {tex}\times{/tex} 6 = 6µC When switch S is opened, the potential difference across C 1 remains 6V, while the charge on capacitor C 2 remain {tex}6 \mu C{/tex} . After insertion of dielectric between the plates of each capacitor, the new capacitance of each capacitor becomes {tex}C_{1}^{\prime}=C_{2}^{\prime}=3 \times 1 \mu F=3 \mu F{/tex} Now, charge on capacitor C 1 , q 1 ' = C 1 'V 1 = {tex}3 \times 6=18 \mu C{/tex} Charge on capacitor C 2 remains the same, i.e., {tex}6 \mu C{/tex} Potential difference across C 1 remains the same. potential difference across, C 2 is {tex}V_{2}^{\prime}=\frac{q_{2}}{C_{2}^{\prime}}=\frac{6}{3}=2 \mathrm{V}{/tex}
solution: When the switch S is closed, potential difference across capacitor is 6V. Potential across the battery, V 1 = V 2 = 6V Capacitance of the capacitor, C 1 = C 2 = 1µC Charge on each capacitor, q 1 = q 2 = C 1 V 1 = 1 {tex}\times{/tex} 6 = 6µC When switch S is opened, the potential difference across C 1 remains 6V, while the charge on capacitor C 2 remain {tex}6 \mu C{/tex} . After insertion of dielectric between the plates of each capacitor, the new capacitance of each capacitor becomes {tex}C_{1}^{\prime}=C_{2}^{\prime}=3 \times 1 \mu F=3 \mu F{/tex} Now, charge on capacitor C 1 , q 1 ' = C 1 'V 1 = {tex}3 \times 6=18 \mu C{/tex} Charge on capacitor C 2 remains the same, i.e., {tex}6 \mu C{/tex} Potential difference across C 1 remains the same. potential difference across, C 2 is {tex}V_{2}^{\prime}=\frac{q_{2}}{C_{2}^{\prime}}=\frac{6}{3}=2 \mathrm{V}{/tex}
solution_html:
When the switch S is closed, potential difference across capacitor is 6V.
Potential across the battery, V1 = V2 = 6V
Capacitance of the capacitor, C1 = C2 = 1µC
Charge on each capacitor, q1 = q2 = C1V1 = 1 {tex}\times{/tex} 6 = 6µC
When switch S is opened, the potential difference across C1 remains 6V, while the charge on capacitor C2 remain {tex}6 \mu C{/tex}. After insertion of dielectric between the plates of each capacitor, the new capacitance of each capacitor becomes
{tex}C_{1}^{\prime}=C_{2}^{\prime}=3 \times 1 \mu F=3 \mu F{/tex}

  1. Now, charge on capacitor C1, q1' = C1'V1 = {tex}3 \times 6=18 \mu C{/tex}
    Charge on capacitor C2 remains the same, i.e., {tex}6 \mu C{/tex}
  2. Potential difference across C1 remains the same.
    potential difference across, C2 is {tex}V_{2}^{\prime}=\frac{q_{2}}{C_{2}^{\prime}}=\frac{6}{3}=2 \mathrm{V}{/tex}

question_html:
Figure shows a sheet of aluminium foil of negligible thickness placed between the plates of a capacitor.
How will its capacitance be affected, if

  1. the foil is electrically insulated?
  2. the foil is connected to the upper plate with a conducting wire?
question_text: Figure shows a sheet of aluminium foil of negligible thickness placed between the plates of a capacitor. How will its capacitance be affected, if the foil is electrically insulated? the foil is connected to the upper plate with a conducting wire?
explanation: If the foil is insulated, then the system will be equivalent to two identical capacitors connected in series combination in which two plates of each capacitor have separation half of the original separation. Thus, new capacitance of each capacitor C' = {tex}\frac {\epsilon _0 A}{\frac {d}{2}} = \frac {2 \epsilon _0 A}{d}{/tex} = 2C ....(a) As two capacitors each of capacitance C' are in series, {tex}\therefore \quad C _ { \text { net } } = \frac { 2 C \times 2 C } { 2 C + 2 C } = C{/tex} C net = C (Original capacitor) If the foil is connected to the upper plate with a wire, then the system reduces to a single capacitor whose only distance of separation of the two plates reduces to half of its original one i.e. d' = d/2, keeping all other variables same as before. {tex}\therefore{/tex} New capacitance, C' = 2C
solution: If the foil is insulated, then the system will be equivalent to two identical capacitors connected in series combination in which two plates of each capacitor have separation half of the original separation. Thus, new capacitance of each capacitor C' = {tex}\frac {\epsilon _0 A}{\frac {d}{2}} = \frac {2 \epsilon _0 A}{d}{/tex} = 2C ....(a) As two capacitors each of capacitance C' are in series, {tex}\therefore \quad C _ { \text { net } } = \frac { 2 C \times 2 C } { 2 C + 2 C } = C{/tex} C net = C (Original capacitor) If the foil is connected to the upper plate with a wire, then the system reduces to a single capacitor whose only distance of separation of the two plates reduces to half of its original one i.e. d' = d/2, keeping all other variables same as before. {tex}\therefore{/tex} New capacitance, C' = 2C
solution_html:
1. If the foil is insulated, then the system will be equivalent to two identical capacitors connected in series combination in which two plates of each capacitor have separation half of the original separation.
    Thus, new capacitance of each capacitor
    C' = {tex}\frac {\epsilon _0 A}{\frac {d}{2}} = \frac {2 \epsilon _0 A}{d}{/tex} = 2C ....(a)
    As two capacitors each of capacitance C' are in series,
    {tex}\therefore \quad C _ { \text { net } } = \frac { 2 C \times 2 C } { 2 C + 2 C } = C{/tex}
    Cnet = C (Original capacitor)
  2. If the foil is connected to the upper plate with a wire, then the system reduces to a single capacitor whose only distance of separation of the two plates reduces to half of its original one i.e. d' = d/2, keeping all other variables same as before.
    {tex}\therefore{/tex} New capacitance, C' = 2C

question_html: Two uniformly large parallel thin plates having charge densities {tex}+ \sigma{/tex} and {tex}- \sigma{/tex} are kept in the XZ-plane at a distance d apart. Sketch an equipotential surface due to electric field between the plates. If a particle of mass m and charge q remains stationary between the plates. What is the magnitude and direction of this field?
question_text: Two uniformly large parallel thin plates having charge densities {tex}+ \sigma{/tex} and {tex}- \sigma{/tex} are kept in the XZ-plane at a distance d apart. Sketch an equipotential surface due to electric field between the plates. If a particle of mass m and charge q remains stationary between the plates. What is the magnitude and direction of this field?
explanation: The equipotential surface is at a distance d/2 from either plate in XZ-plane. -q charge experiences a force in a direction opposite to the direction of electric field i.e. along the direction of the plate having charge density {tex}+ \sigma{/tex} . {tex}\therefore{/tex} The force on the '-q' charge balances when the electrostatic force becomes equal to the weight of the charge itself i.e. {tex}q E = m g \Rightarrow E = \frac { m g } { q }{/tex} The direction of electric field along vertically downward direction. The XZ-plane is so chosen that the direction of electric field due to two plates is along vertically downward direction, otherwise weight (mg) of the charged particle could not be balanced. The equipotential surface lies in between the two given plates and on the XZ - plane, as shown below:

The XZ-plane is so chosen that the direction of electric field due to two plates is along vertically downward direction, otherwise weight (mg) of the charged particle could not be balanced. The equipotential surface lies in between the two given plates and on the XZ - plane, as shown below:
solution_html:
The equipotential surface is at a distance d/2 from either plate in XZ-plane. -q charge experiences a force in a direction opposite to the direction of electric field i.e. along the direction of the plate having charge density {tex}+ \sigma{/tex}.
{tex}\therefore{/tex} The force on the '-q' charge balances when the electrostatic force becomes equal to the weight of the charge itself i.e.
{tex}q E = m g \Rightarrow E = \frac { m g } { q }{/tex}
The direction of electric field along vertically downward direction. The XZ-plane is so chosen that the direction of electric field due to two plates is along vertically downward direction, otherwise weight (mg) of the charged particle could not be balanced. The equipotential surface lies in between the two given plates and on the XZ - plane, as shown below:

1. A parallel plate capacitor is charged by a battery to a potential. The battery is disconnected and a dielectric slab is inserted to completely fill the space between the plates. How will
    1. its capacitance
    2. electric field between the plates and
    3. energy stored in the capacitor be affected?
      Justify your answer giving necessary mathematical expressions for each case.
    1. Draw the electric field lines due to a conducting sphere.
    2. Draw the electric field lines due to a dipole.
question_text: A parallel plate capacitor is charged by a battery to a potential. The battery is disconnected and a dielectric slab is inserted to completely fill the space between the plates. How will its capacitance electric field between the plates and energy stored in the capacitor be affected? Justify your answer giving necessary mathematical expressions for each case. Draw the electric field lines due to a conducting sphere. Draw the electric field lines due to a dipole.
explanation: On introduction of dielectric slab of dielectric constant K in an isolated charged capacitor. The capacitance (C') becomes K times of original capacitor as {tex}C = \frac { \varepsilon _ { 0 } A } { d } \;and\; C ^ { \prime } = \frac { K \varepsilon _ { 0 } A } { d }{/tex} (where A and d being cross-sectional area of each plate and distance of separation of the plates respectively) Charge remains conserved in this phenomenon. CV = C'V' {tex}\Rightarrow V ^ { \prime } = \frac { C V } { C ^ { \prime } } = \frac { C V } { K C }{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad V ^ { \prime } = \frac { V } { K }{/tex} Potential difference decreases and becomes {tex}\frac{1}{K}{/tex} times of its previous original value. Energy stored initially in the capacitor, {tex}U = \frac { q ^ { 2 } } { 2 C }{/tex} Energy stored in the capacitor after inserting the dielectric, {tex}\therefore \quad U ^ { \prime } = \frac { q ^ { 2 } } { 2 K C }{/tex} [ {tex}\because C ^ { \prime } = K C{/tex} ] where, K =dielectric constant of medium {tex}\Rightarrow U ^ { \prime } = \frac { 1 } { K } \left( \frac { q ^ { 2 } } { 2 C } \right) \Rightarrow U ^ { \prime } = \frac { 1 } { K } ( U ) \Rightarrow U ^ { \prime } = \frac { 1 } { K } \times U{/tex} The energy stored in the capacitor decreases and becomes {tex}\frac1K{/tex} times of original energy Electric field lines due to a conducting sphere are shown in the figure: Electric field lines due to an electric dipole are shown in the figure:
solution: On introduction of dielectric slab of dielectric constant K in an isolated charged capacitor. The capacitance (C') becomes K times of original capacitor as {tex}C = \frac { \varepsilon _ { 0 } A } { d } \;and\; C ^ { \prime } = \frac { K \varepsilon _ { 0 } A } { d }{/tex} (where A and d being cross-sectional area of each plate and distance of separation of the plates respectively) Charge remains conserved in this phenomenon. CV = C'V' {tex}\Rightarrow V ^ { \prime } = \frac { C V } { C ^ { \prime } } = \frac { C V } { K C }{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad V ^ { \prime } = \frac { V } { K }{/tex} Potential difference decreases and becomes {tex}\frac{1}{K}{/tex} times of its previous original value. Energy stored initially in the capacitor, {tex}U = \frac { q ^ { 2 } } { 2 C }{/tex} Energy stored in the capacitor after inserting the dielectric, {tex}\therefore \quad U ^ { \prime } = \frac { q ^ { 2 } } { 2 K C }{/tex} [ {tex}\because C ^ { \prime } = K C{/tex} ] where, K =dielectric constant of medium {tex}\Rightarrow U ^ { \prime } = \frac { 1 } { K } \left( \frac { q ^ { 2 } } { 2 C } \right) \Rightarrow U ^ { \prime } = \frac { 1 } { K } ( U ) \Rightarrow U ^ { \prime } = \frac { 1 } { K } \times U{/tex} The energy stored in the capacitor decreases and becomes {tex}\frac1K{/tex} times of original energy Electric field lines due to a conducting sphere are shown in the figure: Electric field lines due to an electric dipole are shown in the figure:
solution_html:
1. On introduction of dielectric slab of dielectric constant K in an isolated charged capacitor.
    1. The capacitance (C') becomes K times of original capacitor as
      {tex}C = \frac { \varepsilon _ { 0 } A } { d } \;and\; C ^ { \prime } = \frac { K \varepsilon _ { 0 } A } { d }{/tex} 
      (where A and d being cross-sectional area of each plate and distance of separation of the plates respectively)
    2. Charge remains conserved in this phenomenon.
       CV = C'V'
       {tex}\Rightarrow V ^ { \prime } = \frac { C V } { C ^ { \prime } } = \frac { C V } { K C }{/tex}
      {tex}\Rightarrow \quad V ^ { \prime } = \frac { V } { K }{/tex}
      Potential difference decreases and becomes {tex}\frac{1}{K}{/tex} times of its previous original value.
    3. Energy stored initially in the capacitor,
      {tex}U = \frac { q ^ { 2 } } { 2 C }{/tex}
       Energy stored in the capacitor after inserting the dielectric,
      {tex}\therefore \quad U ^ { \prime } = \frac { q ^ { 2 } } { 2 K C }{/tex}[{tex}\because C ^ { \prime } = K C{/tex}]
        where, K =dielectric constant of medium
       {tex}\Rightarrow U ^ { \prime } = \frac { 1 } { K } \left( \frac { q ^ { 2 } } { 2 C } \right) \Rightarrow U ^ { \prime } = \frac { 1 } { K } ( U ) \Rightarrow U ^ { \prime } = \frac { 1 } { K } \times U{/tex}
       The energy stored in the capacitor decreases and becomes {tex}\frac1K{/tex} times of original energy

question_html:
Two parallel plate capacitors of capacitances C1 and C2 such that C1 = 2C2 are connected across a battery of V volt as shown in the figure. Initially, the key (k) is kept closed to fully charge the capacitors. The key is now thrown open and a dielectric slab of dielectric constant K is inserted in the two capacitors to completely fill the gap between the plates.
Find the ratio of

  1. the net capacitance and
  2. the energies stored in the combination before and after the introduction of the dielectric slab.
question_text: Two parallel plate capacitors of capacitances C 1 and C 2 such that C 1 = 2C 2 are connected across a battery of V volt as shown in the figure. Initially, the key (k) is kept closed to fully charge the capacitors. The key is now thrown open and a dielectric slab of dielectric constant K is inserted in the two capacitors to completely fill the gap between the plates. Find the ratio of the net capacitance and the energies stored in the combination before and after the introduction of the dielectric slab.
explanation: Net capacitance before filling the gap with dielectric slab Cinitial = C 1 + C 2 = 2C 2 + C 2 = 3C 2 Net capacitance after filling the gap with dielectric slab of dielectric constant 'K' C final = KC 1 + KC 2 = 2KC 2 + KC 2 = 3KC 2 Ratio of net capacitance, {tex}\frac{C_{\text {initial }}}{C_{\text {final }}}=\frac{3 C_{2}}{3 K C_{2}}=\frac{1}{K}{/tex} Energy stored in the combination before introduction of dielectric slab, {tex}\mathrm{U}_{\text {initial }}=\frac{\mathrm{Q}^{2}}{3 \mathrm{C}_{2}}{/tex} Energy stored in the combination after introduction of dielectric slab, {tex}\mathrm{U}_{\mathrm{final}}=\frac{\mathrm{Q}^{2}}{3 \mathrm{KC}_{2}}{/tex} Ratio of energy stored, {tex}\frac{\mathrm{U}_{\text {initial }}}{\mathrm{U}_{\text {final }}}=\frac{\mathrm{K}}{1}{/tex}
solution: Net capacitance before filling the gap with dielectric slab Cinitial = C 1 + C 2 = 2C 2 + C 2 = 3C 2 Net capacitance after filling the gap with dielectric slab of dielectric constant 'K' C final = KC 1 + KC 2 = 2KC 2 + KC 2 = 3KC 2 Ratio of net capacitance, {tex}\frac{C_{\text {initial }}}{C_{\text {final }}}=\frac{3 C_{2}}{3 K C_{2}}=\frac{1}{K}{/tex} Energy stored in the combination before introduction of dielectric slab, {tex}\mathrm{U}_{\text {initial }}=\frac{\mathrm{Q}^{2}}{3 \mathrm{C}_{2}}{/tex} Energy stored in the combination after introduction of dielectric slab, {tex}\mathrm{U}_{\mathrm{final}}=\frac{\mathrm{Q}^{2}}{3 \mathrm{KC}_{2}}{/tex} Ratio of energy stored, {tex}\frac{\mathrm{U}_{\text {initial }}}{\mathrm{U}_{\text {final }}}=\frac{\mathrm{K}}{1}{/tex}
solution_html:
1. Net capacitance before filling the gap with dielectric slab Cinitial = C1 +  C2 = 2C2 + C2 = 3C2
    Net capacitance after filling the gap with dielectric slab of dielectric constant 'K'
    Cfinal = KC1 + KC2 = 2KC2 + KC2 = 3KC2
    Ratio of net capacitance, {tex}\frac{C_{\text {initial }}}{C_{\text {final }}}=\frac{3 C_{2}}{3 K C_{2}}=\frac{1}{K}{/tex}
  2. Energy stored in the  combination before introduction of dielectric slab,
    {tex}\mathrm{U}_{\text {initial }}=\frac{\mathrm{Q}^{2}}{3 \mathrm{C}_{2}}{/tex}
    Energy stored in the combination after introduction of dielectric slab,
    {tex}\mathrm{U}_{\mathrm{final}}=\frac{\mathrm{Q}^{2}}{3 \mathrm{KC}_{2}}{/tex}
    Ratio of energy stored,
    {tex}\frac{\mathrm{U}_{\text {initial }}}{\mathrm{U}_{\text {final }}}=\frac{\mathrm{K}}{1}{/tex}

question_html: Which of the following is not true?
question_text: Which of the following is not true?

options.text: The electric dipole potential varies as {tex}\frac{1}{r}{/tex} at large distance
options.html: The electric dipole potential varies as {tex}\frac{1}{r}{/tex} at large distance
options.explanation: The electric dipole potential at large distance, {tex}V=\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}} \cdot \frac{p \cos \theta}{r^{2}} \text { i.e., } V \propto \frac{1}{r^{2}}{/tex}

options.text: For a point charge, the electrostatic potential varies as {tex}\frac{1}{r}{/tex}
options.html: For a point charge, the electrostatic potential varies as {tex}\frac{1}{r}{/tex}

options.text: For a dipole, the potential depends on the position vector and dipole moment vector
options.html: For a dipole, the potential depends on the position vector and dipole moment vector

options.text: For a point charge, the electrostatic field varies as {tex}\frac{1}{{{r^2}}}{/tex}
options.html: For a point charge, the electrostatic field varies as {tex}\frac{1}{{{r^2}}}{/tex}

correct_option_text: The electric dipole potential varies as {tex}\frac{1}{r}{/tex} at large distance
explanation: The electric dipole potential at large distance, {tex}V=\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}} \cdot \frac{p \cos \theta}{r^{2}} \text { i.e., } V \propto \frac{1}{r^{2}}{/tex}

question_text: A gang capacitor is formed by interlocking a number of plates as shown in the figure. The distance between the consecutive plates is 0.885 cm and the overlapping area of the plates is 5 cm 2 . The capacity of the unit is

question_html: What is the area of the plates of a 2F parallel plate capacitor, given that the separation between the plates is 0.5 cm? [You will realize from your answer why ordinary capacitors are in the range of {tex}\mu{/tex}F or less. However, electrolytic capacitors do have a much larger capacitance (0.1 F) because of very minute separation between the conductors.]
question_text: What is the area of the plates of a 2F parallel plate capacitor, given that the separation between the plates is 0.5 cm? [You will realize from your answer why ordinary capacitors are in the range of {tex}\mu{/tex} F or less. However, electrolytic capacitors do have a much larger capacitance (0.1 F) because of very minute separation between the conductors.]
explanation: Given, the separation between plates (d) = 0.5 cm {tex} = 5 \times {10^{ - 3}}m{/tex} and the capacitance (C) = 2F Now {tex}C = {\varepsilon _0}\frac{A}{d}{/tex} {tex}A = \frac{{Cd}}{{{\varepsilon _0}}}{/tex} (where {tex}\varepsilon _{0} = 8.85 \times {{10}^{ - 12}}{/tex} Fm -1 ) {tex} = \frac{{2 \times 5 \times {{10}^{ - 3}}}}{{8.85 \times {{10}^{ - 12}}}}{/tex} {tex}A = 1.13 \times {10^9}{m^2}{/tex} = 1.13 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 3 km 2 = 1130 km 2 Hence, the area of the plates is too large. To avoid this situation, the capacitance is taken in the range of {tex}\mu{/tex} F.
solution: Given, the separation between plates (d) = 0.5 cm {tex} = 5 \times {10^{ - 3}}m{/tex} and the capacitance (C) = 2F Now {tex}C = {\varepsilon _0}\frac{A}{d}{/tex} {tex}A = \frac{{Cd}}{{{\varepsilon _0}}}{/tex} (where {tex}\varepsilon _{0} = 8.85 \times {{10}^{ - 12}}{/tex} Fm -1 ) {tex} = \frac{{2 \times 5 \times {{10}^{ - 3}}}}{{8.85 \times {{10}^{ - 12}}}}{/tex} {tex}A = 1.13 \times {10^9}{m^2}{/tex} = 1.13 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 3 km 2 = 1130 km 2 Hence, the area of the plates is too large. To avoid this situation, the capacitance is taken in the range of {tex}\mu{/tex} F.
solution_html:
Given, the separation between plates (d) = 0.5 cm {tex} = 5 \times {10^{ - 3}}m{/tex} and the capacitance (C) = 2F
Now
{tex}C = {\varepsilon _0}\frac{A}{d}{/tex}
{tex}A = \frac{{Cd}}{{{\varepsilon _0}}}{/tex} (where {tex}\varepsilon _{0} = 8.85 \times {{10}^{ - 12}}{/tex} Fm-1)
{tex} = \frac{{2 \times 5 \times {{10}^{ - 3}}}}{{8.85 \times {{10}^{ - 12}}}}{/tex}
{tex}A = 1.13 \times {10^9}{m^2}{/tex} = 1.13 {tex}\times{/tex} 103  km2 = 1130 km2
Hence, the area of the plates is too large. To avoid this situation, the capacitance is taken in the range of {tex}\mu{/tex}F.

question_html: Three identical parallel plate capacitor (air) C1, C2, C3  have capacitance C each. The space between their plates is now filled with dielectrics as shown. If all three capacitors still have equal capacitance, obtain the relation between dielectric constants, k, k1, k2, k3 and k4.
question_text: Three identical parallel plate capacitor (air) C 1 , C 2 , C 3 have capacitance C each. The space between their plates is now filled with dielectrics as shown. If all three capacitors still have equal capacitance, obtain the relation between dielectric constants, k, k 1 , k 2 , k 3 and k 4 .
explanation: After introducing the dielectrics the capacitance of capacitors C 1 , C 2 and C 3 respectively is given as {tex}C_1=C = \frac{{k{\varepsilon _0}A}}{d},C_2=C = \frac{{2{\varepsilon _0}A}}{d}\left[ {\frac{{{k_1}{k_2}}}{{{k_1} + {k_2}}}} \right],C_3=C = \frac{{{\varepsilon _0}A}}{{2d}}\left[ {{k_3} + {k_4}} \right]{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \frac{{k{\varepsilon _0}A}}{k} = \frac{{2{\varepsilon _0}A}}{d}\left( {\frac{{{k_1}{k_2}}}{{{k_1} + {k_2}}}} \right) = \frac{{{\varepsilon _0}A}}{{2d}}({k_3} + {k_4}){/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow k = \frac{{2{k_1}{k_2}}}{{{k_1} + {k_2}}} = \frac{{{k_3} + {k_4}}}{2}{/tex}
solution: After introducing the dielectrics the capacitance of capacitors C 1 , C 2 and C 3 respectively is given as {tex}C_1=C = \frac{{k{\varepsilon _0}A}}{d},C_2=C = \frac{{2{\varepsilon _0}A}}{d}\left[ {\frac{{{k_1}{k_2}}}{{{k_1} + {k_2}}}} \right],C_3=C = \frac{{{\varepsilon _0}A}}{{2d}}\left[ {{k_3} + {k_4}} \right]{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \frac{{k{\varepsilon _0}A}}{k} = \frac{{2{\varepsilon _0}A}}{d}\left( {\frac{{{k_1}{k_2}}}{{{k_1} + {k_2}}}} \right) = \frac{{{\varepsilon _0}A}}{{2d}}({k_3} + {k_4}){/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow k = \frac{{2{k_1}{k_2}}}{{{k_1} + {k_2}}} = \frac{{{k_3} + {k_4}}}{2}{/tex}
solution_html:
After introducing the dielectrics the capacitance of capacitors C1, C2 and C3 respectively is given as
{tex}C_1=C = \frac{{k{\varepsilon _0}A}}{d},C_2=C = \frac{{2{\varepsilon _0}A}}{d}\left[ {\frac{{{k_1}{k_2}}}{{{k_1} + {k_2}}}} \right],C_3=C = \frac{{{\varepsilon _0}A}}{{2d}}\left[ {{k_3} + {k_4}} \right]{/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow \frac{{k{\varepsilon _0}A}}{k} = \frac{{2{\varepsilon _0}A}}{d}\left( {\frac{{{k_1}{k_2}}}{{{k_1} + {k_2}}}} \right) = \frac{{{\varepsilon _0}A}}{{2d}}({k_3} + {k_4}){/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow k = \frac{{2{k_1}{k_2}}}{{{k_1} + {k_2}}} = \frac{{{k_3} + {k_4}}}{2}{/tex}
