question_html: Find the expression for the capacitance of a parallel plate capacitor of area A and plate separation d if (i) a dielectric slab of thickness f and (ii) a metallic slab of thickness f, where (t < d) by one between the plates of the capacitor. In which case would the capacitance be more and why?
question_text: Find the expression for the capacitance of a parallel plate capacitor of area A and plate separation d if (i) a dielectric slab of thickness f and (ii) a metallic slab of thickness f, where (t < d) by one between the plates of the capacitor. In which case would the capacitance be more and why?
explanation: Let A is the area of the two plates of the parallel plate capacitor and d is the separation between them. A dielectric slab of thickness t < d and area A is kept between the two plates. The total electric field inside the dielectric slab will be, E = {tex}\frac{E_0}{K}{/tex} = E 0 - E', Where E is the opposite field developed inside the slab due to polarisation of slab. Total potential difference between the plates, V = E 0 (d - t) + Et = {tex}\frac{\sigma}{\epsilon_0}(d-t)+\frac{\sigma}{k \epsilon_0}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{\sigma}{\epsilon_0}{/tex} {tex}\left[(d-t)+\frac{t}{k}\right]{/tex} V = {tex}=\frac q{A\varepsilon_0}\left[\left(d-t\right)+\frac tk\right]{/tex} where q is the charge on each plate. Since, C = {tex}\frac{q}{V}{/tex} or, C = {tex}=\frac q{\frac q{A\varepsilon_0}\left[\left(d-t\right)+\frac tk\right]}{/tex} or, C = {tex}\\=\frac{A\varepsilon_0}{\left[\left(d-t\right)+\frac tk\right]}{/tex} .
solution: Let A is the area of the two plates of the parallel plate capacitor and d is the separation between them. A dielectric slab of thickness t < d and area A is kept between the two plates. The total electric field inside the dielectric slab will be, E = {tex}\frac{E_0}{K}{/tex} = E 0 - E', Where E is the opposite field developed inside the slab due to polarisation of slab. Total potential difference between the plates, V = E 0 (d - t) + Et = {tex}\frac{\sigma}{\epsilon_0}(d-t)+\frac{\sigma}{k \epsilon_0}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{\sigma}{\epsilon_0}{/tex} {tex}\left[(d-t)+\frac{t}{k}\right]{/tex} V = {tex}=\frac q{A\varepsilon_0}\left[\left(d-t\right)+\frac tk\right]{/tex} where q is the charge on each plate. Since, C = {tex}\frac{q}{V}{/tex} or, C = {tex}=\frac q{\frac q{A\varepsilon_0}\left[\left(d-t\right)+\frac tk\right]}{/tex} or, C = {tex}\\=\frac{A\varepsilon_0}{\left[\left(d-t\right)+\frac tk\right]}{/tex} .
solution_html:
Let A is the area of the two plates of the parallel plate capacitor and d is the separation between them. A dielectric slab of thickness t < d and area A is kept between the two plates. The total electric field inside the dielectric slab will be,
E = {tex}\frac{E_0}{K}{/tex} = E0 - E',
Where E is the opposite field developed inside the slab due to polarisation of slab. Total potential difference between the plates,
V = E0(d - t) + Et
= {tex}\frac{\sigma}{\epsilon_0}(d-t)+\frac{\sigma}{k \epsilon_0}{/tex}
= {tex}\frac{\sigma}{\epsilon_0}{/tex}{tex}\left[(d-t)+\frac{t}{k}\right]{/tex}
V = {tex}=\frac q{A\varepsilon_0}\left[\left(d-t\right)+\frac tk\right]{/tex}

where q is the charge on each plate.
Since, C = {tex}\frac{q}{V}{/tex}
or, C = {tex}=\frac q{\frac q{A\varepsilon_0}\left[\left(d-t\right)+\frac tk\right]}{/tex}
or, C = {tex}\\=\frac{A\varepsilon_0}{\left[\left(d-t\right)+\frac tk\right]}{/tex}.

subject: Physics
question_html: A parallel plate capacitor of capacitance C is charged to a potential difference V and then the battery is disconnected. Now a dielectric slab of the dimensions equal to the spacing between the plates is inserted between the plates. What are the changes, if any, in the capacitance, potential difference, charge, electric field and the energy stored?
question_text: A parallel plate capacitor of capacitance C is charged to a potential difference V and then the battery is disconnected. Now a dielectric slab of the dimensions equal to the spacing between the plates is inserted between the plates. What are the changes, if any, in the capacitance, potential difference, charge, electric field and the energy stored?
explanation: Let K be a dielectric constant of the slab and q, E and U be a charge on the plates of the capacitor, electric field between the plates and energy stored in the capacitor before inserting the slab. On inserting a dielectric slab, the capacitance capacitor will become, C' = KC (increases) A potential difference between the plates, V' = {tex}\frac{q}{C^{\prime}}=\frac{q}{K C}=\frac{V}{K}{/tex} (decreases) Charge on the capacitor, Q' = C'V' = KC {tex}\times{/tex} {tex}\frac{\mathrm{V}}{\mathrm{K}}{/tex} = CV = Q (unaffected) Electric field between the plates, E' = {tex}\frac{V^{\prime}}{d}=\left(\frac{V}{K}\right) \times \frac{1}{d}=\frac{E}{K}{/tex} (decreases) Energy stored in the capacitor, U' = {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} C'V' 2 = {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} (KC) {tex}\left(\frac{\mathrm{V}}{\mathrm{K}}\right)^{2}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1}{\mathrm{K}}{/tex} {tex}\left(\frac{1}{2} C V^{2}\right){/tex} = {tex}\frac{\mathbf{U}}{\mathbf{K}}{/tex} (decreases)
solution: Let K be a dielectric constant of the slab and q, E and U be a charge on the plates of the capacitor, electric field between the plates and energy stored in the capacitor before inserting the slab. On inserting a dielectric slab, the capacitance capacitor will become, C' = KC (increases) A potential difference between the plates, V' = {tex}\frac{q}{C^{\prime}}=\frac{q}{K C}=\frac{V}{K}{/tex} (decreases) Charge on the capacitor, Q' = C'V' = KC {tex}\times{/tex} {tex}\frac{\mathrm{V}}{\mathrm{K}}{/tex} = CV = Q (unaffected) Electric field between the plates, E' = {tex}\frac{V^{\prime}}{d}=\left(\frac{V}{K}\right) \times \frac{1}{d}=\frac{E}{K}{/tex} (decreases) Energy stored in the capacitor, U' = {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} C'V' 2 = {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} (KC) {tex}\left(\frac{\mathrm{V}}{\mathrm{K}}\right)^{2}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1}{\mathrm{K}}{/tex} {tex}\left(\frac{1}{2} C V^{2}\right){/tex} = {tex}\frac{\mathbf{U}}{\mathbf{K}}{/tex} (decreases)
solution_html:
Let K be a dielectric constant of the slab and q, E and U be a charge on the plates of the capacitor, electric field between the plates and energy stored in the capacitor before inserting the slab.
On inserting a dielectric slab, the capacitance capacitor will become,
C' = KC (increases)
A potential difference between the plates,
V' = {tex}\frac{q}{C^{\prime}}=\frac{q}{K C}=\frac{V}{K}{/tex} (decreases)
Charge on the capacitor,
Q' = C'V' = KC {tex}\times{/tex} {tex}\frac{\mathrm{V}}{\mathrm{K}}{/tex} = CV = Q (unaffected)
Electric field between the plates,
E' = {tex}\frac{V^{\prime}}{d}=\left(\frac{V}{K}\right) \times \frac{1}{d}=\frac{E}{K}{/tex} (decreases)
Energy stored in the capacitor,
U' = {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex}C'V'2
= {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} (KC){tex}\left(\frac{\mathrm{V}}{\mathrm{K}}\right)^{2}{/tex}
= {tex}\frac{1}{\mathrm{K}}{/tex}{tex}\left(\frac{1}{2} C V^{2}\right){/tex} = {tex}\frac{\mathbf{U}}{\mathbf{K}}{/tex} (decreases)

subject: Physics
question_html:
A parallel plate capacitor is charged by a battery. After some time the battery is disconnected and a dielectric slab with its thickness equal to the plate separation is inserted between the plates. What change, in any will take place in

  1. charge on the plates
  2. electric field intensity between the plates
  3. the capacitance of the capacitor,
  4. a potential difference between the plates and
  5. the energy stored in the capacitor? Justify your answer in each case.
question_text: A parallel plate capacitor is charged by a battery. After some time the battery is disconnected and a dielectric slab with its thickness equal to the plate separation is inserted between the plates. What change, in any will take place in charge on the plates electric field intensity between the plates the capacitance of the capacitor, a potential difference between the plates and the energy stored in the capacitor? Justify your answer in each case.
explanation: The charge q 0 on the capacitor plates remains the same because the battery has been disconnected, before placing the dielectric slab. The surface charges induced on the dielectric slab reduce electric field intensity to a new value given by {tex}E=\frac{E_{0}}{\kappa}{/tex} The reduction in the electric field induces the potential difference {tex}V=E d=\frac{E_{0} d}{k}=\frac{V_{0}}{k}{/tex} Due to the decrease in p.d., the capacitance increases k times {tex}C=\frac{q_{0}}{V}=\frac{q_{0}}{V_{0} / \mathrm{k}}=\mathrm{K} \frac{q_{0}}{V_{0}}=\kappa \mathrm{C}_{0}{/tex} Energy stored decreases by a factor of {tex}\kappa{/tex} : {tex}U=\frac{1}{2} C V^{2}=\frac{1}{2}\left(\kappa C_{0}\right)\left(\frac{V_{0}}{K}\right)^{2}=\frac{1}{\kappa} \cdot \frac{1}{2} C_{0} V_{0}^{2}=\frac{U_{0}}{\kappa}{/tex}
solution: The charge q 0 on the capacitor plates remains the same because the battery has been disconnected, before placing the dielectric slab. The surface charges induced on the dielectric slab reduce electric field intensity to a new value given by {tex}E=\frac{E_{0}}{\kappa}{/tex} The reduction in the electric field induces the potential difference {tex}V=E d=\frac{E_{0} d}{k}=\frac{V_{0}}{k}{/tex} Due to the decrease in p.d., the capacitance increases k times {tex}C=\frac{q_{0}}{V}=\frac{q_{0}}{V_{0} / \mathrm{k}}=\mathrm{K} \frac{q_{0}}{V_{0}}=\kappa \mathrm{C}_{0}{/tex} Energy stored decreases by a factor of {tex}\kappa{/tex} : {tex}U=\frac{1}{2} C V^{2}=\frac{1}{2}\left(\kappa C_{0}\right)\left(\frac{V_{0}}{K}\right)^{2}=\frac{1}{\kappa} \cdot \frac{1}{2} C_{0} V_{0}^{2}=\frac{U_{0}}{\kappa}{/tex}
solution_html:
1. The charge q0 on the capacitor plates remains the same because the battery has been disconnected, before placing the dielectric slab.
  2. The surface charges induced on the dielectric slab reduce electric field intensity to a new value given by {tex}E=\frac{E_{0}}{\kappa}{/tex}
  3. The reduction in the electric field induces the potential difference {tex}V=E d=\frac{E_{0} d}{k}=\frac{V_{0}}{k}{/tex}
  4. Due to the decrease in p.d., the capacitance increases k times {tex}C=\frac{q_{0}}{V}=\frac{q_{0}}{V_{0} / \mathrm{k}}=\mathrm{K} \frac{q_{0}}{V_{0}}=\kappa \mathrm{C}_{0}{/tex}
  5. Energy stored decreases by a factor of {tex}\kappa{/tex}:
    {tex}U=\frac{1}{2} C V^{2}=\frac{1}{2}\left(\kappa C_{0}\right)\left(\frac{V_{0}}{K}\right)^{2}=\frac{1}{\kappa} \cdot \frac{1}{2} C_{0} V_{0}^{2}=\frac{U_{0}}{\kappa}{/tex}

subject: Physics
question_html: Distinguish between polar and non-polar dielectrics.Give one example of each.
question_text: Distinguish between polar and non-polar dielectrics.Give one example of each.
explanation: Polar dielectrics A polar molecule which has permanent electric dipole moment ( {tex}\vec p{/tex} ) in absence of electric field are called polar dielectrics. In these substances the possibilities of centre coinciding of positive and negative charges are zero. Polar molecules are randomly oriented. e.g. Water, alcohol, HCl, NH 3 . Non-polar dielectrics A non-polar molecule having zero dipole moment in its normal state are called non-polar dielectrics. In these substances the centres of both positive as well as negative charges coincide. Non-polar molecules have symmetrical shapes. e.g. Any non-conducting material.
solution: Polar dielectrics A polar molecule which has permanent electric dipole moment ( {tex}\vec p{/tex} ) in absence of electric field are called polar dielectrics. In these substances the possibilities of centre coinciding of positive and negative charges are zero. Polar molecules are randomly oriented. e.g. Water, alcohol, HCl, NH 3 . Non-polar dielectrics A non-polar molecule having zero dipole moment in its normal state are called non-polar dielectrics. In these substances the centres of both positive as well as negative charges coincide. Non-polar molecules have symmetrical shapes. e.g. Any non-conducting material.
solution_html:
Polar dielectrics
A polar molecule which has permanent electric dipole moment ({tex}\vec p{/tex}) in absence of electric field are called polar dielectrics. In these substances the possibilities of centre coinciding of positive and negative charges are zero. Polar molecules are randomly oriented. e.g. Water, alcohol, HCl, NH3.
Non-polar dielectrics
A non-polar molecule having zero dipole moment in its normal state are called non-polar dielectrics. In these substances the centres of both positive as well as negative charges coincide.
Non-polar molecules have symmetrical shapes. e.g. Any non-conducting material.

subject: Physics
question_html:
A dipole with its charge -q and +q located at the points (0, -b, 0) and (0, +b, 0) is present in a uniform electric field E. The equipotential surfaces of this field are planes parallel to the YZ-plane.

  1. What is the direction of the electric field E?
  2. How much torque would the dipole experience in this field?
question_text: A dipole with its charge -q and +q located at the points (0, -b, 0) and (0, +b, 0) is present in a uniform electric field E. The equipotential surfaces of this field are planes parallel to the YZ-plane. What is the direction of the electric field E? How much torque would the dipole experience in this field?
explanation: The direction of electric field is perpendicular to the equipotential surface. Hence, the direction of electric field is along X-axis as it should be perpendicular to equipotential surface lying in YZ- plane and X axis is perpendicular to the YZ-plane. Length of the dipole = 2b As dipole's axis is along the Y-axis. Electric dipole moment, p = q(2b) and is directed from -q charge towards +q charge i.e. along +Y-axis. Hence {tex}\vec p = 2qb \hat j{/tex} Electric field, {tex} { E } = E \hat { { i } }{/tex} {tex}\because \; \vec { \tau} = {\vec p } \times {\vec E } = q ( 2 b ) \hat { { j } } \times E {\hat i }{/tex} {tex}= + 2 q b E (\hat j \times \hat i ) = 2 q b E ( - \hat { k } ){/tex} {tex}\because{/tex} Torque, {tex}| \tau | = 2 q b E{/tex} and is directed along negative Z-axis.
solution: The direction of electric field is perpendicular to the equipotential surface. Hence, the direction of electric field is along X-axis as it should be perpendicular to equipotential surface lying in YZ- plane and X axis is perpendicular to the YZ-plane. Length of the dipole = 2b As dipole's axis is along the Y-axis. Electric dipole moment, p = q(2b) and is directed from -q charge towards +q charge i.e. along +Y-axis. Hence {tex}\vec p = 2qb \hat j{/tex} Electric field, {tex} { E } = E \hat { { i } }{/tex} {tex}\because \; \vec { \tau} = {\vec p } \times {\vec E } = q ( 2 b ) \hat { { j } } \times E {\hat i }{/tex} {tex}= + 2 q b E (\hat j \times \hat i ) = 2 q b E ( - \hat { k } ){/tex} {tex}\because{/tex} Torque, {tex}| \tau | = 2 q b E{/tex} and is directed along negative Z-axis.
solution_html:
1. The direction of electric field is perpendicular to the equipotential surface.
    Hence, the direction of electric field is along X-axis as it should be perpendicular to equipotential surface lying in YZ- plane and X axis is perpendicular to the YZ-plane.
  2. Length of the dipole = 2b
    As dipole's axis is along the Y-axis.
    Electric dipole moment, p = q(2b) and is directed from -q charge towards +q charge i.e. along +Y-axis. Hence {tex}\vec p = 2qb \hat j{/tex}
    Electric field, {tex} { E } = E \hat { { i } }{/tex}
    {tex}\because \; \vec { \tau} = {\vec p } \times {\vec E } = q ( 2 b ) \hat { { j } } \times E {\hat i }{/tex}
    {tex}= + 2 q b E (\hat j \times \hat i ) = 2 q b E ( - \hat { k } ){/tex}
    {tex}\because{/tex} Torque, {tex}| \tau | = 2 q b E{/tex} and is directed along negative Z-axis.

subject: Physics
question_html: Find the ratio of the potential differences that must be applied across the parallel and the series combination of two identical capacitors, so that the energy stored in the two cases becomes the same.
question_text: Find the ratio of the potential differences that must be applied across the parallel and the series combination of two identical capacitors, so that the energy stored in the two cases becomes the same.
explanation: Let V 1 and V 2 are the potential differences across the series and parallel combination of two identical capacitors each of capacitance C. Equivalent capacitance of the series combination of the two capacitors, {tex}C _ { s } = \frac { C } { 2 }{/tex} Equivalent capacitance of the parallel combination of the two capacitors, C p = C + C = 2C According to the question, U s = U P [U S and U P are stored energies in the series and parallel combination of the two capacitors] {tex}\therefore \frac { 1 } { 2 } C _ { s } V _ { s } ^ { 2 } = \frac { 1 } { 2 } C _ { p } V _ { p } ^ { 2 } \Rightarrow \frac { V _ { s } ^ { 2 } } { V _ { p } ^ { 2 } } = \frac { C _ { p } } { C _ { s } } = \frac { 2 C } { ( C / 2 ) }{/tex} {tex}\therefore \frac { V _ { s } ^ { 2 } } { V _ { p } ^ { 2 } } = 4 \Rightarrow \frac { V _ { s } } { V _ { p } } = 2{/tex} {tex}\therefore V _ { s } : V _ { p } = 2 : 1{/tex}
solution: Let V 1 and V 2 are the potential differences across the series and parallel combination of two identical capacitors each of capacitance C. Equivalent capacitance of the series combination of the two capacitors, {tex}C _ { s } = \frac { C } { 2 }{/tex} Equivalent capacitance of the parallel combination of the two capacitors, C p = C + C = 2C According to the question, U s = U P [U S and U P are stored energies in the series and parallel combination of the two capacitors] {tex}\therefore \frac { 1 } { 2 } C _ { s } V _ { s } ^ { 2 } = \frac { 1 } { 2 } C _ { p } V _ { p } ^ { 2 } \Rightarrow \frac { V _ { s } ^ { 2 } } { V _ { p } ^ { 2 } } = \frac { C _ { p } } { C _ { s } } = \frac { 2 C } { ( C / 2 ) }{/tex} {tex}\therefore \frac { V _ { s } ^ { 2 } } { V _ { p } ^ { 2 } } = 4 \Rightarrow \frac { V _ { s } } { V _ { p } } = 2{/tex} {tex}\therefore V _ { s } : V _ { p } = 2 : 1{/tex}
solution_html:
Let V1 and V2 are the potential differences across the series and parallel combination of two identical capacitors each of capacitance C.
Equivalent capacitance of the series combination of the two capacitors,
{tex}C _ { s } = \frac { C } { 2 }{/tex}
Equivalent capacitance of the parallel combination of the two capacitors, Cp = C + C = 2C
According to the question,
Us = UP [US and UP are stored energies in the series and parallel combination of the two capacitors]
{tex}\therefore \frac { 1 } { 2 } C _ { s } V _ { s } ^ { 2 } = \frac { 1 } { 2 } C _ { p } V _ { p } ^ { 2 } \Rightarrow \frac { V _ { s } ^ { 2 } } { V _ { p } ^ { 2 } } = \frac { C _ { p } } { C _ { s } } = \frac { 2 C } { ( C / 2 ) }{/tex}
{tex}\therefore \frac { V _ { s } ^ { 2 } } { V _ { p } ^ { 2 } } = 4 \Rightarrow \frac { V _ { s } } { V _ { p } } = 2{/tex}
{tex}\therefore V _ { s } : V _ { p } = 2 : 1{/tex}

subject: Physics
question_html:
1. How is the electric field due to a charged parallel plate capacitor affected when a dielectric slab is inserted between the plates fully occupying the intervening region?
  2. A slab of material of dielectric constant K has the same area as the plates of a parallel plate capacitor but has thickness {tex}\frac{d}{2}{/tex}. where d is the separation between the plates. Find the expression for the capacitance when the slab is inserted between the plates.
question_text: How is the electric field due to a charged parallel plate capacitor affected when a dielectric slab is inserted between the plates fully occupying the intervening region? A slab of material of dielectric constant K has the same area as the plates of a parallel plate capacitor but has thickness {tex}\frac{d}{2}{/tex} . where d is the separation between the plates. Find the expression for the capacitance when the slab is inserted between the plates.
explanation: The total charge on the capacitor remains conserved on introduction of dielectric slab. Also, the capacitance of capacitor increases to K times of original values. Q = CV = C'V' {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}V ^ { \prime } = \frac { V } { K }{/tex} (because C' = KC) New electric field, E' = {tex}\frac { V ^ { \prime } } { d } = \left( \frac { V / K } { d } \right) = \left( \frac { V } { d } \right) \frac { 1 } { K } = \frac { E } { K }{/tex} (because E = V/K) On introduction of dielectric medium, new electric field E' becomes {tex}\frac{1}{K}{/tex} times of its original electric field (E) value. Capacitance of a parallel plate capacitor partially filled with dielectric medium is given by {tex}C = \frac { \varepsilon _ { 0 } A } { ( d - t )+ \frac{ t }{K} }{/tex} where, t is the thickness of dielectric medium. Here, {tex}t = \frac { d } { 2 }{/tex} {tex}\therefore C = \frac { \varepsilon _ { 0 } A } { (d - \frac { d } { 2 }) + \frac { d } { 2 K } } = \frac { \varepsilon _ { 0 } A } { \frac { d } { 2 } \left( 1 + \frac { 1 } { K } \right) }{/tex} {tex}\therefore \quad C = \frac { 2 \varepsilon _ { 0 } A K } { ( K + 1 ) d }{/tex} This is the required expression for the capacitance of the newly formed capacitor.
solution: The total charge on the capacitor remains conserved on introduction of dielectric slab. Also, the capacitance of capacitor increases to K times of original values. Q = CV = C'V' {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}V ^ { \prime } = \frac { V } { K }{/tex} (because C' = KC) New electric field, E' = {tex}\frac { V ^ { \prime } } { d } = \left( \frac { V / K } { d } \right) = \left( \frac { V } { d } \right) \frac { 1 } { K } = \frac { E } { K }{/tex} (because E = V/K) On introduction of dielectric medium, new electric field E' becomes {tex}\frac{1}{K}{/tex} times of its original electric field (E) value. Capacitance of a parallel plate capacitor partially filled with dielectric medium is given by {tex}C = \frac { \varepsilon _ { 0 } A } { ( d - t )+ \frac{ t }{K} }{/tex} where, t is the thickness of dielectric medium. Here, {tex}t = \frac { d } { 2 }{/tex} {tex}\therefore C = \frac { \varepsilon _ { 0 } A } { (d - \frac { d } { 2 }) + \frac { d } { 2 K } } = \frac { \varepsilon _ { 0 } A } { \frac { d } { 2 } \left( 1 + \frac { 1 } { K } \right) }{/tex} {tex}\therefore \quad C = \frac { 2 \varepsilon _ { 0 } A K } { ( K + 1 ) d }{/tex} This is the required expression for the capacitance of the newly formed capacitor.
solution_html:
1. The total charge on the capacitor remains conserved on introduction of dielectric slab. Also, the capacitance of capacitor increases to K times of original values.
    Q = CV = C'V'
    {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}V ^ { \prime } = \frac { V } { K }{/tex} (because C' = KC)
    New electric field, E' = {tex}\frac { V ^ { \prime } } { d } = \left( \frac { V / K } { d } \right) = \left( \frac { V } { d } \right) \frac { 1 } { K } = \frac { E } { K }{/tex} (because E = V/K)
    On introduction of dielectric medium, new electric field E' becomes {tex}\frac{1}{K}{/tex}  times of its original electric field (E) value.
  2. Capacitance of a parallel plate capacitor partially filled with dielectric medium is given by
    {tex}C = \frac { \varepsilon _ { 0 } A } { ( d - t )+ \frac{ t }{K} }{/tex}
    where, t is the thickness of dielectric medium.
    Here, {tex}t = \frac { d } { 2 }{/tex}
    {tex}\therefore C = \frac { \varepsilon _ { 0 } A } { (d - \frac { d } { 2 }) + \frac { d } { 2 K } } = \frac { \varepsilon _ { 0 } A } { \frac { d } { 2 } \left( 1 + \frac { 1 } { K } \right) }{/tex}
    {tex}\therefore \quad C = \frac { 2 \varepsilon _ { 0 } A K } { ( K + 1 ) d }{/tex}
    This is the required expression for the capacitance of the newly formed capacitor.

question_html:
A network of four capacitors each of 12 µF capacitance, if connected to a 500V supply as shown in the figure

Determine

  1. the equivalent capacitance of the network and
  2. the charge on each capacitor.
question_text: A network of four capacitors each of 12 µF capacitance, if connected to a 500V supply as shown in the figure Determine the equivalent capacitance of the network and the charge on each capacitor.
explanation: In a series combination of infinite number of capacitors, where there is no division of charge {tex}\frac { 1 } { C _ { s } } = \frac { 1 } { C _ { 1 } } + \frac { 1 } { C _ { 2 } } + \frac { 1 } { C _ { 3 } } + \ldots{/tex} In a parallel combination of infinite number of capacitors, where potential difference is same. C P = C 1 + C 2 + C 3 ... Here, C 1 , C 2 and C 3 are in series. Therefore, their equivalent capacitance(C') is {tex}\frac { 1 } { C ^ { \prime } } = \frac { 1 } { C _ { 1 } } + \frac { 1 } { C _ { 2 } } + \frac { 1 } { C _ { 3 } }{/tex} {tex}C ^ { \prime } = \frac { C } { 3 } = \frac { 12 } { 3 } = 4 \mu F{/tex} (since C 1 = C 2 = C 3 = C = 12 {tex}\mu F{/tex} ) Now, C' and C 4 are in parallel combination. Net capacitance, C net = C'+ C 4 = 4µF+ 12µF = 16µF C net = 16µF Being C' and C 4 in parallel and 500 V potential difference applied across them, both will get the same potential difference. Charge on C', q 1 = C'V = (4 µF) {tex}\times{/tex} 500 = 2000 µC C 1 , C 2 and C 3 capacitors each will have 2000 µC of charge. Charge on C 4 , q 2 = C 4 {tex}\times{/tex} V = 12 {tex}\times{/tex} 500 = 6000 µC
solution: In a series combination of infinite number of capacitors, where there is no division of charge {tex}\frac { 1 } { C _ { s } } = \frac { 1 } { C _ { 1 } } + \frac { 1 } { C _ { 2 } } + \frac { 1 } { C _ { 3 } } + \ldots{/tex} In a parallel combination of infinite number of capacitors, where potential difference is same. C P = C 1 + C 2 + C 3 ... Here, C 1 , C 2 and C 3 are in series. Therefore, their equivalent capacitance(C') is {tex}\frac { 1 } { C ^ { \prime } } = \frac { 1 } { C _ { 1 } } + \frac { 1 } { C _ { 2 } } + \frac { 1 } { C _ { 3 } }{/tex} {tex}C ^ { \prime } = \frac { C } { 3 } = \frac { 12 } { 3 } = 4 \mu F{/tex} (since C 1 = C 2 = C 3 = C = 12 {tex}\mu F{/tex} ) Now, C' and C 4 are in parallel combination. Net capacitance, C net = C'+ C 4 = 4µF+ 12µF = 16µF C net = 16µF Being C' and C 4 in parallel and 500 V potential difference applied across them, both will get the same potential difference. Charge on C', q 1 = C'V = (4 µF) {tex}\times{/tex} 500 = 2000 µC C 1 , C 2 and C 3 capacitors each will have 2000 µC of charge. Charge on C 4 , q 2 = C 4 {tex}\times{/tex} V = 12 {tex}\times{/tex} 500 = 6000 µC
solution_html:
In a series combination of infinite number of capacitors, where there is no division of charge
{tex}\frac { 1 } { C _ { s } } = \frac { 1 } { C _ { 1 } } + \frac { 1 } { C _ { 2 } } + \frac { 1 } { C _ { 3 } } + \ldots{/tex}
In a parallel combination of infinite number of capacitors, where potential difference is same.
CP = C1 + C2 + C3...

  1. Here, C1, C2 and C3 are in series. Therefore, their equivalent capacitance(C') is
    {tex}\frac { 1 } { C ^ { \prime } } = \frac { 1 } { C _ { 1 } } + \frac { 1 } { C _ { 2 } } + \frac { 1 } { C _ { 3 } }{/tex}
    {tex}C ^ { \prime } = \frac { C } { 3 } = \frac { 12 } { 3 } = 4 \mu F{/tex} (since C1 = C2 = C3 = C = 12 {tex}\mu F{/tex})
    Now, C' and C4 are in parallel combination.
    Net capacitance, Cnet = C'+ C4  = 4µF+ 12µF = 16µF
    Cnet = 16µF
  2. Being C' and C4  in parallel and 500 V potential difference applied across them, both will get the same potential difference.
    Charge on C',
    q1 = C'V = (4 µF){tex}\times{/tex}500 = 2000 µC
    C1, C2 and C3 capacitors each will have 2000 µC of charge.
    Charge on C4, q2 = C4 {tex}\times{/tex} V = 12 {tex}\times{/tex} 500 = 6000 µC

subject: Physics
question_html:
Four capacitors of values 6 µF, 6 µF, 6 µF and 2 µF are connected to a 6 V battery as shown in the figure.

Determine the

  1. equivalent capacitance of the network.
  2. charge on each capacitor.
question_text: Four capacitors of values 6 µF, 6 µF, 6 µF and 2 µF are connected to a 6 V battery as shown in the figure. Determine the equivalent capacitance of the network. charge on each capacitor.
explanation: In series combination, charge on each capacitor always remains same. All capacitors of capacitances 6 µF are in series combination, then equivalent capacitance of this series combination is given by {tex}\frac{1}{{{C^\prime }}} = \frac{1}{{{C_1}}} + \frac{1}{{{C_2}}} + \frac{1}{{{C_3}}}{/tex} or {tex}{C^\prime } = \frac{C}{n} = \frac{{6\mu F}}{3} = 2\mu {\text{F}}{/tex} Now this C' and another 2µF capacitors are in parallel combination. Hence, the equivalent capacitance of the whole circuit, C eq = C' + 2µF= 2µF + 2µF C eq = 4 µF Since, C' and 2µF are in parallel combination, therefore both of them will get the same potential difference i.e. 6 V. Now charge on C' q' = C'V = (2µF) {tex}\times{/tex} 6V = 12 µC The charge across each capacitor of capacitance 6 µF is same and equal to charge across the series combination of them i.e.12µC Charge on 2µF capacitor, q = CV = (2µF)(6 V) = 12µC
solution: In series combination, charge on each capacitor always remains same. All capacitors of capacitances 6 µF are in series combination, then equivalent capacitance of this series combination is given by {tex}\frac{1}{{{C^\prime }}} = \frac{1}{{{C_1}}} + \frac{1}{{{C_2}}} + \frac{1}{{{C_3}}}{/tex} or {tex}{C^\prime } = \frac{C}{n} = \frac{{6\mu F}}{3} = 2\mu {\text{F}}{/tex} Now this C' and another 2µF capacitors are in parallel combination. Hence, the equivalent capacitance of the whole circuit, C eq = C' + 2µF= 2µF + 2µF C eq = 4 µF Since, C' and 2µF are in parallel combination, therefore both of them will get the same potential difference i.e. 6 V. Now charge on C' q' = C'V = (2µF) {tex}\times{/tex} 6V = 12 µC The charge across each capacitor of capacitance 6 µF is same and equal to charge across the series combination of them i.e.12µC Charge on 2µF capacitor, q = CV = (2µF)(6 V) = 12µC
solution_html:
In series combination, charge on each capacitor always remains same.

  1. All capacitors of capacitances 6 µF are in series combination, then equivalent capacitance of this series combination is given by
    {tex}\frac{1}{{{C^\prime }}} = \frac{1}{{{C_1}}} + \frac{1}{{{C_2}}} + \frac{1}{{{C_3}}}{/tex} or {tex}{C^\prime } = \frac{C}{n} = \frac{{6\mu F}}{3} = 2\mu {\text{F}}{/tex}
    Now this C' and another 2µF capacitors are in parallel combination.
    Hence, the equivalent capacitance of the whole circuit,
    Ceq = C' + 2µF= 2µF + 2µF
    Ceq = 4 µF
  2. Since, C' and 2µF are in parallel combination, therefore both of them will get the same potential difference i.e. 6 V.
    Now charge on C'
    q' = C'V = (2µF) {tex}\times{/tex} 6V = 12 µC
    The charge across each capacitor of capacitance 6 µF is same and equal to charge across the series combination of them i.e.12µC
    Charge on 2µF capacitor, q = CV = (2µF)(6 V) = 12µC

question_html: Find out the expression for the potential energy of a system of three charges q1 , q2 and q3 located at r1 , r2 and r3 with respect to the common origin O.
question_text: Find out the expression for the potential energy of a system of three charges q 1 , q 2 and q 3 located at r 1 , r 2 and r 3 with respect to the common origin O.
explanation: Let three point charges q 1 , q 2 and q 3 have their position vectors r 1 , r 2 and r 3 from the origin O, respectively. Potential energy due to the charges q 1 and q 2 , {tex}U _ { 12 } = \frac { 1 } { 4 \pi \varepsilon _ { 0 } } \cdot \frac { q _ { 1 } q _ { 2 } } { \left| \mathbf {r} _ { 12 } \right| } = \frac { 1 } { 4 \pi \varepsilon _ { 0 } } \cdot \frac { q _ { 1 } q _ { 2 } } { \left| \mathbf {r} _ { 2 } - \mathbf {r} _ { 1 } \right| }{/tex} Slmilary potential energy due to the charges q 2 and q 3 , {tex}U _ { 23 } = \frac { 1 } { 4 \pi \varepsilon _ { 0 } } \cdot \frac { q _ { 2 } q _ { 3 } } { \left| \mathbf {r} _ { 3 } - \mathbf { r} _ { 2 } \right| }{/tex} and the potential energy due to the charges q 1 and q 3 , {tex}U _ { 31 } = \frac { 1 } { 4 \pi \varepsilon _ { 0 } } \cdot \frac { q _ { 1 } q _ { 3 } } { \left| \mathbf {r} _ { 3 } - \mathbf { r} _ { 1 } \right| }{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} Net potential energy of the system of the three charges, U = U 12 + U 23 + U 31 {tex}\Rightarrow U = \frac { 1 } { 4 \pi \varepsilon _ { 0 } } \left[ \frac { q _ { 1 } q _ { 2 } } { \left| \mathbf { r } _ { 2 } - \mathbf { r } _ { 1 } \right| } + \frac { q _ { 2 } q _ { 3 } } { \left| \mathbf {r} _ { 3 } - \mathbf {r} _ { 2 } \right| } + \frac { q _ { 1 } q _ { 3 } } { \left| \mathbf {r} _ { 3 } - \mathbf {r} _ { 1 } \right| } \right]{/tex}
solution: Let three point charges q 1 , q 2 and q 3 have their position vectors r 1 , r 2 and r 3 from the origin O, respectively. Potential energy due to the charges q 1 and q 2 , {tex}U _ { 12 } = \frac { 1 } { 4 \pi \varepsilon _ { 0 } } \cdot \frac { q _ { 1 } q _ { 2 } } { \left| \mathbf {r} _ { 12 } \right| } = \frac { 1 } { 4 \pi \varepsilon _ { 0 } } \cdot \frac { q _ { 1 } q _ { 2 } } { \left| \mathbf {r} _ { 2 } - \mathbf {r} _ { 1 } \right| }{/tex} Slmilary potential energy due to the charges q 2 and q 3 , {tex}U _ { 23 } = \frac { 1 } { 4 \pi \varepsilon _ { 0 } } \cdot \frac { q _ { 2 } q _ { 3 } } { \left| \mathbf {r} _ { 3 } - \mathbf { r} _ { 2 } \right| }{/tex} and the potential energy due to the charges q 1 and q 3 , {tex}U _ { 31 } = \frac { 1 } { 4 \pi \varepsilon _ { 0 } } \cdot \frac { q _ { 1 } q _ { 3 } } { \left| \mathbf {r} _ { 3 } - \mathbf { r} _ { 1 } \right| }{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} Net potential energy of the system of the three charges, U = U 12 + U 23 + U 31 {tex}\Rightarrow U = \frac { 1 } { 4 \pi \varepsilon _ { 0 } } \left[ \frac { q _ { 1 } q _ { 2 } } { \left| \mathbf { r } _ { 2 } - \mathbf { r } _ { 1 } \right| } + \frac { q _ { 2 } q _ { 3 } } { \left| \mathbf {r} _ { 3 } - \mathbf {r} _ { 2 } \right| } + \frac { q _ { 1 } q _ { 3 } } { \left| \mathbf {r} _ { 3 } - \mathbf {r} _ { 1 } \right| } \right]{/tex}
solution_html:
Let three point charges q1, q2 and q3 have their position vectors r1, r2 and r3 from the origin O, respectively.

Potential energy due to the charges q1 and q2,
{tex}U _ { 12 } = \frac { 1 } { 4 \pi \varepsilon _ { 0 } } \cdot \frac { q _ { 1 } q _ { 2 } } { \left| \mathbf {r} _ { 12 } \right| } = \frac { 1 } { 4 \pi \varepsilon _ { 0 } } \cdot \frac { q _ { 1 } q _ { 2 } } { \left| \mathbf {r} _ { 2 } - \mathbf {r} _ { 1 } \right| }{/tex}
Slmilary potential energy due to the charges q2 and q3, {tex}U _ { 23 } = \frac { 1 } { 4 \pi \varepsilon _ { 0 } } \cdot \frac { q _ { 2 } q _ { 3 } } { \left| \mathbf {r} _ { 3 } - \mathbf { r} _ { 2 } \right| }{/tex} and the potential energy due to the charges q1 and q3,
{tex}U _ { 31 } = \frac { 1 } { 4 \pi \varepsilon _ { 0 } } \cdot \frac { q _ { 1 } q _ { 3 } } { \left| \mathbf {r} _ { 3 } - \mathbf { r} _ { 1 } \right| }{/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Net potential energy of the system of the three charges,
U = U12 + U23 + U31
{tex}\Rightarrow U = \frac { 1 } { 4 \pi \varepsilon _ { 0 } } \left[ \frac { q _ { 1 } q _ { 2 } } { \left| \mathbf { r } _ { 2 } - \mathbf { r } _ { 1 } \right| } + \frac { q _ { 2 } q _ { 3 } } { \left| \mathbf {r} _ { 3 } - \mathbf {r} _ { 2 } \right| } + \frac { q _ { 1 } q _ { 3 } } { \left| \mathbf {r} _ { 3 } - \mathbf {r} _ { 1 } \right| } \right]{/tex}

question_html:
1. Plot a graph comparing the variation of potential V and electric field E due to a point charge Q as a function of distance R from the point charge.
  2. Find the ratio of the potential differences that must be applied across the parallel and the series combination of two capacitors, C1 and C2 with their capacitances in the ratio 1 : 2, so that the energy stored in the two cases becomes the same.
question_text: Plot a graph comparing the variation of potential V and electric field E due to a point charge Q as a function of distance R from the point charge. Find the ratio of the potential differences that must be applied across the parallel and the series combination of two capacitors, C 1 and C 2 with their capacitances in the ratio 1 : 2, so that the energy stored in the two cases becomes the same.
explanation: The graph comparing the variation of potential V and electric field is shown below: Let C 1 = C and C 2 = 2C Equivalent capacitance in parallel combination, C P = 2C + C = 3C and in series combination, {tex}C _ { s } = \frac { 2 C \times C } { 2 C + c } = \frac { 2 C ^ { 2 } } { 3 C } = \frac { 2 C } { 3 }{/tex} Let V P and V s are potential difference across the equivalent capacitance in parallel and series combination respectively, to have same potential energy. i.e. U P = U s {tex}\therefore \frac { 1 } { 2 } C _ { p } V _ { p } ^ { 2 } = \frac { 1 } { 2 } C _ { s } V _ { s } ^ { 2 } \Rightarrow \frac { V _ { p } } { V _ { s } } = \sqrt { \frac { C _ { s } } { C _ { p } } }{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac {V_P}{V_S} = \sqrt { \frac { ( 2 C / 3 ) } { ( 3 C ) } } = \sqrt { \frac { 2 } { 9 } }{/tex} {tex}\therefore V _ { p } : V _ { s } = \sqrt { 2 } : 3{/tex} This is the required ratio of the potential differences across the parallel and series combination of the capacitors.
solution: The graph comparing the variation of potential V and electric field is shown below: Let C 1 = C and C 2 = 2C Equivalent capacitance in parallel combination, C P = 2C + C = 3C and in series combination, {tex}C _ { s } = \frac { 2 C \times C } { 2 C + c } = \frac { 2 C ^ { 2 } } { 3 C } = \frac { 2 C } { 3 }{/tex} Let V P and V s are potential difference across the equivalent capacitance in parallel and series combination respectively, to have same potential energy. i.e. U P = U s {tex}\therefore \frac { 1 } { 2 } C _ { p } V _ { p } ^ { 2 } = \frac { 1 } { 2 } C _ { s } V _ { s } ^ { 2 } \Rightarrow \frac { V _ { p } } { V _ { s } } = \sqrt { \frac { C _ { s } } { C _ { p } } }{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac {V_P}{V_S} = \sqrt { \frac { ( 2 C / 3 ) } { ( 3 C ) } } = \sqrt { \frac { 2 } { 9 } }{/tex} {tex}\therefore V _ { p } : V _ { s } = \sqrt { 2 } : 3{/tex} This is the required ratio of the potential differences across the parallel and series combination of the capacitors.
solution_html:
1. The graph comparing the variation of potential V and electric field is shown below:
  2. Let C1 = C and C2 = 2C
    Equivalent capacitance in parallel combination, CP = 2C + C = 3C
    and in series combination, {tex}C _ { s } = \frac { 2 C \times C } { 2 C + c } = \frac { 2 C ^ { 2 } } { 3 C } = \frac { 2 C } { 3 }{/tex}
    Let VP and Vs are potential difference across the equivalent capacitance in parallel and series combination respectively, to have same potential energy.
    i.e. UP = Us
    {tex}\therefore \frac { 1 } { 2 } C _ { p } V _ { p } ^ { 2 } = \frac { 1 } { 2 } C _ { s } V _ { s } ^ { 2 } \Rightarrow \frac { V _ { p } } { V _ { s } } = \sqrt { \frac { C _ { s } } { C _ { p } } }{/tex}
    {tex}\Rightarrow \frac {V_P}{V_S} = \sqrt { \frac { ( 2 C / 3 ) } { ( 3 C ) } } = \sqrt { \frac { 2 } { 9 } }{/tex}
    {tex}\therefore V _ { p } : V _ { s } = \sqrt { 2 } : 3{/tex}
    This is the required ratio of the potential differences across the parallel and series combination of the capacitors.

question_html:
1. Depict the equipotential surfaces for a system of two identical positive point charges placed a distance d apart.
  2. Deduce the expression for the potential energy of a system of two point charges q1 and q2 brought from infinity to the points with positions r1 and r2 respectively, in presence of external electric field E.
question_text: Depict the equipotential surfaces for a system of two identical positive point charges placed a distance d apart. Deduce the expression for the potential energy of a system of two point charges q 1 and q 2 brought from infinity to the points with positions r 1 and r 2 respectively, in presence of external electric field E.
explanation: The figure of equipotential surfaces of two identical positive charges is given below: By definition, electric potential energy of any charge q placed in the region of electric field is equal to the work done in bringing charge q from infinity to that point and given by U = qV = qEr (as electric field intensity, E = V/r ; V being electrostatic potential). Now the potential energy of the system mentioned in the question, U' = E(q 1 r 1 + q 2 r 2 )
solution: The figure of equipotential surfaces of two identical positive charges is given below: By definition, electric potential energy of any charge q placed in the region of electric field is equal to the work done in bringing charge q from infinity to that point and given by U = qV = qEr (as electric field intensity, E = V/r ; V being electrostatic potential). Now the potential energy of the system mentioned in the question, U' = E(q 1 r 1 + q 2 r 2 )
solution_html:
1. The figure of equipotential surfaces of two identical positive charges is given below:
  2. By definition, electric potential energy of any charge q placed in the region of electric field is equal to the work done in bringing charge q from infinity to that point and given by
    U = qV = qEr (as electric field intensity, E = V/r ; V being electrostatic potential).
    Now the potential energy of the system mentioned in the question, U' = E(q1r1 + q2r2)

subject: Physics
question_html:
A parallel plate capacitor, each with plate area A and separation d is charged to a potential difference V. The battery used to charge it remains connected. A dielectric slab of thickness d and dielectric constant K is now placed between the plates. What change if any will take place in

  1. charge on plates?
  2. electric field intensity between the plates?
  3. capacitance of the capacitor? Justify your answer in each case.
question_text: A parallel plate capacitor, each with plate area A and separation d is charged to a potential difference V. The battery used to charge it remains connected. A dielectric slab of thickness d and dielectric constant K is now placed between the plates. What change if any will take place in charge on plates? electric field intensity between the plates? capacitance of the capacitor? Justify your answer in each case.
explanation: On introduction of dielectric slab of dielectric constant K to fill the gap between plates of capacitor of separation d completely and also connected with a battery, the given quantities become as follows: The potential difference V between the plates of capacitor remains same due to connectivity with battery and hence, charge q' becomes K times of original charge as q' = C'V' = (KC)(V) = K(CV) = Kq => q' = Kq Electric field intensity remains same as potential difference and distance of separation between two plates remain unaffected as E = {tex}\frac Vd{/tex} The capacitance of the capacitor becomes K times of original capacitance of the capacitor. {tex}\therefore \quad C ^ { \prime } = K C = \frac { K \varepsilon _ { 0 } A } { d }{/tex}
solution: On introduction of dielectric slab of dielectric constant K to fill the gap between plates of capacitor of separation d completely and also connected with a battery, the given quantities become as follows: The potential difference V between the plates of capacitor remains same due to connectivity with battery and hence, charge q' becomes K times of original charge as q' = C'V' = (KC)(V) = K(CV) = Kq => q' = Kq Electric field intensity remains same as potential difference and distance of separation between two plates remain unaffected as E = {tex}\frac Vd{/tex} The capacitance of the capacitor becomes K times of original capacitance of the capacitor. {tex}\therefore \quad C ^ { \prime } = K C = \frac { K \varepsilon _ { 0 } A } { d }{/tex}
solution_html:
On introduction of dielectric slab of dielectric constant K to fill the gap between plates of capacitor of separation d completely and also connected with a battery, the given quantities become as follows:

  1. The potential difference V between the plates of capacitor remains same due to connectivity with battery and hence, charge q' becomes K times of original charge as
     q' = C'V' = (KC)(V) = K(CV) = Kq
    => q' = Kq
  2. Electric field intensity remains same as potential difference and distance of separation between two plates remain unaffected as
    E = {tex}\frac Vd{/tex}
  3. The capacitance of the capacitor becomes K times of original capacitance of the capacitor.
    {tex}\therefore \quad C ^ { \prime } = K C = \frac { K \varepsilon _ { 0 } A } { d }{/tex}

subject: Physics
question_html: Show that the capacitance of a spherical conductor is 4{tex}\pi{/tex}{tex}\varepsilon _0{/tex} times the radius of the spherical conductor.
question_text: Show that the capacitance of a spherical conductor is 4 {tex}\pi{/tex} {tex}\varepsilon _0{/tex} times the radius of the spherical conductor.
explanation: As we know, to determine the electric field at any point on the surface of a charged spherical conductor of radius r applying Gauss's theorem, {tex}E = \frac { q } { 4 \pi \varepsilon _ { 0 } r ^ { 2 } }{/tex} Hence electrostatic potential, {tex}V = -\int \mathbf { E } \cdot d \mathbf { r } = \frac { q } { 4 \pi \varepsilon _ { 0 } \cdot r }{/tex} where, {tex}\varepsilon _ { 0 } = 8.854 \times 10 ^ { - 12 } \mathrm { C } ^ { 2 } \mathrm { N } ^ { - 1 } \mathrm { m } ^ { - 2}{/tex} = permittivity of free space or vacuum The capacitance of the spherical conductor situated in vacuum is given by {tex}C = \frac { q } { V } = \frac { q } { \frac { 1 } { 4 \pi \varepsilon _ { 0 } } \cdot \frac { q } { r } } \Rightarrow C = 4 \pi \varepsilon _ { 0 } r{/tex} Hence, the capacitance of an isolated spherical conductor situated in a vacuum is always {tex}4 \pi \varepsilon _ { 0 }{/tex} times of its radius.
solution: As we know, to determine the electric field at any point on the surface of a charged spherical conductor of radius r applying Gauss's theorem, {tex}E = \frac { q } { 4 \pi \varepsilon _ { 0 } r ^ { 2 } }{/tex} Hence electrostatic potential, {tex}V = -\int \mathbf { E } \cdot d \mathbf { r } = \frac { q } { 4 \pi \varepsilon _ { 0 } \cdot r }{/tex} where, {tex}\varepsilon _ { 0 } = 8.854 \times 10 ^ { - 12 } \mathrm { C } ^ { 2 } \mathrm { N } ^ { - 1 } \mathrm { m } ^ { - 2}{/tex} = permittivity of free space or vacuum The capacitance of the spherical conductor situated in vacuum is given by {tex}C = \frac { q } { V } = \frac { q } { \frac { 1 } { 4 \pi \varepsilon _ { 0 } } \cdot \frac { q } { r } } \Rightarrow C = 4 \pi \varepsilon _ { 0 } r{/tex} Hence, the capacitance of an isolated spherical conductor situated in a vacuum is always {tex}4 \pi \varepsilon _ { 0 }{/tex} times of its radius.
solution_html:
As we know, to determine the electric field at any point on the surface of a charged spherical conductor of radius r applying Gauss's theorem, 
{tex}E = \frac { q } { 4 \pi \varepsilon _ { 0 } r ^ { 2 } }{/tex}
Hence electrostatic potential, {tex}V = -\int \mathbf { E } \cdot d \mathbf { r } = \frac { q } { 4 \pi \varepsilon _ { 0 } \cdot r }{/tex}
where, {tex}\varepsilon _ { 0 } = 8.854 \times 10 ^ { - 12 } \mathrm { C } ^ { 2 } \mathrm { N } ^ { - 1 } \mathrm { m } ^ { - 2}{/tex} = permittivity of free space or vacuum
The capacitance of the spherical conductor situated in vacuum is given by
{tex}C = \frac { q } { V } = \frac { q } { \frac { 1 } { 4 \pi \varepsilon _ { 0 } } \cdot \frac { q } { r } } \Rightarrow C = 4 \pi \varepsilon _ { 0 } r{/tex}
Hence, the capacitance of an isolated spherical conductor situated in a vacuum is always {tex}4 \pi \varepsilon _ { 0 }{/tex} times of its radius.

subject: Physics
question_html:
A parallel plate capacitor is charged by a battery. After sometime, the battery is disconnected and a dielectric slab with its thickness equal to the plate separation is inserted between the plates. How will

  1. the capacitances of the capacitor,
  2. potential difference between the plates and
  3. the energy stored in the capacitors be affected?
    Justify your answer in each case.
question_text: A parallel plate capacitor is charged by a battery. After sometime, the battery is disconnected and a dielectric slab with its thickness equal to the plate separation is inserted between the plates. How will the capacitances of the capacitor, potential difference between the plates and the energy stored in the capacitors be affected? Justify your answer in each case.
explanation: Say, the dielectric constant of the inserted dielectric is K. Now, after introduction of the dielectric slab in the isolated charged capacitor, The new capacitance (C') becomes K times of original capacitance(C) of the capacitor as {tex}C = \frac { \varepsilon _ { 0 } A } { d } \;and\; C ^ { \prime } = \frac { K \varepsilon _ { 0 } A } { d }{/tex} We know that charge remains same as before (according to the law of conservation of charge) in this phenomenon. i.e. Q = CV = C'V' Hence, {tex}V ^ { \prime } = \frac { C V } { C ^ { \prime } } = \frac { C V } { K C }{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad V ^ { \prime } = \frac { V } { K }{/tex} Potential difference decreases and becomes {tex}\frac{1}{K}{/tex} times of the previous value. Energy stored initially in the capacitor, {tex}U = \frac { q ^ { 2 } } { 2 C }{/tex} Energy stored in the capacitor after inserting the dielectric slab of dielectric constant K, {tex}\therefore \quad U ^ { \prime } = \frac { q ^ { 2 } } { 2 K C }{/tex} [ {tex}\because C ^ { \prime } = K C{/tex} ] where, K =dielectric constant of the medium {tex}\Rightarrow U ^ { \prime } = \frac { 1 } { K } \left( \frac { q ^ { 2 } } { 2 C } \right) \Rightarrow U ^ { \prime } = \frac { 1 } { K } ( U ) \Rightarrow U ^ { \prime } = \frac { 1 } { K } \times U{/tex} The energy stored in the capacitor decreases and becomes {tex}\frac1K{/tex} times of the previous stored energy.
solution: Say, the dielectric constant of the inserted dielectric is K. Now, after introduction of the dielectric slab in the isolated charged capacitor, The new capacitance (C') becomes K times of original capacitance(C) of the capacitor as {tex}C = \frac { \varepsilon _ { 0 } A } { d } \;and\; C ^ { \prime } = \frac { K \varepsilon _ { 0 } A } { d }{/tex} We know that charge remains same as before (according to the law of conservation of charge) in this phenomenon. i.e. Q = CV = C'V' Hence, {tex}V ^ { \prime } = \frac { C V } { C ^ { \prime } } = \frac { C V } { K C }{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad V ^ { \prime } = \frac { V } { K }{/tex} Potential difference decreases and becomes {tex}\frac{1}{K}{/tex} times of the previous value. Energy stored initially in the capacitor, {tex}U = \frac { q ^ { 2 } } { 2 C }{/tex} Energy stored in the capacitor after inserting the dielectric slab of dielectric constant K, {tex}\therefore \quad U ^ { \prime } = \frac { q ^ { 2 } } { 2 K C }{/tex} [ {tex}\because C ^ { \prime } = K C{/tex} ] where, K =dielectric constant of the medium {tex}\Rightarrow U ^ { \prime } = \frac { 1 } { K } \left( \frac { q ^ { 2 } } { 2 C } \right) \Rightarrow U ^ { \prime } = \frac { 1 } { K } ( U ) \Rightarrow U ^ { \prime } = \frac { 1 } { K } \times U{/tex} The energy stored in the capacitor decreases and becomes {tex}\frac1K{/tex} times of the previous stored energy.
solution_html:
Say, the dielectric constant of the inserted dielectric is K. Now, after introduction of the dielectric slab in the isolated charged capacitor,

  1. The new capacitance (C') becomes K times of original capacitance(C) of the capacitor as
    {tex}C = \frac { \varepsilon _ { 0 } A } { d } \;and\; C ^ { \prime } = \frac { K \varepsilon _ { 0 } A } { d }{/tex}
  2. We know that charge remains same as before (according to the law of conservation of charge) in this phenomenon.
    i.e. Q = CV = C'V'
    Hence, {tex}V ^ { \prime } = \frac { C V } { C ^ { \prime } } = \frac { C V } { K C }{/tex}
    {tex}\Rightarrow \quad V ^ { \prime } = \frac { V } { K }{/tex} Potential difference decreases and becomes {tex}\frac{1}{K}{/tex} times of the previous value.
  3. Energy stored initially in the capacitor,
    {tex}U = \frac { q ^ { 2 } } { 2 C }{/tex}
     Energy stored in the capacitor after inserting the dielectric slab of dielectric constant K,
    {tex}\therefore \quad U ^ { \prime } = \frac { q ^ { 2 } } { 2 K C }{/tex}[{tex}\because C ^ { \prime } = K C{/tex}]
      where, K =dielectric constant of the medium
     {tex}\Rightarrow U ^ { \prime } = \frac { 1 } { K } \left( \frac { q ^ { 2 } } { 2 C } \right) \Rightarrow U ^ { \prime } = \frac { 1 } { K } ( U ) \Rightarrow U ^ { \prime } = \frac { 1 } { K } \times U{/tex}
     The energy stored in the capacitor decreases and becomes {tex}\frac1K{/tex} times of the previous stored energy.

subject: Physics
question_html:
1. Deduce the expression for the energy stored in a charged capacitor
  2. Show that the effective capacitance C of a series combination of three capacitors C1, C2 and C3 is given by {tex}\frac1C=\;\frac1{C_1}+\frac1{C_2}+\frac1{C_3}{/tex}.
question_text: Deduce the expression for the energy stored in a charged capacitor Show that the effective capacitance C of a series combination of three capacitors C 1 , C 2 and C 3 is given by {tex}\frac1C=\;\frac1{C_1}+\frac1{C_2}+\frac1{C_3}{/tex} .
explanation: During charging of the capacitor, work is done by the battery which is stored in the form of potential energy inside the capacitor. Consider a capacitor which is to be charged by charge Q with the help of a battery. Let at any instant charge on the capacitor is q and the potential difference between the two plates of the capacitor is V. We know that, q = CV {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} V = q/C Now small work done to charge the capacitor by small charge dq, dW = Vdq = {tex}\frac { q } { C } d q{/tex} where, q = instantaneous charge, C = capacitance and V = voltage {tex}\therefore{/tex} Total work done in storing charge from 0 to Q(total charge) is given by {tex}\Rightarrow W=\int_0^Q\frac qCdq\;=\frac{Q^2}{2C}{/tex} In a series combination of capacitors, the same charge lie on each capacitor for any value of capacitances. Also, the net potential difference across the combination is equal to the algebraic sum of potential differences across each capacitor i.e. V = V 1 + V 2 + V 3 ..........(i) where V 1 , V 2 , V 3 and V are the potential differences across C 1 , C 2 , C 3 and equivalent capacitor, respectively. Again {tex}q_1 =C_1 V_1 \Rightarrow V_1 = \frac {q_1}{C_1}{/tex} Similarly, {tex}V _ { 2 } = \frac { q } { C _ { 2 } } ~and~ V _ { 3 } = \frac { q } { C _ { 3 } }{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} Total potential difference [From Eq.(i)] {tex}\Rightarrow V = \frac { q } { C _ { 1 } } + \frac { q } { C _ { 2 } } + \frac { q } { C _ { 3 } }{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac { V } { q } = \frac { 1 } { C _ { 1 } } + \frac { 1 } { C _ { 2 } } + \frac { 1 } { C _ { 3 } }{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac { 1 } { C } = \frac { 1 } { C _ { 1 } } + \frac { 1 } { C _ { 2 } } + \frac { 1 } { C _ { 3 } }{/tex} [ {tex}\frac { V } { q } = \frac { 1 } { C }{/tex} , where C is equivalent capacitance]
solution: During charging of the capacitor, work is done by the battery which is stored in the form of potential energy inside the capacitor. Consider a capacitor which is to be charged by charge Q with the help of a battery. Let at any instant charge on the capacitor is q and the potential difference between the two plates of the capacitor is V. We know that, q = CV {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} V = q/C Now small work done to charge the capacitor by small charge dq, dW = Vdq = {tex}\frac { q } { C } d q{/tex} where, q = instantaneous charge, C = capacitance and V = voltage {tex}\therefore{/tex} Total work done in storing charge from 0 to Q(total charge) is given by {tex}\Rightarrow W=\int_0^Q\frac qCdq\;=\frac{Q^2}{2C}{/tex} In a series combination of capacitors, the same charge lie on each capacitor for any value of capacitances. Also, the net potential difference across the combination is equal to the algebraic sum of potential differences across each capacitor i.e. V = V 1 + V 2 + V 3 ..........(i) where V 1 , V 2 , V 3 and V are the potential differences across C 1 , C 2 , C 3 and equivalent capacitor, respectively. Again {tex}q_1 =C_1 V_1 \Rightarrow V_1 = \frac {q_1}{C_1}{/tex} Similarly, {tex}V _ { 2 } = \frac { q } { C _ { 2 } } ~and~ V _ { 3 } = \frac { q } { C _ { 3 } }{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} Total potential difference [From Eq.(i)] {tex}\Rightarrow V = \frac { q } { C _ { 1 } } + \frac { q } { C _ { 2 } } + \frac { q } { C _ { 3 } }{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac { V } { q } = \frac { 1 } { C _ { 1 } } + \frac { 1 } { C _ { 2 } } + \frac { 1 } { C _ { 3 } }{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac { 1 } { C } = \frac { 1 } { C _ { 1 } } + \frac { 1 } { C _ { 2 } } + \frac { 1 } { C _ { 3 } }{/tex} [ {tex}\frac { V } { q } = \frac { 1 } { C }{/tex} , where C is equivalent capacitance]
solution_html:
1. During charging of the capacitor, work is done by the battery which is stored in the form of potential energy inside the capacitor.
    Consider a capacitor which is to be charged by charge Q with the help of a battery. Let at any instant charge on the capacitor is q and the potential difference between the two plates of the capacitor is V.
    We know that,
    q = CV {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} V = q/C
    Now small work done to charge the capacitor by small charge dq,
    dW = Vdq = {tex}\frac { q } { C } d q{/tex}
    where, q = instantaneous charge, C = capacitance and  V = voltage
    {tex}\therefore{/tex} Total work done in storing charge from 0 to Q(total charge) is given by
    {tex}\Rightarrow W=\int_0^Q\frac qCdq\;=\frac{Q^2}{2C}{/tex}

  2. In a series combination of capacitors, the same charge lie on each capacitor for any value of capacitances.

    Also, the net potential difference across the combination is equal to the algebraic sum of potential differences across each capacitor
    i.e. V = V1 + V2 + V3 ..........(i)
    where V1 , V2, V3 and V are the potential differences across C1, C2, C3 and equivalent capacitor, respectively.
    Again {tex}q_1 =C_1 V_1 \Rightarrow V_1 = \frac {q_1}{C_1}{/tex}
    Similarly, {tex}V _ { 2 } = \frac { q } { C _ { 2 } } ~and~ V _ { 3 } = \frac { q } { C _ { 3 } }{/tex}
     {tex}\therefore{/tex} Total potential difference [From Eq.(i)]
    {tex}\Rightarrow V = \frac { q } { C _ { 1 } } + \frac { q } { C _ { 2 } } + \frac { q } { C _ { 3 } }{/tex}
    {tex}\Rightarrow \frac { V } { q } = \frac { 1 } { C _ { 1 } } + \frac { 1 } { C _ { 2 } } + \frac { 1 } { C _ { 3 } }{/tex}
    {tex}\Rightarrow \frac { 1 } { C } = \frac { 1 } { C _ { 1 } } + \frac { 1 } { C _ { 2 } } + \frac { 1 } { C _ { 3 } }{/tex} [ {tex}\frac { V } { q } = \frac { 1 } { C }{/tex} , where C is equivalent capacitance]

question_html:
Show that the effective capacitance C of a series combination of three capacitors of capacitances C1, C2 and C3 is given by
{tex}C=\frac{C_{1} C_{2} C_{3}}{C_{1} C_{2}+C_{2} C_{3}+C_{3} C_{1}}{/tex}
question_text: Show that the effective capacitance C of a series combination of three capacitors of capacitances C 1 , C 2 and C 3 is given by {tex}C=\frac{C_{1} C_{2} C_{3}}{C_{1} C_{2}+C_{2} C_{3}+C_{3} C_{1}}{/tex}
explanation: The given figure shows the method of connecting three capacitors of capacitances C 1 , C 2 and C 3 in series. When charge +q is given to plate A of the capacitor C 1 it induces charge -q on left face and +q on right face of the plate B. Thus, plate C of the capacitor C 2 also gets charge +q on it. Then, it induces charge -q on left face of plate D and charge +q on plate E of the capacitor C 3 . The charge +q on plate E induces charge -q on left face of plate F, while the charge +q induced on its right face flows to earth. Thus, the three capacitors get charged with same amount of charge +q, when charge +q is given to the first capacitor. Let V 1 , V 2 and V 3 be potential differences across the plates of the three capacitors. If V is the potential difference across the series combination, then V = V 1 + V 2 + V 3 From the definition of capacitance, {tex}\mathrm{V}_{1}=\frac{q}{\mathrm{C}_{1}} ; \mathrm{V}_{2}=\frac{q}{\mathrm{C}_{2}} \text { and } \mathrm{V}_{3}=\frac{q}{\mathrm{C}_{3}}{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\mathrm{V}=\frac{q}{\mathrm{C}_{1}}+\frac{q}{\mathrm{C}_{2}}+\frac{q}{\mathrm{C}_{3}}=q\left[\frac{1}{\mathrm{C}_{1}}+\frac{1}{\mathrm{C}_{2}}+\frac{1}{\mathrm{C}_{3}}\right]{/tex} .....(i) Let C be the equivalent capacitance of the series combination. Therefore, when a potential difference V is applied across a capacitor of capacitance C, it will also store the same amount of charge. Hence, {tex}\mathrm{V}=\frac{q}{\mathrm{C}}{/tex} .....(ii) From the equations (i) and (ii), we have {tex}\frac{q}{\mathrm{C}}=q\left(\frac{1}{\mathrm{C}_{1}}+\frac{1}{\mathrm{C}_{2}}+\frac{1}{\mathrm{C}_{3}}\right){/tex} or {tex}\frac{1}{C}=\frac{1}{C_{1}}+\frac{1}{C_{2}}+\frac{1}{C_{3}}{/tex} Thus, the reciprocal of the resultant (equivalent) capacitance of the series combination of a number of capacitors is equal to the sum of the reciprocals of the capacitances of individual capacitors. Hence, {tex}C=\frac{C_{1} C_{2} C_{3}}{C_{1} C_{2}+C_{2} C_{3}+C_{3} C_{1}}{/tex}
solution: The given figure shows the method of connecting three capacitors of capacitances C 1 , C 2 and C 3 in series. When charge +q is given to plate A of the capacitor C 1 it induces charge -q on left face and +q on right face of the plate B. Thus, plate C of the capacitor C 2 also gets charge +q on it. Then, it induces charge -q on left face of plate D and charge +q on plate E of the capacitor C 3 . The charge +q on plate E induces charge -q on left face of plate F, while the charge +q induced on its right face flows to earth. Thus, the three capacitors get charged with same amount of charge +q, when charge +q is given to the first capacitor. Let V 1 , V 2 and V 3 be potential differences across the plates of the three capacitors. If V is the potential difference across the series combination, then V = V 1 + V 2 + V 3 From the definition of capacitance, {tex}\mathrm{V}_{1}=\frac{q}{\mathrm{C}_{1}} ; \mathrm{V}_{2}=\frac{q}{\mathrm{C}_{2}} \text { and } \mathrm{V}_{3}=\frac{q}{\mathrm{C}_{3}}{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\mathrm{V}=\frac{q}{\mathrm{C}_{1}}+\frac{q}{\mathrm{C}_{2}}+\frac{q}{\mathrm{C}_{3}}=q\left[\frac{1}{\mathrm{C}_{1}}+\frac{1}{\mathrm{C}_{2}}+\frac{1}{\mathrm{C}_{3}}\right]{/tex} .....(i) Let C be the equivalent capacitance of the series combination. Therefore, when a potential difference V is applied across a capacitor of capacitance C, it will also store the same amount of charge. Hence, {tex}\mathrm{V}=\frac{q}{\mathrm{C}}{/tex} .....(ii) From the equations (i) and (ii), we have {tex}\frac{q}{\mathrm{C}}=q\left(\frac{1}{\mathrm{C}_{1}}+\frac{1}{\mathrm{C}_{2}}+\frac{1}{\mathrm{C}_{3}}\right){/tex} or {tex}\frac{1}{C}=\frac{1}{C_{1}}+\frac{1}{C_{2}}+\frac{1}{C_{3}}{/tex} Thus, the reciprocal of the resultant (equivalent) capacitance of the series combination of a number of capacitors is equal to the sum of the reciprocals of the capacitances of individual capacitors. Hence, {tex}C=\frac{C_{1} C_{2} C_{3}}{C_{1} C_{2}+C_{2} C_{3}+C_{3} C_{1}}{/tex}
solution_html:
The given figure shows the method of connecting three capacitors of capacitances C1, C2 and C3 in series.
When charge +q is given to plate A of the capacitor C1 it induces charge -q on left face and +q on right face of the plate B. Thus, plate C of the capacitor C2 also gets charge +q on it. Then, it induces charge -q on left face of plate D and charge +q on plate E of the capacitor C3. The charge +q on plate E induces charge -q on left face of plate F, while the charge +q induced on its right face flows to earth. Thus, the three capacitors get charged with same amount of charge +q, when charge +q is given to the first capacitor.
Let V1, V2 and V3 be potential differences across the plates of the three capacitors. If V is the potential difference across the series combination, then
V = V1 + V2 + V3
From the definition of capacitance,
{tex}\mathrm{V}_{1}=\frac{q}{\mathrm{C}_{1}} ; \mathrm{V}_{2}=\frac{q}{\mathrm{C}_{2}} \text { and } \mathrm{V}_{3}=\frac{q}{\mathrm{C}_{3}}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\mathrm{V}=\frac{q}{\mathrm{C}_{1}}+\frac{q}{\mathrm{C}_{2}}+\frac{q}{\mathrm{C}_{3}}=q\left[\frac{1}{\mathrm{C}_{1}}+\frac{1}{\mathrm{C}_{2}}+\frac{1}{\mathrm{C}_{3}}\right]{/tex} .....(i)
Let C be the equivalent capacitance of the series combination. Therefore, when a potential difference V is applied across a capacitor of capacitance C, it will also store the same amount of charge.
Hence, {tex}\mathrm{V}=\frac{q}{\mathrm{C}}{/tex} .....(ii)
From the equations (i) and (ii), we have
{tex}\frac{q}{\mathrm{C}}=q\left(\frac{1}{\mathrm{C}_{1}}+\frac{1}{\mathrm{C}_{2}}+\frac{1}{\mathrm{C}_{3}}\right){/tex}
or {tex}\frac{1}{C}=\frac{1}{C_{1}}+\frac{1}{C_{2}}+\frac{1}{C_{3}}{/tex}
Thus, the reciprocal of the resultant (equivalent) capacitance of the series combination of a number of capacitors is equal to the sum of the reciprocals of the capacitances of individual capacitors.
Hence, {tex}C=\frac{C_{1} C_{2} C_{3}}{C_{1} C_{2}+C_{2} C_{3}+C_{3} C_{1}}{/tex}

question_text: An uncharged capacitor with a solid dielectric is connected to a similar air capacitor charged to a potential of V 0 . If the common potential after sharing of charges becomes V, then the dielectric constant of the dielectric must be:

options.explanation: Common potential {tex}V=\frac{C_{1} V_{1}+C_{2} V_{2}}{C_{1}+C_{2}}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{0+C V_{0}}{\kappa C+C}=\frac{C V_{0}}{C(1+\kappa)}=\frac{V_{0}}{1+\kappa}{/tex}

explanation: Common potential {tex}V=\frac{C_{1} V_{1}+C_{2} V_{2}}{C_{1}+C_{2}}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{0+C V_{0}}{\kappa C+C}=\frac{C V_{0}}{C(1+\kappa)}=\frac{V_{0}}{1+\kappa}{/tex}

A network of four capacitors each of 12 {tex}\mu{/tex}F. Capacitance is connected to a 500 V supply as shown in Fig. Determine:

question_text: A network of four capacitors each of 12 {tex}\mu{/tex} F. Capacitance is connected to a 500 V supply as shown in Fig. Determine: equivalent capacitance of the network, and charge on each capacitor

question_html: Define the term potential energy for charge q at a distance r in an external field.
question_text: Define the term potential energy for charge q at a distance r in an external field.
explanation: The electrostatic potential energy at any point lying at a distance r from a source charge q due to a test charge Q is equal to the amount of work done in moving the positive test charge q from infinity to that point without any acceleration against electrostatic force. The expression for the electrostatic potential energy for the source charge q due to a test charge Q placed at a distance r from it, U = {tex}\frac {Qq}{4 {tex}\pi{/tex} \epsilon_0 r}{/tex}
solution: The electrostatic potential energy at any point lying at a distance r from a source charge q due to a test charge Q is equal to the amount of work done in moving the positive test charge q from infinity to that point without any acceleration against electrostatic force. The expression for the electrostatic potential energy for the source charge q due to a test charge Q placed at a distance r from it, U = {tex}\frac {Qq}{4 {tex}\pi{/tex} \epsilon_0 r}{/tex}
solution_html:
The electrostatic potential energy at any point lying at a distance r from a source charge q due to a test charge Q is equal to the amount of work done in moving the positive test charge q from infinity to that point without any acceleration against electrostatic force.

The expression for the electrostatic potential energy for the source charge q due to a test charge Q placed at a distance r from it, U = {tex}\frac {Qq}{4{tex}\pi{/tex} \epsilon_0 r}{/tex}

question_html: What is the electric potential due to an electric dipole at an equatorial point?
question_text: What is the electric potential due to an electric dipole at an equatorial point?
explanation: The distance of the equatorial point from both the charges of an electric dipole is same. Electrostatic potential, V = {tex}\frac {Q}{4 {tex}\pi{/tex} \epsilon_0 r}{/tex} . Now for the dipole V = V 1 + V 2 = {tex}\frac {+Q}{4 {tex}\pi{/tex} \epsilon_0r}+\frac{-Q}{4 {tex}\pi{/tex} \epsilon _0r}{/tex} = Zero, as potential on equatorial point due to charges of electric dipole, is equal in magnitude but opposite in nature and hence, their resultant is zero.
solution: The distance of the equatorial point from both the charges of an electric dipole is same. Electrostatic potential, V = {tex}\frac {Q}{4 {tex}\pi{/tex} \epsilon_0 r}{/tex} . Now for the dipole V = V 1 + V 2 = {tex}\frac {+Q}{4 {tex}\pi{/tex} \epsilon_0r}+\frac{-Q}{4 {tex}\pi{/tex} \epsilon _0r}{/tex} = Zero, as potential on equatorial point due to charges of electric dipole, is equal in magnitude but opposite in nature and hence, their resultant is zero.
solution_html:
The distance of the equatorial point from both the charges of an electric dipole is same.
Electrostatic potential, V = {tex}\frac {Q}{4{tex}\pi{/tex} \epsilon_0 r}{/tex}.
Now for the dipole V = V1 + V2 = {tex}\frac {+Q}{4{tex}\pi{/tex} \epsilon_0r}+\frac{-Q}{4{tex}\pi{/tex} \epsilon _0r}{/tex}= Zero, as potential on equatorial point due to charges of electric dipole, is equal in magnitude but opposite in nature and hence, their resultant is zero.

question_html: What is the work done in moving a test charge q through a distance of 1 cm along the equatorial axis of an electric dipole?
question_text: What is the work done in moving a test charge q through a distance of 1 cm along the equatorial axis of an electric dipole?
explanation: The electric potential at any point on equatorial line due to an electric dipole is equal to zero as every point lies at the same distances from both the charges. No work is done in moving a test charge by 1 cm along the equatorial line, as given below. {tex}W = q \Delta V = q \left( V _ { B } - V _ { A } \right){/tex} {tex}\because \; V _ { B } = V _ { A } = 0 \Rightarrow W = 0{/tex}
solution: The electric potential at any point on equatorial line due to an electric dipole is equal to zero as every point lies at the same distances from both the charges. No work is done in moving a test charge by 1 cm along the equatorial line, as given below. {tex}W = q \Delta V = q \left( V _ { B } - V _ { A } \right){/tex} {tex}\because \; V _ { B } = V _ { A } = 0 \Rightarrow W = 0{/tex}
solution_html:
The electric potential at any point on equatorial line due to an electric dipole is equal to zero as every point lies at the same distances from both the charges. No work is done in moving a test charge by 1 cm along the equatorial line, as given below.
{tex}W = q \Delta V = q \left( V _ { B } - V _ { A } \right){/tex}
{tex}\because \; V _ { B } = V _ { A } = 0 \Rightarrow W = 0{/tex}

question_html: Draw three equipotential surfaces corresponding to a field that uniformly increases in magnitude but remains constant along Z-direction. How are these surfaces different from that of a constant electric field along Z-direction?
question_text: Draw three equipotential surfaces corresponding to a field that uniformly increases in magnitude but remains constant along Z-direction. How are these surfaces different from that of a constant electric field along Z-direction?
explanation: Electric field is always directed from higher to lower potential and perpendicular to the equipotential surfaces. The figure is shown as below : We know that the electric field direction remains always normal at any point on an equipotential plane surface. Now, here in case of constant electric field along Z-direction, the perpendicular distance between equipotential surfaces remains same, whereas for field of increasing magnitude, equipotential surfaces get closer as we go away from the origin.(As we know that, more closer the electric field lines, more will be the electric field intensity) In both cases, surfaces will be planes parallel to XY-plane.
solution: Electric field is always directed from higher to lower potential and perpendicular to the equipotential surfaces. The figure is shown as below : We know that the electric field direction remains always normal at any point on an equipotential plane surface. Now, here in case of constant electric field along Z-direction, the perpendicular distance between equipotential surfaces remains same, whereas for field of increasing magnitude, equipotential surfaces get closer as we go away from the origin.(As we know that, more closer the electric field lines, more will be the electric field intensity) In both cases, surfaces will be planes parallel to XY-plane.
solution_html:
Electric field is always directed from higher to lower potential and perpendicular to the equipotential surfaces.
The figure is shown as below :

We know that the electric field direction remains always normal at any point on an equipotential plane surface. Now, here in case of constant electric field along Z-direction, the perpendicular distance between equipotential surfaces remains same, whereas for field of increasing magnitude, equipotential surfaces get closer as we go away from the origin.(As we know that, more closer the electric field lines, more will be the electric field intensity)
In both cases, surfaces will be planes parallel to XY-plane.

question_html:
1. Can two equipotential surfaces intersect each other? Give reasons.
  2. Two charges -q and +q are located at points A (0, 0, -a) and B(0, 0, +a) respectively. How much work is done in moving a test charge from point P(7, 0, 0) to Q(-3, 0, 0)?
question_text: Can two equipotential surfaces intersect each other? Give reasons. Two charges -q and +q are located at points A (0, 0, -a) and B(0, 0, +a) respectively. How much work is done in moving a test charge from point P(7, 0, 0) to Q(-3, 0, 0)?
explanation: No, two equipotential surfaces cannot intersect each other because two normals can be drawn at intersecting point on two surfaces which give two directions of same E at the same point which is impossible. Every point on X-axis is on equatorial line of electric dipole i.e. on the perpendicular bisector of the dipole(system of two unlike charges). Potential on it is equal to zero since each and every point on the equatorial line are at same distances from both the charges. {tex}\therefore{/tex} No work is done in moving a test charge. {tex}W = q \; \Delta V = q \times 0 = 0. \quad [ \because \Delta V = 0 ]{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} Work done in moving a charge on an equipotential surface is zero.
solution: No, two equipotential surfaces cannot intersect each other because two normals can be drawn at intersecting point on two surfaces which give two directions of same E at the same point which is impossible. Every point on X-axis is on equatorial line of electric dipole i.e. on the perpendicular bisector of the dipole(system of two unlike charges). Potential on it is equal to zero since each and every point on the equatorial line are at same distances from both the charges. {tex}\therefore{/tex} No work is done in moving a test charge. {tex}W = q \; \Delta V = q \times 0 = 0. \quad [ \because \Delta V = 0 ]{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} Work done in moving a charge on an equipotential surface is zero.
solution_html:
1. No, two equipotential surfaces cannot intersect each other because two normals can be drawn at intersecting point on two surfaces which give two directions of same E at the same point which is impossible.
  2. Every point on X-axis is on equatorial line of electric dipole i.e. on the perpendicular bisector of the dipole(system of two unlike charges). Potential on it is equal to zero since each and every point on the equatorial line are at same distances from both the charges.
    {tex}\therefore{/tex} No work is done in moving a test charge.
    {tex}W = q \; \Delta V = q \times 0 = 0. \quad [ \because \Delta V = 0 ]{/tex}

    {tex}\therefore{/tex} Work done in moving a charge on an equipotential surface is zero.
