question_html:
Three identical capacitors C1, C2 and C3 of capacitances 6 µF each are connected to a 12 V battery as shown below:

Find

  1. the charge on each capacitor
  2. the equivalent capacitance of the network
  3. the energy stored in the network of capacitors.
question_text: Three identical capacitors C 1 , C 2 and C 3 of capacitances 6 µF each are connected to a 12 V battery as shown below: Find the charge on each capacitor the equivalent capacitance of the network the energy stored in the network of capacitors.
explanation: The equivalent capacitance of C 1 and C 2 connected in series is C' and calculated by, {tex}\frac { 1 } { C ^ { \prime } } = \frac { 1 } { C _ { 1 } } + \frac { 1 } { C _ { 2 } } \Rightarrow C ^ { \prime } = \frac { 6 } { 2 } = 3 \mu F{/tex} Charge on this combination, q' = C'V = (3µF) × 12 = 36 µC Charge on each capacitor of C 1 and C 2 is 36 µC (since charge remains same in series combination) Now, charge on C 3 , q 3 = C 3 V = (6 µF) {tex}\times{/tex} 12 = 72µC Equivalent capacitance of whole network, {tex}C _ { \mathrm { eq } } = \frac { C _ { 1 } C _ { 2 } } { C _ { 1 } + C _ { 2 } } + C _ { 3 }{/tex} {tex}= \frac { 6 \times 6 } { 6 + 6 } + 6 = 3 + 6 = 9 \mu F{/tex} C eq = 9 µF Energy stored in the network of capacitors U = U 1 + U 2 + U 3 = {tex}\frac { q_1 ^ { 2 } } { 2 C _ { 1 } } + \frac { q_2 ^ { 2 } } { 2 C _ { 2 } } + \frac { q_3 ^ { 2 } } { 2 C _ { 3 } }{/tex} With C 1 = C 2 = C 3 = 6 µF {tex}U = \frac { 1 } { 12 } \left[ q_1 ^ { 2 } + q_2 ^ { 2 } + q_3 ^ { 2 } \right]{/tex} {tex}= \frac { 1 } { 12 } [ 36^2 + 36^2 + 72^2] {/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} U = 648 µJ
solution: The equivalent capacitance of C 1 and C 2 connected in series is C' and calculated by, {tex}\frac { 1 } { C ^ { \prime } } = \frac { 1 } { C _ { 1 } } + \frac { 1 } { C _ { 2 } } \Rightarrow C ^ { \prime } = \frac { 6 } { 2 } = 3 \mu F{/tex} Charge on this combination, q' = C'V = (3µF) × 12 = 36 µC Charge on each capacitor of C 1 and C 2 is 36 µC (since charge remains same in series combination) Now, charge on C 3 , q 3 = C 3 V = (6 µF) {tex}\times{/tex} 12 = 72µC Equivalent capacitance of whole network, {tex}C _ { \mathrm { eq } } = \frac { C _ { 1 } C _ { 2 } } { C _ { 1 } + C _ { 2 } } + C _ { 3 }{/tex} {tex}= \frac { 6 \times 6 } { 6 + 6 } + 6 = 3 + 6 = 9 \mu F{/tex} C eq = 9 µF Energy stored in the network of capacitors U = U 1 + U 2 + U 3 = {tex}\frac { q_1 ^ { 2 } } { 2 C _ { 1 } } + \frac { q_2 ^ { 2 } } { 2 C _ { 2 } } + \frac { q_3 ^ { 2 } } { 2 C _ { 3 } }{/tex} With C 1 = C 2 = C 3 = 6 µF {tex}U = \frac { 1 } { 12 } \left[ q_1 ^ { 2 } + q_2 ^ { 2 } + q_3 ^ { 2 } \right]{/tex} {tex}= \frac { 1 } { 12 } [ 36^2 + 36^2 + 72^2] {/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} U = 648 µJ
solution_html:
1. The equivalent capacitance of C1 and C2 connected in series is C' and calculated by,
    {tex}\frac { 1 } { C ^ { \prime } } = \frac { 1 } { C _ { 1 } } + \frac { 1 } { C _ { 2 } } \Rightarrow C ^ { \prime } = \frac { 6 } { 2 } = 3 \mu F{/tex}
    Charge on this combination, q' = C'V = (3µF) × 12 = 36 µC
    Charge on each capacitor of C1 and C2 is 36 µC (since charge remains same in series combination)
    Now, charge on C3,
    q3 = C3V = (6 µF) {tex}\times{/tex} 12 = 72µC
  2. Equivalent capacitance of whole network,
    {tex}C _ { \mathrm { eq } } = \frac { C _ { 1 } C _ { 2 } } { C _ { 1 } + C _ { 2 } } + C _ { 3 }{/tex}
    {tex}= \frac { 6 \times 6 } { 6 + 6 } + 6 = 3 + 6 = 9 \mu F{/tex}
    Ceq = 9 µF
  3.  Energy stored in the network of capacitors
    U = U1 + U2 + U3 = {tex}\frac { q_1 ^ { 2 } } { 2 C _ { 1 } } + \frac { q_2 ^ { 2 } } { 2 C _ { 2 } } + \frac { q_3 ^ { 2 } } { 2 C _ { 3 } }{/tex}
    With C1 = C2 = C3 = 6 µF
    {tex}U = \frac { 1 } { 12 } \left[ q_1 ^ { 2 } + q_2 ^ { 2 } + q_3 ^ { 2 } \right]{/tex}
    {tex}= \frac { 1 } { 12 } [ 36^2 + 36^2 + 72^2] {/tex}
    {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} U = 648 µJ

subject: Physics
question_html:
1. Show that in a parallel plate capacitor, if the medium between the plates of a capacitor is filled with an insulating substance of dielectric constant K, its capacitance increases.
  2. Deduce the expression for the energy stored in a capacitor of capacitance C with charge Q.
question_text: Show that in a parallel plate capacitor, if the medium between the plates of a capacitor is filled with an insulating substance of dielectric constant K, its capacitance increases. Deduce the expression for the energy stored in a capacitor of capacitance C with charge Q.
explanation: Electric field in between the two plates of a capacitor, E = {tex}\frac {\sigma}{ \epsilon_0}{/tex} (when there is air or vacuum) and E = {tex}\frac {\sigma }{K \epsilon_0}{/tex} (when there is dielectric between the plates). Again for a parallel plate capacitor, capacitance C = q/V = {tex}\frac {\sigma A}{E ×d}{/tex} The capacitance (C') becomes K times of original capacitance of the capacitor as {tex}C = \frac { \varepsilon _ { 0 } A } { d } \;and\; C ^ { \prime } = \frac { K \varepsilon _ { 0 } A } { d }{/tex} The work done in charging the capacitor is stored as its electrical potential energy. Hence considering a capacitor of capacitance C, initially whose two plates are uncharged, let Q and -Q are charges on the two plates and produces a uniform electric field, {tex}E = \frac { \sigma } { \varepsilon _ { 0 } }{/tex} between the plates and a potential difference {tex}V = \frac { q } { C }{/tex} ...(i) If the capacitor is charged by a small charge dq at any instant then work done, dW = dq.V ...(ii) From Eqs. (i) and (ii), we get {tex}d W = d q \left( \frac { q } { C } \right), \therefore W = \int d W{/tex} This work done is stored as electrical potential U in the capacitor to charge it to maximum value i.e. Q {tex}\therefore U = W = \int\limits_0^Q {\frac{q}{C}} ×dq = \frac{{{Q^2}}}{{2C}}{/tex} {tex}U = \frac { Q ^ { 2 } } {2C } = \frac { 1 } { 2 } C V ^ { 2 } = \frac { 1 } { 2 } Q V{/tex} {tex}[ \because Q = C V ]{/tex}
solution: Electric field in between the two plates of a capacitor, E = {tex}\frac {\sigma}{ \epsilon_0}{/tex} (when there is air or vacuum) and E = {tex}\frac {\sigma }{K \epsilon_0}{/tex} (when there is dielectric between the plates). Again for a parallel plate capacitor, capacitance C = q/V = {tex}\frac {\sigma A}{E ×d}{/tex} The capacitance (C') becomes K times of original capacitance of the capacitor as {tex}C = \frac { \varepsilon _ { 0 } A } { d } \;and\; C ^ { \prime } = \frac { K \varepsilon _ { 0 } A } { d }{/tex} The work done in charging the capacitor is stored as its electrical potential energy. Hence considering a capacitor of capacitance C, initially whose two plates are uncharged, let Q and -Q are charges on the two plates and produces a uniform electric field, {tex}E = \frac { \sigma } { \varepsilon _ { 0 } }{/tex} between the plates and a potential difference {tex}V = \frac { q } { C }{/tex} ...(i) If the capacitor is charged by a small charge dq at any instant then work done, dW = dq.V ...(ii) From Eqs. (i) and (ii), we get {tex}d W = d q \left( \frac { q } { C } \right), \therefore W = \int d W{/tex} This work done is stored as electrical potential U in the capacitor to charge it to maximum value i.e. Q {tex}\therefore U = W = \int\limits_0^Q {\frac{q}{C}} ×dq = \frac{{{Q^2}}}{{2C}}{/tex} {tex}U = \frac { Q ^ { 2 } } {2C } = \frac { 1 } { 2 } C V ^ { 2 } = \frac { 1 } { 2 } Q V{/tex} {tex}[ \because Q = C V ]{/tex}
solution_html:
1. Electric field in between the two plates of a capacitor, E = {tex}\frac {\sigma}{ \epsilon_0}{/tex}(when there is air or vacuum) and E = {tex}\frac {\sigma }{K \epsilon_0}{/tex}(when there is dielectric between the plates). Again for a parallel plate capacitor, capacitance C = q/V = {tex}\frac {\sigma A}{E ×d}{/tex}
    The capacitance (C') becomes K times of original capacitance of the capacitor as {tex}C = \frac { \varepsilon _ { 0 } A } { d } \;and\; C ^ { \prime } = \frac { K \varepsilon _ { 0 } A } { d }{/tex}

  2. The work done in charging the capacitor is stored as its electrical potential energy.
    Hence considering a capacitor of capacitance C, initially whose two plates are uncharged, let Q and -Q are charges on the two plates and produces a uniform electric field, {tex}E = \frac { \sigma } { \varepsilon _ { 0 } }{/tex} between the plates and a potential difference {tex}V = \frac { q } { C }{/tex}...(i)
    If the capacitor is charged by a small charge dq at any instant then
    work done, dW = dq.V ...(ii)
    From Eqs. (i) and (ii), we get
    {tex}d W = d q \left( \frac { q } { C } \right), \therefore W = \int d W{/tex}
    This work done is stored as electrical potential U in the capacitor to charge it to maximum value i.e. Q
    {tex}\therefore U = W = \int\limits_0^Q {\frac{q}{C}} ×dq = \frac{{{Q^2}}}{{2C}}{/tex}
    {tex}U = \frac { Q ^ { 2 } } {2C } = \frac { 1 } { 2 } C V ^ { 2 } = \frac { 1 } { 2 } Q V{/tex} {tex}[ \because Q = C V ]{/tex}

subject: Physics
question_html:
The equivalent capacitance of the combination between points A and B in the given figure is 4 µF.

  1. Calculate the capacitance of the capacitor C.
  2. Calculate the charge on each capacitor if a 12 V battery is connected across terminals A and B.
  3. What will be the potential drop across each capacitor?
question_text: The equivalent capacitance of the combination between points A and B in the given figure is 4 µF. Calculate the capacitance of the capacitor C. Calculate the charge on each capacitor if a 12 V battery is connected across terminals A and B. What will be the potential drop across each capacitor?
explanation: Capacitors of 20 µF and C are connected in series. The equivalent capacitance, {tex}4 = \frac { 20 \times C } { 20 + C }{/tex} (applying the formula of equivalent capacitance in series connection) {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} (20 + C) = 5C {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 4C = 20 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} C = 5µF, Hence the value of C = 5 {tex}\mu F{/tex} Charge on capacitor (equivalent), q = (4µF) {tex}\times{/tex} 12= 48µC. This charge (48 µC) lies on both the capacitors. Potential drop across the capacitor of capacitance 20 µF, {tex}V _ { 1 } = \frac { q } { C _ { 1 } } = \frac { 48 \mu C } { 20 \mu F } = 2.4 V{/tex} Potential drop across 5 µF capacitor, {tex}V _ { 2 } = \frac { q } { C _ { 2 } } = \frac { 48 \mu C } { 5 \mu F } = 9.6 V{/tex}
solution: Capacitors of 20 µF and C are connected in series. The equivalent capacitance, {tex}4 = \frac { 20 \times C } { 20 + C }{/tex} (applying the formula of equivalent capacitance in series connection) {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} (20 + C) = 5C {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 4C = 20 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} C = 5µF, Hence the value of C = 5 {tex}\mu F{/tex} Charge on capacitor (equivalent), q = (4µF) {tex}\times{/tex} 12= 48µC. This charge (48 µC) lies on both the capacitors. Potential drop across the capacitor of capacitance 20 µF, {tex}V _ { 1 } = \frac { q } { C _ { 1 } } = \frac { 48 \mu C } { 20 \mu F } = 2.4 V{/tex} Potential drop across 5 µF capacitor, {tex}V _ { 2 } = \frac { q } { C _ { 2 } } = \frac { 48 \mu C } { 5 \mu F } = 9.6 V{/tex}
solution_html:
1. Capacitors of 20 µF and C are connected in series. The equivalent capacitance,
    {tex}4 = \frac { 20 \times C } { 20 + C }{/tex} (applying the formula of equivalent capacitance in series connection)
    {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} (20 + C) = 5C {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 4C = 20
    {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} C = 5µF, Hence the value of C = 5 {tex}\mu F{/tex}
  2. Charge on capacitor (equivalent), q = (4µF) {tex}\times{/tex} 12= 48µC. This charge (48 µC) lies on both the capacitors.
  3. Potential drop across the capacitor of capacitance 20 µF,
    {tex}V _ { 1 } = \frac { q } { C _ { 1 } } = \frac { 48 \mu C } { 20 \mu F } = 2.4 V{/tex}
    Potential drop across 5 µF capacitor,
    {tex}V _ { 2 } = \frac { q } { C _ { 2 } } = \frac { 48 \mu C } { 5 \mu F } = 9.6 V{/tex}

subject: Physics
question_html:
Two parallel plate capacitors X and Y have the same area of the plates and same separation between them. X has air between the plates while Y contains a dielectric medium of {tex}\varepsilon_r=4 {/tex}.

  1. Calculate the capacitance of each capacitor if equivalent of the combination is 4 µF.
  2. Calculate the potential difference between the plates of X and Y.
  3. What is the ratio of electrostatic energy stored in X and Y?
question_text: Two parallel plate capacitors X and Y have the same area of the plates and same separation between them. X has air between the plates while Y contains a dielectric medium of {tex}\varepsilon_r=4 {/tex} . Calculate the capacitance of each capacitor if equivalent of the combination is 4 µF. Calculate the potential difference between the plates of X and Y. What is the ratio of electrostatic energy stored in X and Y?
explanation: A dielectric medium of dielectric constant K is inserted between the plates of a condenser in place of air, its capacity becomes K times of the original one. Here the capacitance of two capacitors are {tex}C _ { X } = \frac { \varepsilon _ { 0 } A } { d } = C{/tex} {tex}C _ { Y } = \frac { 4 \varepsilon _ { 0 } A } { d } = 4 C{/tex} According to the problem, C eq = 4µF Now, {tex}C _ { \mathrm { eq } } = \frac { C _ { X } C _ { Y } } { C _ { X } + C _ { Y } }{/tex} {tex}\therefore 4 \mu F = \frac { C \times 4 C } { C + 4 C } = \frac { 4 C } { 5 }{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} C = 5µF Hence, C x = C = 5µF and C Y = 4C = 4 {tex}\times{/tex} 5µF = 20µF Charge across the combination, q = C eq V = (4µF) {tex}\times{/tex} 12 = 48µC Same charge, i.e. 48 µC lies on each capacitor being in series combination i.e. both X and Y capacitors will get the charge 48 {tex}\mu C{/tex} Now potential difference across the capacitor X having capacitance C x , {tex}V _ { 1 } = \frac { q } { C _ { X } } = \frac { 48 \mu C } { 5 \mu F } = 9.6 V{/tex} and potential difference across the capacitor Y having capacitance C Y , {tex}V _ { 2 } = \frac { q } { C _ { Y } } = \frac { 48 \mu C } { 20 \mu F } = 2.4 V{/tex} Energy stored in capacitor X with capacitance C X , U x {tex}= \frac { q ^ { 2 } } { 2 C _ { X } }{/tex} Energy stored in capacitor Y with capacitance C Y , U Y = {tex}\frac { q ^ { 2 } } { 2 C _ { Y } }{/tex} [ Since, in series, the charge will remain same] {tex}\therefore \quad \frac { U _ { X } } { U _ { Y} } = \frac { q ^ { 2 } } { 2 C _ { X } } \times \frac { 2 C _ { Y} } { q ^ { 2 } } = \frac { C _ { Y } } { C _ { X} } = \frac { 20 \mu F } { 5 \mu F } = 4{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow U _ { X } : U _ { Y } = 4 : 1{/tex}
solution: A dielectric medium of dielectric constant K is inserted between the plates of a condenser in place of air, its capacity becomes K times of the original one. Here the capacitance of two capacitors are {tex}C _ { X } = \frac { \varepsilon _ { 0 } A } { d } = C{/tex} {tex}C _ { Y } = \frac { 4 \varepsilon _ { 0 } A } { d } = 4 C{/tex} According to the problem, C eq = 4µF Now, {tex}C _ { \mathrm { eq } } = \frac { C _ { X } C _ { Y } } { C _ { X } + C _ { Y } }{/tex} {tex}\therefore 4 \mu F = \frac { C \times 4 C } { C + 4 C } = \frac { 4 C } { 5 }{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} C = 5µF Hence, C x = C = 5µF and C Y = 4C = 4 {tex}\times{/tex} 5µF = 20µF Charge across the combination, q = C eq V = (4µF) {tex}\times{/tex} 12 = 48µC Same charge, i.e. 48 µC lies on each capacitor being in series combination i.e. both X and Y capacitors will get the charge 48 {tex}\mu C{/tex} Now potential difference across the capacitor X having capacitance C x , {tex}V _ { 1 } = \frac { q } { C _ { X } } = \frac { 48 \mu C } { 5 \mu F } = 9.6 V{/tex} and potential difference across the capacitor Y having capacitance C Y , {tex}V _ { 2 } = \frac { q } { C _ { Y } } = \frac { 48 \mu C } { 20 \mu F } = 2.4 V{/tex} Energy stored in capacitor X with capacitance C X , U x {tex}= \frac { q ^ { 2 } } { 2 C _ { X } }{/tex} Energy stored in capacitor Y with capacitance C Y , U Y = {tex}\frac { q ^ { 2 } } { 2 C _ { Y } }{/tex} [ Since, in series, the charge will remain same] {tex}\therefore \quad \frac { U _ { X } } { U _ { Y} } = \frac { q ^ { 2 } } { 2 C _ { X } } \times \frac { 2 C _ { Y} } { q ^ { 2 } } = \frac { C _ { Y } } { C _ { X} } = \frac { 20 \mu F } { 5 \mu F } = 4{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow U _ { X } : U _ { Y } = 4 : 1{/tex}
solution_html:
A dielectric medium of dielectric constant K is inserted between the plates of a condenser in place of air, its capacity becomes K times of the original one.
Here the capacitance of two capacitors are
{tex}C _ { X } = \frac { \varepsilon _ { 0 } A } { d } = C{/tex}
{tex}C _ { Y } = \frac { 4 \varepsilon _ { 0 } A } { d } = 4 C{/tex}

  1. According to the problem,
    Ceq = 4µF
    Now, {tex}C _ { \mathrm { eq } } = \frac { C _ { X } C _ { Y } } { C _ { X } + C _ { Y } }{/tex}
    {tex}\therefore 4 \mu F = \frac { C \times 4 C } { C + 4 C } = \frac { 4 C } { 5 }{/tex}
    {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} C = 5µF
    Hence, Cx = C = 5µF
    and CY = 4C = 4 {tex}\times{/tex} 5µF = 20µF
  2. Charge across the combination,
    q = CeqV = (4µF) {tex}\times{/tex} 12 = 48µC
    Same charge, i.e. 48 µC lies on each capacitor being in series combination i.e. both X and Y capacitors will get the charge 48{tex}\mu C{/tex}
    Now potential difference across the capacitor X having capacitance Cx,
    {tex}V _ { 1 } = \frac { q } { C _ { X } } = \frac { 48 \mu C } { 5 \mu F } = 9.6 V{/tex}
    and potential difference across the capacitor Y having capacitance CY,
    {tex}V _ { 2 } = \frac { q } { C _ { Y } } = \frac { 48 \mu C } { 20 \mu F } = 2.4 V{/tex}
  3. Energy stored in capacitor X with capacitance CX, Ux{tex}= \frac { q ^ { 2 } } { 2 C _ { X } }{/tex}
    Energy stored in capacitor Y with capacitance CY, UY = {tex}\frac { q ^ { 2 } } { 2 C _ { Y } }{/tex}
    [ Since, in series, the charge will remain same]
    {tex}\therefore \quad \frac { U _ { X } } { U _ { Y} } = \frac { q ^ { 2 } } { 2 C _ { X } } \times \frac { 2 C _ { Y} } { q ^ { 2 } } = \frac { C _ { Y } } { C _ { X} } = \frac { 20 \mu F } { 5 \mu F } = 4{/tex}
    {tex}\Rightarrow U _ { X } : U _ { Y } = 4 : 1{/tex}

subject: Physics
question_html: A system of capacitors connected as shown in the figure has a total energy of 160 mJ stored in it. Obtain the value of the equivalent capacitance of this system and the value of Z.
question_text: A system of capacitors connected as shown in the figure has a total energy of 160 mJ stored in it. Obtain the value of the equivalent capacitance of this system and the value of Z.
explanation: Given, total energy = 160mJ Let equivalent capacitance of the combination of the given capacitors in the above circuit is C. According to the question, {tex}\frac { 1 } { 2 } C V ^ { 2 } = 160 \times 10 ^ { - 3 } \mathrm { J }{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \frac { 1 } { 2 } \times C \times ( 200 ) ^ { 2 } = 160 \times 10 ^ { - 3 }{/tex} Equivalent capacitance, C = 8 µF....(i) Equivalent capacitance of 7 µF and 3µF capacitors connected in parallel, C" = 7 + 3 = 10µF Now C" =10µF with another 10 µF and 15µF capacitors are in series combination. Therefore, their equivalent capacitance(C') {tex}\frac { 1 } { C ^ { \prime } } = \frac { 1 } { 10 } + \frac { 1 } { 10 } + \frac { 1 } { 15 } = \frac { 3 + 3 + 2 } { 30 } = \frac { 8 } { 30 } \Rightarrow C ^ { \prime } = \frac { 15 } { 4 } \mu F{/tex} But, C' and Z are in parallel combination, therefore net equivalent capacitance of the whole circuit, {tex}\frac { 15 } { 4 } + Z = C{/tex} [ From Eq(i)] {tex}\Rightarrow \frac { 15 } { 4 } + Z = 8 \Rightarrow Z = 8 - \frac { 15 } { 4 } = \frac { 32 - 15 } { 4 }{/tex} {tex}\therefore Z = \frac { 17 } { 4 } \mu F \Rightarrow Z = 4.25 \mu F{/tex} This is the required value of Z of the given circuit. And equivalent capacitance of the system is 8 {tex}\mu F.{/tex}
solution: Given, total energy = 160mJ Let equivalent capacitance of the combination of the given capacitors in the above circuit is C. According to the question, {tex}\frac { 1 } { 2 } C V ^ { 2 } = 160 \times 10 ^ { - 3 } \mathrm { J }{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \frac { 1 } { 2 } \times C \times ( 200 ) ^ { 2 } = 160 \times 10 ^ { - 3 }{/tex} Equivalent capacitance, C = 8 µF....(i) Equivalent capacitance of 7 µF and 3µF capacitors connected in parallel, C" = 7 + 3 = 10µF Now C" =10µF with another 10 µF and 15µF capacitors are in series combination. Therefore, their equivalent capacitance(C') {tex}\frac { 1 } { C ^ { \prime } } = \frac { 1 } { 10 } + \frac { 1 } { 10 } + \frac { 1 } { 15 } = \frac { 3 + 3 + 2 } { 30 } = \frac { 8 } { 30 } \Rightarrow C ^ { \prime } = \frac { 15 } { 4 } \mu F{/tex} But, C' and Z are in parallel combination, therefore net equivalent capacitance of the whole circuit, {tex}\frac { 15 } { 4 } + Z = C{/tex} [ From Eq(i)] {tex}\Rightarrow \frac { 15 } { 4 } + Z = 8 \Rightarrow Z = 8 - \frac { 15 } { 4 } = \frac { 32 - 15 } { 4 }{/tex} {tex}\therefore Z = \frac { 17 } { 4 } \mu F \Rightarrow Z = 4.25 \mu F{/tex} This is the required value of Z of the given circuit. And equivalent capacitance of the system is 8 {tex}\mu F.{/tex}
solution_html:
Given, total energy = 160mJ
Let equivalent capacitance of the combination of the given capacitors in the above circuit is C.
According to the question, {tex}\frac { 1 } { 2 } C V ^ { 2 } = 160 \times 10 ^ { - 3 } \mathrm { J }{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad \frac { 1 } { 2 } \times C \times ( 200 ) ^ { 2 } = 160 \times 10 ^ { - 3 }{/tex}
Equivalent capacitance, C = 8 µF....(i)
Equivalent capacitance of 7 µF and 3µF capacitors connected in parallel, C" = 7 + 3 = 10µF
Now C" =10µF with another 10 µF and 15µF capacitors are in series combination.
Therefore, their equivalent capacitance(C')
{tex}\frac { 1 } { C ^ { \prime } } = \frac { 1 } { 10 } + \frac { 1 } { 10 } + \frac { 1 } { 15 } = \frac { 3 + 3 + 2 } { 30 } = \frac { 8 } { 30 } \Rightarrow C ^ { \prime } = \frac { 15 } { 4 } \mu F{/tex}
But, C' and Z are in parallel combination, therefore net equivalent capacitance of the whole circuit,
{tex}\frac { 15 } { 4 } + Z = C{/tex} [ From Eq(i)]
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac { 15 } { 4 } + Z = 8 \Rightarrow Z = 8 - \frac { 15 } { 4 } = \frac { 32 - 15 } { 4 }{/tex}
{tex}\therefore Z = \frac { 17 } { 4 } \mu F \Rightarrow Z = 4.25 \mu F{/tex}
This is the required value of Z of the given circuit. And equivalent capacitance of the system is 8 {tex}\mu F.{/tex}

subject: Physics
question_html: A parallel plate capacitor is charged by a battery. After sometime, the battery is disconnected and a dielectric slab of dielectric constant K is inserted between the plates. How would (i) the electric field between the plates? (ii) the energy stored in the capacitor be affected? Justify your answer.
question_text: A parallel plate capacitor is charged by a battery. After sometime, the battery is disconnected and a dielectric slab of dielectric constant K is inserted between the plates. How would (i) the electric field between the plates? (ii) the energy stored in the capacitor be affected? Justify your answer.
explanation: Charge on capacitor remains same. i.e, CV = C'V' (where V and V ​​​​​ ​​​​​​' are the potential between the plates of the capacitor before and after insertion of the dielectric medium) {tex}\Rightarrow \quad C V = \left( KC \right) V ^ { \prime } \Rightarrow V ^ { \prime } = \frac {V}{K}{/tex} Now electric field between the plates {tex}E ^ { \prime } = \frac { V ^ { \prime } } { d ^ { \prime } } = \frac { V } { K d }{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow E ^ { \prime } = \frac {E}{K}{/tex} Electric field between the two plates becomes 1/K times the previous original value. Energy stored initially, {tex}U = \frac { q ^ { 2 } } { 2 C }{/tex} Energy stored later, {tex}\therefore \quad U ^ { \prime } = \frac { q ^ { 2 } } { 2 K C }{/tex} [ {tex}\because C ^ { \prime } = K C{/tex} ] where, K =dielectric constant of the medium {tex}\Rightarrow U ^ { \prime } = \frac { 1 } { K } \left( \frac { q ^ { 2 } } { 2 C } \right) \Rightarrow U ^ { \prime } = \frac { 1 } { K } ( U ) \Rightarrow U ^ { \prime } = \frac { 1 } { K } \times U{/tex} That means the stored energy in the capacitor decreases and becomes {tex}\frac1K{/tex} times of previous original stored energy value.
solution: Charge on capacitor remains same. i.e, CV = C'V' (where V and V ​​​​​ ​​​​​​' are the potential between the plates of the capacitor before and after insertion of the dielectric medium) {tex}\Rightarrow \quad C V = \left( KC \right) V ^ { \prime } \Rightarrow V ^ { \prime } = \frac {V}{K}{/tex} Now electric field between the plates {tex}E ^ { \prime } = \frac { V ^ { \prime } } { d ^ { \prime } } = \frac { V } { K d }{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow E ^ { \prime } = \frac {E}{K}{/tex} Electric field between the two plates becomes 1/K times the previous original value. Energy stored initially, {tex}U = \frac { q ^ { 2 } } { 2 C }{/tex} Energy stored later, {tex}\therefore \quad U ^ { \prime } = \frac { q ^ { 2 } } { 2 K C }{/tex} [ {tex}\because C ^ { \prime } = K C{/tex} ] where, K =dielectric constant of the medium {tex}\Rightarrow U ^ { \prime } = \frac { 1 } { K } \left( \frac { q ^ { 2 } } { 2 C } \right) \Rightarrow U ^ { \prime } = \frac { 1 } { K } ( U ) \Rightarrow U ^ { \prime } = \frac { 1 } { K } \times U{/tex} That means the stored energy in the capacitor decreases and becomes {tex}\frac1K{/tex} times of previous original stored energy value.
solution_html:
1. Charge on capacitor remains same.
    i.e, CV = C'V' (where V and V​​​​​​​​​​​' are the potential between the plates of the capacitor before and after insertion of the dielectric medium)
    {tex}\Rightarrow \quad C V = \left( KC \right) V ^ { \prime } \Rightarrow V ^ { \prime } = \frac {V}{K}{/tex}
    Now electric field between the plates
    {tex}E ^ { \prime } = \frac { V ^ { \prime } } { d ^ { \prime } } = \frac { V } { K d }{/tex}
    {tex}\Rightarrow E ^ { \prime } = \frac {E}{K}{/tex}
    Electric field between the two plates becomes 1/K times the previous original value.
  2. Energy stored initially,
    {tex}U = \frac { q ^ { 2 } } { 2 C }{/tex}
    Energy stored later,
    {tex}\therefore \quad U ^ { \prime } = \frac { q ^ { 2 } } { 2 K C }{/tex}[{tex}\because C ^ { \prime } = K C{/tex}]
     where, K =dielectric constant of the medium {tex}\Rightarrow U ^ { \prime } = \frac { 1 } { K } \left( \frac { q ^ { 2 } } { 2 C } \right) \Rightarrow U ^ { \prime } = \frac { 1 } { K } ( U ) \Rightarrow U ^ { \prime } = \frac { 1 } { K } \times U{/tex}
    That means the stored energy in the capacitor decreases and becomes {tex}\frac1K{/tex} times of previous original stored energy value.

question_html: A 800 pF capacitor is charged by a 100 V battery. After some time the battery is disconnected. The capacitor is then connected to another 800 pF capacitor. What is the electrostatic energy stored?
question_text: A 800 pF capacitor is charged by a 100 V battery. After some time the battery is disconnected. The capacitor is then connected to another 800 pF capacitor. What is the electrostatic energy stored?
explanation: Here C 1 = C 2 = 800 pF = 8 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -10 F V 1 = 100 V, V 2 = 0 Common potential, {tex}V=\frac{C_{1} V_{1}+C_{2} V_{2}}{C_{1}+C_{2}}=\frac{8 \times 10^{-10} \times 100+0}{8 \times 10^{-10}+8 \times 10^{-10}}{/tex} = 50V {tex}U_{f}=\frac{1}{2}\left(C_{1}+C_{2}\right) V^{2}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} (8 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -10 +8 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -10 ) {tex}\times{/tex} (50) 2 = 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -6 J
solution: Here C 1 = C 2 = 800 pF = 8 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -10 F V 1 = 100 V, V 2 = 0 Common potential, {tex}V=\frac{C_{1} V_{1}+C_{2} V_{2}}{C_{1}+C_{2}}=\frac{8 \times 10^{-10} \times 100+0}{8 \times 10^{-10}+8 \times 10^{-10}}{/tex} = 50V {tex}U_{f}=\frac{1}{2}\left(C_{1}+C_{2}\right) V^{2}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} (8 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -10 +8 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -10 ) {tex}\times{/tex} (50) 2 = 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -6 J
solution_html:
Here C1 = C2 = 800 pF = 8 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-10 F
V1 = 100 V, V2 = 0
Common potential,
{tex}V=\frac{C_{1} V_{1}+C_{2} V_{2}}{C_{1}+C_{2}}=\frac{8 \times 10^{-10} \times 100+0}{8 \times 10^{-10}+8 \times 10^{-10}}{/tex} = 50V
{tex}U_{f}=\frac{1}{2}\left(C_{1}+C_{2}\right) V^{2}{/tex}
= {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex}(8 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-10+8 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-10) {tex}\times{/tex} (50)2 = 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-6J

question_html: A conducting sphere of radius R carrying charge Q lies inside an uncharged conducting shell of radius 2R. If they are joined by a metal wire, the amount of heat that will be produced is
question_text: A conducting sphere of radius R carrying charge Q lies inside an uncharged conducting shell of radius 2R. If they are joined by a metal wire, the amount of heat that will be produced is

options.explanation: Capacitance of first capacitor, {tex}C_{1}=4 \pi \varepsilon_{0} R{/tex} Capacitance of second capacitor, {tex}C_{2}=4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}(2 R){/tex} Initial energy, {tex}U_{i}=\frac{Q^{2}}{2 C_{1}}=\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}} \cdot \frac{Q^{2}}{2 R}{/tex} Final energy, {tex}U_{f}=\frac{Q^{2}}{2 C_{2}}=\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}} \cdot \frac{Q^{2}}{4 R}{/tex} Heat produced =U i - U f {tex}=\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}} \frac{Q^{2}}{2 R}\left[1-\frac{1}{2}\right]=\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}} \cdot \frac{Q^{2}}{4 R}{/tex}

explanation: Capacitance of first capacitor, {tex}C_{1}=4 \pi \varepsilon_{0} R{/tex} Capacitance of second capacitor, {tex}C_{2}=4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}(2 R){/tex} Initial energy, {tex}U_{i}=\frac{Q^{2}}{2 C_{1}}=\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}} \cdot \frac{Q^{2}}{2 R}{/tex} Final energy, {tex}U_{f}=\frac{Q^{2}}{2 C_{2}}=\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}} \cdot \frac{Q^{2}}{4 R}{/tex} Heat produced =U i - U f {tex}=\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}} \frac{Q^{2}}{2 R}\left[1-\frac{1}{2}\right]=\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}} \cdot \frac{Q^{2}}{4 R}{/tex}

subject: Physics
question_html: If the circumference of a sphere is 2 m, then the capacitance of the sphere in water would be
question_text: If the circumference of a sphere is 2 m, then the capacitance of the sphere in water would be

options.explanation: Capacitance is given as C = {tex}\varepsilon_{0} \frac{\mathrm{A}}{\mathrm{d}}{/tex} For a sphere placed in water, the capacitance will be C = {tex}4 \pi \varepsilon \mathrm{R}{/tex} Here, {tex}\varepsilon{/tex} is the permittivity of water. In terms of permittivity of free space and dielectric constant of water, we get C = {tex}4 \pi \varepsilon_{0} \mathrm{kR}{/tex} Where, {tex}\kappa{/tex} (relative permittivity) = {tex}\frac{\varepsilon}{\varepsilon_{o}}{/tex} = 79.1 at 35 o C Circumference, {tex}2\pi R{/tex} = 2 m {tex}\Rightarrow R=\frac{1}{\pi}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \mathrm{C}=4 \pi \varepsilon_{0} \mathrm{k} \frac{1}{\pi}=4 \varepsilon_{0} \mathrm{k}{/tex} {tex}\mathrm{C}=4 \times 8.85 \times 0^{-12} \times 79.1{/tex} C = {tex}2800 \times 10^{-12} \mathrm{~F}{/tex} C = 2800pF Hence, capacitance is 2800 pF.

explanation: Capacitance is given as C = {tex}\varepsilon_{0} \frac{\mathrm{A}}{\mathrm{d}}{/tex} For a sphere placed in water, the capacitance will be C = {tex}4 \pi \varepsilon \mathrm{R}{/tex} Here, {tex}\varepsilon{/tex} is the permittivity of water. In terms of permittivity of free space and dielectric constant of water, we get C = {tex}4 \pi \varepsilon_{0} \mathrm{kR}{/tex} Where, {tex}\kappa{/tex} (relative permittivity) = {tex}\frac{\varepsilon}{\varepsilon_{o}}{/tex} = 79.1 at 35 o C Circumference, {tex}2\pi R{/tex} = 2 m {tex}\Rightarrow R=\frac{1}{\pi}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \mathrm{C}=4 \pi \varepsilon_{0} \mathrm{k} \frac{1}{\pi}=4 \varepsilon_{0} \mathrm{k}{/tex} {tex}\mathrm{C}=4 \times 8.85 \times 0^{-12} \times 79.1{/tex} C = {tex}2800 \times 10^{-12} \mathrm{~F}{/tex} C = 2800pF Hence, capacitance is 2800 pF.

question_html:
Two point charges of +3{tex}\mu{/tex}C and -3{tex}\mu{/tex}C are placed 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-3 m apart from each other. Calculate:

  1. electric field and electric potential at a distance of 0.6 m from the dipole in a broad-side-on position
  2. electric field and electric potential at the same point after rotating the dipole through 90°.
question_text: Two point charges of +3 {tex}\mu{/tex} C and -3 {tex}\mu{/tex} C are placed 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -3 m apart from each other. Calculate: electric field and electric potential at a distance of 0.6 m from the dipole in a broad-side-on position electric field and electric potential at the same point after rotating the dipole through 90°.
explanation: Points P and Q are located on the equatorial line of the electric dipole therefore the net potential of the dipole at an equatorial point is zero. {tex}\therefore{/tex} Work done in moving a test charge q 0 from P to Q W = q 0 (V Q - V P ) = q 0 (0 - 0) = 0.
solution: Points P and Q are located on the equatorial line of the electric dipole therefore the net potential of the dipole at an equatorial point is zero. {tex}\therefore{/tex} Work done in moving a test charge q 0 from P to Q W = q 0 (V Q - V P ) = q 0 (0 - 0) = 0.
solution_html:
1. Points P and Q are located on the equatorial line of the electric dipole therefore the net  potential of the dipole at an equatorial point is zero. 
  2. {tex}\therefore{/tex} Work done in moving a test charge q0 from P to Q

W = q0(VQ - VP) = q0(0 - 0) = 0.

subject: Physics
question_html: Derive an expression for the potential energy of an electric dipole of dipole moment {tex}\vec p{/tex} in an electric field {tex}\vec E{/tex}.
question_text: Derive an expression for the potential energy of an electric dipole of dipole moment {tex}\vec p{/tex} in an electric field {tex}\vec E{/tex} .
explanation: Suppose that an electric dipole having dipole moment p is placed along a direction making an angle {tex}\theta{/tex} with the direction of an external uniform electric field E. Then, torque acting on the dipole is given by {tex}\tau{/tex} = pE sin {tex}\theta{/tex} If the dipole is rotated through an infinitesimally small angle d {tex}\theta{/tex} against the torque acting on it, then small work done dW = {tex}\tau{/tex} d {tex}\theta{/tex} = pE sin {tex}\theta{/tex} d {tex}\theta{/tex} Therefore, work done in rotating the dipole from its orientation making an angle {tex}\theta_1{/tex} with the direction of electric field to {tex}\theta_2{/tex} is given by {tex}\mathrm{W}=\int_{\theta_{1}}^{\theta_{2}} p \mathrm{E} \sin \theta d \theta=p \mathrm{E}|-\cos \theta|_{\theta_{1}}^{\theta_{2}}{/tex} or W = pE (cos {tex}\theta_1{/tex} - cos {tex}\theta_2{/tex} ) This work done is stored in the dipole in the form of its potential energy. Therefore, when an electric dipole is rotated inside the electric field, potential energy stored in the electric dipole and it is given by U = pE (cos {tex}\theta_1{/tex} - cos {tex}\theta_2{/tex} ) ...(i) Let us assume that dipole is initially oriented perpendicular to the electric field and is then brought to the orientation making an angle {tex}\theta{/tex} with the field direction. Then, potential energy of the dipole, when it makes angle {tex}\theta{/tex} with the direction of the electric field can be obtained from equation (i) by setting {tex}\theta_1{/tex} = 90° and {tex}\theta_2{/tex} = {tex}\theta{/tex} . Therefore, U = pE (cos 90 o - cos {tex}\theta{/tex} ) U = -pE cos {tex}\theta{/tex} .....(ii) In vector notation, U = - {tex}\vec{p} . \overrightarrow{\mathrm{E}}{/tex}
solution: Suppose that an electric dipole having dipole moment p is placed along a direction making an angle {tex}\theta{/tex} with the direction of an external uniform electric field E. Then, torque acting on the dipole is given by {tex}\tau{/tex} = pE sin {tex}\theta{/tex} If the dipole is rotated through an infinitesimally small angle d {tex}\theta{/tex} against the torque acting on it, then small work done dW = {tex}\tau{/tex} d {tex}\theta{/tex} = pE sin {tex}\theta{/tex} d {tex}\theta{/tex} Therefore, work done in rotating the dipole from its orientation making an angle {tex}\theta_1{/tex} with the direction of electric field to {tex}\theta_2{/tex} is given by {tex}\mathrm{W}=\int_{\theta_{1}}^{\theta_{2}} p \mathrm{E} \sin \theta d \theta=p \mathrm{E}|-\cos \theta|_{\theta_{1}}^{\theta_{2}}{/tex} or W = pE (cos {tex}\theta_1{/tex} - cos {tex}\theta_2{/tex} ) This work done is stored in the dipole in the form of its potential energy. Therefore, when an electric dipole is rotated inside the electric field, potential energy stored in the electric dipole and it is given by U = pE (cos {tex}\theta_1{/tex} - cos {tex}\theta_2{/tex} ) ...(i) Let us assume that dipole is initially oriented perpendicular to the electric field and is then brought to the orientation making an angle {tex}\theta{/tex} with the field direction. Then, potential energy of the dipole, when it makes angle {tex}\theta{/tex} with the direction of the electric field can be obtained from equation (i) by setting {tex}\theta_1{/tex} = 90° and {tex}\theta_2{/tex} = {tex}\theta{/tex} . Therefore, U = pE (cos 90 o - cos {tex}\theta{/tex} ) U = -pE cos {tex}\theta{/tex} .....(ii) In vector notation, U = - {tex}\vec{p} . \overrightarrow{\mathrm{E}}{/tex}
solution_html:
Suppose that an electric dipole having dipole moment p is placed along a direction making an angle {tex}\theta{/tex} with the direction of an external uniform electric field E. Then, torque acting on the dipole is given by
{tex}\tau{/tex}= pE sin {tex}\theta{/tex}
If the dipole is rotated through an infinitesimally small angle d{tex}\theta{/tex} against the torque acting on it, then small work done
dW = {tex}\tau{/tex}d{tex}\theta{/tex} = pE sin {tex}\theta{/tex} d{tex}\theta{/tex}
Therefore, work done in rotating the dipole from its orientation making an angle {tex}\theta_1{/tex} with the direction of electric field to {tex}\theta_2{/tex} is given by
{tex}\mathrm{W}=\int_{\theta_{1}}^{\theta_{2}} p \mathrm{E} \sin \theta d \theta=p \mathrm{E}|-\cos \theta|_{\theta_{1}}^{\theta_{2}}{/tex}
or W = pE (cos {tex}\theta_1{/tex} - cos {tex}\theta_2{/tex})
This work done is stored in the dipole in the form of its potential energy. Therefore, when an electric dipole is rotated inside the electric field, potential energy stored in the electric dipole and it is given by
U = pE (cos {tex}\theta_1{/tex} - cos {tex}\theta_2{/tex}) ...(i)
Let us assume that dipole is initially oriented perpendicular to the electric field and is then brought to the orientation making an angle {tex}\theta{/tex} with the field direction. Then, potential energy of the dipole, when it makes angle {tex}\theta{/tex} with the direction of the electric field can be obtained from equation (i) by setting {tex}\theta_1{/tex} = 90° and {tex}\theta_2{/tex} = {tex}\theta{/tex}. Therefore,
U = pE (cos 90o - cos {tex}\theta{/tex})
U = -pE cos {tex}\theta{/tex} .....(ii)
In vector notation,
U = - {tex}\vec{p} . \overrightarrow{\mathrm{E}}{/tex}

subject: Physics
question_html: The following graph shows the variation of charge Q with voltage V for two capacitors K and L in which capacitor is more electrostatic energy stored?
question_text: The following graph shows the variation of charge Q with voltage V for two capacitors K and L in which capacitor is more electrostatic energy stored?
explanation: The slope of Q-V graph of the capacitor gives capacitance, therefore, capacitor L has greater capacitance than K, thus the capacitance will be more for L. Since two capacitors are given the same voltage V, therefore energy stored in a capacitor will be given by the formula. U = {tex}\frac{1}{2} {/tex} CV 2 and for the same V {tex}U \propto C{/tex} {tex}\because{/tex} C L > C k {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} U L > U k {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} Capacitor L will store more electrostatic energy.
solution: The slope of Q-V graph of the capacitor gives capacitance, therefore, capacitor L has greater capacitance than K, thus the capacitance will be more for L. Since two capacitors are given the same voltage V, therefore energy stored in a capacitor will be given by the formula. U = {tex}\frac{1}{2} {/tex} CV 2 and for the same V {tex}U \propto C{/tex} {tex}\because{/tex} C L > C k {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} U L > U k {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} Capacitor L will store more electrostatic energy.
solution_html:
The slope of Q-V graph of the capacitor gives capacitance, therefore, capacitor L has greater capacitance than K, thus the capacitance will be more for L. Since two capacitors are given the same voltage V, therefore energy stored in a capacitor will be given by the formula.
U = {tex}\frac{1}{2} {/tex} CV2 and for the same V
{tex}U \propto C{/tex}
{tex}\because{/tex} CL > Ck
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} UL > Uk
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} Capacitor L will store more electrostatic energy.

question_html: In the figure, the graph shows the variation of the total energy (U) stored in a capacitor against the value of the capacitance (C) itself. Which of the two - the charge on the capacitor or the potential used to charge it, is kept constant for this graph?
question_text: In the figure, the graph shows the variation of the total energy (U) stored in a capacitor against the value of the capacitance (C) itself. Which of the two - the charge on the capacitor or the potential used to charge it, is kept constant for this graph?
explanation: The graph shows that, U {tex}\propto{/tex} 1/C The energy stored in a capacitor is given by, U = {tex}\frac{q^{2}}{2 C}{/tex} Therefore, if U {tex}\propto{/tex} 1/C, then q must be kept constant.
solution: The graph shows that, U {tex}\propto{/tex} 1/C The energy stored in a capacitor is given by, U = {tex}\frac{q^{2}}{2 C}{/tex} Therefore, if U {tex}\propto{/tex} 1/C, then q must be kept constant.
solution_html:
The graph shows that,
U {tex}\propto{/tex} 1/C
The energy stored in a capacitor is given by,
U = {tex}\frac{q^{2}}{2 C}{/tex}
Therefore, if U {tex}\propto{/tex} 1/C, then q must be kept constant.

question_html: The two plates of a parallel plate capacitor are 4 mm apart. A slab of dielectric constant 3 and thickness 3 mm is introduced between the plates with its faces parallel to them. The distance between the plates is so adjusted that the capacitance of the capacitor becomes {tex}\frac{2}{3}{/tex}rd of its original value. What is the new distance between the plates?
question_text: The two plates of a parallel plate capacitor are 4 mm apart. A slab of dielectric constant 3 and thickness 3 mm is introduced between the plates with its faces parallel to them. The distance between the plates is so adjusted that the capacitance of the capacitor becomes {tex}\frac{2}{3}{/tex} rd of its original value. What is the new distance between the plates?
explanation: {tex}\frac{2}{3}{/tex} {tex}\times{/tex} Capacitance with air = Capacitance with dielectric or {tex}\frac{2}{3} \frac{\varepsilon_{0} A}{d}=\frac{\varepsilon_{0} A}{d^{\prime}-t+\frac{t}{k}}{/tex} or {tex}\frac{2}{3}\left(d^{\prime}-3+\frac{3}{3}\right)=d{/tex} = 4 mm or d' = 8 mm
solution: {tex}\frac{2}{3}{/tex} {tex}\times{/tex} Capacitance with air = Capacitance with dielectric or {tex}\frac{2}{3} \frac{\varepsilon_{0} A}{d}=\frac{\varepsilon_{0} A}{d^{\prime}-t+\frac{t}{k}}{/tex} or {tex}\frac{2}{3}\left(d^{\prime}-3+\frac{3}{3}\right)=d{/tex} = 4 mm or d' = 8 mm
solution_html:
{tex}\frac{2}{3}{/tex} {tex}\times{/tex} Capacitance with air = Capacitance with dielectric
or {tex}\frac{2}{3} \frac{\varepsilon_{0} A}{d}=\frac{\varepsilon_{0} A}{d^{\prime}-t+\frac{t}{k}}{/tex}
or {tex}\frac{2}{3}\left(d^{\prime}-3+\frac{3}{3}\right)=d{/tex} = 4 mm or d' = 8 mm

question_html: A {tex}500\mu C{/tex} charge is at the centre of a square of side 10 cm. Find the work done in moving a charge of {tex}10\mu C{/tex} between two diagonally opposite points on the square.
question_text: A {tex}500\mu C{/tex} charge is at the centre of a square of side 10 cm. Find the work done in moving a charge of {tex}10\mu C{/tex} between two diagonally opposite points on the square.
explanation: The points A and B are equidistant from the centre of square where charge q = 500μC is located; therefore, points A and B are at the same potential i.e., V A = V B Work done in moving charge q 0 = 10μC from A to B is W = q 0 (V B - V A ) = 0
solution: The points A and B are equidistant from the centre of square where charge q = 500μC is located; therefore, points A and B are at the same potential i.e., V A = V B Work done in moving charge q 0 = 10μC from A to B is W = q 0 (V B - V A ) = 0
solution_html:
The points A and B are equidistant from the centre of square where charge q = 500μC is located; therefore, points A and B are at the same potential i.e., VA = VB  

Work done in moving charge q0 = 10μC from A to B is W = q0 (VB - VA) = 0

question_html: Two condensers, one of capacity C and the other of capacity {tex}\frac{C}{2}{/tex} are connected to a V volt battery, as shown. The work done in charging fully both the condensers is:
question_text: Two condensers, one of capacity C and the other of capacity {tex}\frac{C}{2}{/tex} are connected to a V volt battery, as shown. The work done in charging fully both the condensers is:

options.explanation: The equivalent capacitance of the two condensers connected in parallel is {tex}C^{\prime}=C+\frac{C}{2}=\frac{3 C}{2}{/tex} The work done in charging both the capacitors is stored as potential energy. {tex}\therefore W=U=\frac{1}{2} C V^{2}=\frac{1}{2}\left(\frac{3 C}{2}\right) V^{2}=\frac{3}{4} C V^{2}{/tex}

explanation: The equivalent capacitance of the two condensers connected in parallel is {tex}C^{\prime}=C+\frac{C}{2}=\frac{3 C}{2}{/tex} The work done in charging both the capacitors is stored as potential energy. {tex}\therefore W=U=\frac{1}{2} C V^{2}=\frac{1}{2}\left(\frac{3 C}{2}\right) V^{2}=\frac{3}{4} C V^{2}{/tex}

question_text: Charges + q and - q are placed at points A and B respectively which are a distance 2 L apart, C is the midpoint between A and B. The work done in moving a charge + Q along the semicircle CRD is

options.explanation: {tex}V_{C}=\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}}\left[\frac{q}{A C}+\frac{-q}{B C}\right]=\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}}\left[\frac{q}{L}-\frac{q}{L}\right]=0{/tex} {tex}V_{D}=\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}}\left[\frac{q}{A D}+\frac{-q}{B D}\right]=\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}}\left[\frac{q}{3 L}-\frac{q}{L}\right]=-\frac{q}{6 \pi \varepsilon_{0} L}{/tex} Work done in moving the charge +Q along the semicircle CRD is {tex}W=+Q\left(V_{D}-V_{C}\right)=+Q\left(-\frac{q}{6 \pi \varepsilon_{0} L}-0\right)=-\frac{q Q}{6 \pi \varepsilon_{0} L}{/tex}

explanation: {tex}V_{C}=\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}}\left[\frac{q}{A C}+\frac{-q}{B C}\right]=\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}}\left[\frac{q}{L}-\frac{q}{L}\right]=0{/tex} {tex}V_{D}=\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}}\left[\frac{q}{A D}+\frac{-q}{B D}\right]=\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}}\left[\frac{q}{3 L}-\frac{q}{L}\right]=-\frac{q}{6 \pi \varepsilon_{0} L}{/tex} Work done in moving the charge +Q along the semicircle CRD is {tex}W=+Q\left(V_{D}-V_{C}\right)=+Q\left(-\frac{q}{6 \pi \varepsilon_{0} L}-0\right)=-\frac{q Q}{6 \pi \varepsilon_{0} L}{/tex}

subject: Physics
question_html: Define electric potential. Is it a scalar or a vector quantity?
question_text: Define electric potential. Is it a scalar or a vector quantity?
explanation: Electric potential is a pure Scalar quantity. The reason is as follows. The Electric Potential is defined as the amount of work done per unit positive charge to bring from infinity to that point under the influence of the primary charge only. U = {tex}\frac{W}{q}{/tex} and work done is defined as the dot product of force and displacement which is a scalar quantity. W = F.S
solution: Electric potential is a pure Scalar quantity. The reason is as follows. The Electric Potential is defined as the amount of work done per unit positive charge to bring from infinity to that point under the influence of the primary charge only. U = {tex}\frac{W}{q}{/tex} and work done is defined as the dot product of force and displacement which is a scalar quantity. W = F.S
solution_html:
Electric potential is a pure Scalar quantity.
The reason is as follows. The Electric Potential is defined as the amount of work done per unit positive charge to bring from infinity to that point under the influence of the primary charge only.
U = {tex}\frac{W}{q}{/tex} and work done is defined as the dot product of force and displacement which is a scalar quantity. W = F.S

question_html: Define dielectric constant in terms of the capacitance of a capacitor.
question_text: Define dielectric constant in terms of the capacitance of a capacitor.
explanation: The ratio of the capacitance (C d ) of the capacitor completely filled with the dielectric material to the capacitance (C d ) of the same capacitor with a vacuum between its plates is called the dielectric constant. {tex}\kappa=\frac{C_{d}}{C_{0}}{/tex}
solution: The ratio of the capacitance (C d ) of the capacitor completely filled with the dielectric material to the capacitance (C d ) of the same capacitor with a vacuum between its plates is called the dielectric constant. {tex}\kappa=\frac{C_{d}}{C_{0}}{/tex}
solution_html:
The ratio of the capacitance (Cd) of the capacitor completely filled with the dielectric material to the capacitance (Cd) of the same capacitor with a vacuum between its plates is called the dielectric constant.
{tex}\kappa=\frac{C_{d}}{C_{0}}{/tex}

question_text: A parallel plate air capacitor is charged to a potential difference of V volt. After disconnecting the charging battery, the distance between the plates of the capacitor is increased using an insulating handle. As a result, the potential difference between the plates.

options.explanation: As the battery is disconnected, q = constant. New capacitance, {tex}C^{\prime}=\frac{\varepsilon_{0} A}{d^{\prime}}{/tex} But d' > d, so C' < C Now {tex}V^{\prime}=\frac{q}{C^{\prime}}{/tex} As capacitance decreases, so potential difference increases.

question_html: Five capacitors, each of capacitance value C are connected as shown in the figure. The ratio of capacitance between P and R; and the capacitance between P and Q is
question_text: Five capacitors, each of capacitance value C are connected as shown in the figure. The ratio of capacitance between P and R; and the capacitance between P and Q is

options.explanation: {tex}\frac{1}{C_{P T S R}}=\frac{1}{C}+\frac{1}{C}+\frac{1}{C}=\frac{3}{C}{/tex} {tex}\frac{1}{C_{P Q R}}=\frac{1}{C}+\frac{1}{C}=\frac{2}{C}{/tex} C PR = C PTSR + C PQR = {tex}\frac{C}{3}+\frac{C}{2}=\frac{5 C}{6}{/tex} {tex}\frac{1}{C_{\text {PTSRQ }}}=\frac{1}{C}+\frac{1}{C}+\frac{1}{C}+\frac{1}{C}=\frac{4}{C}{/tex} {tex}C_{P Q}=C+\frac{C}{4}=\frac{5 C}{4}{/tex} {tex}\frac{C_{P R}}{C_{P Q}}=\frac{5 C}{6} \times \frac{4}{5 C}=\frac{2}{3}{/tex} = 2 : 3

explanation: {tex}\frac{1}{C_{P T S R}}=\frac{1}{C}+\frac{1}{C}+\frac{1}{C}=\frac{3}{C}{/tex} {tex}\frac{1}{C_{P Q R}}=\frac{1}{C}+\frac{1}{C}=\frac{2}{C}{/tex} C PR = C PTSR + C PQR = {tex}\frac{C}{3}+\frac{C}{2}=\frac{5 C}{6}{/tex} {tex}\frac{1}{C_{\text {PTSRQ }}}=\frac{1}{C}+\frac{1}{C}+\frac{1}{C}+\frac{1}{C}=\frac{4}{C}{/tex} {tex}C_{P Q}=C+\frac{C}{4}=\frac{5 C}{4}{/tex} {tex}\frac{C_{P R}}{C_{P Q}}=\frac{5 C}{6} \times \frac{4}{5 C}=\frac{2}{3}{/tex} = 2 : 3

question_html: In the circuit shown in the figure, the potential difference across the 4.5 {tex}\mu{/tex}F capacitor is:
question_text: In the circuit shown in the figure, the potential difference across the 4.5 {tex}\mu{/tex} F capacitor is:

options.explanation: Effective capacitance of 3 {tex}\mu{/tex} F and 6 {tex}\mu{/tex} F capacitors connected in parallel, C' = 3 + 6 = 9 {tex}\mu{/tex} F Now C' and 4.5 {tex}\mu{/tex} F capacitors are in series {tex}\therefore C_{e q}=\frac{9 \times 4.5}{9+4.5}=3 \mu \mathrm{F}{/tex} Charge through the circuit, q = C eq V = 3 {tex}\times{/tex} 12 = 36 {tex}\mu{/tex} C P.D. across 4.5 pF capacitor, {tex}V=\frac{q}{C}=\frac{36 \mu C}{4.5 \mu F}{/tex} = 8 volt.

explanation: Effective capacitance of 3 {tex}\mu{/tex} F and 6 {tex}\mu{/tex} F capacitors connected in parallel, C' = 3 + 6 = 9 {tex}\mu{/tex} F Now C' and 4.5 {tex}\mu{/tex} F capacitors are in series {tex}\therefore C_{e q}=\frac{9 \times 4.5}{9+4.5}=3 \mu \mathrm{F}{/tex} Charge through the circuit, q = C eq V = 3 {tex}\times{/tex} 12 = 36 {tex}\mu{/tex} C P.D. across 4.5 pF capacitor, {tex}V=\frac{q}{C}=\frac{36 \mu C}{4.5 \mu F}{/tex} = 8 volt.

question_html: A network of four capacitors of capacitances equal to C1 = C, C2 = 2C, C3 = 3C and C4 = 4C are connected to a battery as shown in the figure. The ratio of the charges on C2 and C4 is:
question_text: A network of four capacitors of capacitances equal to C 1 = C, C 2 = 2C, C 3 = 3C and C 4 = 4C are connected to a battery as shown in the figure. The ratio of the charges on C 2 and C 4 is:

options.explanation: The series combination of C 1 , C 2 and C 3 is in parallel with C 4 . The equivalent capacitance C 123 of the series combination is given by {tex}\frac{1}{C_{123}}=\frac{1}{C_{1}}+\frac{1}{C_{2}}+\frac{1}{C_{3}}=\frac{1}{C}+\frac{1}{2 C}+\frac{1}{3 C}=\frac{11}{6 C}{/tex} or {tex}C_{123}=\frac{6 C}{11}{/tex} Let q 1 , q 2 , q 3 , and q 4 be the charges on the respective capacitors. Since in a series combination, charge on all the capacitors is same, q 1 = q 2 = q 3 = C 123 {tex} \times V=\frac{6 C V}{11}{/tex} Also, the charge on the capacitor C 4 , q 4 = C 4 V = 4CV {tex}\therefore \frac{q_{2}}{q_{4}}=\frac{6 \mathrm{CV} / 11}{4 \mathrm{CV}}=\frac{3}{22}{/tex}

explanation: The series combination of C 1 , C 2 and C 3 is in parallel with C 4 . The equivalent capacitance C 123 of the series combination is given by {tex}\frac{1}{C_{123}}=\frac{1}{C_{1}}+\frac{1}{C_{2}}+\frac{1}{C_{3}}=\frac{1}{C}+\frac{1}{2 C}+\frac{1}{3 C}=\frac{11}{6 C}{/tex} or {tex}C_{123}=\frac{6 C}{11}{/tex} Let q 1 , q 2 , q 3 , and q 4 be the charges on the respective capacitors. Since in a series combination, charge on all the capacitors is same, q 1 = q 2 = q 3 = C 123 {tex} \times V=\frac{6 C V}{11}{/tex} Also, the charge on the capacitor C 4 , q 4 = C 4 V = 4CV {tex}\therefore \frac{q_{2}}{q_{4}}=\frac{6 \mathrm{CV} / 11}{4 \mathrm{CV}}=\frac{3}{22}{/tex}

subject: Physics
question_html: In a certain charge distribution, all points having zero potential can be joined by a circle S. Points inside S have positive potential and points outside S have negative potential. A positive charge, which is free to move, is placed inside S.
question_text: In a certain charge distribution, all points having zero potential can be joined by a circle S. Points inside S have positive potential and points outside S have negative potential. A positive charge, which is free to move, is placed inside S.

options.text: It must cross S at some time
options.html: It must cross S at some time
options.explanation: A positive charge tends to move from a region of higher potential to that of lower potential. So the positive charge will cross the circle S at some time.

options.text: It can move inside S, but it cannot cross S
options.html: It can move inside S, but it cannot cross S

options.text: It may move, but will ultimately return to its starting point
options.html: It may move, but will ultimately return to its starting point

correct_option_text: It must cross S at some time
explanation: A positive charge tends to move from a region of higher potential to that of lower potential. So the positive charge will cross the circle S at some time.

question_html:
A 10 {tex}\mu{/tex}F capacitor is charged by a 30 V d.c. supply and then connected across an uncharged 50 {tex}\mu{/tex}F capacitor. Calculate

  1. the final potential difference across the combination,
  2. the initial and final energies. How will you account for the difference in energy?
question_text: A 10 {tex}\mu{/tex} F capacitor is charged by a 30 V d.c. supply and then connected across an uncharged 50 {tex}\mu{/tex} F capacitor. Calculate the final potential difference across the combination, the initial and final energies. How will you account for the difference in energy?
explanation: Here C 1 = 10 {tex}\mu{/tex} F = 10 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -6 F, V 1 = 30 V C 2 = 50 {tex}\mu{/tex} F = 50 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -6 F, V 2 = 0 Common potential, {tex}V=\frac{C_{1} V_{1}+C_{2} V_{2}}{C_{1}+C_{2}}=\frac{10 \times 10^{-6} \times 30+0}{(10+50) \times 10^{-6}}=5 \mathrm{~V}{/tex} Initial electrostatic energy of 10 {tex}\mu{/tex} F capacitor, {tex}u_{i}=\frac{1}{2} C_{1} V_{1}^{2}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1}{2} \times{/tex} 10 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -6 {tex}\times{/tex} (30) 2 = 4.5 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -3 J Final electrostatic energy of the combination, U f = {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} (10+50) {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -6 {tex}\times{/tex} (5) 2 = 0.75 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -3 J Loss in energy = U i - U f = 3.75 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -3 J The difference in energy is lost in the form of heat and electromagnetic radiation as the charge flows from first capacitor to the second capacitor.
solution: Here C 1 = 10 {tex}\mu{/tex} F = 10 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -6 F, V 1 = 30 V C 2 = 50 {tex}\mu{/tex} F = 50 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -6 F, V 2 = 0 Common potential, {tex}V=\frac{C_{1} V_{1}+C_{2} V_{2}}{C_{1}+C_{2}}=\frac{10 \times 10^{-6} \times 30+0}{(10+50) \times 10^{-6}}=5 \mathrm{~V}{/tex} Initial electrostatic energy of 10 {tex}\mu{/tex} F capacitor, {tex}u_{i}=\frac{1}{2} C_{1} V_{1}^{2}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1}{2} \times{/tex} 10 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -6 {tex}\times{/tex} (30) 2 = 4.5 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -3 J Final electrostatic energy of the combination, U f = {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} (10+50) {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -6 {tex}\times{/tex} (5) 2 = 0.75 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -3 J Loss in energy = U i - U f = 3.75 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -3 J The difference in energy is lost in the form of heat and electromagnetic radiation as the charge flows from first capacitor to the second capacitor.
solution_html:
Here C1 = 10 {tex}\mu{/tex}F = 10 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-6 F, V1 = 30 V
C2 = 50 {tex}\mu{/tex} F = 50 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-6F, V2 = 0

  1. Common potential,
    {tex}V=\frac{C_{1} V_{1}+C_{2} V_{2}}{C_{1}+C_{2}}=\frac{10 \times 10^{-6} \times 30+0}{(10+50) \times 10^{-6}}=5 \mathrm{~V}{/tex}
  2. Initial electrostatic energy of 10{tex}\mu{/tex}F capacitor,
    {tex}u_{i}=\frac{1}{2} C_{1} V_{1}^{2}{/tex}
    ={tex}\frac{1}{2} \times{/tex} 10 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-6 {tex}\times{/tex}(30)2 = 4.5 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-3 J
    Final electrostatic energy of the combination,
    Uf = {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex}(10+50) {tex}\times{/tex} 10-6 {tex}\times{/tex} (5)2 = 0.75 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-3 J
    Loss in energy = Ui - Uf = 3.75 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-3 J
    The difference in energy is lost in the form of heat and electromagnetic radiation as the charge flows from first capacitor to the second capacitor.

question_text: A 40 F capacitor in a defibrillator is charged to 3000 V. The energy stored in the capacitor is sent through the patient during a pulse of duration 2 ms. The power delivered to the patient is

question_html: Three capacitors each of capacity {tex}4\mu \text F{/tex} are to be connected in such a way that the effective capacitance is {tex}6\mu \text F{/tex}. This can be done by:
question_text: Three capacitors each of capacity {tex}4\mu \text F{/tex} are to be connected in such a way that the effective capacitance is {tex}6\mu \text F{/tex} . This can be done by:

options.text: Connecting two in series and one in parallel
options.html: Connecting two in series and one in parallel
options.explanation: The series combination of two capacitors should be connected in parallel with the third capacitor. Then {tex}C_{\text {eff }}=\frac{C_{1} C_{2}}{C_{1}+C_{2}}+C_{3}=\frac{4 \times 4}{4+4}+4=2+4=6 \mu \mathrm{F}{/tex}

options.text: Connecting two in parallel and one in series
options.html: Connecting two in parallel and one in series

options.text: Connecting all of them in series
options.html: Connecting all of them in series

correct_option_text: Connecting two in series and one in parallel
explanation: The series combination of two capacitors should be connected in parallel with the third capacitor. Then {tex}C_{\text {eff }}=\frac{C_{1} C_{2}}{C_{1}+C_{2}}+C_{3}=\frac{4 \times 4}{4+4}+4=2+4=6 \mu \mathrm{F}{/tex}

subject: Physics
question_html: A battery of 10 V is connected to a capacitor of capacity 0.1 F. The battery is now removed and this capacitor is connected to a second uncharged capacitor. If the charge distributes equally on these two capacitors, find the total energy stored in the two capacitors. Further, compare this energy with the initial energy stored in the first capacitor.
question_text: A battery of 10 V is connected to a capacitor of capacity 0.1 F. The battery is now removed and this capacitor is connected to a second uncharged capacitor. If the charge distributes equally on these two capacitors, find the total energy stored in the two capacitors. Further, compare this energy with the initial energy stored in the first capacitor.
explanation: Initial energy stored in the first capacitor is {tex}U_{i}=\frac{1}{2} C V^{2}=\frac{1}{2} \times 0.1 \times(10)^{2}{/tex} = 5.0 When the first capacitor is connected to the second uncharged capacitor, the charge distributes equally. This implies that the capacitance of the second capacitor is also C. The voltage across each capacitor is now {tex}\frac V2{/tex} . The final total energy stored in the two capacitors is {tex}U_{f}=\frac{1}{2} C\left(\frac{V}{2}\right)^{2}+\frac{1}{2} C\left(\frac{V}{2}\right)^{2}=\frac{1}{4} C V^{2}{/tex} = 2.5 {tex}\therefore \frac{U_{f}}{U_{i}}=\frac{2.5}{5.0}=\frac{1}{2}{/tex} = 1 : 2
solution: Initial energy stored in the first capacitor is {tex}U_{i}=\frac{1}{2} C V^{2}=\frac{1}{2} \times 0.1 \times(10)^{2}{/tex} = 5.0 When the first capacitor is connected to the second uncharged capacitor, the charge distributes equally. This implies that the capacitance of the second capacitor is also C. The voltage across each capacitor is now {tex}\frac V2{/tex} . The final total energy stored in the two capacitors is {tex}U_{f}=\frac{1}{2} C\left(\frac{V}{2}\right)^{2}+\frac{1}{2} C\left(\frac{V}{2}\right)^{2}=\frac{1}{4} C V^{2}{/tex} = 2.5 {tex}\therefore \frac{U_{f}}{U_{i}}=\frac{2.5}{5.0}=\frac{1}{2}{/tex} = 1 : 2
solution_html:
Initial energy stored in the first capacitor is {tex}U_{i}=\frac{1}{2} C V^{2}=\frac{1}{2} \times 0.1 \times(10)^{2}{/tex} = 5.0
When the first capacitor is connected to the second uncharged capacitor, the charge distributes equally. This implies that the capacitance of the second capacitor is also C. The voltage across each capacitor is now {tex}\frac V2{/tex}. The final total energy stored in the two capacitors is
{tex}U_{f}=\frac{1}{2} C\left(\frac{V}{2}\right)^{2}+\frac{1}{2} C\left(\frac{V}{2}\right)^{2}=\frac{1}{4} C V^{2}{/tex} = 2.5
{tex}\therefore \frac{U_{f}}{U_{i}}=\frac{2.5}{5.0}=\frac{1}{2}{/tex} = 1 : 2

subject: Physics
question_html: Calculate the equivalent capacitance between points A and B of the circuit given below.
question_text: Calculate the equivalent capacitance between points A and B of the circuit given below.
explanation: Capacitors C 2 , C 3 and C 4 are connected in series, their equivalent capacitance q is given by {tex}\frac{1}{C_{7}}=\frac{1}{C_{2}}+\frac{1}{C_{3}}+\frac{1}{C_{4}}=\frac{1}{4}+\frac{1}{2}+\frac{1}{4}=\frac{1}{1}{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} C 7 = 1 {tex}\mu{/tex} F Also, C 5 and C 6 are in series, the equivalent capacitance is {tex}C_{8}=\frac{C_{5} \times C_{6}}{C_{5}+C_{6}}=\frac{2 \times 4}{2+4}=\frac{4}{3} \mu F{/tex} C 7 and C 8 form a parallel combination of capacitance, {tex}C_{9}=C_{7}+C_{8}=1+\frac{4}{3}=\frac{7}{3} \mu F{/tex}
solution: Capacitors C 2 , C 3 and C 4 are connected in series, their equivalent capacitance q is given by {tex}\frac{1}{C_{7}}=\frac{1}{C_{2}}+\frac{1}{C_{3}}+\frac{1}{C_{4}}=\frac{1}{4}+\frac{1}{2}+\frac{1}{4}=\frac{1}{1}{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} C 7 = 1 {tex}\mu{/tex} F Also, C 5 and C 6 are in series, the equivalent capacitance is {tex}C_{8}=\frac{C_{5} \times C_{6}}{C_{5}+C_{6}}=\frac{2 \times 4}{2+4}=\frac{4}{3} \mu F{/tex} C 7 and C 8 form a parallel combination of capacitance, {tex}C_{9}=C_{7}+C_{8}=1+\frac{4}{3}=\frac{7}{3} \mu F{/tex}
solution_html:
Capacitors C2, C3 and C4 are connected in series, their equivalent capacitance q is given by
{tex}\frac{1}{C_{7}}=\frac{1}{C_{2}}+\frac{1}{C_{3}}+\frac{1}{C_{4}}=\frac{1}{4}+\frac{1}{2}+\frac{1}{4}=\frac{1}{1}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} C7 = 1{tex}\mu{/tex}F
Also, C5 and C6 are in series, the equivalent capacitance is
{tex}C_{8}=\frac{C_{5} \times C_{6}}{C_{5}+C_{6}}=\frac{2 \times 4}{2+4}=\frac{4}{3} \mu F{/tex}
C7 and C8 form a parallel combination of capacitance,
{tex}C_{9}=C_{7}+C_{8}=1+\frac{4}{3}=\frac{7}{3} \mu F{/tex}

question_html: Calculate the capacitance of the capacitor in Fig. if the equivalent capacitance of the combination between A and B is 15 {tex}\mu{/tex}F.
question_text: Calculate the capacitance of the capacitor in Fig. if the equivalent capacitance of the combination between A and B is 15 {tex}\mu{/tex} F.
explanation: The combined capacitance of the parallel combination of two 10 {tex}\mu{/tex} F capacitors is 20 {tex}\mu{/tex} F. This combination is connected in series with capacitance C. {tex}\therefore \frac{1}{20}+\frac{1}{C}=\frac{1}{15}{/tex} or {tex}\frac{1}{C}=\frac{1}{15}-\frac{1}{20}=\frac{4-3}{60}=\frac{1}{60}{/tex} or C = 60 {tex}\mu{/tex} F
solution: The combined capacitance of the parallel combination of two 10 {tex}\mu{/tex} F capacitors is 20 {tex}\mu{/tex} F. This combination is connected in series with capacitance C. {tex}\therefore \frac{1}{20}+\frac{1}{C}=\frac{1}{15}{/tex} or {tex}\frac{1}{C}=\frac{1}{15}-\frac{1}{20}=\frac{4-3}{60}=\frac{1}{60}{/tex} or C = 60 {tex}\mu{/tex} F
solution_html:
The combined capacitance of the parallel combination of two 10 {tex}\mu{/tex}F capacitors is 20 {tex}\mu{/tex}F. This combination is connected in series with capacitance C.
{tex}\therefore \frac{1}{20}+\frac{1}{C}=\frac{1}{15}{/tex} or {tex}\frac{1}{C}=\frac{1}{15}-\frac{1}{20}=\frac{4-3}{60}=\frac{1}{60}{/tex}
or C = 60 {tex}\mu{/tex}F

subject: Physics
question_html:
1. A 900 pF capacitor is charged by a 100 V battery. How much electrostatic energy is stored by the capacitor?
  2. The capacitor is disconnected from the battery and connected to another 900 pF capacitor. What is the electrostatic energy stored by the system?
  3. Where has the remainder of the energy gone?
question_text: A 900 pF capacitor is charged by a 100 V battery. How much electrostatic energy is stored by the capacitor? The capacitor is disconnected from the battery and connected to another 900 pF capacitor. What is the electrostatic energy stored by the system? Where has the remainder of the energy gone?
explanation: The charge on the capacitor is q = C V = 900 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -12 F {tex}\times{/tex} 100 V = 9 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 8 C The energy stored by the capacitor is {tex}U=\frac{1}{2} C V^{2}=\frac{1}{2} q V=\frac{1}{2} \times 9 \times 10^{-8} \mathrm{C} \times 100 \mathrm{~V}{/tex} = 4.5 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -6 J. In the steady situation, the two capacitors have their positive plates at the same potential and their negative plates at the same potential. Let the common potential difference be V'. The charge on each capacitor is then q = CV'. By charge conservation, q' = {tex}\frac{q}{C}{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} Total energy of the system {tex}=2 \times \frac{1}{2} q^{\prime} V^{\prime}=q^{\prime} V^{\prime}=q^{\prime} \cdot \frac{q^{\prime}}{C}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{1}{4}, \frac{q^{2}}{C}=\frac{1}{4} \cdot q V=\frac{1}{2} \times \frac{1}{2} q V\left[\because q^{\prime}=\frac{q}{2} \text { and } \frac{q}{C}=V\right]{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1}{2} \times {/tex} 4.5 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -6 J = 2.25 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -6 J There is a transient period before the system settles to the situation (ii). During this period, a transient current flows from the first capacitor to the second. Energy is lost during this time in the form of heat and electromagnetic radiation.
solution: The charge on the capacitor is q = C V = 900 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -12 F {tex}\times{/tex} 100 V = 9 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 8 C The energy stored by the capacitor is {tex}U=\frac{1}{2} C V^{2}=\frac{1}{2} q V=\frac{1}{2} \times 9 \times 10^{-8} \mathrm{C} \times 100 \mathrm{~V}{/tex} = 4.5 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -6 J. In the steady situation, the two capacitors have their positive plates at the same potential and their negative plates at the same potential. Let the common potential difference be V'. The charge on each capacitor is then q = CV'. By charge conservation, q' = {tex}\frac{q}{C}{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} Total energy of the system {tex}=2 \times \frac{1}{2} q^{\prime} V^{\prime}=q^{\prime} V^{\prime}=q^{\prime} \cdot \frac{q^{\prime}}{C}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{1}{4}, \frac{q^{2}}{C}=\frac{1}{4} \cdot q V=\frac{1}{2} \times \frac{1}{2} q V\left[\because q^{\prime}=\frac{q}{2} \text { and } \frac{q}{C}=V\right]{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1}{2} \times {/tex} 4.5 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -6 J = 2.25 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -6 J There is a transient period before the system settles to the situation (ii). During this period, a transient current flows from the first capacitor to the second. Energy is lost during this time in the form of heat and electromagnetic radiation.
solution_html:
1. The charge on the capacitor is q = C V = 900 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-12 F {tex}\times{/tex} 100 V = 9 {tex}\times{/tex} 108 C
    The energy stored by the capacitor is
    {tex}U=\frac{1}{2} C V^{2}=\frac{1}{2} q V=\frac{1}{2} \times 9 \times 10^{-8} \mathrm{C} \times 100 \mathrm{~V}{/tex}
    = 4.5 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-6 J.
  2. In the steady situation, the two capacitors have their positive plates at the same potential and their negative plates at the same potential. Let the common potential difference be V'. The charge on each capacitor is then q = CV'. By charge conservation, q' = {tex}\frac{q}{C}{/tex}
    {tex}\therefore{/tex} Total energy of the system
    {tex}=2 \times \frac{1}{2} q^{\prime} V^{\prime}=q^{\prime} V^{\prime}=q^{\prime} \cdot \frac{q^{\prime}}{C}{/tex}
    {tex}=\frac{1}{4}, \frac{q^{2}}{C}=\frac{1}{4} \cdot q V=\frac{1}{2} \times \frac{1}{2} q V\left[\because q^{\prime}=\frac{q}{2} \text { and } \frac{q}{C}=V\right]{/tex}
    = {tex}\frac{1}{2} \times {/tex}4.5 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-6 J = 2.25 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-6 J
  3. There is a transient period before the system settles to the situation (ii). During this period, a transient current flows from the first capacitor to the second. Energy is lost during this time in the form of heat and electromagnetic radiation.
